text: It was exhausting work, carried on, hour after hour, at top speed. Out on the broad verandas of the hotel, men and women, in cool white, sipped iced drinks and kept their circulation down. But in the laundry the air was sizzling. The huge stove roared red hot and white hot, while the irons, moving over the damp cloth, sent up clouds of steam. The heat of these irons was different from that used by housewives. An iron that stood the ordinary test of a wet finger was too cold for Joe and Martin, and such test was useless. They went wholly by holding the irons close to their cheeks, gauging the heat by some secret mental process that Martin admired but could not understand. When the fresh irons proved too hot, they hooked them on iron rods and dipped them into cold water. This again required a precise and subtle judgment. A fraction of a second too long in the water and the fine and silken edge of the proper heat was lost, and Martin found time to marvel at the accuracy he developed—an automatic accuracy, founded upon criteria that were machine-like and unerring.

The work was brutally draining, pushed along at breakneck pace for hour after unrelenting hour. Out on the hotel’s wide, shaded verandas, men and women clad in crisp, cool white clothing sipped frosty drinks to keep their bodies from overheating. But inside the laundry room, the air was so scorching it practically sizzled. The massive heating stove roared, glowing first dull red then searing white, and every time the irons glided over damp fabric, thick plumes of steam billowed upward. These irons ran far hotter than the ones ordinary housewives used at home: if an iron passed the standard test of a flicked wet finger hissing on its surface, it was still far too cool for Joe and Martin to use, making that old trick completely useless here. Instead, they relied entirely on holding the irons just a hair away from their cheeks, judging the temperature through some quiet, instinctive calculation that Martin found impressive even though he couldn’t wrap his head around how it worked. When the newly heated irons turned out to be too hot, they slung them onto metal hooks attached to iron rods and dipped them briefly in cold water. This step, too, demanded an incredibly precise, nuanced sense of timing. Even a split second too long submerged, and that perfect, delicate sweet spot of ideal heat was gone entirely. Martin still found himself awed sometimes at the precision he’d built up over time—an automatic, unthinking accuracy, rooted in standards as consistent and flawless as any well-oiled machine.

After the ceremony was performed a large party assembled at my father’s, but it was agreed that Elizabeth and I should commence our journey by water, sleeping that night at Evian and continuing our voyage on the following day. The day was fair, the wind favourable; all smiled on our nuptial embarkation.

Once the marriage rites were concluded, a great company gathered at my father’s house, yet it was arranged that Elizabeth and I would begin our journey by water, resting that night at Evian and continuing our travels the next morning. The day was bright and the wind proved steady; everything seemed to smile upon our wedding departure.

“Now, Cainy, shoulder your two, and off. Good morning, Mr. Boldwood.” The shepherd lifted the sixteen large legs and four small bodies he had himself brought, and vanished with them in the direction of the lambing field hard by—their frames being now in a sleek and hopeful state, pleasantly contrasting with their death’s-door plight of half an hour before.

“Now, Cainy, shoulder your two and be off. Good morning, Mr. Boldwood.” The shepherd hoisted up the sixteen long legs and four little bodies that he himself had fetched, and disappeared with them toward the lambing field nearby—their bodies now in a smooth, thriving condition that made a pleasing contrast with their near-dead state only half an hour earlier.

After a time she arose, and with feeble and tottering steps ascended the street. The astonished listener remained motionless on his post for some minutes afterwards, and having ascertained, with many cautious glances round him, that he was again alone, crept slowly from his hiding-place, and returned, stealthily and in the shade of the wall, in the same manner as he had descended.

After a time she rose and, with weak and unsteady steps, went up the street. The astonished listener stood motionless at his post for several minutes afterward, and, after satisfying himself with many wary glances around that he was once more alone, he crept slowly out of his hiding-place and returned, stealthily and in the shadow of the wall, by the same route he had taken in coming down.

“You can bring up some reed-sheaves to me, one by one, ma’am; if you are not afraid to come up the ladder in the dark,” said Gabriel. “Every moment is precious now, and that would save a good deal of time. It is not very dark when the lightning has been gone a bit.”

“You might pass the bundled stalks to me, one after another, ma’am, if you’ve no dread of climbing the rungs in the gloom,” said Aldric. “Each instant matters now, and that would spare us a fair stretch of time. The darkness lifts a little once the lightning has flickered away.”

Topic-aware Convolutional Seq2Seq. The Topic-aware Convolutional Sequence to Sequence model (TCONVS2S) introduced by Narayan et al. (2018a) is an abstractive system which is conditioned on the article's topics and based entirely on Convolutional Neural Networks (Gehring et al., 2017) . TCONVS2S is better suited for extreme summarization, as convolution layers capture long-range dependencies between words in the document more effectively than RNNs. Simultaneously, the convolutional encoder associates each word with a topic vector, capturing whether it is representative of the document's content.

metadata.title: On Faithfulness and Factuality in Abstractive Summarization

text: Topic-aware Convolutional Seq2Seq. The Topic-aware Convolutional Sequence-to-Sequence model (TCONVS2S) proposed by Narayan et al. (2018a) is an abstractive summarization system conditioned on the article’s topics and built entirely on Convolutional Neural Networks (Gehring et al., 2017). TCONVS2S is particularly well suited to extreme summarization, since convolutional layers can model long-range dependencies among document words more effectively than RNNs. At the same time, the convolutional encoder links each token to a topic vector, thereby capturing whether it is representative of the document’s content.

We introduce BPE-dropout -simple and effective subword regularization, which operates within the standard BPE framework. The only difference from BPE is how a word is segmented during model training: BPE-dropout randomly drops some merges from the BPE merge table, which results in different segmentations for the same word. Models trained with BPE-dropout (1) outperform BPE and the previous subword regularization on a wide range of translation tasks, (2) have better quality of learned embeddings, (3) are more robust to noisy input. Future research directions include adaptive dropout rates for different merges and an in-depth analysis of other pathologies in learned token embeddings for different segmentations.

metadata.title: BPE-Dropout: Simple and Effective Subword Regularization

text: We propose BPE-dropout, a straightforward yet effective subword regularization technique that functions within the conventional BPE pipeline. Its sole departure from standard BPE lies in how words are segmented during training: BPE-dropout stochastically omits certain merges from the BPE merge table, yielding varied segmentations of the same word. Models trained with BPE-dropout (1) surpass both BPE and prior subword regularization methods across diverse translation benchmarks, (2) exhibit enhanced learned embedding quality, and (3) demonstrate greater resilience to noisy inputs. Promising directions for future work include adapting dropout rates per merge and conducting a deeper examination of other pathologies in token embeddings induced by differing segmentation schemes.

Existing semantically-motivated metrics for reference-free evaluation of MT systems have so far displayed rather poor correlation with human estimates of translation quality. In this work, we investigate a range of reference-free metrics based on cutting-edge models for inducing cross-lingual semantic representations: cross-lingual (contextualized) word embeddings and cross-lingual sentence embeddings. We have identified some scenarios in which these metrics fail, prominently their inability to punish literal word-by-word translations (the so-called "translationese"). We have investigated two different mechanisms for mitigating this undesired phenomenon: (1) an additional (weakly-supervised) cross-lingual alignment step, reducing the mismatch between representations of mutual translations, and 2language modeling (LM) on the target side, which is inherently equipped to punish "unnatural" sentences in the target language. We show that the reference-free coupling of cross-lingual similarity scores with the target-side language model surpasses the reference-based BLEU in segment-level MT evaluation. We believe our results have two relevant implications. First, they portray the viability of referencefree MT evaluation and warrant wider research efforts in this direction. Second, they indicate that reference-free MT evaluation may be the most challenging ("adversarial") evaluation task for multilingual text encoders as it uncovers some of their shortcomings-prominently, the inability to capture semantically non-sensical word-by-word translations or paraphrases-which remain hidden in their common evaluation scenarios.

metadata.title: On the Limitations of Cross-lingual Encoders as Exposed by Reference-Free Machine Translation Evaluation

text: Existing semantically grounded metrics for reference-free machine translation (MT) system evaluation have thus far exhibited consistently weak correlation with human judgments of translation quality. In the present work, we examine a suite of reference-free metrics built on state-of-the-art models for deriving cross-lingual semantic representations: cross-lingual contextualized word embeddings and cross-lingual sentence embeddings. We identify key failure modes for these metrics, most notably their failure to penalize literal, word-for-word translations, a phenomenon widely referred to as “translationese.” We test two distinct mechanisms to mitigate this undesirable limitation: (1) an auxiliary weakly supervised cross-lingual alignment step that reduces representational mismatch between pairs of mutual translations, and (2) target-side language modeling (LM), which is natively suited to penalizing unnatural phrasing and ungrammatical sentences in the target language. We demonstrate that a reference-free metric combining cross-lingual similarity scores with target-side LM scoring outperforms the reference-based BLEU metric on segment-level MT evaluation tasks. Our findings carry two notable implications. First, they confirm the feasibility of reference-free MT evaluation and justify expanded research investment in this direction. Second, they indicate that reference-free MT evaluation represents a uniquely challenging “adversarial” evaluation task for multilingual text encoders, as it exposes critical limitations that remain hidden in these encoders’ standard evaluation scenarios—most notably, an inability to detect semantically incoherent word-for-word translations or invalid paraphrases.

She had spoken frankly, and neither asked nor expected any reply from Gabriel more satisfactory than that she had obtained. Yet in the centremost parts of her complicated heart there existed at this minute a little pang of disappointment, for a reason she would not allow herself to recognize. Oak had not once wished her free that he might marry her himself—had not once said, “I could wait for you as well as he.” That was the insect sting. Not that she would have listened to any such hypothesis. O no—for wasn’t she saying all the time that such thoughts of the future were improper, and wasn’t Gabriel far too poor a man to speak sentiment to her? Yet he might have just hinted about that old love of his, and asked, in a playful off-hand way, if he might speak of it. It would have seemed pretty and sweet, if no more; and then she would have shown how kind and inoffensive a woman’s “No” can sometimes be. But to give such cool advice—the very advice she had asked for—it ruffled our heroine all the afternoon.

She had spoken plainly, and she did not ask or expect any answer from Gabriel more satisfying than the one she had received. And yet, at the very center of her complicated heart, there was at that moment a small stab of disappointment for a reason she refused to admit even to herself. Oak had not once wished her free so that he might marry her himself—had not once said, “I could wait for you as well as he.” That was the little sting. Not that she would have listened to any such notion. Oh no—for wasn’t she saying all the while that thoughts of the future were improper, and wasn’t Gabriel far too poor a man for sentiment with her? And yet he might at least have hinted at that old love of his, and asked, in some lightly teasing way, whether he might mention it. It would have been touching and sweet, if nothing else; and then she would have shown how gentle and harmless a woman’s “No” can sometimes be. But to offer such cool advice—the very advice she had sought—kept our heroine unsettled all afternoon.

Mr. Britling dwelt upon this idea of the specialised character of the British army and navy and government. It seemed to him to be the clue to everything that was jarring in the London spectacle. The army had been a thing aloof, for a special end. It had developed all the characteristics of a caste. It had very high standards along the lines of its specialisation, but it was inadaptable and conservative. Its exclusiveness was not so much a deliberate culture as a consequence of its detached function. It touched the ordinary social body chiefly through three other specialised bodies, the court, the church, and the stage. Apart from that it saw the great unofficial civilian world as something vague, something unsympathetic, something possibly antagonistic, which it comforted itself by snubbing when it dared and tricking when it could, something that projected members of Parliament towards it and was stingy about money. Directly one grasped how apart the army lived from the ordinary life of the community, from industrialism or from economic necessities, directly one understood that the great mass of Englishmen were simply "outsiders" to the War Office mind, just as they were "outsiders" to the political clique, one began to realise the complete unfitness of either government or War Office for the conduct of so great a national effort as was now needed. These people "up there" did not know anything of the broad mass of English life at all, they did not know how or where things were made; when they wanted things they just went to a shop somewhere and got them. This was the necessary psychology of a small army under a clique government. Nothing else was to be expected. But now--somehow--the nation had to take hold of the government that it had neglected so long....

Mr. Sterling reflected deeply on the insular nature of the nation’s military and administrative machinery. To his mind, this provided the essential explanation for every dissonance currently marring the metropolitan scene. The armed forces had long been a sequestered entity, forged for a narrow utility. It had acquired the rigid features of an exclusive guild, maintaining exacting standards within its own sphere while remaining profoundly inflexible. This seclusion was not a calculated affectation so much as a natural result of its specialized role. It engaged with the broader social fabric only through a few similarly distinct channels: the Court, the Cathedral, and the Playhouse. Beyond these narrow apertures, it viewed the sprawling civilian world as a misty, indifferent, and potentially hostile landscape—a place that merely spawned meddling politicians and withheld necessary funds—which it sought to either ignore with contempt or circumvent with craft. As soon as one perceived the profound distance between the military life and the realities of commerce or labor, and understood that the average citizen was a mere "layman" to the official mind, the utter incapacity of the Ministry to direct a massive national struggle became obvious. Those in power lacked any grasp of the fundamental rhythms of the country; they were oblivious to the origins of goods, operating on the assumption that anything required could be simply procured from a storefront. This was the inevitable perspective of a professional cadre under a government of cliques. No other outcome was possible. But now, through some urgent necessity, the populace had to seize control of the state it had for too long allowed to drift.

“There is a great deal to fear,” was gravely answered, in a voice which Eustacia recognized as that of the only surgeon in the district. “She has suffered somewhat from the bite of the adder; but it is exhaustion which has overpowered her. My impression is that her walk must have been exceptionally long.”

“We have no small cause for alarm,” came the grave, measured reply, and Elowen recognized the tone instantly as belonging to the hamlet’s only surgeon. “The adder’s bite has caused her real distress, but it is utter exhaustion that has overpowered her. My sense is that she must have been walking for an extraordinarily long distance before she collapsed.”

“But I have not finished my story,” she said. “He worked, so father says, as no other office boy he ever had. Mr. Butler was always eager to work. He never was late, and he was usually at the office a few minutes before his regular time. And yet he saved his time. Every spare moment was devoted to study. He studied book-keeping and type-writing, and he paid for lessons in shorthand by dictating at night to a court reporter who needed practice. He quickly became a clerk, and he made himself invaluable. Father appreciated him and saw that he was bound to rise. It was on father’s suggestion that he went to law college. He became a lawyer, and hardly was he back in the office when father took him in as junior partner. He is a great man. He refused the United States Senate several times, and father says he could become a justice of the Supreme Court any time a vacancy occurs, if he wants to. Such a life is an inspiration to all of us. It shows us that a man with will may rise superior to his environment.”

I haven't finished my account yet," she remarked. "According to my father, he worked with a level of dedication unlike any other office assistant he’d ever hired. Mr. Butler was always driven. He was never late; in fact, he usually arrived at the office a few minutes ahead of schedule. Even so, he was incredibly disciplined with his time, dedicating every spare moment to his studies. He mastered bookkeeping and typing, and he even managed to pay for shorthand lessons by spending his evenings dictating for a court reporter in need of practice. He was quickly promoted to a clerkship and made himself indispensable. Father recognized his value and saw that he was destined for success. It was actually at Father's suggestion that he attended law school. He became an attorney, and he had barely returned to the firm before Father made him a junior partner. He is an extraordinary man. He has turned down a seat in the U.S. Senate several times, and Father claims he could be appointed to the Supreme Court whenever a vacancy appears, if he so chose. A life like his is an inspiration to us all. It demonstrates that a person with enough willpower can rise above their circumstances.

And God, like a father, rejoicing to see
His children as pleasant and happy as He,
Would have no more quarrel with the Devil or the barrel,
But kiss him, and give him both drink and apparel.

metadata.title: Songs of Innocence and of Experience

text:
And God, like a doting father, overjoyed to see
His children as warm and carefree as He,
Would start no more feuds with the Devil or the barrel,
But kiss him, and hand him both drink and new clothing.

He had discovered, in the course of his reading, two schools of fiction. One treated of man as a god, ignoring his earthly origin; the other treated of man as a clod, ignoring his heaven-sent dreams and divine possibilities. Both the god and the clod schools erred, in Martin’s estimation, and erred through too great singleness of sight and purpose. There was a compromise that approximated the truth, though it flattered not the school of god, while it challenged the brute-savageness of the school of clod. It was his story, “Adventure,” which had dragged with Ruth, that Martin believed had achieved his ideal of the true in fiction; and it was in an essay, “God and Clod,” that he had expressed his views on the whole general subject.

Through his reading, he had come to recognize two distinct traditions in fiction. One portrayed man as a god, disregarding his earthbound beginnings; the other cast him as mere dirt, blind to his heaven-sent visions and divine potential. In Martin’s view, both the godly and the earthy traditions failed, each erring through an overly narrow focus and intent. A middle path came closer to the truth—one that offered no flattery to the godly school, yet pushed back against the brute savagery of the earthy school. Martin believed his story “Adventure,” which had bored Ruth so thoroughly, had reached his ideal of truth in fiction, and he had laid out his broader thoughts on the matter in an essay titled “God and Clod.”

“One day, when the sun shone on the red leaves that strewed the ground and diffused cheerfulness, although it denied warmth, Safie, Agatha, and Felix departed on a long country walk, and the old man, at his own desire, was left alone in the cottage. When his children had departed, he took up his guitar and played several mournful but sweet airs, more sweet and mournful than I had ever heard him play before. At first his countenance was illuminated with pleasure, but as he continued, thoughtfulness and sadness succeeded; at length, laying aside the instrument, he sat absorbed in reflection.

On a day when the sun sparkled across the crimson leaves covering the ground, bringing a sense of brightness despite the biting cold, Safie, Agatha, and Felix set off for a long stroll through the countryside. The elderly man, by his own choice, stayed behind alone in the cottage. Once his children had gone, he reached for his guitar and played several somber yet beautiful melodies, more haunting and lovely than any I had ever heard him perform. Initially, his face glowed with a quiet pleasure, but as he continued, a pensive sadness took hold; eventually, setting the instrument down, he sat motionless, lost in deep reflection.

CoMMA is designed to measure the target token dependency in a variety of sequence generations, including AR (unidirectional) generation, NAR generation, bidirectional generation or even identity copy. To this end, we vary the mask probability p (the ratio of the masked tokens in the whole target tokens 1 ) in a uniform distribution p ∼ U (0.0, 1.0) when training CoMMA. In this way, p = 1 covers NAR generation, p = 0 covers identity copy, and in some cases, p can also cover AR generation.

metadata.title: A Study of Non-autoregressive Model for Sequence Generation

text: CoMMA is constructed to capture target token dependencies across diverse sequence generation paradigms, including autoregressive (unidirectional) generation, non-autoregressive (NAR) generation, bidirectional generation, and identity copying. For this purpose, during training we sample the mask probability p—defined as the proportion of masked tokens among all target tokens—from a uniform distribution p ∼ U(0.0, 1.0). Under this scheme, p = 1 corresponds to NAR generation, p = 0 corresponds to identity copying, and intermediate values can also accommodate AR generation in certain settings.

The final dataset contains 96 unique puzzles from 81 languages that span 39 different language families from all over the world, as well as two creoles and two artificial languages (see Appendix A.6 for the full list). Some of the large language families have multiple representatives, e.g. there are 13 Indo-European languages, seven Austronesian and six from the Niger-Congo family. But the majority of languages are single representatives of their respective family. This genealogical diversity leads to a great diversity in the linguistic phenomena attested in the data. Some puzzles are designed to explore a specific aspect of the unknown language in isolation, e.g. case markers on demonstrative pronouns in Hungarian (Pudeyev, 2009) . In general, however, the correct solution of a puzzle involves processing on the level of syntax, morphology, phonology, and semantics all at once.

metadata.title: PuzzLing Machines: A Challenge on Learning From Small Data

text: The compiled dataset comprises 96 distinct puzzles drawn from 81 languages, which together represent 39 separate language families distributed across the globe, along with two creoles and two constructed languages (the complete inventory is provided in Appendix A.6). Several major families contribute multiple languages—for instance, 13 Indo-European languages, seven Austronesian, and six from Niger-Congo are included—while the bulk of the languages stand as the sole exemplars of their families. This genealogical breadth engenders a correspondingly wide range of linguistic phenomena captured in the data. Certain puzzles isolate a particular feature of the unknown language, such as case marking on demonstrative pronouns in Hungarian (Pudeyev, 2009). More typically, however, arriving at the correct solution demands simultaneous processing across syntax, morphology, phonology, and semantics.

“Very well,” said Wildeve, rising. Searching about with the lantern, he found a large flat stone, which he placed between himself and Christian, and sat down again. The lantern was opened to give more light, and its rays directed upon the stone. Christian put down a shilling, Wildeve another, and each threw. Christian won. They played for two, Christian won again.

"Fine," Wildeve replied, getting to his feet. Scouring the area by the lantern’s glow, he located a wide, level stone, positioned it between himself and Christian, and sat back down. He opened the lantern to brighten the area, focusing its beams onto the rock. Christian set down a shilling, Wildeve matched the bet, and each of them threw. Christian won. They played for two shillings, and Christian was victorious again.

He made a long story of it; but the part that had immediate interest for me was this: He said I was Sir Kay's prisoner, and that in the due course of custom I would be flung into a dungeon and left there on scant commons until my friends ransomed me--unless I chanced to rot, first. I saw that the last chance had the best show, but I didn't waste any bother about that; time was too precious. The page said, further, that dinner was about ended in the great hall by this time, and that as soon as the sociability and the heavy drinking should begin, Sir Kay would have me in and exhibit me before King Arthur and his illustrious knights seated at the Table Round, and would brag about his exploit in capturing me, and would probably exaggerate the facts a little, but it wouldn't be good form for me to correct him, and not over safe, either; and when I was done being exhibited, then ho for the dungeon; but he, Clarence, would find a way to come and see me every now and then, and cheer me up, and help me get word to my friends.

He dragged the tale out at length, but the only details that held immediate urgency for me were these: I was Sir Kay’s prisoner, he said, and per longstanding custom I would be thrown into a dungeon, kept on meager rations until my people paid my ransom—if I did not waste away and rot there first. I knew that second outcome was far more likely, but I did not waste breath fretting over it; every moment was too precious to squander. The page went on to explain that dinner in the great hall was nearly finished by now, and once the merrymaking and heavy drinking got underway, Sir Kay would bring me in to parade before King Arthur and his famed knights gathered at the Round Table. He would boast of his exploit in capturing me, likely embroidering the facts a fair bit, and it would be neither proper nor particularly safe for me to correct him. Once the showing off was done, it would be straight to the dungeon—but he, Clarence, would find a way to slip in to see me now and then, lift my spirits, and help me send word back to my friends.

She was not past seventeen. Cast in so slight and exquisite a mould; so mild and gentle; so pure and beautiful; that earth seemed not her element, nor its rough creatures her fit companions. The very intelligence that shone in her deep blue eye, and was stamped upon her noble head, seemed scarcely of her age, or of the world; and yet the changing expression of sweetness and good humour, the thousand lights that played about the face, and left no shadow there; above all, the smile, the cheerful, happy smile, were made for Home, and fireside peace and happiness.

She could not have been more than seventeen. So delicately and exquisitely fashioned; so gentle, so mild, so pure and beautiful; it seemed as though the earth were not her proper element, nor its coarse inhabitants suitable companions for her. The very intelligence that shone in her deep blue eye, and seemed written on her noble head, appeared scarcely to belong to her years, or even to this world; and yet the shifting sweetness and good temper of her expression, the countless lights that played across her face and left no shadow there; above all, the smile—the bright, contented, happy smile—seemed made for home, and for the peace and happiness of the hearth.

To get a deeper comparison between CLIReval and the most popular MT metric, BLEU, we randomly select two systems (Baidu-system for zh-en and UEDIN for en-gu) and calculate sentence-level BLEU and sentence-level NDCG@10 scores 13 on both systems. As we can see in Figure 5 , there is no clear correlation between sentence-level NDCG@10 and sentence-level BLEU scores. To be more exact, the Pearson correlations between the two metrics is almost non-existent, at -0.021 and -0.032 for zh-en and en-gu respectively. This shows that the two metrics are qualitatively different and contribute different perspectives to MT evaluation.

metadata.title: CLIReval: Evaluating Machine Translation as a Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval Task

text: To enable a more detailed comparison between CLIReval and the widely used MT metric BLEU, we randomly choose two systems, Baidu-system for zh-en and UEDIN for en-gu, and compute sentence-level BLEU as well as sentence-level NDCG@10 scores 13 for both systems. As shown in Figure 5, there is no evident correlation between sentence-level NDCG@10 and sentence-level BLEU. More precisely, the Pearson correlation between these two metrics is nearly absent, with values of -0.021 and -0.032 for zh-en and en-gu, respectively. These results indicate that the two metrics are qualitatively distinct and provide different perspectives on MT evaluation.

metadata.title: A Monolingual Approach to Contextualized Word Embeddings for Mid-Resource Languages

text: To train the chosen parser and tagger (cf. Section 4.2) and to evaluate the pretrained language models across our five languages, we conduct our experiments under the Universal Dependencies (UD) 14 framework and its associated UD POS tag set (Petrov et al., 2012). We employ all treebanks available for these five languages in the UD treebank collection version 2.2, which served as the dataset for the CoNLL 2018 shared task; accordingly, our evaluation is carried out on six distinct treebanks (see Table 4 for treebank size details).

“I can’t help it,” said Charley, “I can’t help it! To see him splitting away at that pace, and cutting round the corners, and knocking up again’ the posts, and starting on again as if he was made of iron as well as them, and me with the wipe in my pocket, singing out arter him—oh, my eye!” The vivid imagination of Master Bates presented the scene before him in too strong colours. As he arrived at this apostrophe, he again rolled upon the door-step, and laughed louder than before.

“I can’t help it,” cried Tom, “I really can’t help it! To watch him tearing off at that rate, and whipping round the turns, and banging up against the posts, and then off again as though he were made of iron like they were, and I with the strap in my pocket, shouting after him—oh, it’s too much!” The lively fancy of young Stokes drew the picture before him in colours too sharp to bear. When he came to this outburst, he once more tumbled against the doorstep and laughed more uproariously than before.

“He can’t never keep his money, that’s sure,” Hermann von Schmidt confided to his wife. “He got mad when I spoke of interest, an’ he said damn the principal and if I mentioned it again, he’d punch my Dutch head off. That’s what he said—my Dutch head. But he’s all right, even if he ain’t no business man. He’s given me my chance, an’ he’s all right.”

“He’s incapable of holding on to his cash, that much is certain,” Hermann von Schmidt admitted to his wife. “He flew into a rage when I brought up the interest, and he declared to hell with the principal, and if I so much as breathed another word about it, he’d knock my Dutch block clean off. His exact words—my Dutch block. Still, he’s a decent fellow, even if he’s no businessman. He handed me my opportunity, and he’s a decent fellow.”

Throughout the week-end Mr. Britling had kept this trouble well battened down. He had written to Mrs. Harrowdean a brief ambiguous note saying, "I am thinking over all that you have said," and after that he had scarcely thought about her at all. Or at least he had always contrived to be much more vividly thinking about something else. But now in these night silences the suppressed trouble burst hatches and rose about him.

Over the weekend, Mr. Britling had succeeded in keeping his distress tightly contained. He had dispatched a short, noncommittal note to Mrs. Harrowdean, telling her he was reflecting on her words, and from that moment on, she had rarely entered his mind. More precisely, he had ensured that his attention was always occupied by more vivid interests. Now, however, in the hush of the late hours, the buried tension forced its way out and surged up to confront him.

“I’m sure that all I know is that she arrived in the morning and died in the evening without further parley. What Oak and Mr. Boldwood told me was only these few words. ‘Little Fanny Robin is dead, Joseph,’ Gabriel said, looking in my face in his steady old way. I was very sorry, and I said, ‘Ah!—and how did she come to die?’ ‘Well, she’s dead in Casterbridge Union,’ he said, ‘and perhaps ’tisn’t much matter about how she came to die. She reached the Union early Sunday morning, and died in the afternoon—that’s clear enough.’ Then I asked what she’d been doing lately, and Mr. Boldwood turned round to me then, and left off spitting a thistle with the end of his stick. He told me about her having lived by seampstering in Melchester, as I mentioned to you, and that she walked therefrom at the end of last week, passing near here Saturday night in the dusk. They then said I had better just name a hint of her death to you, and away they went. Her death might have been brought on by biding in the night wind, you know, ma’am; for people used to say she’d go off in a decline: she used to cough a good deal in winter time. However, ’tisn’t much odds to us about that now, for ’tis all over.”

“All I can tell you, ma’am, is that she came in the morning and was gone by nightfall, without any more to be said. The only words Gabriel Oak and Mr. Boldwood gave me were these. ‘Little Fanny Robin is dead, Joseph,’ Gabriel said, fixing his gaze on me in that calm, old-fashioned manner of his. I felt a real sorrow, and I asked, ‘Ah—and what brought her to it?’ ‘She’s dead in the Casterbridge Union,’ he answered, ‘and the how of it hardly signifies now. She was taken in there early on Sunday morning, and by afternoon she was gone—that much is plain.’ I pressed then about her recent doings, and Mr. Boldwood swung toward me, ceasing to pick at a thistle with the tip of his staff. He told me she had kept herself by needlework in Melchester, as I passed on to you before, and that she set out walking from there at the close of last week, going by this way on Saturday evening in the half-light. They then said I should just breathe a word of her passing to you, and off they went. It may have been the night air that finished her, you know, ma’am; for folk used to say she would sink in a consumption—she had a bad cough in the cold months. Still, it makes little difference to us now, since it’s all done with.”

Poor girl, her jaws had had a wearisome rest now for a while, and I was expecting to get the consequences. I was right; but she had stood by me most helpfully in the castle, and had mightily supported and reinforced me with gigantic foolishnesses which were worth more for the occasion than wisdoms double their size; so I thought she had earned a right to work her mill for a while, if she wanted to, and I felt not a pang when she started it up:

In the evening, at which season of all others Saint Antoine turned himself inside out, and sat on door-steps and window-ledges, and came to the corners of vile streets and courts, for a breath of air, Madame Defarge with her work in her hand was accustomed to pass from place to place and from group to group: a Missionary--there were many like her--such as the world will do well never to breed again. All the women knitted. They knitted worthless things; but, the mechanical work was a mechanical substitute for eating and drinking; the hands moved for the jaws and the digestive apparatus: if the bony fingers had been still, the stomachs would have been more famine-pinched.

At twilight, when the district of Saint Antoine turned itself out into the streets, its people sat on doorsteps and leaned from windows, seeking a breath of air in the wretched alleys. Madame Defarge moved among them with her knitting, passing from group to group like a dark apostle—one of a type the world would be wise never to produce again. All the women there were knitting. They crafted useless things, yet the repetitive motion served as a physical substitute for sustenance. Their fingers worked in place of their idle jaws; had those skeletal hands ever gone still, the gnawing ache of famine in their bellies would have been far more agonizing.

This seemed feasible enough, for Turpin’s speeches were not many or long, the fascination of the piece lying entirely in the action; and accordingly the play began, and at the appointed time Black Bess leapt into the grassy circle amid the plaudits of the spectators. At the turnpike scene, where Bess and Turpin are hotly pursued at midnight by the officers, and the half-awake gatekeeper in his tasselled nightcap denies that any horseman has passed, Coggan uttered a broad-chested “Well done!” which could be heard all over the fair above the bleating, and Poorgrass smiled delightedly with a nice sense of dramatic contrast between our hero, who coolly leaps the gate, and halting justice in the form of his enemies, who must needs pull up cumbersomely and wait to be let through. At the death of Tom King, he could not refrain from seizing Coggan by the hand, and whispering, with tears in his eyes, “Of course he’s not really shot, Jan—only seemingly!” And when the last sad scene came on, and the body of the gallant and faithful Bess had to be carried out on a shutter by twelve volunteers from among the spectators, nothing could restrain Poorgrass from lending a hand, exclaiming, as he asked Jan to join him, “’Twill be something to tell of at Warren’s in future years, Jan, and hand down to our children.” For many a year in Weatherbury, Joseph told, with the air of a man who had had experiences in his time, that he touched with his own hand the hoof of Bess as she lay upon the board upon his shoulder. If, as some thinkers hold, immortality consists in being enshrined in others’ memories, then did Black Bess become immortal that day if she never had done so before.

The scheme felt entirely doable, for Harker had barely a dozen lines across the whole production, all of them clipped and brisk; the play’s entire draw rested on its stunts, not its dialogue. So the performance kicked off right on schedule, and at the prearranged beat, Red Molly bounded into the sun-dappled ring to raucous cheers from the crowd. When the turnpike sequence unfolded, with Harker and Molly sprinting through the midnight dark, constables hard on their heels, and the groggy tollkeeper in a frayed flannel nightcap swearing no rider had come through his gate, Gideon Hale let loose a deep, chest-thumping “Good on him!” that cut clear over the fairground’s lowing and chatter for every soul to hear. Toby Prewitt’s face split into a giddy, wide grin, tickled by the sharp dramatic irony: their hero cleared the gate in one effortless leap, while the bumbling forces of the law had to yank their own mounts to a clumsy halt and wait for the tollkeeper to fumble the lock open. When Jem Roach fell dead mid-scene, Toby couldn’t stop himself from clutching Gideon’s wrist, eyes glistening as he breathed, “He ain’t actually hit, Gid—just putting on a show, right?” And when the final, somber sequence played out, and the body of the brave, loyal Molly was meant to be borne off on a wooden shutter by twelve volunteer spectators, Toby couldn’t hold himself back from darting forward to help, calling over his shoulder for Gideon to come with him: “Think of the stories we’ll tell down at Merton’s for years, Gid! We’ll pass this down to the little ones, mark my words.” For decades after, folks in Bramble End would listen to Toby hold forth like a man with a lifetime of grand adventures under his belt, recounting how he’d rested the shutter on his own shoulder, and felt the hard curve of Molly’s hoof under his palm as he carried her out. If, as certain philosophers claim, immortality is nothing more than being held fast in the memories of other people, then Red Molly earned eternal life that afternoon, even if she’d never come close to it before.

"Our guns behind felt for the German guns. It was the damnest racket. Like giant lunatics smashing about amidst colossal pots and pans. They fired different sorts of shells; stink shells as well as Jack Johnsons, and though we didn't get much of that at our corner there was a sting of chlorine in the air all through the afternoon. Most of the stink shells fell short. We hadn't masks, but we rigged up a sort of protection with our handkerchiefs. And it didn't amount to very much. It was rather like the chemistry room after Heinrich and the kids had been mixing things. Most of the time I was busy helping with the men who had got hurt. Suddenly there came a lull. Then some one said the Germans were coming, and I had a glimpse of them.

Our guns behind us were searching for the German guns. It was the worst din imaginable, like a pair of giant madmen battering away among enormous pots and pans. They fired all kinds of shells, including stink shells and Jack Johnsons, and though our corner did not get much of the stink shells, there was a faint bite of chlorine in the air all afternoon. Most of the stink shells fell short. We had no masks, but we improvised a kind of protection with our handkerchiefs. It did not help much. It was rather like the chemistry room after Heinrich and the boys had been mixing up things. For the most part I was occupied helping the wounded men. Then, all at once, there was a lull. After that someone said the Germans were coming, and I caught sight of them.

For weeks his mind had been playing about this idea. He had talked to Letty of this Finite God, who is the king of man's adventure in space and time. But hitherto God had been for him a thing of the intelligence, a theory, a report, something told about but not realised.... Mr. Britling's thinking about God hitherto had been like some one who has found an empty house, very beautiful and pleasant, full of the promise of a fine personality. And then as the discoverer makes his lonely, curious explorations, he hears downstairs, dear and friendly, the voice of the Master coming in....

For weeks, the thought had been tumbling around the edges of his mind. He’d even brought the idea up with Letty: this Finite God, the figure who presides over all of humanity’s bold, meandering adventures across space and time. But up until that point, God had only ever been an intellectual exercise for him: a theory, a secondhand account, something other people spoke of that he’d never truly felt or recognized in his own life. Mr. Britling’s prior thoughts of God had felt exactly like stumbling across an empty, beautiful house, warm and welcoming, thrumming with the quiet promise that its owner was a deeply kind, vivid personality. And just as he pokes through the rooms alone, curiosity guiding his slow, quiet exploration, he hears the dear, friendly lilt of the Master’s voice drifting up from the floor below, soft and present as the owner steps through the front door.

"My driving leaves something to be desired," said Mr. Britling with an air of frank impartiality. "But I have only just got this car for myself--after some years of hired cars--the sort of lazy arrangement where people supply car, driver, petrol, tyres, insurance and everything at so much a month. It bored me abominably. I can't imagine now how I stood it for so long. They sent me down a succession of compact, scornful boys who used to go fast when I wanted to go slow, and slow when I wanted to go fast, and who used to take every corner on the wrong side at top speed, and charge dogs and hens for the sport of it, and all sorts of things like that. They would not even let me choose my roads. I should have got myself a car long ago, and driven it, if it wasn't for that infernal business with a handle one had to do when the engine stopped. But here, you see, is a reasonably cheap car with an electric starter--American, I need scarcely say. And here I am--going at my own pace."

“My motoring is not above criticism,” said Mr. Alden with a candor that seemed almost judicial. “But I have only lately come into this machine for myself—after a good many years of hiring cars, that indolent contrivance by which people provide car, chauffeur, fuel, tires, insurance, and Heaven knows what else for a fixed sum each month. It used to weary me beyond expression. I cannot now understand how I endured it so long. They would send me one after another of these trim, disdainful lads, who raced when I wished to crawl and crawled when I wished to race, who took every curve on the wrong hand at full tilt, and pursued dogs and poultry as if it were the finest amusement in the world, and all the rest of it. They would not even suffer me to choose my own roads. I should have bought a car years ago and driven it myself, if it had not been for that abominable business of the crank one had to turn when the engine failed. But here, as you see, is a tolerably inexpensive motor with an electric starter—American, I need hardly tell you. And here I am, at last, proceeding at my own tempo.”

So it was, as he listened to Professor Caldwell’s easy flow of speech—the conversation of a clever, cultured man—that Martin kept seeing himself down all his past. He saw himself when he had been quite the hoodlum, wearing a “stiff-rim” Stetson hat and a square-cut, double-breasted coat, with a certain swagger to the shoulders and possessing the ideal of being as tough as the police permitted. He did not disguise it to himself, nor attempt to palliate it. At one time in his life he had been just a common hoodlum, the leader of a gang that worried the police and terrorized honest, working-class householders. But his ideals had changed. He glanced about him at the well-bred, well-dressed men and women, and breathed into his lungs the atmosphere of culture and refinement, and at the same moment the ghost of his early youth, in stiff-rim and square-cut, with swagger and toughness, stalked across the room. This figure, of the corner hoodlum, he saw merge into himself, sitting and talking with an actual university professor.

So it was that, as he listened to Professor Caldwell’s easy, fluent talk—the discourse of a man of breeding and intellect—Martin kept seeing the whole sweep of his past before him. He saw himself in the days when he had been nothing more than a hoodlum, wearing a stiff-rimmed Stetson and a square-cut, double-breasted coat, carrying his shoulders with a certain swagger and holding fast to the ideal of being as hard as the police would allow. He made no attempt to soften it for himself or excuse it in any way. There had been a time when he was simply a common tough, the leader of a gang that harassed the police and kept honest working-class families in fear. But his ideals had altered. He looked around at the well-bred, well-dressed men and women, and drew into his lungs the air of culture and refinement, while at the same instant the ghost of his former youth, in stiff-rim and square-cut coat, swaggering and hard, crossed the room before him. That corner hoodlum, he saw, melted into himself, sitting there and conversing with an actual university professor.

The story of the deaths of Eustacia and Wildeve was told throughout Egdon, and far beyond, for many weeks and months. All the known incidents of their love were enlarged, distorted, touched up, and modified, till the original reality bore but a slight resemblance to the counterfeit presentation by surrounding tongues. Yet, upon the whole, neither the man nor the woman lost dignity by sudden death. Misfortune had struck them gracefully, cutting off their erratic histories with a catastrophic dash, instead of, as with many, attenuating each life to an uninteresting meagreness, through long years of wrinkles, neglect, and decay.

For many months, the news of how Eustacia and Wildeve perished echoed across Egdon and into the distant countryside. Every detail of their romance was inflated, twisted, and embellished by local gossip until the actual facts were barely recognizable in the stories being told. Nevertheless, their abrupt passing did not diminish the dignity of either the man or the woman. Tragedy had claimed them with a certain elegance, ending their turbulent lives with a dramatic finality rather than letting them wither away through decades of aging, obscurity, and physical decline.

To achieve that goal, we propose a novel method ( Figure 2 ) that allows either generating words from the vocabulary or selecting words from the translation candidates of the words in the source article. Specifically, we first employ the encoderdecoder attention distribution to help determine which source word should be translated. Then we present three strategies, i.e., Naive, Equal, and Adapt, to obtain the translation probability from a probabilistic bilingual lexicon. The translation distribution can be acquired based on the encoderdecoder attention distribution and the translation probability. Next, we add an extra translation layer to calculate a translating probability. The final distribution is the weighted sum (weighed by the translating probability) of the translation distribution and the neural distribution.

metadata.title: Attend, Translate and Summarize: An Efficient Method for Neural Cross-Lingual Summarization

text: To achieve this objective, we propose a novel method (Figure 2) that enables either generating words from the vocabulary or selecting words from the translation candidates of the words in the source article. Specifically, we first use the encoder-decoder attention distribution to identify which source word should be translated. We then introduce three strategies, namely Naive, Equal, and Adapt, to derive the translation probability from a probabilistic bilingual lexicon. The translation distribution can be obtained by combining the encoder-decoder attention distribution with the translation probability. Next, we add an additional translation layer to compute a translating probability. The final distribution is the weighted sum, weighted by the translating probability, of the translation distribution and the neural distribution.

And one night in April he was perplexed by a commotion among the pheasants and a barking of distant dogs, and then to his great astonishment he heard noises like a distant firework display and saw something like a phantom yellowish fountain-pen in the sky far away to the east lit intermittently by a quivering search-light and going very swiftly. And after he had rubbed his eyes and looked again, he realised that he was looking at a Zeppelin--a Zeppelin flying Londonward over Essex.

One April night, he was puzzled by a disturbance among the pheasants and the sound of dogs barking in the distance. To his total surprise, he then heard a faint crackling like far-off fireworks and spotted a ghostly, yellowish shape—resembling a fountain pen—streaking across the eastern sky, caught intermittently in the beam of a flickering searchlight. After rubbing his eyes and staring again, he realized he was looking at a Zeppelin, flying over Essex on its way toward London.

Mr. Bumble, having spread a handkerchief over his knees to prevent the crumbs from sullying the splendour of his shorts, began to eat and drink; varying these amusements, occasionally, by fetching a deep sigh; which, however, had no injurious effect upon his appetite, but, on the contrary, rather seemed to facilitate his operations in the tea and toast department.

The tea was prepared and served in absolute silence. Mr. Bumble, having draped a handkerchief across his knees to shield the glory of his breeches from stray crumbs, began to eat and drink. He punctuated these activities with an occasional heavy sigh, which appeared to have no ill effect on his hunger; instead, the outbursts seemed to only hasten his industrious progress through the tea and toast.

“I’m pleased to hear you say so,” came the reply. “The thought that my scattered scraps of learning can clear a shorter path to what is true feels deeply comforting. For my own part, I never trouble myself over whether I’m correct. In the end, none of it holds any worth. The final certainties will always lie beyond human grasp.”

The pigeons were flying about her head with the greatest unconcern, and the face of her aunt was just visible above the floor of the loft, lit by a few stray motes of light, as she stood halfway up the ladder, looking at a spot into which she was not climber enough to venture.

The pigeons wheeled about her head with complete indifference, and her aunt’s face was just visible above the loft floor, touched by a few wandering specks of light, as she stood halfway up the ladder, gazing toward a place she was not bold enough to climb to.

“Ah—your mind runs off to the philosophical side of it. Well, these sad and hopeless obstacles are welcome in one sense, for they enable us to look with indifference upon the cruel satires that Fate loves to indulge in. I have heard of people, who, upon coming suddenly into happiness, have died from anxiety lest they should not live to enjoy it. I felt myself in that whimsical state of uneasiness lately; but I shall be spared it now. Let us walk on.”

“Ah, I see your thoughts have turned toward the philosophical. In a way, these bleak and insurmountable barriers are almost welcome; they permit us to gaze with indifference upon the cruel ironies that Fate so enjoys. I have heard of those who, when suddenly finding happiness, died from the sheer terror that they might not live to experience it. I found myself in that strange state of agitation recently, but I am relieved of it now. Let us continue our walk.”

“No, Miss Manette; all through it, I have known myself to be quite undeserving. And yet I have had the weakness, and have still the weakness, to wish you to know with what a sudden mastery you kindled me, heap of ashes that I am, into fire--a fire, however, inseparable in its nature from myself, quickening nothing, lighting nothing, doing no service, idly burning away.”

“No, Miss Manette. All this while, I have known full well that I am entirely unworthy of your regard. Even so, I have had this weakness, and carry it still: I want you to understand how suddenly, how completely you kindled a flame in me, heap of cold ash that I am. It is a fire, though, inextricable from my very nature. It rouses nothing, lights no dark space, does no good to anyone. It only burns idle, wasting itself slowly away.”

"I'd stop his _Daily Mail_," said Raeburn. "I'd leave _The Times_, but I'd stop the _Daily Mail_ on the score of its placards alone. It overdoes Northcliffe. It translates him into the shrieks and yells of underlings. The plain fact is that Northcliffe is scared out of his wits by German efficiency--and in war time when a man is scared out of his wits, whether he is honest or not, you put his head in a bag or hold a pistol to it to calm him.... What is the good of all this clamouring for a change of government? We haven't a change of government. It's like telling a tramp to get a change of linen. Our men, all our public men, are second-rate men, with the habits of advocates. There is nothing masterful in their minds. How can you expect the system to produce anything else? But they are doing as well as they can, and there is no way of putting in any one else now, and there you are."

I'd cancel his _Daily Mail_," Raeburn said. "I'd keep _The Times_, but I'd drop the _Daily Mail_ for its placards alone. It exaggerates Northcliffe. It turns him into the screeching and howling of subordinates. The simple truth is that Northcliffe is terrified of German efficiency—and in wartime, when a man is terrified, whether he's honest or not, you put a bag over his head or hold a pistol to it to steady him.... What's the use of all this noise about changing the government? We haven't got a change of government. It's like telling a beggar to get a change of clothes. Our men, all our public men, are second-rate, with the instincts of lawyers. There's nothing commanding in their minds. How can you expect the system to produce anything different? But they're doing the best they can, and there's no way of putting anyone else in now, and that's how it stands.

As CGC-QG performs word-level content selection, we observe that it tends to include many irrelevant words in the question, leading to lengthy questions (33.7 tokens on average, while 17.7 for ground-truth questions and 19.3 for our model) that are unanswerable or with semantic errors. Our model greatly reduces the error with node-level content selection based on semantic relations (shown in Table 3 ). 3. While both SRL-based and DP-based semantic graph models (P1 and P2) achieve state-of-theart BLEU, DP-based graph (P2) performs slightly better (+3.3% in BLEU-4). A possible explanation is that SRL fails to include fine-grained semantic information into the graph, as the parsing often results in nodes containing a long sequence of tokens.

metadata.title: Semantic Graphs for Generating Deep Questions
