The performance profile of CGC-QG (B6) deviates notably from that of all competing baselines: it attains the highest METEOR and ROUGE-L scores across all evaluated methods, yet ranks lowest on BLEU-1. Since CGC-QG relies on word-level content selection, we find it consistently introduces a large number of extraneous tokens into generated questions, resulting in overly long outputs (a mean length of 33.7 tokens, compared to 17.7 tokens for ground-truth questions and 19.3 tokens for our proposed model) that are either unanswerable or contain semantic inconsistencies. Our approach mitigates this error substantially via node-level content selection guided by semantic relational structure, as quantified in Table 3.
3. While both the SRL-based and DP-based semantic graph architectures (P1 and P2, respectively) achieve state-of-the-art BLEU performance, the DP-based graph model (P2) yields a small but consistent performance gain, with a 3.3% improvement in BLEU-4. A plausible explanation for this gap is that SRL parsing is unable to encode fine-grained semantic information within the graph structure, as SRL parsers frequently produce nodes that comprise long, unsegmented token sequences.

Following Wu et al. (2019) , we also evaluate the effect of mixing a small percentage of real training data in our augmented training sets. We use a naive few-shot training strategy, where we directly add a portion of the original training data in the domain of interest to the training set. Fig. 4 plots the joint accuracy achieved on the new domain with the addition of different percentages of real training data. The results for 0% are the same as the zero-shot experiment. The advantage of the synthesized training data decreases as the percent of real data increases, because real data is more varied, informative, and more representative of the distribution in the test set. The impact of synthesized data is more pronounced for SUMBT than TRADE for all domains even with 5% real data, and it is significant for the "Attraction" domain with 10% real data. This suggests that SUMBT needs more data to train, due to having more parameters, but can utilize additional synthesized data better to improve its training.

metadata.title: Zero-Shot Transfer Learning with Synthesized Data for Multi-Domain Dialogue State Tracking

text: In line with Wu et al. (2019), we also assess the impact of incorporating a small fraction of real training data into our augmented training sets. We adopt a straightforward few-shot training approach, in which we directly add a portion of the original training data from the target domain to the training set. Figure 4 shows the joint accuracy attained on the new domain as varying percentages of real training data are introduced. The results at 0% correspond to the zero-shot setting. The benefit of the synthesized training data diminishes as the proportion of real data grows, since real data is more diverse, more informative, and better reflects the test-set distribution. The influence of synthesized data is more pronounced for SUMBT than for TRADE across all domains even when 5% real data is included, and it remains notable for the “Attraction” domain with 10% real data. This indicates that SUMBT, because of its larger parameter count, requires more data for training but can more effectively leverage additional synthesized data to enhance its performance.

You should have seen those acres of people throw themselves down in that water and kiss it; kiss it, and pet it, and fondle it, and talk to it as if it were alive, and welcome it back with the dear names they gave their darlings, just as if it had been a friend who was long gone away and lost, and was come home again. Yes, it was pretty to see, and made me think more of them than I had done before.

You ought to have seen those acres of people hurl themselves down into that water and press their lips to it; kiss its surface, cup it gently in their palms, caress it, and murmur to it as if it were a living, breathing thing, greeting its return with the soft, dear pet names they saved for their own darlings, just as if it were a long-lost, deeply beloved friend finally coming home at last. It was such a sweet, lovely sight to witness, and it made me think far more fondly of them than I ever had before.

On Thursday, the 30th of July, Caillaux, Carson, strikes, and all the common topics of life had been swept out of the front page of the paper altogether; the stock exchanges were in a state of wild perturbation, and food prices were leaping fantastically. Austria was bombarding Belgrade, contrary to the rules of war hitherto accepted; Russia was mobilising; Mr. Asquith was, he declared, not relaxing his efforts "to do everything possible to circumscribe the area of possible conflict," and the Vienna Conference of Peace Societies was postponed. "I do not see why a conflict between Russia and Austria should involve Western Europe," said Mr. Britling. "Our concern is only for Belgium and France."

On Thursday, the thirtieth of July, Cailloux, Carson, the strikes, and all the ordinary concerns of daily life had been driven clean from the front page of the newspaper; the stock markets were in a frenzy of disorder, and food prices were mounting in wild, absurd leaps. Austria was shelling Belgrade, in defiance of the accepted usages of war; Russia was mobilising; Mr. Aitken declared that he was not slackening his efforts “to do everything possible to circumscribe the area of possible conflict,” and the Vienna Conference of Peace Societies was put off. “I do not see why a conflict between Russia and Austria should involve Western Europe,” said Mr. Berridge. “Our concern is only for Belgium and France.”

There are several limitations of this work. First, we distinguish between supplements and drugs. Both supplements and drugs are pharmacologic entities, with their separate classification more attributable to marketing and social pressures rather than functional differences. However, due to this somewhat arbitrary distinction, supplement entities are not well represented in databases of pharmaceutical entities, and less information is publicly available on their interactions. We also use UMLS CUIs as a way of identifying supplement and drug entities. The lack of a standardized terminology to describe dietary supplements is discussed in Manohar et al. (2015a) and , which estimate UMLS coverage of these terms to be between 14-54%. This limitation prevents us from identifying many supplement entities. Lastly, our dependence on NLP-pipeline tools sets a performance ceiling due to unsolved problems in NER and linking. Although scispaCy is performant and detects a large number of relevant entities, our evaluations show that many supplement and drug entities are missed. A system such as MetaMapLite (Demner-Fushman et al., 2017) has higher recall, but performance is slow and there are practical challenges to using it to process large numbers of documents.

metadata.title: SUPP.AI: Finding Evidence for Supplement-Drug Interactions

text: There are several limitations to this study. First, we draw a distinction between supplements and drugs. Although both are pharmacologic entities, their separate categorization appears to reflect marketing and social pressures more than any substantive functional difference. Because this distinction is somewhat arbitrary, supplement entities are not well covered in pharmaceutical entity databases, and relatively little public information is available regarding their interactions. We further rely on UMLS CUIs to identify supplement and drug entities. The absence of a standardized terminology for dietary supplements is noted in Manohar et al. (2015a) and, which estimate UMLS coverage for these terms to range from 14–54%. As a result, many supplement entities cannot be identified by our system. Finally, our reliance on NLP pipeline components imposes an upper bound on performance due to unresolved challenges in NER and entity linking. While scispaCy is effective and identifies a large number of relevant entities, our evaluations indicate that many supplement and drug entities are still missed. An alternative such as MetaMapLite (Demner-Fushman et al., 2017) achieves higher recall, but it is slow in practice and presents operational challenges for processing large document collections.

Well, the king was out of the hole; and on terms satisfactory to the Church and the rest of the aristocracy, no doubt. Men write many fine and plausible arguments in support of monarchy, but the fact remains that where every man in a State has a vote, brutal laws are impossible. Arthur's people were of course poor material for a republic, because they had been debased so long by monarchy; and yet even they would have been intelligent enough to make short work of that law which the king had just been administering if it had been submitted to their full and free vote. There is a phrase which has grown so common in the world's mouth that it has come to seem to have sense and meaning--the sense and meaning implied when it is used; that is the phrase which refers to this or that or the other nation as possibly being “capable of self-government”; and the implied sense of it is, that there has been a nation somewhere, some time or other which _wasn't_ capable of it--wasn't as able to govern itself as some self-appointed specialists were or would be to govern it. The master minds of all nations, in all ages, have sprung in affluent multitude from the mass of the nation, and from the mass of the nation only--not from its privileged classes; and so, no matter what the nation's intellectual grade was; whether high or low, the bulk of its ability was in the long ranks of its nameless and its poor, and so it never saw the day that it had not the material in abundance whereby to govern itself. Which is to assert an always self-proven fact: that even the best governed and most free and most enlightened monarchy is still behind the best condition attainable by its people; and that the same is true of kindred governments of lower grades, all the way down to the lowest.

The king had finally escaped his predicament, surely under conditions that satisfied both the Church and the nobility. Many eloquent justifications are written in defense of kingship, but the truth is simple: wherever universal suffrage exists, cruel laws cannot endure. Arthur’s subjects were admittedly poor candidates for a republic, having been diminished by centuries of royal oppression; yet even they would have been wise enough to dismantle the harsh statutes the king had just upheld if the matter were put to a free vote. A particular phrase has become so common that it is often mistaken for logic—the idea that a nation might not be “capable of self-government.” This suggests that there has ever been a people less fit to govern themselves than the self-anointed specialists who would presume to do it for them. In every era, the finest minds have risen in great numbers from the common folk, not the privileged few. No matter the general state of a nation’s intellect, the vast majority of its capability lies with the nameless poor; consequently, no society has ever lacked the internal strength to manage its own destiny. It is an undeniable fact that even the most enlightened monarchy lags behind the potential of a self-governing people, a truth that applies to every form of autocracy, from the most refined to the most base.

They traversed the lower room, slowly, and with caution; for Monks started at every shadow; and Mr. Bumble, holding his lantern a foot above the ground, walked not only with remarkable care, but with a marvellously light step for a gentleman of his figure: looking nervously about him for hidden trap-doors. The gate at which they had entered, was softly unfastened and opened by Monks; merely exchanging a nod with their mysterious acquaintance, the married couple emerged into the wet and darkness outside.

They crossed the lower chamber with slow and wary steps; for Valen started at each passing shade, while Mr. Pruitt, carrying his lantern a foot above the floor, moved not only with singular caution, but with a wonderfully light tread for so stout a gentleman, glancing anxiously this way and that for concealed trap-doors. The gate by which they had come in was silently unbarred and drawn open by Valen; and after a grave nod to their secretive companion, the wedded pair passed forth into the dripping blackness beyond.

And she slipped down from the horse and ran a little way and stood. I looked up and saw, far off in the shade of a tree, half a dozen armed knights and their squires; and straightway there was bustle among them and tightening of saddle-girths for the mount. My pipe was ready and would have been lit, if I had not been lost in thinking about how to banish oppression from this land and restore to all its people their stolen rights and manhood without disobliging anybody. I lit up at once, and by the time I had got a good head of reserved steam on, here they came. All together, too; none of those chivalrous magnanimities which one reads so much about --one courtly rascal at a time, and the rest standing by to see fair play. No, they came in a body, they came with a whirr and a rush, they came like a volley from a battery; came with heads low down, plumes streaming out behind, lances advanced at a level. It was a handsome sight, a beautiful sight--for a man up a tree. I laid my lance in rest and waited, with my heart beating, till the iron wave was just ready to break over me, then spouted a column of white smoke through the bars of my helmet. You should have seen the wave go to pieces and scatter! This was a finer sight than the other one.

And she slid from the horse, ran a short distance, and stopped. Looking up, I saw, in the shade of a tree far off, some half dozen armed knights with their squires; and at once there was commotion among them and a tightening of saddle-girths for the charge. My pipe was ready and would have been lit, had I not been absorbed in plans for how to sweep oppression from this land and restore to all its people their stolen rights and manhood without offending anyone. I lit it at once, and by the time I had built up a proper reserve of steam, there they came. All at once, too; none of those chivalrous courtesies one reads of so often--one polished villain at a time, while the rest stand aside to watch fair play. No, they came together, they came with a whir and a rush, they came like a battery’s volley; came with heads bent low, plumes streaming behind, lances leveled straight ahead. It was a fine sight, a splendid sight—for a man in a tree. I set my lance in rest and waited, my heart pounding, until the iron wave was just about to break over me, then blew a column of white smoke through the bars of my helmet. You should have seen that wave break apart and scatter! That was a finer sight than the first.

Quantitative Evaluation We use two quantitative measures to evaluate our model. (1) Word-level accuracy: for a given word 8 , we consider the prediction correct if and only if all the diacritic marks on the word are correct, including gemination and the 'shin' dot, with all matres lectionis removed. 2Character-level accuracy: For each Hebrew letter in the input text we check if the model predicted the correct set of diacritic marks for the letter (and, for matres lectionis, we check that the model predicted their removal).

metadata.title: Nakdan: Professional Hebrew Diacritizer

text: Quantitative Evaluation We utilize two distinct quantitative metrics to evaluate the performance of our proposed model. (1) Word-level accuracy: For a given target word, a prediction is deemed correct if and only if all diacritic marks associated with the word are fully accurate, including gemination markers and the “shin” dot, with all matres lectionis properly excluded from the output. (2) Character-level accuracy: For each individual Hebrew letter present in the input text, we validate whether the model generates the correct complete set of diacritic marks for that character; for matres lectionis specifically, we separately verify that the model correctly predicts their removal as required.

“When I speak of success, I speak of success with the young lady; and when I speak of causes and reasons to make success probable, I speak of causes and reasons that will tell as such with the young lady. The young lady, my good sir,” said Mr. Lorry, mildly tapping the Stryver arm, “the young lady. The young lady goes before all.”

“When I talk about winning, I mean winning over the young lady; and when I talk about factors and arguments that might make winning likely, I mean factors and arguments that will carry weight with her. The young lady, my dear fellow,” Mr. Lorry said, gently patting Stryver’s arm, “the young lady. She comes before everything.”

The main focus of this work is not to propose a solution to hallucination related issues, but to achieve a better understanding of hallucinations in abstractive summarization through their human assessment. We randomly sampled 500 articles from the test set to facilitate our study. Using the full test set was unfeasible given its size and the cost of human judgments. We have trained annotators (fluent in English) specifically for our assessment. Our annotators went through two pilot studies to have a better understanding of intrinsic and extrinsic hallucinations, and factuality of summaries. Documents used in the pilot studies were not used in the final annotation. We also report on ROUGE (Lin and Hovy, 2003) scores, BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020) and semantic inference metric such as textual entailment (Pasunuru and Bansal, 2018; Welleck et al., 2019; Falke et al., 2019; Kryscinski et al., 2019b) and question answering (Arumae and Liu, 2019; Wang et al., 2020) .

metadata.title: On Faithfulness and Factuality in Abstractive Summarization

text: This study does not aim to resolve issues associated with hallucinations; rather, it seeks to deepen the understanding of hallucinations in abstractive summarization through human evaluation. To support this investigation, we randomly selected 500 articles from the test set, as utilizing the entire test set was impractical due to its scale and the expense of obtaining human judgments. Annotators, who were fluent in English, received specialized training for the evaluation task. They participated in two pilot studies designed to enhance their comprehension of intrinsic and extrinsic hallucinations, as well as the factuality of summaries. Documents employed in the pilot studies were excluded from the final annotation phase. We additionally present results for ROUGE (Lin and Hovy, 2003) scores, BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020), and semantic inference metrics, including textual entailment (Pasunuru and Bansal, 2018; Welleck et al., 2019; Falke et al., 2019; Kryscinski et al., 2019b) and question answering (Arumae and Liu, 2019; Wang et al., 2020).

In one of the great juvenile periodicals he noted whole columns of incident and anecdote. Here was a chance. His paragraphs were returned, and though he tried repeatedly he never succeeded in placing one. Later on, when it no longer mattered, he learned that the associate editors and sub-editors augmented their salaries by supplying those paragraphs themselves. The comic weeklies returned his jokes and humorous verse, and the light society verse he wrote for the large magazines found no abiding-place. Then there was the newspaper storiette. He knew that he could write better ones than were published. Managing to obtain the addresses of two newspaper syndicates, he deluged them with storiettes. When he had written twenty and failed to place one of them, he ceased. And yet, from day to day, he read storiettes in the dailies and weeklies, scores and scores of storiettes, not one of which would compare with his. In his despondency, he concluded that he had no judgment whatever, that he was hypnotized by what he wrote, and that he was a self-deluded pretender.

In one of the leading juvenile magazines he discovered whole columns of anecdote and incident. Here, surely, was his opening. Yet his paragraphs were sent back, and though he tried again and again, he never managed to get a single one accepted. Much later, when it no longer mattered, he learned that the associate editors and sub-editors padded their salaries by writing those very paragraphs themselves. The comic weeklies rejected his jokes and comic verse, and the light society verse he contributed to the larger magazines found no foothold anywhere. Then there was the newspaper storiette. He knew he could write better ones than the ones in print. After obtaining the addresses of two newspaper syndicates, he showered them with storiettes. When he had written twenty and failed to place even one, he gave up. And yet day after day he read storiettes in the dailies and weeklies—scores upon scores of them—not one of which could bear comparison with his. In his dejection, he came to believe that he possessed no judgment at all, that he was mesmerized by his own writing, and that he was merely a self-deceived pretender.

“I am,” said she, “the cousin of the unhappy child who was murdered, or rather his sister, for I was educated by and have lived with his parents ever since and even long before his birth. It may therefore be judged indecent in me to come forward on this occasion, but when I see a fellow creature about to perish through the cowardice of her pretended friends, I wish to be allowed to speak, that I may say what I know of her character. I am well acquainted with the accused. I have lived in the same house with her, at one time for five and at another for nearly two years. During all that period she appeared to me the most amiable and benevolent of human creatures. She nursed Madame Frankenstein, my aunt, in her last illness, with the greatest affection and care and afterwards attended her own mother during a tedious illness, in a manner that excited the admiration of all who knew her, after which she again lived in my uncle’s house, where she was beloved by all the family. She was warmly attached to the child who is now dead and acted towards him like a most affectionate mother. For my own part, I do not hesitate to say that, notwithstanding all the evidence produced against her, I believe and rely on her perfect innocence. She had no temptation for such an action; as to the bauble on which the chief proof rests, if she had earnestly desired it, I should have willingly given it to her, so much do I esteem and value her.”

“I am,” she began, “the kinswoman of the wretched boy who was slain—his sister, in truth, for I was reared alongside his parents and have dwelt with them from long before his birth. It may be thought unseemly that I should step forward at such a moment, yet when I behold a fellow soul doomed by the faint-heartedness of those who feign friendship, I crave leave to speak, that I may tell what I know of her nature. I have been intimately acquainted with the prisoner. I shared a roof with her, once for a span of five years, and again for nearly two. Throughout that time she seemed to me the gentlest and kindest of beings. She tended Madame Frankenstein, my aunt, through her final decline with the tenderest devotion and vigilance, and later cared for her own mother in a protracted sickness with a grace that drew the wonder of all who witnessed it; afterward she returned to my uncle’s household, where every member cherished her. She was deeply devoted to the child now lost, and behaved toward him as the fondest of mothers. For myself, I do not scruple to declare that, despite every proof arrayed against her, I trust wholly in her blamelessness. She lacked any motive for such a deed; as for the trinket on which the accusation so heavily leans, had she truly longed for it, I would have bestowed it upon her freely—so dearly do I prize and reverence her.”

He could think continuously day by day of nothing else. His mind was as caught as a galley slave, as unable to escape from tugging at this oar. All his universe was a magnetic field which oriented everything, whether he would have it so or not, to this one polar question.

Day after day, he couldn’t turn his mind to anything else at all, no matter how hard he tried. His thoughts were trapped like a galley slave chained to his bench, utterly unable to stop straining against that same heavy oar. Every part of his world functioned like a magnetic field, tugging every last thing in his orbit toward that one sharp, polar question, whether he wanted it to or not.

“He’s a clever man, and ’tis a true comfort for us to have such a shepherd,” murmured Joseph Poorgrass, in a soft cadence. “We ought to feel full o’ thanksgiving that he’s not a player of ba’dy songs instead of these merry tunes; for ’twould have been just as easy for God to have made the shepherd a loose low man—a man of iniquity, so to speak it—as what he is. Yes, for our wives’ and daughters’ sakes we should feel real thanksgiving.”

“He is a man of keen parts, and it is a genuine solace to have such a guardian among us,” murmured Silas Mellows in a low, rhythmic drawl. “We should be brimming with gratitude that he does not peddle lewd verses instead of these honest strains; for the Creator might just as easily have fashioned the fellow as a profligate—a vessel of vice, to put it plainly—as the man he stands. Truly, for the sake of our wives and daughters, we must feel a profound sense of thanks.”

“You must not shrink from me, dear Clym,” said Thomasin earnestly, in that sweet voice of hers which came to a sufferer like fresh air into a Black Hole. “Nothing in you can ever shock me or drive me away. I have been here before, but you don’t remember it.”

“You mustn’t shrink back from me, dear Clym,” Thomasin said earnestly, that soft, sweet voice of hers wrapping around a hurting soul like fresh air drifting into the suffocating dark of a Black Hole. “Nothing about you could ever shock me, or drive me away. I’ve come here before, you see—you just don’t remember it.”

“That’s right, that’s right. Courage! Business! You have business before you; useful business. Miss Manette, your mother took this course with you. And when she died--I believe broken-hearted--having never slackened her unavailing search for your father, she left you, at two years old, to grow to be blooming, beautiful, and happy, without the dark cloud upon you of living in uncertainty whether your father soon wore his heart out in prison, or wasted there through many lingering years.”

“That is so, yes, that is so. Courage! Duty! You have duty before you; useful duty. Miss Manette, your mother followed this path with you. And when she died—I believe heartbroken—after never once relaxing her fruitless search for your father, she left you, at two years old, to grow up blooming, beautiful, and happy, with no dark cloud hanging over you in the doubt whether your father had worn away his heart in prison, or lingered there through many long years and wasted away.”

“You will decide quickly,” said Mr. Brownlow, with perfect firmness and composure. “If you wish me to prefer my charges publicly, and consign you to a punishment the extent of which, although I can, with a shudder, foresee, I cannot control, once more, I say, for you know the way. If not, and you appeal to my forbearance, and the mercy of those you have deeply injured, seat yourself, without a word, in that chair. It has waited for you two whole days.”

“You must choose at once,” Mr. Brownlow said, his voice steady and unyielding. “Should you want me to bring these accusations out into the open and deliver you to a fate whose full horror I can glimpse with dread but have no power to limit, then—as I’ve told you already—you know the path. Otherwise, if you throw yourself on my leniency and the compassion of those you have so gravely wronged, sit down in that chair without uttering a sound. It has stood empty for you these two full days.”

I believe I should load you all into Gladys and start off westward with a kind of exhilaration...."

The curious thing," said Mr. Britling, continuing his examination of the matter, "is that when one looks at such things now, as one looks at them as perfectly possible, they are not nearly so terrifying and overwhelming to the mind as they would have seemed—last week. I believe I should pack you all into Gladys and set off westward with a sort of exhilaration....

Apparently he had some time ago reached that entrance to middle age at which a man’s aspect naturally ceases to alter for the term of a dozen years or so; and, artificially, a woman’s does likewise. Thirty-five and fifty were his limits of variation—he might have been either, or anywhere between the two.

It seemed he had crossed that unmarked threshold of midlife some years prior, the point at which a man’s countenance naturally stops shifting for a span of a dozen years or more; with a little deliberate upkeep, the same holds true for women. Thirty-five and fifty marked the outer bounds of any plausible guess at his age—he could have passed for either, or any age tucked neatly between the two.

“Reminds me of being nabbed by the devil,” returned Sikes. “There never was another man with such a face as yours, unless it was your father, and I suppose _he_ is singeing his grizzled red beard by this time, unless you came straight from the old ’un without any father at all betwixt you; which I shouldn’t wonder at, a bit.”

“Feels just like being snatched by the devil,” Sikes retorted. “There never was another man with a face as wretched as yours, save for your father, and I reckon he’s singeing that grizzled red beard of his over hell’s fires by now. Unless you came straight from the old devil himself with no father between you two at all—and I wouldn’t be the least bit surprised if that’s the truth.”

The fact was that Yeobright’s fame had spread to an awkward extent before he left home. “It is bad when your fame outruns your means,” said the Spanish Jesuit Gracian. At the age of six he had asked a Scripture riddle: “Who was the first man known to wear breeches?” and applause had resounded from the very verge of the heath. At seven he painted the Battle of Waterloo with tiger-lily pollen and black-currant juice, in the absence of water-colours. By the time he reached twelve he had in this manner been heard of as artist and scholar for at least two miles round. An individual whose fame spreads three or four thousand yards in the time taken by the fame of others similarly situated to travel six or eight hundred, must of necessity have something in him. Possibly Clym’s fame, like Homer’s, owed something to the accidents of his situation; nevertheless famous he was.

The truth was that Yeobright’s reputation had spread far too widely before he ever left home. “It is a bad thing when your fame runs ahead of your means,” observed the Spanish Jesuit Gracian. At six, he had posed a Scripture riddle: “Who was the first man known to wear breeches?” and applause had rolled in from the far edge of the heath. At seven he had painted the Battle of Waterloo with tiger-lily pollen and blackcurrant juice, there being no watercolors to hand. By the time he was twelve, he had in this way become known as both artist and scholar for at least two miles in every direction. A person whose renown travels three or four thousand yards in the time it takes others in like circumstances to get six or eight hundred must surely have something in him. Perhaps Clym’s fame, like Homer’s, owed something to the accidents of his position; still, famous he was.

Half-adventurers—brave and splendid figures—have been numerous. From the Crusades to the Palisades they have enriched the arts of history and fiction and the trade of historical fiction. But each of them had a prize to win, a goal to kick, an axe to grind, a race to run, a new thrust in tierce to deliver, a name to carve, a crow to pick—so they were not followers of true adventure.

Semi-adventurers—those gallant and impressive characters—have always been plentiful. From the medieval Crusades to the American Palisades, they have populated our histories and provided endless fodder for the writers of period dramas. But each of these men was driven by a specific motive: a prize to claim, a victory to achieve, a grudge to satisfy, or a legacy to forge. Because they were bound by their own agendas, they could never be considered genuine devotees of pure adventure.

It perplexed her first. If there had been a respectable minority on either side, the case would have been most natural. If nobody had regarded her, she would have taken the matter indifferently—such cases had occurred. If everybody, this man included, she would have taken it as a matter of course—people had done so before. But the smallness of the exception made the mystery.

At first, it baffled her. Had a decent few stood on either side, the situation would have seemed entirely ordinary. Had no one paid her any mind, she would have met it with cool indifference—that had happened before. Had everyone, this man among them, done so, she would have accepted it as perfectly natural—such things were known to occur. But the sheer narrowness of the exception turned it into a riddle.

The TAC Relation Extraction Dataset 3 , introduced by Zhang et al. 2017, is a fully supervised dataset of sentence-level binary relation mentions. It consists of 106k sentences with entity mention pairs collected from the TAC KBP 4 evaluations 2009-2014, with the years 2009 to 2012 used for training, 2013 for development, and 2014 for testing. Each sentence is labeled with one of 41 person-and organization-oriented relation types, e.g. per:title, org:founded, or the label no relation for negative instances. Table 1 summarizes key statistics of the dataset. All relation labels were obtained by crowdsourcing, using Amazon Mechanical Turk. Crowd workers were shown the example text, with head (subject) and tail (object) mentions highlighted, and asked to select among a set of relation label suggestions, or to assign no relation. Label suggestions were limited to relations compatible with the head and tail types. 5 The data quality is estimated as relatively high by Zhang et al. (2017) , based on a manual verification of 300 randomly sampled examples (93.3% validated as correct). The inter-annotator kappa label agreement of crowd workers was moderate at κ = 0.54 for 761 randomly selected mention pairs.

metadata.title: TACRED Revisited: A Thorough Evaluation of the TACRED Relation Extraction Task

text: Zhang et al. (2017) present the TAC Relation Extraction Dataset 3, a fully supervised collection of sentence-level binary relation mentions. Comprising 106,000 sentences with entity mention pairs drawn from the TAC KBP evaluations spanning 2009–2014, the dataset partitions the 2009–2012 data for training, 2013 for development, and 2014 for testing. Each sentence is annotated with one of 41 person- and organization-centric relation types—such as per:title or org:founded—or with the label no_relation for negative cases. Key dataset statistics are provided in Table 1. All relation annotations were gathered via crowdsourcing on Amazon Mechanical Turk. Workers viewed the example text with the head (subject) and tail (object) mentions highlighted and were instructed to choose from a set of suggested relation labels or to indicate no_relation. The label suggestions were restricted to relations consistent with the head and tail entity types. Zhang et al. (2017) estimate the data quality to be relatively high based on manual inspection of 300 randomly sampled instances, of which 93.3% were confirmed as correct. Inter-annotator agreement among crowd workers, measured by Cohen’s κ on 761 randomly selected mention pairs, was moderate at 0.54.

It proved a momentous night for Martin, for after dinner he met Russ Brissenden. How he chanced to come there, whose friend he was or what acquaintance brought him, Martin did not know. Nor had he the curiosity to inquire about him of Ruth. In short, Brissenden struck Martin as anaemic and feather-brained, and was promptly dismissed from his mind. An hour later he decided that Brissenden was a boor as well, what of the way he prowled about from one room to another, staring at the pictures or poking his nose into books and magazines he picked up from the table or drew from the shelves. Though a stranger in the house he finally isolated himself in the midst of the company, huddling into a capacious Morris chair and reading steadily from a thin volume he had drawn from his pocket. As he read, he abstractedly ran his fingers, with a caressing movement, through his hair. Martin noticed him no more that evening, except once when he observed him chaffing with great apparent success with several of the young women.

It proved a memorable night for Martin, for after dinner he met Russ Brissenden. How he had come there, whose friend he was, or by what acquaintance he had been introduced, Martin did not know. Nor did he care enough to ask Ruth about him. In brief, Brissenden seemed to Martin pale and foolish, and was at once dismissed from his thoughts. An hour later he decided that Brissenden was also a boor, from the way he drifted from room to room, peering at the pictures and thrusting his nose into the books and magazines he lifted from the table or took down from the shelves. Although a stranger in the house, he finally withdrew from the rest of the company and settled himself in the middle of it all, crouched in a roomy Morris chair, reading steadily from a slender book he had taken from his pocket. As he read, he idly passed his fingers through his hair with a tender, absent motion. Martin noticed him no more that evening, save once when he saw him joking with what seemed great success with several of the young women.

Here, screened by the outwork, she waited. In a few moments a splash was audible from the pond outside. Had the child been there he would have said that a second frog had jumped in; but by most people the sound would have been likened to the fall of a stone into the water. Eustacia stepped upon the bank.

Concealed by the crumbling embankment, she remained motionless. Moments later, a sudden plash disturbed the stillness of the water outside. A child, listening, would have imagined a bullfrog taking flight; however, to a more discerning ear, the sound was that of a stone sinking fast. Beatrix stepped toward the water’s edge.

metadata.title: Automatic Detection of Generated Text is Easiest when Humans are Fooled

text: The human evaluation task is set up in a manner similar to the automatic one. We ask raters to determine whether a passage of text was produced by a human or by a computer algorithm. (Complete instructions are provided in the Appendix.) Raters may select among four options: “definitely” or “possibly” machine-generated, and “definitely” or “possibly” human-written. They are first presented with an excerpt consisting of 16 WordPiece tokens. After they make Table 1: Performance (accuracy and AUC) of the fine-tuned BERT classifier and several simple baselines on detecting length-192 sequences generated with one word of priming (1worccond). Note that p1.0 denotes untruncated random sampling, in which we sample from 100% of the probability mass. The last column reports human performance on the same task, where accuracy with a 50% baseline is calculated by randomly pairing samples from each decoding strategy with a human-written sample.

Meanwhile Venn had left the heath and gone to call upon Mrs. Yeobright, with whom he had been on friendly terms since she had learnt what a providential countermove he had made towards the restitution of the family guineas. She wondered at the lateness of his call, but had no objection to see him.

By then, Silas had retreated from the wild scrub to wait upon Mrs. Wentworth. A certain warmth had characterized their relations since the hour she first grasped the fortuitous agency by which he had secured the return of the ancestral coins. She was struck by the unseasonable moment of his appearance, yet she entertained no thought of refusing his company.

Madame Defarge slightly waved her hand, to imply that she heard, and might be relied upon to arrive in good time, and so went through the mud, and round the corner of the prison wall. The Vengeance and the Juryman, looking after her as she walked away, were highly appreciative of her fine figure, and her superb moral endowments.

Madame Defarge gave a small wave of her hand, signaling that she had taken note and could be counted on to show up punctually, then trudged off through the mire and disappeared around the bend of the prison wall. The Vengeance and the Juryman, watching her retreat, greatly admired her striking form and her remarkable strength of character.

He was still saying it, over and over,—demanding, entreating, threatening, to know if it wanted any more,—when he felt the fellows of his gang laying hands on him, patting him on the back and trying to put his coat on him. And then came a sudden rush of blackness and oblivion.

He was still repeating it, again and again—insisting, pleading, threatening, demanding to know whether it wanted any more—when he felt the members of his gang seize him, slap him on the back, and try to get his coat onto him. Then a sudden surge of blackness swept over him, and he slipped into oblivion.

Copy Vs. Context We observe that models allowing for augmented input sentences effectively learn to output the target words used as augmented translations. Table 5 illustrates the rates of usage. We compute for each word added in the input sentence as T (part of a lexicalised match), R (not in the match) and E (from an embedding match), how often they appear in the translated sentence. Results show that T words increase their usage rate by more than 10% compared to the corresponding base models. Considering R words, models incorporating fuzzy matches increase their usage rate compared to base models, albeit with lower rates than for T words. Furthermore, the number of R words output by FM + is clearly lower than those output by FM # , demonstrating the effect of marking unrelated matching words. Thus, we can confirm the copy behaviour of the networks with lexicalised matches. Words marked as E (embedding matches) increase their usage rates when compared to base models but are far from the rates of T words. We hypothesize that these sentences are not copied by the translation model, rather they are used to further contextualise translations.

metadata.title: Boosting Neural Machine Translation with Similar Translations
metadata.section: Individual comparison of Matching algorithms and Augmentation methods

text: Copy vs. Context We observe that models that permit augmented input sentences effectively learn to output the target words used as augmented translations. Table 5 illustrates the rates of usage. For each word added to the input sentence as T (part of a lexicalized match), R (not in the match), or E (from an embedding match), we calculate how often it appears in the translated sentence. The results show that T words increase their usage rate by more than 10% relative to the corresponding baseline models. For R words, models incorporating fuzzy matches increase their usage rate compared with the baseline models, although the rates remain lower than those for T words. Moreover, the number of R words output by FM+ is clearly lower than that produced by FM#, demonstrating the effect of marking unrelated matching words. We can therefore confirm the copying behavior of the networks with lexicalized matches. Words labeled as E (embedding matches) also show increased usage rates compared with the baseline models, but these remain far below the rates observed for T words. We hypothesize that these sentences are not copied by the translation model; rather, they are used to further contextualize the translations.

Five dollars for five thousand words, ten words for a cent—that particular thought took up its residence in his brain, and he could no more escape it than he could the “$3.85” under his eyelids. A change seemed to come over the latter, and he watched curiously, till “$2.00” burned in its stead. Ah, he thought, that was the baker. The next sum that appeared was “$2.50.” It puzzled him, and he pondered it as if life and death hung on the solution. He owed somebody two dollars and a half, that was certain, but who was it? To find it was the task set him by an imperious and malignant universe, and he wandered through the endless corridors of his mind, opening all manner of lumber rooms and chambers stored with odds and ends of memories and knowledge as he vainly sought the answer. After several centuries it came to him, easily, without effort, that it was Maria. With a great relief he turned his soul to the screen of torment under his lids. He had solved the problem; now he could rest. But no, the “$2.50” faded away, and in its place burned “$8.00.” Who was that? He must go the dreary round of his mind again and find out.

Five dollars for five thousand words, ten words to a cent—this specific calculation had wedged itself firmly in his skull, and he could no more shake it loose than he could dismiss the glowing “$3.85” burned into the backs of his eyelids. The figure shifted abruptly, and he watched it through heavy lids, curious, until “$2.00” blazed in its place. Ah, he thought, that was what he owed the baker. Next, “$2.50” flickered into view. It stumped him, and he turned the number over and over as if life itself hung on working out who it was for. He definitely owed someone two dollars and fifty cents, that much was certain, but who? The cold, imperious world seemed to demand he find the answer at once, so he wandered the endless winding halls of his memory, prying open every cluttered storage closet and dusty cranny stuffed with half-forgotten facts and random fragments of the past, turning up empty no matter how hard he looked. It felt like centuries passed before the answer hit him out of nowhere, no effort at all: that was Maria. He exhaled in sharp relief, ready to let his frayed mind rest now that he’d cracked the puzzle. But no sooner had he relaxed than the “$2.50” dimmed, and “$8.00” burned bright in its spot. Who did he owe eight dollars to? He’d have to drag himself through that same tedious, exhausting sweep of his memories all over again to find out.

There are three main reasons the HCN + machine teaching combination is so effective. First, dialog authors are generally subject matter experts who can make well-informed decisions about which actions the DM should perform in individual situations. If the DM has to automatically learn an action policy from logs, a large corpus of data is required. Second, the HCN allows dialog authors to explicitly encode domain-specific knowledge as action templates (bot activities or responses) and action masks (when a given response should be disallowed) without learning. Third, we can use intelligent filtering to select the most impactful failed dialogs for teachers to review.

metadata.title: Conversation Learner -A Machine Teaching Tool for Building Dialog Managers for Task-Oriented Dialog Systems

text: The integration of HCN with machine teaching proves highly effective for three primary reasons. First, dialogue authors are typically subject matter experts equipped to make authoritative decisions regarding the specific actions a dialogue manager (DM) should take in given contexts. If the DM were instead required to autonomously derive an action policy from logs, an extensive data corpus would be necessary. Second, the HCN framework allows authors to directly incorporate domain-specific knowledge through action templates—defining bot activities or responses—and action masks, which specify when certain responses are prohibited, without the need for learning. Third, intelligent filtering can be applied to isolate the most significant failed dialogues for review by teachers.

They were both wrapped in old and shabby outer garments, which might, perhaps, serve the double purpose of protecting their persons from the rain, and sheltering them from observation. The husband carried a lantern, from which, however, no light yet shone; and trudged on, a few paces in front, as though—the way being dirty—to give his wife the benefit of treading in his heavy footprints. They went on, in profound silence; every now and then, Mr. Bumble relaxed his pace, and turned his head as if to make sure that his helpmate was following; then, discovering that she was close at his heels, he mended his rate of walking, and proceeded, at a considerable increase of speed, towards their place of destination.

They were both wrapped in old, shabby outer clothes, which may have served the double purpose of shielding their bodies from the rain and keeping them out of sight. The husband carried a lantern, though no light had yet been lit in it, and walked a few paces ahead, as if, the road being muddy, to let his wife place her feet in his heavy tracks. They moved along in complete silence; from time to time Mr. Bumble slackened his pace and looked back, as though to make sure his wife was still behind him. Then, finding her close at his heels, he quickened his step and went on, at a much greater speed, toward their destination.

“I speak, miss, of twenty years ago. He married--an English lady--and I was one of the trustees. His affairs, like the affairs of many other French gentlemen and French families, were entirely in Tellson’s hands. In a similar way I am, or I have been, trustee of one kind or other for scores of our customers. These are mere business relations, miss; there is no friendship in them, no particular interest, nothing like sentiment. I have passed from one to another, in the course of my business life, just as I pass from one of our customers to another in the course of my business day; in short, I have no feelings; I am a mere machine. To go on--”

“I speak to you, miss, of twenty years past. He wed an English gentlewoman, and I counted myself among his appointed trustees. His affairs, like those of a great many French gentlemen and their households, were held entirely in the keeping of Haberdashers’ & Cleeve’s. In much the same fashion, I currently serve, or have at one time served, as some sort of fiduciary for dozens of our patrons. These are mere professional arrangements, miss; there is no friendship between us, no special regard, nothing resembling personal sentiment. I have passed from one matter to the next across the length of my career, just as I move from one patron’s account to another over the course of a single workday; in short, I set all personal feeling aside, I am little more than a business machine. To continue—”

She danc'd along with vague, regardless eyes,
Anxious her lips, her breathing quick and short:
The hallow'd hour was near at hand: she sighs
Amid the timbrels, and the throng'd resort
Of whisperers in anger, or in sport;
'Mid looks of love, defiance, hate, and scorn,
Hoodwink'd with faery fancy; all amort,
Save to St. Agnes and her lambs unshorn,
And all the bliss to be before to-morrow morn.

text:
She moved in dance with distant, careless gaze,
Her lips tight-pressed, her breath a hurried stream:
The sacred moment hovered in its place: she sighs
Among the tambourines, amid the teeming dream
Of murmurers in fury, or in jest supreme;
Through glances fond, defiant, bitter, cold,
Enchanted by a fey and phantom gleam;
All dead to earth, save Agnes' lambs unshorn,
And all the joy to wake before the coming morn.

A sudden idea came into the head of the young architect. He would ask this girl to dine with him. Here was the element that his splendid but solitary periodic feasts had lacked. His brief season of elegant luxury would be doubly enjoyable if he could add to it a lady’s society. This girl was a lady, he was sure—her manner and speech settled that. And in spite of her extremely plain attire he felt that he would be pleased to sit at table with her.

A sudden thought struck the young architect unbidden: he would invite this girl to dine with him. It was exactly the missing piece of his lavish, if lonely, recurring feasts. The brief spell of elegant luxury he carved out for himself would feel twice as joyful if he could share it with a lady’s company. He had no doubt she was a gentlewoman—her bearing and the way she spoke settled that beyond question. And even though her attire was remarkably plain, he knew he would be thoroughly pleased to sit across a table from her.

One day when Mr. Seeders came in to dinner he had been drinking beer. There were only two or three customers in the restaurant. When Mr. Seeders had finished his weakfish he got up, put his arm around Tildy’s waist, kissed her loudly and impudently, walked out upon the street, snapped his fingers in the direction of the laundry, and hied himself to play pennies in the slot machines at the Amusement Arcade.

One afternoon, Mr. Seeders arrived for his meal with the flush of beer already upon him. The restaurant was nearly empty, holding just a couple of other patrons. Once he had polished off his weakfish, he rose, slid an arm around Tildy’s waist, and planted a loud, brazen kiss on her. Then he sauntered out to the street, snapped his fingers toward the laundry, and headed off to lose pennies in the slot machines at the Amusement Arcade.

For example, noun phrase (colored in blue) is critical and should be copied. Function words (colored in red) is of little relevance and can be generated by IRN itself. However, distant supervision treats them the same. Secondly, rule-based matching may cause semantic ambiguity (dashed line colored in black). Lastly, the training criterion of cross entropy is not directly relevant to the evaluation metric using slot error rate. To address these issues, we use reinforcement learning to obtain the optimal actions.

metadata.title: Slot-consistent NLG for Task-oriented Dialogue Systems with Iterative Rectification Network
metadata.section: Training with Supervised Learning and Distant Supervision

text: However, there are several issues when trying to leverage labels generated by distant supervision for training. First, the importance of tokens within a candidate is not uniform. For instance, the noun phrase (colored in blue) is crucial and should be copied, whereas function words (colored in red) are of little relevance and can be produced by IRN itself. Nonetheless, distant supervision treats all tokens identically. Second, rule-based matching may introduce semantic ambiguity (dashed line colored in black). Finally, the cross-entropy training objective is not directly aligned with the evaluation metric based on slot error rate. To address these issues, we employ reinforcement learning to learn the optimal actions.

If this journey had taken place during my days of study and happiness, it would have afforded me inexpressible pleasure. But a blight had come over my existence, and I only visited these people for the sake of the information they might give me on the subject in which my interest was so terribly profound. Company was irksome to me; when alone, I could fill my mind with the sights of heaven and earth; the voice of Henry soothed me, and I could thus cheat myself into a transitory peace. But busy, uninteresting, joyous faces brought back despair to my heart. I saw an insurmountable barrier placed between me and my fellow men; this barrier was sealed with the blood of William and Justine, and to reflect on the events connected with those names filled my soul with anguish.

Had I undertaken this voyage in the years of my contentment and learning, it would have filled me with unutterable delight. But a shadow had fallen across my life, and I sought out these people only for what they might reveal about the matter that consumed me with such dreadful intensity. The presence of others wearied me; in solitude, I could steep my mind in the visions of earth and sky; Henry’s voice brought me comfort, and I could deceive myself into a fleeting calm. Yet bustling, indifferent, cheerful faces plunged me back into despair. I perceived an impassable gulf fixed between me and my fellow creatures, a gulf sealed with the blood of William and Justine, and to dwell upon the memories those names summoned wracked my soul with torment.

The few-shot scenario poses unique challenges in learning the emission and transition scores of CRF. It is infeasible to learn the transition on the few labeled data, and prior label dependency in source domain cannot be directly transferred due to discrepancy in label set. To tackle the label discrepancy problem, we introduce the collapsed dependency transfer mechanism. It transfers label dependency information from source domains to target domains by abstracting domain-specific labels into abstract domain-independent labels and modeling the label dependencies between these abstract labels.

metadata.title: Few-shot Slot Tagging with Collapsed Dependency Transfer and Label-enhanced Task-adaptive Projection Network

text: The few-shot setting introduces distinct difficulties for estimating both emission and transition potentials in CRFs. Learning transitions from a handful of labeled examples is impractical, and label dependencies from a source domain cannot be naively transferred because of mismatches between label sets. To address this label-set discrepancy, we propose the collapsed dependency transfer mechanism, which propagates label dependency knowledge from source to target domains by mapping domain-specific labels onto abstract, domain-agnostic labels and capturing dependencies among these abstracted labels.

“And now, what do you know of matter, according to your own positive science? You know it only by its phenomena, its appearances. You are aware only of its changes, or of such changes in it as cause changes in your consciousness. Positive science deals only with phenomena, yet you are foolish enough to strive to be ontologists and to deal with noumena. Yet, by the very definition of positive science, science is concerned only with appearances. As somebody has said, phenomenal knowledge cannot transcend phenomena.

“And now, what do you truly know of matter, if you speak only from your own exact science? You know it merely through its manifestations, through what it seems to present to you. You are conscious only of its alterations, or of those alterations in it that produce alterations in your own consciousness. Positive science concerns itself solely with phenomena, and yet you are rash enough to aspire to be ontologists and to traffic in noumena. By the very definition of positive science, however, science has to do only with appearances. As another has observed, knowledge of the phenomenal order can never pass beyond phenomena.

But his heart was heavy, notwithstanding; and he wished, as he crept into his narrow bed, that that were his coffin, and that he could be lain in a calm and lasting sleep in the churchyard ground, with the tall grass waving gently above his head, and the sound of the old deep bell to soothe him in his sleep.

Even so, a heavy, aching weight settled in his chest; as he slipped into his cramped, narrow bed, he wished it were his coffin instead. He longed to lie in quiet, unending rest beneath the churchyard dirt, tall grass swaying softly above his head, the deep, resonant chime of the old church bell lulling him gently through his sleep.

Occasionally she came to a spot where independent worlds of ephemerons were passing their time in mad carousal, some in the air, some on the hot ground and vegetation, some in the tepid and stringy water of a nearly dried pool. All the shallower ponds had decreased to a vaporous mud amid which the maggoty shapes of innumerable obscure creatures could be indistinctly seen, heaving and wallowing with enjoyment. Being a woman not disinclined to philosophize she sometimes sat down under her umbrella to rest and to watch their happiness, for a certain hopefulness as to the result of her visit gave ease to her mind, and between important thoughts left it free to dwell on any infinitesimal matter which caught her eyes.

Every now and then she came upon a spot where entire self-contained worlds of ephemerons whiled away their short lives in giddy, frantic revelry: some darted through the open air, some scurried over sunbaked earth and foliage, others wriggled through the lukewarm, slimy water of a half-evaporated pool. All the shallower ponds in the area had shrunk to little more than steamy, mist-wreathed mud, where the maggot-like forms of countless unidentifiable small creatures drifted in and out of view, squirming and rolling contentedly in the muck. As a woman given to quiet philosophical musing, she would sometimes settle beneath her umbrella to rest and observe their small, uncomplicated joy. A soft, steady hopefulness about the outcome of her visit had lightened her mind entirely, so that in the lulls between weightier ruminations, she was free to fix her attention on whatever tiny, trivial thing happened to catch her eye.

“I confess, my son, that I have always looked forward to your marriage with our dear Elizabeth as the tie of our domestic comfort and the stay of my declining years. You were attached to each other from your earliest infancy; you studied together, and appeared, in dispositions and tastes, entirely suited to one another. But so blind is the experience of man that what I conceived to be the best assistants to my plan may have entirely destroyed it. You, perhaps, regard her as your sister, without any wish that she might become your wife. Nay, you may have met with another whom you may love; and considering yourself as bound in honour to Elizabeth, this struggle may occasion the poignant misery which you appear to feel.”

“I’ll be frank with you, my son: I have long held out hope that your marriage to our dear Elizabeth would be the thread that knits our household’s comfort tight, and a steady support for me as I enter my final years. The two of you were fond of one another from earliest babyhood, learned side by side, and seemed perfectly matched in every turn of temperament and every personal taste. But human foresight is so painfully blind that the very details I thought would ensure this plan’s success may have undone it completely. You may see Elizabeth as nothing more than your sister, with no desire to ever make her your wife. What’s more, you might have met another woman you love, and believing you are honor-bound to Elizabeth, that internal struggle could be the cause of the sharp, gnawing misery you’ve been carrying so plainly of late.”

On Jenks mode, NDCG@10 outperforms BLEU and NIST on 10 out of the 16 language directions. Further, the 4 IR metrics collectively hold the top scores for 6 language directions. BEER seems to be a little bit better than the IR metrics, claiming the top spot for 7 language directions. Note that the participating BEER system is trained on provided in-domain data, while we are getting comparable results without any tuning. It is also worth pointing out that the intrinsic MT metrics work at sentence level while in comparison, CLIReval works at the documentlevel. Nonetheless, the results are encouraging and show the potential of CLIR as a proxy task for MT evaluation.

metadata.title: CLIReval: Evaluating Machine Translation as a Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval Task

text: We use the official evaluation scripts 12 to compute linear correlations between IR metrics and human judgments. Table 1 reports the results for 16 language directions, and the IR metrics perform well. Under Jenks mode, NDCG@10 outperforms BLEU and NIST in 10 of the 16 language directions. Moreover, the four IR metrics together achieve the highest scores for six language directions. BEER appears to perform slightly better than the IR metrics, taking the top position for seven language directions. It should be noted that the participating BEER system is trained on the provided in-domain data, whereas we obtain comparable results without any tuning. It is also worth emphasizing that the intrinsic MT metrics operate at the sentence level, whereas CLIReval, in comparison, works at the document level. Nonetheless, the results are encouraging and indicate the potential of CLIR as a proxy task for MT evaluation.

Chandler protracted his walk until the Forties began to intersect the great and glittering primrose way, for the evening was yet young, and when one is of the _beau monde_ only one day in seventy, one loves to protract the pleasure. Eyes bright, sinister, curious, admiring, provocative, alluring were bent upon him, for his garb and air proclaimed him a devotee to the hour of solace and pleasure.

Chandler stretched his stroll until the Forties met the broad, shimmering avenue lit like pale gold, for the evening was still fresh, and when one belongs to the fashionable world just one day in seventy, one likes to draw out the delight. Bright, sly, inquisitive, admiring, challenging, enticing eyes turned his way, since his attire and manner marked him as a worshipper of the hour of ease and enjoyment.

As he gazed at her and listened, his thoughts grew daring. He reviewed all the wild delight of the pressure of her hand in his at the door, and longed for it again. His gaze wandered often toward her lips, and he yearned for them hungrily. But there was nothing gross or earthly about this yearning. It gave him exquisite delight to watch every movement and play of those lips as they enunciated the words she spoke; yet they were not ordinary lips such as all men and women had. Their substance was not mere human clay. They were lips of pure spirit, and his desire for them seemed absolutely different from the desire that had led him to other women’s lips. He could kiss her lips, rest his own physical lips upon them, but it would be with the lofty and awful fervor with which one would kiss the robe of God. He was not conscious of this transvaluation of values that had taken place in him, and was unaware that the light that shone in his eyes when he looked at her was quite the same light that shines in all men’s eyes when the desire of love is upon them. He did not dream how ardent and masculine his gaze was, nor that the warm flame of it was affecting the alchemy of her spirit. Her penetrative virginity exalted and disguised his own emotions, elevating his thoughts to a star-cool chastity, and he would have been startled to learn that there was that shining out of his eyes, like warm waves, that flowed through her and kindled a kindred warmth. She was subtly perturbed by it, and more than once, though she knew not why, it disrupted her train of thought with its delicious intrusion and compelled her to grope for the remainder of ideas partly uttered. Speech was always easy with her, and these interruptions would have puzzled her had she not decided that it was because he was a remarkable type. She was very sensitive to impressions, and it was not strange, after all, that this aura of a traveller from another world should so affect her.

As he looked at her and listened, his thoughts grew bold. He recalled all the wild rapture of her hand pressed in his at the door, and longed for that touch again. His eyes kept drifting to her lips, and he hungered for them with an almost fierce desire. Yet there was nothing coarse or earthly in it. It was pure delight to watch the movement and subtle play of those lips as she shaped the words she spoke; and yet they were no common lips such as belong to all men and women. Their substance was not mere flesh. They were lips of spirit, and his longing for them seemed wholly unlike the desire that had drawn him to other women’s mouths. He might kiss her lips, lay his own bodily lips upon them, but it would be with the grave and exalted ardor with which one might kiss the garment of God. He was unaware of this change in the measure of things that had come over him, and did not know that the light in his eyes as he looked at her was the same light that shines in every man’s eyes when love’s desire is upon him. He did not suspect how hot and manly his gaze was, or that its warm flame was working some alchemy in her spirit. Her keen virginity ennobled and disguised his own feeling, lifting his thoughts into a starry chastity, and he would have been astonished to learn that there shone from his eyes, like warm waves, something that passed into her and awakened an answering heat. She was faintly troubled by it, and more than once, though she knew not why, it broke in upon her thought with its sweet intrusion and forced her to search for the rest of ideas only half spoken. Speech was always easy to her, and such interruptions would have bewildered her had she not decided that it was because he was an unusual sort of man. She was highly susceptible to impressions, and it was not strange, after all, that the aura of a traveler from another world should move her so.

text:
Set the flood free, and you’ll see it plain,
Wave on wave, held back for you;
Crimson trial! doubting Thomas,
Now, do you still deny your bird was true?

“If it had been possible, Miss Manette, that you could have returned the love of the man you see before yourself--flung away, wasted, drunken, poor creature of misuse as you know him to be--he would have been conscious this day and hour, in spite of his happiness, that he would bring you to misery, bring you to sorrow and repentance, blight you, disgrace you, pull you down with him. I know very well that you can have no tenderness for me; I ask for none; I am even thankful that it cannot be.”

“Had it been within the realm of possibility, Miss Manette, for you to return the affection of the man who stands before you—squandered, dissolute, and ruined as you know him to be—he would be certain, even in his greatest happiness, that he could only bring you to wretchedness. He would burden you with sorrow and regret, staining your life and dragging you down into his own disgrace. I understand perfectly that you can feel no love for me; I do not seek it; indeed, I am grateful that such a thing can never be.”

Meantime there was one thing which had got pushed into the background of my mind. That was the half-conviction that when the nature of my proposed calamity should be reported to those superstitious people, it would have such an effect that they would want to compromise. So, by and by when I heard footsteps coming, that thought was recalled to me, and I said to myself, “As sure as anything, it's the compromise. Well, if it is good, all right, I will accept; but if it isn't, I mean to stand my ground and play my hand for all it is worth.”

Meanwhile, a thought had been lurking in the back of my mind, half-formed: that once word of the disaster I intended reached those superstitious folk, they’d be shaken enough to seek a settlement. So when I caught the sound of approaching footsteps, that notion surfaced again, and I told myself, “This will be the compromise, I’m certain. If it’s fair, fine—I’ll take it. But if not, I’m resolved to hold firm and play my hand for everything it’s worth.”

There was also a cat in a willow basket, from the partly-opened lid of which she gazed with half-closed eyes, and affectionately surveyed the small birds around.

Perched right on the very top of the stacked moving load, the girl sat perfectly still. Upside-down tables and chairs ringed her on all sides, with a heavy oak settle propped up behind her back. Lined up in front of her were potted geraniums, myrtles, and cacti, alongside a canary in a small cage—every last one of these items had almost certainly been pulled straight from the windows of the house they’d just vacated. There was a cat tucked into a woven willow basket too, peeking out through its half-opened lid with half-closed, drowsy eyes, as it gazed fondly at the small birds gathered nearby.

He slowly withdrew from the grave. He did not attempt to fill up the hole, replace the flowers, or do anything at all. He simply threw up his cards and forswore his game for that time and always. Going out of the churchyard silently and unobserved—none of the villagers having yet risen—he passed down some fields at the back, and emerged just as secretly upon the high road. Shortly afterwards he had gone from the village.

He retreated slowly from the graveside. He made no effort to shovel the earth back, restore the flowers, or repair the site in any way. He simply surrendered his hand and renounced his schemes, both for the present moment and for all time. Departing the churchyard in silence while the townspeople still slept, he cut through the rear pastures and emerged stealthily onto the main road. A short while later, he had vanished from the village entirely.

His big atlas, from which papers projected, seemed forgotten by his side. So he sat for a long time, and neither he nor Letty moved or spoke. But they were in the same shadow. He remained like that for a long while, and neither he nor Letty stirred or spoke. Yet they shared the same shadow. They drew deep solace from each other’s presence—a comfort they had not known since their sorrows first descended.

Well, he would fight his way on and up higher. And he would take Ruth with him. Her he dearly loved, and he was confident that she would shine anywhere. As it was clear to him that he had been handicapped by his early environment, so now he perceived that she was similarly handicapped. She had not had a chance to expand. The books on her father’s shelves, the paintings on the walls, the music on the piano—all was just so much meretricious display. To real literature, real painting, real music, the Morses and their kind, were dead. And bigger than such things was life, of which they were densely, hopelessly ignorant. In spite of their Unitarian proclivities and their masks of conservative broadmindedness, they were two generations behind interpretative science: their mental processes were mediaeval, while their thinking on the ultimate data of existence and of the universe struck him as the same metaphysical method that was as young as the youngest race, as old as the cave-man, and older—the same that moved the first Pleistocene ape-man to fear the dark; that moved the first hasty Hebrew savage to incarnate Eve from Adam’s rib; that moved Descartes to build an idealistic system of the universe out of the projections of his own puny ego; and that moved the famous British ecclesiastic to denounce evolution in satire so scathing as to win immediate applause and leave his name a notorious scrawl on the page of history.

Well, he would keep fighting his way onward and upward. And he would take Ruth with him. He loved her dearly, and he felt sure she would succeed anywhere. Just as he knew he had been held back by the surroundings of his youth, so he now saw that she had been held back too. She had never been given room to grow. The books on her father’s shelves, the paintings on the walls, the music on the piano—all of it was merely showy pretension. As for true literature, true painting, true music, the Morses and people like them were dead to such things. And greater than such things was life itself, of which they were profoundly and hopelessly ignorant. Despite their Unitarian leanings and their pose of conservative broadmindedness, they were two generations behind interpretive science: their minds were medieval, while their thinking about the final realities of existence and the universe seemed to him to be the same metaphysical habit of thought that was as young as the youngest race and as old as the cave man, and older still—the same impulse that drove the first Pleistocene ape-man to fear the darkness; that led the first impulsive Hebrew savage to embody Eve from Adam’s rib; that prompted Descartes to construct an idealist universe from the projections of his own meager ego; and that inspired the celebrated British churchman to rail against evolution in a satire so biting that it earned instant applause and left his name a notorious scribble on the page of history.

To a soul so isolated, any human resonance, however threatening, was a reprieve from the void. The advent of a visible enemy reignited his spent spirit; he hauled himself to his feet and plunged headlong into the gloom.
