Nah. First she moved to a garrison town on the far side of Wessex, then she spent several months earning her keep doing seamstress work in Melchester, boarding with a very respectable widow who takes in that kind of piece work. She only turned up at the Union house Sunday morning, as far as I know, and folks around here figure she tramped every last step of the way from Melchester. I can’t tell you why she left her position, because I truly don’t know, and I’d never lie just to make up an answer. That’s all there is to the story, ma’am.

The days were too short. There was so much he wanted to study. He cut his sleep down to five hours and found that he could get along upon it. He tried four hours and a half, and regretfully came back to five. He could joyfully have spent all his waking hours upon any one of his pursuits. It was with regret that he ceased from writing to study, that he ceased from study to go to the library, that he tore himself away from that chart-room of knowledge or from the magazines in the reading-room that were filled with the secrets of writers who succeeded in selling their wares. It was like severing heart strings, when he was with Ruth, to stand up and go; and he scorched through the dark streets so as to get home to his books at the least possible expense of time. And hardest of all was it to shut up the algebra or physics, put note-book and pencil aside, and close his tired eyes in sleep. He hated the thought of ceasing to live, even for so short a time, and his sole consolation was that the alarm clock was set five hours ahead. He would lose only five hours anyway, and then the jangling bell would jerk him out of unconsciousness and he would have before him another glorious day of nineteen hours.

The days never stretched long enough for him. There was an endless list of subjects he ached to dig into. He trimmed his sleep down to five hours first, and found he could get by perfectly well on that amount of rest. When he tested four and a half, though, he had to grudgingly bump it back up to five. He would have gladly devoted every single waking minute to any one of his pursuits, if he could. It always stung to set his writing aside to hit the textbooks, to step away from his studies to head to the library, to tear himself away from that vault of reference materials or the reading room magazines crammed with trade secrets from writers who’d cracked the code of selling their work. When he was with Ruth, standing up to leave felt like yanking his own heartstrings out by the root; he’d race home down the dark streets as fast as he could, to cut down the time he lost away from his books. The hardest part of all, though, was shutting his algebra or physics textbooks, tucking his notebook and pencil to the side, and letting his heavy, tired eyes slip shut for sleep. He hated the thought of pausing his life even for that short stretch, and his only comfort was knowing his alarm was set to go off five hours later. He was only losing five hours, after all, and then that harsh jangling bell would yank him out of his unconscious haze, and he’d have a whole other glorious nineteen-hour day stretched out ahead of him.

There are steamers that cross the Channel from there, and by that means I should reach Paris, which is where I long to be. Why didn’t you do it in Paris?—it is more the fashion there. You have come only to distress me, a lonely woman, and shorten my days! I wish that you would bestow your presence where you bestow your love!”

“O Clym! Don’t lay the blame on me for what is simply your own stubborn folly. If you were determined to tie yourself to someone beneath you, why come back here to do it? Why not do it in Paris?—it’s far more the custom there. You have returned only to wound me, a solitary woman, and cut short my days. I wish you would give your company where you give your heart!”

metadata.title: Attend, Translate and Summarize: An Efficient Method for Neural Cross-Lingual Summarization

text: However, the source article reports only the approximate value “nearly 340,000 yuan”, while the CLSMT-generated summary incorrectly renders this as a precise figure, an inappropriate deviation from the source content. Both the CLSMT and CLSMS generated summaries also exhibit the drawback of containing redundant information, as their output is overly lengthy. The summary generated by our proposed ATS-A method matches the reference summary most closely, and captures nearly all key informational points from the source article. These findings confirm that our approach can generate summaries with more accurate semantic content than the evaluated baseline models.

The undertaker gentlemen who had the charge of the affair offered a small remonstrance against these alterations in the rites; but, as the river lay uncomfortably close, and as more than one voice observed how effectually a plunge in cold water might restore obstinate members of the craft to prudence, the remonstrance was but weak and short-lived. So the new-ordered procession set forth, with a chimney-sweep at the reins of the hearse, instructed by the ordinary driver, who sat beside him under strict observation for that very end; while a pie-man, likewise accompanied by his own minister of state, guided the mourning coach. Before they had proceeded far down the Strand, a bear-keeper, one of the well-known street figures of the day, was enlisted as an extra embellishment; and his bear, a black creature, rough and most unkempt, lent a singularly funerary dignity to that section of the procession in which it marched.

metadata.title: Learning and Evaluating Emotion Lexicons for 91 Languages
metadata.section: A Novel Approach to Lexicon Creation

text: TargetMT is subsequently deployed as a distant supervision signal to train a model that generates word-level emotion predictions from target-language word embeddings. The TargetMT-train and TargetMT-dev splits are used for model parameter fitting and hyperparameter optimization respectively, while the TargetMT-test partition is reserved for held-out downstream evaluation. Once training is finalized, the model is applied to generate new emotion labels for all terms included in TargetMT, producing a second target-side emotion lexicon designated TargetPred. Our rationale for this step rests on the assumption that a sufficiently well-trained model will exhibit strong generalizability. Figure 1 presents a schematic overview of the full methodology for generating and evaluating an emotion lexicon for a specified target language using source-language supervision. The diagram includes a toy example that initiates with an English Valence-Arousal (VA) lexicon containing the terms sunshine, nuclear, and terrorism alongside their associated numerical Valence and Arousal scores, and yields an expanded German lexicon that incorporates both translated entries with adjusted VA scores and additional entries derived from the embedding model with newly inferred scores.

We use the datasets from the Surface Realization 2019 Shared Task (Mille et al., 2019) in our experiments, which includes 11 languages in 20 treebanks from the Universal Dependencies (Nivre et al., 2016) . We experiment on the shallow track, i.e., all tokens in the output are present in the input tree. We only report the BLEU score (Papineni et al., 2002) as the evaluation metric, since we mostly evaluate on the lemma level, where the metrics involving word forms are irrelevant.

metadata.title: Fast and Accurate Non-Projective Dependency Tree Linearization

text: We use the datasets from the Surface Realization 2019 Shared Task (Mille et al., 2019) in our experiments, which comprise 11 languages across 20 treebanks from Universal Dependencies (Nivre et al., 2016). We conduct experiments on the shallow track, that is, all tokens in the output are present in the input tree. We report only the BLEU score (Papineni et al., 2002) as the evaluation metric, since we mainly evaluate at the lemma level, where metrics based on word forms are not relevant.

The attack upon the Maeterlinck school could not have been made at a more opportune moment. A fierce controversy was precipitated. Saleeby and Haeckel indorsed and defended “The Shame of the Sun,” for once finding themselves on the same side of a question. Crookes and Wallace ranged up on the opposing side, while Sir Oliver Lodge attempted to formulate a compromise that would jibe with his particular cosmic theories. Maeterlinck’s followers rallied around the standard of mysticism. Chesterton set the whole world laughing with a series of alleged non-partisan essays on the subject, and the whole affair, controversy and controversialists, was well-nigh swept into the pit by a thundering broadside from George Bernard Shaw. Needless to say the arena was crowded with hosts of lesser lights, and the dust and sweat and din became terrific.

Harthaven, Hale & Rowe had tentatively pressed a first run of fifteen hundred copies, but glowing early notices sent a second edition of double that size whirring through the presses before the first had even fully shipped; before the second run could hit bookstalls, a third printing of five thousand had already been commissioned. A London publishing house struck a deal via transatlantic cable to secure UK distribution rights, and hard on the heels of that announcement came word that French, German, and Norwegian translation efforts were already underway. The volume’s takedown of the neo-symbolist school of continental thought could not have landed at a more serendipitous cultural moment. A roaring public dispute erupted almost overnight. Biologists Julian Hargrove and Ernst Voss stepped forward to endorse and defend *The Blight of the Noon Sky*, for the first time in their decades-long public rivalry finding themselves aligned on an intellectual question. Physicists Arthur Croft and Alfred Waller lined up in the opposing camp, while Sir Alistair Loddon tried to craft a middle ground that aligned neatly with his own idiosyncratic theories of cosmic order. The neo-symbolist faithful rallied behind the banner of transcendental mysticism, penning fiery open letters and essays in popular weeklies. Humorist and essayist Gilbert K. Pembroke sent the entire reading public cackling with a string of supposedly neutral takes on the fracas, and the entire debate, along with nearly every public figure tangled up in it, was almost blown clean off the cultural stage by a scathing, thunderous takedown from playwright George Seaborne. Needless to say, the arena was packed with throngs of lesser-known commentators, and the dust, sweat, and roar of clashing opinions grew deafening.

I swore him to secrecy and then whispered her name--“Puss Flanagan.” He looked disappointed, and said he didn't remember the countess. How natural it was for the little courtier to give her a rank. He asked me where she lived.

He noted her pale face, her eyes wide and tense, and her clenched hands, with secret satisfaction. He had succeeded. He had communicated the stuff of fancy and feeling from out of his brain. It had struck home. No matter whether she liked it or not, it had gripped her and mastered her, made her sit there and listen and forget details.

Julian observed the bloodless pallor of Elara’s skin, the frantic dilation of her gaze, and the white-knuckled tension of her fingers with a hidden, predatory pride. He had won. The raw essence of his imagination had been transplanted from his consciousness into hers. It had struck deep. Whether she found it abhorrent or exquisite mattered little; it had enslaved her attention, compelling her into a breathless silence that rendered the rest of the world invisible.

They strolled towards a place of seats and hammocks between the big trees and the rose garden, and the talk turned for a time upon Rendezvous. "They have the tidiest garden in Essex," said Manning. "It's not Mrs. Rendezvous' fault that it is so. Mrs. Rendezvous, as a matter of fact, has a taste for the picturesque. She just puts the things about in groups in the beds. She wants them, she says, to grow anyhow. She desires a romantic disorder. But she never gets it. When he walks down the path all the plants dress instinctively.... And there's a tree near their gate; it used to be a willow. You can ask any old man in the village. But ever since Rendezvous took the place it's been trying to present arms. With the most extraordinary results. I was passing the other day with old Windershin. 'You see that there old poplar,' he said. 'It's a willow,' said I. 'No,' he said, 'it did used to be a willow before Colonel Rendezvous he came. But now it's a poplar.'... And, by Jove, it is a poplar!"...

They wandered toward a cluster of wicker chairs and canvas swings nestled between the towering elms and the flowering thickets, where the conversation eventually drifted to Highwood. "It is the most disciplined plot in the county," Sterling remarked. "One cannot blame Clara for its severity. Left to her own devices, she prefers the wild and the whimsical; she scatters seeds with a carefree hand, hoping for a riot of untamed color. She yearns for a beautiful chaos, yet it never quite materializes. The moment the General steps onto the gravel, the perennials seem to snap to attention of their own accord. There is even a specimen near the entrance—a willow, by all accounts. Any local veteran will swear to it. But since the General’s arrival, that tree has been straining to assume a military posture, with the most bizarre consequences. I happened to be walking past with old Barnaby recently. 'Have a look at that tall cypress,' he muttered. 'That’s a willow, surely,' I replied. 'No,' he insisted, 'it was a willow once, before the Colonel took command. But these days, sir, it’s a cypress.' And remarkably enough, it truly has become a cypress!"

And Cecily made everything far more difficult. She showed him no mercy. She held him at a distance. “How can I let you make love to me,” she said, “when all our English men are going off to the war, when Teddy is a prisoner and Hugh is in the trenches? If I were a man—!”

He had many invitations to dinner, some of which he accepted. Persons got themselves introduced to him in order to invite him to dinner. And he went on puzzling over the little thing that was becoming a great thing. Bernard Higginbotham invited him to dinner. He puzzled the harder. He remembered the days of his desperate starvation when no one invited him to dinner. That was the time he needed dinners, and went weak and faint for lack of them and lost weight from sheer famine. That was the paradox of it. When he wanted dinners, no one gave them to him, and now that he could buy a hundred thousand dinners and was losing his appetite, dinners were thrust upon him right and left. But why? There was no justice in it, no merit on his part. He was no different. All the work he had done was even at that time work performed. Mr. and Mrs. Morse had condemned him for an idler and a shirk and through Ruth had urged that he take a clerk’s position in an office. Furthermore, they had been aware of his work performed. Manuscript after manuscript of his had been turned over to them by Ruth. They had read them. It was the very same work that had put his name in all the papers, and, it was his name being in all the papers that led them to invite him.

He received dozens of dinner invitations, and said yes to a handful of them. People went out of their way to get formally introduced to him just so they could extend a dinner invite. All the while, he kept turning over the same small, swelling puzzle in his mind. When Bernard Higginbotham invited him to dinner, that confusion only deepened. He thought back to the days when he was half-dead with starvation, when no one had ever asked him out to eat. That was when he’d needed those dinners most: he’d grown weak and lightheaded for lack of food, shedding pounds from sheer, unrelenting hunger. It was such a cruel paradox. When he’d been desperate for a hot meal, no one had offered him one. Now, when he could afford to buy a hundred thousand dinners on a whim and had even started losing his appetite for them, invites were being pressed on him from every direction. What was the reason? There was no fairness to it, no credit he had newly earned. He was exactly the same man he’d always been. Every line of the work he’d produced had already been written back in those hungry days. Mr. and Mrs. Morse had dismissed him as a lazy idler and a shirker, and had pushed Ruth to convince him to take a clerk’s position at an office. They’d even seen all that work of his, too: Ruth had handed them manuscript after manuscript, and they’d read each one. It was that exact same work that had gotten his name printed in every newspaper, and it was that very newspaper notoriety, not the work itself, that was leading them to invite him now.

He turned to an opening in the hedge, which he found to be a gate, and mounting thereon, he sat meditating whether to seek a cheap lodging in the village, or to ensure a cheaper one by lying under some hay or corn-stack. The crunching jangle of the waggon died upon his ear. He was about to walk on, when he noticed on his left hand an unusual light—appearing about half a mile distant. Oak watched it, and the glow increased. Something was on fire.

He veered toward a gap in the thorny hedge, and found it was a rickety wooden gate; he hoisted himself up onto its upper rail, and sat mulling over whether to hunt for an affordable room in the village just down the lane, or save what little coin he had left by curling up for the night under a hay or rick stack. The gritty, jangling clatter of the passing wagon faded slowly from his ears. He was just about to climb down and keep moving, when an odd, flickering light snagged his attention on his left side, glowing roughly half a mile away across the fields. Thorne watched as the golden glow brightened, seeping higher over the treeline. Something was on fire.

The glimmering vision was rent asunder and dissipated by Arthur, who, all evening, had been trying to draw his wild man out. Martin Eden remembered his decision. For the first time he became himself, consciously and deliberately at first, but soon lost in the joy of creating in making life as he knew it appear before his listeners’ eyes. He had been a member of the crew of the smuggling schooner _Halcyon_ when she was captured by a revenue cutter. He saw with wide eyes, and he could tell what he saw. He brought the pulsing sea before them, and the men and the ships upon the sea. He communicated his power of vision, till they saw with his eyes what he had seen. He selected from the vast mass of detail with an artist’s touch, drawing pictures of life that glowed and burned with light and color, injecting movement so that his listeners surged along with him on the flood of rough eloquence, enthusiasm, and power. At times he shocked them with the vividness of the narrative and his terms of speech, but beauty always followed fast upon the heels of violence, and tragedy was relieved by humor, by interpretations of the strange twists and quirks of sailors’ minds.

The shimmering tableau was shattered and scattered by Arthur, who had spent the entire evening coaxing forth his untamed companion. Martin Eden recalled his resolve. For the first time he stepped fully into himself, at first with conscious intent, but swiftly surrendering to the rapture of shaping life as he had lived it into vivid being before his audience. He had sailed among the crew of the smuggler _Halcyon_ on the night a revenue cutter ran her down. He had watched with hungry eyes, and he knew how to render what he saw. He summoned the heaving ocean into the room, the men and the vessels that rode upon it. He lent them his own sight until they gazed through his eyes at all he had witnessed. With an artist’s instinct he plucked from the immense clutter of particulars, sketching scenes that blazed and smoldered with light and hue, infusing such motion that his hearers were swept beside him on a torrent of raw eloquence, fervor, and force. He startled them at turns with the starkness of the tale and his coarse words, yet loveliness always came hard upon the heels of brutality, and sorrow was leavened by laughter, by glimpses into the crooked and curious workings of seamen’s minds.

“Cainy’s grandfather was a very clever man,” said Matthew Moon. “Invented a’ apple-tree out of his own head, which is called by his name to this day—the Early Ball. You know ’em, Jan? A Quarrenden grafted on a Tom Putt, and a Rathe-ripe upon top o’ that again. ’Tis trew ’a used to bide about in a public-house wi’ a ’ooman in a way he had no business to by rights, but there—’a were a clever man in the sense of the term.”

“Cainy’s grandfather was a very clever man,” said Matthew Moon. “He invented an apple tree entirely from his own head, and it’s still called by his name to this day—the Early Ball. You know the sort, Jan? A Quarrenden grafted onto a Tom Putt, with a Rathe-ripe on top of that. It’s true he used to linger about in a public house with a woman in a way he really had no business doing, by rights, but there—he was a clever man, in the proper sense of the word.”

Well, while they were gradually playing out, it suddenly occurred to me to wonder what had become of Marco. I looked around; he was nowhere to be seen. Oh, but this was ominous! I pulled the king's sleeve, and we glided away and rushed for the hut. No Marco there, no Phyllis there! They had gone to the road for help, sure. I told the king to give his heels wings, and I would explain later. We made good time across the open ground, and as we darted into the shelter of the wood I glanced back and saw a mob of excited peasants swarm into view, with Marco and his wife at their head. They were making a world of noise, but that couldn't hurt anybody; the wood was dense, and as soon as we were well into its depths we would take to a tree and let them whistle. Ah, but then came another sound--dogs! Yes, that was quite another matter. It magnified our contract--we must find running water.

Well, as they were slowly wearing themselves out, it came into my head all at once to ask what had become of Rinaldo. I looked about me; he was nowhere in sight. Ah, that was an evil sign! I caught the prince by the sleeve, and we slipped away and ran for the cottage. No Rinaldo there, no Beatrice there! They had surely gone up the lane to fetch aid. I told the prince to put wings on his heels, and that I would tell him the reason later. We made swift progress across the open stretch, and just as we flashed into the cover of the trees I looked back and saw a noisy throng of peasants pouring into view, with Rinaldo and his wife at their head. They were making an infernal uproar, but that could do no one any harm; the wood was thick, and once we were fairly deep in it we could climb a tree and let them bay below. But then came another sound—hounds! Yes, that was a very different matter. It sharpened our peril—we must find water, and quickly.

The idea took hold of Mr. Direck's mind that until they got through the park gate it would be quite out of order to say anything. The lane and the road and the stile and the gate were all so much preliminary stuff to be got through before one could get to business. But after the little white gate the way was clear, the park opened out and one could get ahead without bothering about the steering. And Mr. Direck had, he felt, been diplomatically involved in lanes and by-ways long enough.

Mr. Direck was fully settled in the belief that bringing up what he needed to say before they passed through the park gate would be entirely out of line. The winding lane, the open road, the wooden stile, and the entry gate all felt like nothing more than formalities to work through before he could get to the actual point. Once they stepped past that small white gate, though, the path would clear, the whole park would open up around them, and they could move forward without having to fuss over navigating every tricky little turn. And Mr. Direck felt he’d already spent far too long tiptoeing around diplomatic niceties, stuck in those meandering, roundabout lead-up moments.

There was no need to despair because he himself was one of the feeble folk. God was with him indeed, and he was with God. The King was coming to his own. Amidst the darknesses and confusions, the nightmare cruelties and the hideous stupidities of the great war, God, the Captain of the World Republic, fought his way to empire. So long as one did one's best and utmost in a cause so mighty, did it matter though the thing one did was little and poor?

There was no cause for despair, for he counted himself among the world’s feeble, unassuming souls. Truly, God abided with him, and he abided in God. The King was coming to claim what was rightfully his. Amid the darkness and disarray, the nightmarish cruelties and grotesque, senseless folly of the great war, God, Captain of the World Republic, fought his way to rightful empire. As long as one gave their full, unreserved effort to such a mighty cause, did the smallness and plainness of the task they carried out really hold any weight?

Now, as they thus pursued their way, the reddleman occasionally left his companion’s side, and, stepping behind the van, looked into its interior through a small window. The look was always anxious. He would then return to the old man, who made another remark about the state of the country and so on, to which the reddleman again abstractedly replied, and then again they would lapse into silence. The silence conveyed to neither any sense of awkwardness; in these lonely places wayfarers, after a first greeting, frequently plod on for miles without speech; contiguity amounts to a tacit conversation where, otherwise than in cities, such contiguity can be put an end to on the merest inclination, and where not to put an end to it is intercourse in itself.

As they continued their journey, the reddleman would occasionally drift away from his companion and move to the back of the wagon, peering through a small window into its interior. Each time he looked, his expression was one of deep concern. He would then rejoin the old man, who might offer another observation about the local landscape, to which the reddleman would give a distracted reply before they both lapsed back into quiet. Neither of them found this silence uncomfortable; in such desolate regions, travelers often walk together for miles without speaking after their initial greetings. In these isolated places, proximity serves as a silent form of communication, especially since—unlike in a crowded city—such closeness can be ended at any moment. To choose to remain together is, in itself, a kind of conversation.

Automated conversational agents can reduce the costs of customer support, a necessary service in just about every business. However, training a goal-directed dialogue agent for a domain often requires acquiring annotated dialogues to cover all possible conversation flows. Commonly, this is done using the Wizard-of-Oz technique (Kelley, 1984) , where two crowdsource workers converse with each other, while also annotating the state at each turn. This technique has been employed to construct several datasets (Hemphill et al., 1990; . Recently, it has been used to build the MultiWOZ dataset (Budzianowski et al., 2018) , a large corpus of dialogues across 7 domains.

metadata.title: Zero-Shot Transfer Learning with Synthesized Data for Multi-Domain Dialogue State Tracking

text: Automated conversational agents offer the potential to lower customer support expenses, a function essential to nearly every business. Yet, developing a goal-oriented dialogue system for a specific domain typically demands the collection of annotated dialogues that capture all plausible conversational trajectories. The standard approach relies on the Wizard-of-Oz method (Kelley, 1984), in which two crowdworkers interact while simultaneously labeling the dialogue state at each turn. This method has been applied to create multiple datasets (Hemphill et al., 1990; …). Most recently, it was employed to produce the MultiWOZ corpus (Budzianowski et al., 2018), a large-scale dataset spanning seven domains.

In the black prison of the Conciergerie, the doomed of the day awaited their fate. They were in number as the weeks of the year. Fifty-two were to roll that afternoon on the life-tide of the city to the boundless everlasting sea. Before their cells were quit of them, new occupants were appointed; before their blood ran into the blood spilled yesterday, the blood that was to mingle with theirs to-morrow was already set apart.

Within the dark keep of the Conciergerie, the day’s condemned held vigil for their end. Their count matched the full cycle of the seasons’ weeks. Fifty-two souls were to be swept that afternoon upon the city’s living current into the shoreless, eternal ocean. Ere their chambers were emptied of them, fresh lodgers had been named; before their lifeblood joined the gore shed the day before, the blood destined to blend with theirs on the morrow was already chosen.

I stayed in Lausanne for two days, burdened by this painful state of mind. I looked out over the lake: the water was still, everything around me was quiet, and the snowy mountains, “the palaces of nature,” remained unchanged. Little by little, that serene and heavenly scene soothed me, and I went on with my journey toward Geneva.

Current state of the art conditional text generation models accomplish a high level of fluency and coherence, mostly thanks to advances in sequenceto-sequence architectures with attention and copy (Sutskever et al., 2014; Bahdanau et al., 2015; Gu et al., 2016) , fully attention-based Transformer architectures (Vaswani et al., 2017; and more recently pretrained language modeling for natural language understanding Radford et al., 2018; . There has been a growing interest in understanding how maximum likelihood training and approximate beam-search decoding in these models lead to less human-like text in open-ended text generation such as language modeling and story generation (Holtzman et al., 2020; Welleck et al., 2020; See et al., 2019) . In this paper we investigate how these models are prone to generate hallucinated text in conditional text generation, specifically, extreme abstractive document summarization (Narayan et al., 2018a) . Document summarization -the task of producing a shorter version of a document while preserving its information content (Mani, 2001; Nenkova and McKeown, 2011) -requires models to generate text that is not only human-like but also faithful and/or factual given the document. The example in Figure 1 illustrates that the faithfulness and factuality are yet to be conquered by conditional text generators. The article describes an event of "Conservative MP Zac Smith winning the primary for 2016 London mayoral election", but summaries often forge entities (e.g., "Nigel Goldsmith" or "Zac Goldwin") or information (e.g., "UKIP leader Nigel Goldsmith", "Nigel Goldsmith winning the mayoral election", "Sadiq Khan being the former London mayor" or "Zac Goldwin being the Labour's candidate") that are not supported by the document or are factually wrong. Interestingly, all summaries are topical and fluent, and perform well in terms of ROUGE scores (Lin and Hovy, 2003) .

metadata.title: On Faithfulness and Factuality in Abstractive Summarization

text: Current state-of-the-art conditional text generation models achieve a high level of fluency and coherence, largely due to advances in sequence-to-sequence architectures with attention and copy mechanisms (Sutskever et al., 2014; Bahdanau et al., 2015; Gu et al., 2016), fully attention-based Transformer architectures (Vaswani et al., 2017), and more recently, pretrained language modeling for natural language understanding (Radford et al., 2018). There has been growing interest in understanding how maximum likelihood training and approximate beam-search decoding in these models can lead to text that is less human-like in open-ended generation tasks such as language modeling and story generation (Holtzman et al., 2020; Welleck et al., 2020; See et al., 2019). In this paper, we investigate how these models are prone to generating hallucinated text in conditional text generation, specifically in extreme abstractive document summarization (Narayan et al., 2018a). Document summarization—the task of producing a shorter version of a document while preserving its informational content (Mani, 2001; Nenkova and McKeown, 2011)—requires models to generate text that is not only human-like but also faithful and/or factual with respect to the source document. The example in Figure 1 illustrates that faithfulness and factuality remain unresolved challenges for conditional text generators. The article describes an event in which “Conservative MP Zac Smith” wins the primary for the 2016 London mayoral election, but summaries often invent entities (e.g., “Nigel Goldsmith” or “Zac Goldwin”) or information (e.g., “UKIP leader Nigel Goldsmith,” “Nigel Goldsmith winning the mayoral election,” “Sadiq Khan being the former London mayor,” or “Zac Goldwin being Labour’s candidate”) that are unsupported by the document or factually incorrect. Interestingly, all of these summaries are topical and fluent, and they also achieve strong ROUGE scores (Lin and Hovy, 2003).

text: Fagin laid his hand upon the bundle, and locking it in the cupboard, sat down again without speaking. But he did not take his eyes off the robber, for an instant, during this action; and now that they sat over against each other, face to face, he looked fixedly at him, with his lips quivering so violently, and his face so altered by the emotions which had mastered him, that the housebreaker involuntarily drew back his chair, and surveyed him with a look of real affright.

Fagin rested his hand on the wrapped bundle, stowed it in the cupboard and turned the key in the lock, then settled back into his seat without uttering a sound. Not once during this whole sequence did his gaze stray from the robber’s face, and now that they sat directly across from each other, face to face, he pinned the other man with a fixed stare. His lips quivered so violently, and his features were so warped by the overwhelming emotions that had seized him, that the housebreaker pulled his chair back without thinking, eyeing him with a look of unfeigned, sharp fright.

“Never! I’ll hold my tongue like the very death that I don’t mind meeting, even though I can clear myself of half you believe by speaking. Yes. I will! Who of any dignity would take the trouble to clear cobwebs from a wild man’s mind after such language as this? No; let him go on, and think his narrow thoughts, and run his head into the mire. I have other cares.”

Never going to happen! I’ll keep my mouth clamped shut as tight as the grave I don’t even fear facing, even though speaking up right now would clear me of half the garbage you buy into about me. That’s right, I mean it. What person with an ounce of dignity would bother scraping the cobwebs out of some unreasonable brute’s head after he’s spewed language like that? No. Let him keep right on, cling to his narrow, small-minded thoughts, and run his own head straight into the mire for all I care. I’ve got far more important things to tend to.

The long narrow table under the big sycamores between the house and the adapted barn that Mr. Direck learnt was used for "dancing and all that sort of thing," was covered with a blue linen diaper cloth, and that too surprised him. This was his first meal in a private household in England, and for obscure reasons he had expected something very stiff and formal with "spotless napery." He had also expected a very stiff and capable service by implacable parlourmaids, and the whole thing indeed highly genteel. But two cheerful women servants appeared from what was presumably the kitchen direction, wheeling a curious wicker erection, which his small guide informed him was called Aunt Clatter--manifestly deservedly--and which bore on its shelves the substance of the meal. And while the maids at this migratory sideboard carved and opened bottles and so forth, the small boy and a slightly larger brother, assisted a little by two young men of no very defined position and relationship, served the company. Mrs. Britling sat at the head of the table, and conversed with Mr. Direck by means of hostess questions and imperfectly accepted answers while she kept a watchful eye on the proceedings.

The long, narrow table beneath the great sycamores, set between the house and the converted barn that Mr. Direck had learned was used for “dancing and all that sort of thing,” was laid with a blue linen diaper cloth, and that, too, took him by surprise. This was his first meal in a private English household, and for reasons he could not clearly have explained, he had expected something very formal and severe, with “spotless napery.” He had also imagined a rigid, efficient service from unyielding parlourmaids, and the whole affair, indeed, to be intensely genteel. Instead, two smiling women servants emerged from what was presumably the kitchen side, pushing a strange wicker contrivance, which his little guide told him was called Aunt Clatter—an apt name, plainly—and which carried the meal upon its shelves. And while the maids at this travelling sideboard carved the meat, opened bottles, and did the like, the small boy and a slightly older brother, with some help from two young men of no very clear standing or connection, served the guests. Mrs. Britling sat at the head of the table and spoke with Mr. Direck in the manner of a hostess asking questions and receiving answers only half accepted, all the while keeping a vigilant eye on the proceedings.

My mind went half mad as I remembered my promise to make another being like him, and, trembling with fury, I ripped apart the work I had been engaged upon. The wretch saw me destroy the creature on whose future existence his own happiness depended, and with a howl of fiendish despair and vengeance, he turned away and left.

text: For future research, we plan to integrate our approach into a multi-task learning framework. Additionally, we aim to investigate how a probabilistic bilingual lexicon, induced solely from monolingual corpora, affects our method. Table 9 presents the corpus statistics for En2ZhSum. #Avg-Words (S) indicates the mean count of English words per source document. #AvgChars (R) represents the average number of Chinese characters in the reference. #AvgSentWords captures the average word count per sentence in the source documents, while #AvgSents reflects the average number of sentences per source document.

“Take heed, Oliver! take heed!” said the old man, shaking his right hand before him in a warning manner. “He’s a rough man, and thinks nothing of blood when his own is up. Whatever falls out, say nothing; and do what he bids you. Mind!” Placing a strong emphasis on the last word, he suffered his features gradually to resolve themselves into a ghastly grin, and, nodding his head, left the room.

“Pay attention, Oliver—pay real close attention!” the old man said, shaking his right hand sharply in front of him as a warning. “That man’s a vicious brute, and he doesn’t bat an eye at spilling blood when his temper’s fully riled. No matter what happens, don’t utter a single word, and do exactly what he tells you. Got it?” He laid heavy stress on that final word, let his features slowly twist into a ghastly, cold grin, gave a short nod of his head, and walked out of the room.

You have seen that kind of people who will never let on that they don't know the meaning of a new big word. The more ignorant they are, the more pitifully certain they are to pretend you haven't shot over their heads. The queen was just one of that sort, and was always making the stupidest blunders by reason of it. She hesitated a moment; then her face brightened up with sudden comprehension, and she said she would do it for me.

You’ve likely met the sort of person who would rather die than admit a complex word is over their head. In fact, the less they truly understand, the more pathetically determined they are to pretend your vocabulary hasn't left them in the dust. The queen was exactly like that, and her pride frequently resulted in the most idiotic mistakes. After a brief pause, her expression cleared as if she’d finally caught on, and she promised to handle the matter for me.

We explore the use of different rewards to guide Neural Temporal Difference (NTD), a RL-based multi-document summarizer (Gao et al., 2019a) . We consider three unsupervised reward functions: two baseline methods REAPER and JS (see §3 and selects the top 10 (TAC'08) or 15 (TAC'09) sentences from each source document to build pseudo references and uses SBERT to measure the similarity between summaries and pseudo references. In addition, we consider a non-RL-based stateof-the-art unsupervised summarizer proposed by Yogatama et al. (2015) (YLS15) . We use ROUGE to measure the quality of the generated summaries and leave human evaluations for future work. Table 5 presents the results. We find SUPERT is the strongest reward among the considered rewards: it helps NTD perform on par with YSL15 on TAC'08 and perform significantly better on TAC'09.

metadata.title: SUPERT: Towards New Frontiers in Unsupervised Evaluation Metrics for Multi-Document Summarization

text: We investigate the use of different rewards to guide Neural Temporal Difference (NTD), a reinforcement learning-based multi-document summarization model (Gao et al., 2019a). We consider three unsupervised reward functions: two baseline methods, REAPER and JS (see §3), which select the top 10 sentences for TAC’08 or 15 sentences for TAC’09 from each source document to construct pseudo references and use SBERT to assess the similarity between summaries and pseudo references. In addition, we include a non-RL-based state-of-the-art unsupervised summarizer proposed by Yogatama et al. (2015) (YLS15). We use ROUGE to evaluate the quality of the generated summaries and leave human evaluation for future work. Table 5 reports the results. We find that SUPERT is the strongest reward among those considered: it enables NTD to perform on par with YLS15 on TAC’08 and significantly better on TAC’09.

“O yes. I once heard of an Italian, who sat down at a gaming table with only a louis, (that’s a foreign sovereign), in his pocket. He played on for twenty-four hours, and won ten thousand pounds, stripping the bank he had played against. Then there was another man who had lost a thousand pounds, and went to the broker’s next day to sell stock, that he might pay the debt. The man to whom he owed the money went with him in a hackney-coach; and to pass the time they tossed who should pay the fare. The ruined man won, and the other was tempted to continue the game, and they played all the way. When the coachman stopped he was told to drive home again: the whole thousand pounds had been won back by the man who was going to sell.”

“Oh yes. I once heard of an Italian who sat down at a gaming table with only a louis— that is, a foreign coin— in his pocket. He kept playing for twenty-four hours and won ten thousand pounds, cleaning out the bank he was playing against. Then there was another man who had lost a thousand pounds and went to the broker’s the next day to sell stock so he could pay the debt. The man he owed the money to went with him in a hackney coach, and to while away the time they tossed up to see who should pay the fare. The ruined man won, and the other was tempted to go on with the game, so they kept playing all the way. When the coachman stopped, he was told to drive back home again: the whole thousand pounds had been won back by the man who had gone to sell.”

And thus the night crept slowly on. Oliver lay awake for some time, counting the little circles of light which the reflection of the rushlight-shade threw upon the ceiling; or tracing with his languid eyes the intricate pattern of the paper on the wall. The darkness and the deep stillness of the room were very solemn; as they brought into the boy’s mind the thought that death had been hovering there, for many days and nights, and might yet fill it with the gloom and dread of his awful presence, he turned his face upon the pillow, and fervently prayed to Heaven.

And so the night dragged slowly onward. Oliver lay awake for some time, counting the small rings of light that the reflection from the rushlight shade cast upon the ceiling, or following with his weary eyes the intricate pattern of the wallpaper. The darkness and deep stillness of the room were very solemn, and as they brought to the boy’s mind the thought that death had hovered there for many days and nights, and might still fill it with the gloom and terror of his dreadful presence, he turned his face to the pillow and prayed fervently to Heaven.

Nae mercy then, for airn or steel;
The brawnie, banie, ploughman chiel,
Brings hard owrehip, wi’ sturdy wheel,
The strong forehammer,
Till block an’ studdie ring an reel,
Wi’ dinsome clamour.

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
No mercy then for iron or steel;  
The brawny, bonny ploughman’s heel  
Drives hard o’er all with sturdy wheel,  
And forceful stroke,  
Till block and anvil ring and reel  
With din and smoke.

“The carriage left the streets behind, passed the North Barrier, and emerged upon the country road. At two-thirds of a league from the Barrier--I did not estimate the distance at that time, but afterwards when I traversed it--it struck out of the main avenue, and presently stopped at a solitary house, We all three alighted, and walked, by a damp soft footpath in a garden where a neglected fountain had overflowed, to the door of the house. It was not opened immediately, in answer to the ringing of the bell, and one of my two conductors struck the man who opened it, with his heavy riding glove, across the face.

The coach rumbled beyond the city’s edge, clearing the Northern Gate to meet the winding country road. Scarcely two miles from the threshold—a span I only calculated later upon retracing those steps—the vehicle veered from the main thoroughfare, coming to a halt before a desolate manor. The three of us dismounted and followed a sodden, yielding trail through a courtyard where a choked fountain spilled over its rim, advancing toward the entrance. When the bell’s summons went unanswered for a moment too long, one of my escorts lashed out, striking the servant who finally appeared across the mouth with a weighted gauntlet.

London!—that great place!—nobody—not even Mr. Bumble—could ever find him there! He had often heard the old men in the workhouse, too, say that no lad of spirit need want in London; and that there were ways of living in that vast city, which those who had been bred up in country parts had no idea of. It was the very place for a homeless boy, who must die in the streets unless some one helped him. As these things passed through his thoughts, he jumped upon his feet, and again walked forward.

London! That sprawling, endless city! No one could ever track him down there, not even Mr. Bumble himself. He had often heard the elderly men in the workhouse say, too, that no plucky lad need ever go hungry in London, that the vast metropolis held all sorts of ways to get by that people raised in the countryside could never even fathom. It was exactly the place for a homeless boy, one who would surely die out on the roads if no one stopped to help him. As these thoughts ran through his mind, he leaped to his feet and kept moving forward.

“‘Do you consider,’ said his companion to him, ‘that you will be obliged to pay three months’ rent and to lose the produce of your garden? I do not wish to take any unfair advantage, and I beg therefore that you will take some days to consider of your determination.’

“Think about it,” his friend said to him, “you’ll have to cover three months’ rent and give up whatever your garden yields. I’ve no desire to gain at your expense, so I ask you to take a few days and reflect before you decide.”

The animal looked up into his master’s face while these preparations were making; whether his instinct apprehended something of their purpose, or the robber’s sidelong look at him was sterner than ordinary, he skulked a little farther in the rear than usual, and cowered as he came more slowly along. When his master halted at the brink of a pool, and looked round to call him, he stopped outright.

The creature lifted its eyes to his master’s face while these arrangements were being made; whether some dim instinct had half-divined their meaning, or whether the outlaw’s oblique glance had in it a harsher edge than common, he lagged a little farther behind than was his wont, and crept onward with a more subdued pace. When his master paused at the margin of a pool and turned to summon him, he came to a dead stop.

“Urged by this impulse, I seized on the boy as he passed and drew him towards me. As soon as he beheld my form, he placed his hands before his eyes and uttered a shrill scream; I drew his hand forcibly from his face and said, ‘Child, what is the meaning of this? I do not intend to hurt you; listen to me.’

Driven by a sudden compulsion, I caught the lad mid-stride and pulled him close. The moment his gaze fell upon my figure, he clapped both palms over his eyes and let out a piercing cry; I wrenched his hands away from his countenance and spoke, "Boy, why do you shrink so? I mean you no harm; hear what I have to say."

We were at our supper of beef stew and dried apples when he trotted in as if on the heels of a dog team, and made one of the mess at our table. With the freedom of the camps he assaulted our ears and claimed the fellowship of men lost in the wilds of a hash house. We embraced him as a specimen, and in three minutes we had all but died for one another as friends.

We sat around our evening meal of mutton broth and withered apricots when he strode through the doorway like a man chasing a sled pack, and settled himself among the clutter of our board. Armed with the rough liberty of trail life, he stormed our silence and demanded the brotherhood of souls adrift in a greasy cook tent. We took him up as a curiosity, and within moments had nearly perished of devotion, each for the other, like sworn companions.

“It is not too late,” she cried. “I will show you. I will prove to you that my love has grown, that it is greater to me than my class and all that is dearest to me. All that is dearest to the bourgeoisie I will flout. I am no longer afraid of life. I will leave my father and mother, and let my name become a by-word with my friends. I will come to you here and now, in free love if you will, and I will be proud and glad to be with you. If I have been a traitor to love, I will now, for love’s sake, be a traitor to all that made that earlier treason.”

“It is not too late,” she cried out. “Let me show you. Let me prove my love has deepened—that it now outweighs my class and everything I once held sacred. I will scorn all that the bourgeoisie cherishes. I am no longer afraid of living. I will abandon my father and mother, let my name become a mockery among my friends. I will come to you here, this very moment, in free love if you’ll have me, and I shall be proud and joyful at your side. If I once betrayed love, I will now, for love’s sake, betray everything that drove me to that earlier betrayal.”

Then it was that the Madness of Manhattan, the frenzy of Fuss and Feathers, the Bacillus of Brag, the Provincial Plague of Pose seized upon Towers Chandler. He was on Broadway, surrounded by pomp and style, and there were eyes to look at him. On the stage of that comedy he had assumed to play the one-night part of a butterfly of fashion and an idler of means and taste. He was dressed for the part, and all his good angels had not the power to prevent him from acting it.

At that instant, the New York mania for status and the infectious drive to show off took hold of Towers Chandler. There on Broadway, amidst the glitter and the gaze of the crowd, he found himself on a stage of his own making. He had decided to spend one night portraying a wealthy man of fashion and taste. Clad in the perfect attire, he was beyond the reach of his better judgment; he was committed to the performance, and nothing could stop him from playing the role.

“When they had been separated for some time,” returned Mr. Brownlow, “and your mother, wholly given up to continental frivolities, had utterly forgotten the young husband ten good years her junior, who, with prospects blighted, lingered on at home, he fell among new friends. This circumstance, at least, you know already.”

“When they had been parted for some time,” returned Mr. Brownlow, “and your mother, having surrendered herself entirely to continental frivolities, had quite forgotten the young husband who was a full ten years her junior, and who, with his prospects ruined, remained at home in misery, he fell in with new acquaintances. This much, at any rate, you already know.”

In truth, they were children together, so far as love was concerned, and they were as naive and immature in the expression of their love as a pair of children, and this despite the fact that she was crammed with a university education and that his head was full of scientific philosophy and the hard facts of life.

Where their romantic feelings were concerned, Lila and Elias might as well have been small children, every bit as unworldly and clumsy when it came to showing their affection for one another as a pair of gawky grade-schoolers with their first crush. That wide-eyed innocence held even though Lila had completed a full four years of university study, and Elias’s mind was crammed full of rigorous scientific philosophy and the unvarnished, hard-won truths of adult life.

Mr. Losberne was with them, and would be for the next two days; but Rose was too well acquainted with the excellent gentleman’s impetuosity, and foresaw too clearly the wrath with which, in the first explosion of his indignation, he would regard the instrument of Oliver’s recapture, to trust him with the secret, when her representations in the girl’s behalf could be seconded by no experienced person. These were all reasons for the greatest caution and most circumspect behaviour in communicating it to Mrs. Maylie, whose first impulse would infallibly be to hold a conference with the worthy doctor on the subject. As to resorting to any legal adviser, even if she had known how to do so, it was scarcely to be thought of, for the same reason. Once the thought occurred to her of seeking assistance from Harry; but this awakened the recollection of their last parting, and it seemed unworthy of her to call him back, when—the tears rose to her eyes as she pursued this train of reflection—he might have by this time learnt to forget her, and to be happier away.

Mr. Losberne was staying with them and would remain for two more days. Rose, however, understood the good man’s rash temper all too well and could vividly imagine the fury he would unleash upon the person responsible for Oliver’s capture during that first surge of outrage. She dared not confide in him while no one with more experience could support her arguments on the girl’s behalf. These considerations demanded the utmost prudence and careful restraint in revealing the matter to Mrs. Maylie, whose immediate instinct would certainly be to consult the estimable doctor about it. Turning to any lawyer was equally unthinkable, even had she known how to proceed, for the very same reason. At one moment she considered asking Harry for help, but that thought stirred the memory of their last farewell, and it felt beneath her to summon him back when—tears welled in her eyes as she followed this line of thought—he might by now have learned to forget her and found greater happiness elsewhere.

Among the men and women the multitude,
I perceive one picking me out by secret and divine signs,
Acknowledging none else, not parent, wife, husband, brother, child,
any nearer than I am,
Some are baffled, but that one is not--that one knows me.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Among the men and women in the crowd,  
I feel one choosing me by hidden, holy signs,  
Seeing no one else—not parent, wife, husband, brother, child,  
No one closer than I am,  
Some are confused, but that one is not—that one knows me.

His sentence was pronounced, and I could only grieve and be patient. I sat by his bed, watching him; his eyes were closed, and I thought he slept; but presently he called to me in a feeble voice, and bidding me come near, said, “Alas! The strength I relied on is gone; I feel that I shall soon die, and he, my enemy and persecutor, may still be in being. Think not, Walton, that in the last moments of my existence I feel that burning hatred and ardent desire of revenge I once expressed; but I feel myself justified in desiring the death of my adversary. During these last days I have been occupied in examining my past conduct; nor do I find it blamable. In a fit of enthusiastic madness I created a rational creature and was bound towards him to assure, as far as was in my power, his happiness and well-being. This was my duty, but there was another still paramount to that. My duties towards the beings of my own species had greater claims to my attention because they included a greater proportion of happiness or misery. Urged by this view, I refused, and I did right in refusing, to create a companion for the first creature. He showed unparalleled malignity and selfishness in evil; he destroyed my friends; he devoted to destruction beings who possessed exquisite sensations, happiness, and wisdom; nor do I know where this thirst for vengeance may end. Miserable himself that he may render no other wretched, he ought to die. The task of his destruction was mine, but I have failed. When actuated by selfish and vicious motives, I asked you to undertake my unfinished work, and I renew this request now, when I am only induced by reason and virtue.

His fate was sealed, and I had no choice but to bear my grief quietly and hold fast to patience. I sat at his bedside keeping watch; his eyes were shut, and I assumed he slept, until he called to me in a thin, wavering voice, bidding me lean closer. “Alas,” he said, “the strength I once relied on has vanished entirely. I know I will die soon, and he, my enemy and lifelong persecutor, may still draw breath. Do not think, Walton, that in these final moments I carry the same searing hatred and ravenous hunger for revenge I once spoke of. Even so, I cannot call my desire to see my adversary dead unjust. These past days I have spent turning over every choice of my past, and I find no fault in how I acted. In a fever of reckless, all-consuming passion I brought a thinking, rational being into the world, and I owed him every effort I could make to secure his joy and well-being. That was my obligation, but I had another duty that outweighed it entirely. My responsibilities to the rest of my own species deserved greater care, for the harm or good I did them touched far more lives, for better or worse. Guided by that truth, I refused to make a companion for my first creation, and that refusal was the right choice. He acted with unmatchable cruelty and cold self-interest in every wicked deed; he murdered my friends, he snuffed out the lives of people brimming with tender feeling, joy, and wisdom, and I have no way of knowing where his ravenous thirst for vengeance will stop. Miserable as he is, he deserves death so he can bring no more suffering to others. It was my task to destroy him, and I have failed. When I first asked you to take up this unfinished work, I was driven by selfish, bitter impulses—but I renew that request now, moved only by reason and virtue.”

The man agreed, and wondered at such a mood in a visitor who wore not a shred of mourning. Troy then wrote the words which were to form the inscription, settled the account and went away. In the afternoon he came back again, and found that the lettering was almost done. He waited in the yard till the tomb was packed, and saw it placed in the cart and starting on its way to Weatherbury, giving directions to the two men who were to accompany it to inquire of the sexton for the grave of the person named in the inscription.

The man consented, though inwardly he marveled at the visitor’s state of mind, for Troy wore not a single token of grief. Troy then set down the words for the epitaph, paid what was owed, and departed. Later that afternoon he returned and discovered the lettering nearly finished. He lingered in the yard until the headstone was wrapped, watched it being lifted onto the cart, and saw it begin its journey toward Weatherbury, instructing the two bearers to seek out the sexton and ask for the grave of the one named in the inscription.

For the first six months after Oliver Twist was removed, the system was in full operation. It was rather expensive at first, in consequence of the increase in the undertaker’s bill, and the necessity of taking in the clothes of all the paupers, which fluttered loosely on their wasted, shrunken forms, after a week or two’s gruel. But the number of workhouse inmates got thin as well as the paupers; and the board were in ecstasies.

During the initial half-year following Oliver Twist’s departure, the scheme ran at full tilt. At the outset it proved somewhat costly, owing to a steep rise in the undertaker’s charges and the need to alter the paupers’ garments, which, after a fortnight of thin gruel, hung slackly on their gaunt and dwindling frames. Yet the workhouse population thinned out along with the paupers themselves, and the board was enraptured.

“Not till you let me go—not till you let me go—Never—never!” screamed the girl. Sikes looked on, for a minute, watching his opportunity, and suddenly pinioning her hands dragged her, struggling and wrestling with him by the way, into a small room adjoining, where he sat himself on a bench, and thrusting her into a chair, held her down by force. She struggled and implored by turns until twelve o’clock had struck, and then, wearied and exhausted, ceased to contest the point any further. With a caution, backed by many oaths, to make no more efforts to go out that night, Sikes left her to recover at leisure and rejoined Fagin.

“Not until you let me go—not until you let me go—Never—never!” the girl shrieked. Sikes watched her for a moment, waiting for his chance, and then, with a sudden grip that trapped her arms, hauled her, fighting and twisting against him, into a little room next door. There he settled himself on a bench, and, forcing her into a chair, held her there by main strength. She struggled and pleaded by turns until twelve o’clock struck, and then, tired out and spent, gave over the struggle at last. After warning her, with many oaths, not to make any further attempt to leave that night, Sikes left her to come round as she could and went back to Fagin.

But why should I dwell upon the incidents that followed this last overwhelming event? Mine has been a tale of horrors; I have reached their _acme_, and what I must now relate can but be tedious to you. Know that, one by one, my friends were snatched away; I was left desolate. My own strength is exhausted, and I must tell, in a few words, what remains of my hideous narration.

Yet what reason have I to linger over the incidents that followed that final, world-shattering blow? Every word I have spoken so far has been stitched from dread and bloodshed; I have already scaled the highest, most ghoulish peak of this nightmare, and the scraps left to tell will almost certainly feel hollow and wearisome to you. Understand this, if nothing else: my dearest companions were torn from my side one after the next, until no one remained but me, adrift in utter ruin. I have no more strength left to spin out the story at length, so I will give you the last of this ghastly account as briefly as I am able.

With his straw in his mouth, Mr. Cruncher sat watching the two streams, like the heathen rustic who has for several centuries been on duty watching one stream--saving that Jerry had no expectation of their ever running dry. Nor would it have been an expectation of a hopeful kind, since a small part of his income was derived from the pilotage of timid women (mostly of a full habit and past the middle term of life) from Tellson’s side of the tides to the opposite shore. Brief as such companionship was in every separate instance, Mr. Cruncher never failed to become so interested in the lady as to express a strong desire to have the honour of drinking her very good health. And it was from the gifts bestowed upon him towards the execution of this benevolent purpose, that he recruited his finances, as just now observed.

Clamping a frayed wheat stalk between his teeth, Mr. Rook sat gazing at the two churning currents, much like the pagan goatherd of local lore who has stood watch over a single moorland stream for near a thousand years—except that Tom Rook held no expectation those waters would ever run dry. That would hardly have been a hopeful wish anyway, for a tidy slice of his income came from escorting skittish gentlewomen (most of them plump, well past middle age) across the slippery crossing from Calthorpe & Sons Bank’s side of the tide to the opposite embankment. Brief as each of these little journeys was, Mr. Rook never failed to work up such a show of fond regard for his passenger that he would profess a fervent wish to have the honor of toasting her excellent health. And the small gratuities pressed upon him to fund this kindly act were exactly the contributions that padded his earnings, as just observed.

The passage to the Conciergerie was short and dark; the night in its vermin-haunted cells was long and cold. Next day, fifteen prisoners were put to the bar before Charles Darnay’s name was called. All the fifteen were condemned, and the trials of the whole occupied an hour and a half.

The corridor leading to the Conciergerie was short and swallowed up in total darkness; the night spent in its vermin-crawling holding cells dragged on endlessly, sharp with unforgiving cold. The following day, fifteen prisoners were brought before the tribunal before Charles Darnay’s name was finally called. Every single one of the fifteen was condemned, and the full batch of trials wrapped up in only an hour and a half total.

From this place, they passed through several strong gates, opened by other turnkeys from the inner side; and, having entered an open yard, ascended a flight of narrow steps, and came into a passage with a row of strong doors on the left hand. Motioning them to remain where they were, the turnkey knocked at one of these with his bunch of keys. The two attendants, after a little whispering, came out into the passage, stretching themselves as if glad of the temporary relief, and motioned the visitors to follow the jailer into the cell. They did so.

Leaving that spot, the group proceeded through a succession of stout barriers, which were unfastened by warders stationed on the interior; having crossed a vacant courtyard, they mounted a steep, constricted staircase and reached a gallery flanked on the left by a sequence of massive doors. Gesturing for them to pause, the keeper struck one of these panels with his heavy iron ring. Two guards, after a moment of low-voiced consultation, stepped out into the hall, extending their weary frames as if savoring the short reprieve, and signaled for the newcomers to follow the gaoler into the room. They obeyed.

Last Monday (July 31st) we were nearly surrounded by ice, which closed in the ship on all sides, scarcely leaving her the sea-room in which she floated. Our situation was somewhat dangerous, especially as we were compassed round by a very thick fog. We accordingly lay to, hoping that some change would take place in the atmosphere and weather.

On the final day of July, a Monday, the vessel found itself all but imprisoned by a vast ring of ice that pressed in from every quarter, granting the hull barely enough open water to breathe. A measure of peril attended our position, made graver still by the dense fog that wrapped us completely about. We therefore hove to, trusting that the skies and the elements would soon shift their mood.

Since he lived six times as many working-days as Sundays, Oak’s appearance in his old clothes was most peculiarly his own—the mental picture formed by his neighbours in imagining him being always dressed in that way. He wore a low-crowned felt hat, spread out at the base by tight jamming upon the head for security in high winds, and a coat like Dr. Johnson’s; his lower extremities being encased in ordinary leather leggings and boots emphatically large, affording to each foot a roomy apartment so constructed that any wearer might stand in a river all day long and know nothing of damp—their maker being a conscientious man who endeavoured to compensate for any weakness in his cut by unstinted dimension and solidity.

Because Oak spent six times as many days laboring as he did resting, his working attire became his truest signature—the image his neighbors conjured whenever they thought of him. He wore a low-crowned felt hat, its brim splayed wide from being jammed down firmly against the wind, and a coat reminiscent of Dr. Johnson’s. His legs were sheathed in ordinary leather gaiters, and his feet in boots of such emphatic size that each one formed a roomy chamber, so stoutly built that a man could stand in a river all day and never feel the damp—their maker being a scrupulous craftsman who made up for any shortcomings in his pattern with unsparing dimension and heft.
