Our model jointly learns the GSL and SFL to optimize the argument representations. On the one hand, the GSL maps the discourse relation between two arguments to the low-dimensional embedding space and obtains the vectors h s , r s , t s with geometric structure information to constrain the SFL. On the other hand, the SFL alternately optimizes the discourse representations and provides the necessary semantic clues for geometric structure information mining. Generally, the GSL and SFL reinforce with each other, and finally get the better argument representations containing the semantics and the latent geometric structure information of argument-relation.

metadata.title: TransS-Driven Joint Learning Architecture for Implicit Discourse Relation Recognition

text: Here, \(L_{\mathrm{GSL}}\) and \(L_{\mathrm{SFL}}\) are drawn from Eq. 7 and Eq. 10, respectively; \(\lambda\) is a trade-off parameter that regulates the balance between the GSL and the SFL. Our model jointly learns the GSL and the SFL to optimize the argument representations. The GSL maps the discourse relation between two arguments into a low-dimensional embedding space, producing vectors \(h_s\), \(r_s\), and \(t_s\) that encode geometric structure information to constrain the SFL. Meanwhile, the SFL alternately refines the discourse representations and supplies the semantic cues necessary for extracting geometric structure information. In general, the GSL and the SFL reinforce one another, ultimately yielding richer argument representations that capture both the semantics and the latent geometric structure of the argument-relation.

Anyone who had stood by now would have pitied her, not so much on account of her exposure to weather, and isolation from all of humanity except the mouldered remains inside the tumulus; but for that other form of misery which was denoted by the slightly rocking movement that her feelings imparted to her person. Extreme unhappiness weighed visibly upon her. Between the drippings of the rain from her umbrella to her mantle, from her mantle to the heather, from the heather to the earth, very similar sounds could be heard coming from her lips; and the tearfulness of the outer scene was repeated upon her face. The wings of her soul were broken by the cruel obstructiveness of all about her; and even had she seen herself in a promising way of getting to Budmouth, entering a steamer, and sailing to some opposite port, she would have been but little more buoyant, so fearfully malignant were other things. She uttered words aloud. When a woman in such a situation, neither old, deaf, crazed, nor whimsical, takes upon herself to sob and soliloquize aloud there is something grievous the matter.

Any onlooker lingering at the spot would have felt their heart twist for her, not first for the lash of the cold rain on her skin or her utter solitude, broken only by the decayed remains tucked inside the ancient barrow at her back; but for the slow, unsteady sway of her frame, a physical echo of the agony churning beneath her skin. A suffocating weight of despair hung off her, impossible to miss even through the fog of falling rain. Rain pattered off the edge of her oilskin umbrella to her thick wool cloak, dripped from her cloak to the gorse and heather at her feet, seeped from the heather down into the dark loam below; and between those soft, pattering beats came the faint, hitching sound of her sobs, the damp grief of the storm mirrored in the streaks of tears carving tracks through the rain on her cheeks. The very wings of her spirit had been snapped by the cold, unyielding cruelty of every turn her life had taken lately; even if she’d suddenly found herself on the clear road to Brineport, stepping onto a steam packet bound for some distant shore across the channel, she would not have felt a scrap lighter, so heavy and unforgiving was the weight of the wrongs that had been done to her. She spoke fragments of sentences out loud to the empty air. When a woman in such straits, who is neither elderly, hard of hearing, unwell in the mind, nor given to fanciful displays, weeps and murmurs to herself in the open wild, you know her suffering is beyond bearing.

Although the Doctor’s daughter had known nothing of the country of her birth, she appeared to have innately derived from it that ability to make much of little means, which is one of its most useful and most agreeable characteristics. Simple as the furniture was, it was set off by so many little adornments, of no value but for their taste and fancy, that its effect was delightful. The disposition of everything in the rooms, from the largest object to the least; the arrangement of colours, the elegant variety and contrast obtained by thrift in trifles, by delicate hands, clear eyes, and good sense; were at once so pleasant in themselves, and so expressive of their originator, that, as Mr. Lorry stood looking about him, the very chairs and tables seemed to ask him, with something of that peculiar expression which he knew so well by this time, whether he approved?

Though the surgeon’s daughter had never once set foot in the land where she’d been born, she seemed to have inherited instinctively its signature knack for weaving plenty out of scarce resources, a trait as practical as it was endearing to anyone who witnessed it. Modest as the household furnishings were, they were elevated by dozens of tiny, hand-fashioned touches, worthless in coin but brimming with thoughtful taste and quiet whimsy, so that the entire space felt uncommonly welcoming and charming. The placement of every item in the chambers, from the heaviest oak chest to the smallest glass trinket; the pairing of soft, muted hues, the gentle variety and clever contrast achieved by making the most of trivial odds and ends, by deft fingers, sharp kind eyes, and sound practical judgment; were all so innately cheering on their own, and so clearly a reflection of the woman who’d arranged them, that as Mr. Thorne stood surveying the space, even the plain wooden chairs and scrubbed table seemed to tilt toward him with that familiar, quiet half-smile he’d come to know so well, as if curious if he found their arrangement to his liking.

It was a fine and quiet afternoon, about three o’clock; but the winter solstice having stealthily come on, the lowness of the sun caused the hour to seem later than it actually was, there being little here to remind an inhabitant that he must unlearn his summer experience of the sky as a dial. In the course of many days and weeks sunrise had advanced its quarters from northeast to southeast, sunset had receded from northwest to southwest; but Egdon had hardly heeded the change.

The day was tranquil and bright, the hour nearing three; yet the winter solstice had arrived with such quietude that the sun’s shallow path made the time feel much later than the reality. In this place, there was little to warn a traveler that he must discard his summer reliance on the heavens as a reliable clock. Through the slow procession of weeks, the dawn had shifted its anchor from the northeast toward the south, and the dusk had retreated accordingly; yet the vastness of Blackmoor remained utterly unmoved by the change.

“You forget how I talked when you first met me. I have learned a new language since then. Before that time I talked as that girl talks. Now I can manage to make myself understood sufficiently in your language to explain that you do not know that other girl’s language. And do you know why she carries herself the way she does? I think about such things now, though I never used to think about them, and I am beginning to understand—much.”

“You don’t recall how I spoke when we first crossed paths. I’ve picked up a whole different way of speaking since those days. Back then, I sounded just like that girl does. Now I’ve gotten good enough in your tongue to tell you that you don’t grasp the language she’s using. And have you any idea why she holds herself the way she does? I ponder such matters nowadays, though I never used to, and I’m starting to see—quite a bit.”

“For the love of Heaven, of justice, of generosity, of the honour of your noble name, I supplicate you, Monsieur heretofore the Marquis, to succour and release me. My fault is, that I have been true to you. Oh Monsieur heretofore the Marquis, I pray you be you true to me!

By all that is holy, by justice, by mercy, and for the sake of your family’s honorable name, I beg of you, Monsieur who was once the Marquis, to rescue me and grant me my freedom. My only crime is that I remained loyal to you. Oh, Monsieur formerly the Marquis, I pray that you will now be loyal to me!

She ascended to her bedroom and dressed herself with scrupulous care. To an onlooker her beauty would have made her feelings almost seem reasonable. The gloomy corner into which accident as much as indiscretion had brought this woman might have led even a moderate partisan to feel that she had cogent reasons for asking the Supreme Power by what right a being of such exquisite finish had been placed in circumstances calculated to make of her charms a curse rather than a blessing.

She climbed the stairs to her bedroom and got dressed with painstaking, deliberate care. To anyone watching, her sheer, unmissable beauty would have made the weight of her feelings feel almost entirely reasonable. The bleak, dead-end situation this woman had landed in, brought on as much by random misfortune as her own past bad calls, would have convinced even a casual, half-hearted supporter that she had perfectly valid grounds to ask whatever higher power ruled the world, by what right a being so exquisitely crafted had been placed in circumstances intentionally designed to turn her most dazzling charms into a curse rather than a blessing.

Till the little ones, weary,
No more can be merry:
The sun does descend,
And our sports have an end.
Round the laps of their mothers
Many sisters and brothers,
Like birds in their nest,
Are ready for rest,
And sport no more seen
On the darkening green.

metadata.title: Songs of Innocence and of Experience

text:
Till the little ones grow weary,  
And can be merry no more,  
The sun is sinking lower,  
And our games are finished now.  
About their mothers’ laps  
Many sisters and brothers,  
Like birds within their nest,  
Are ready now for rest,  
And no more sport is seen  
On the meadow turning dim.

An answer so unlike the one he had looked to draw forth, and was accustomed to hearing, quite disconcerted Mr. Weller. He recoiled from the keyhole; straightened himself to his full stature; and, in silent amazement, let his gaze pass from one to the other of the three persons standing by.

"Mind you, I don't admit a thing that Cissie has ever said about America--not one thing. You don't understand the sort of proposition America is up against. America is the New World, where there are no races and nations any more; she is the Melting Pot, from which we will cast the better state. I've believed that always--in spite of a thousand little things I believe it now. I go back on nothing. I'm not fighting as an American either. I'm fighting simply as myself.... I'm not going fighting for England, mind you. Don't you fancy that. I don't know I'm so particularly in love with a lot of English ways as to do that. I don't see how any one can be very much in love with your Empire, with its dead-alive Court, its artful politicians, its lords and ladies and snobs, its way with the Irish and its way with India, and everybody shifting responsibility and telling lies about your common people. I'm not going fighting for England. I'm going fighting for Cissie--and justice and Belgium and all that--but more particularly for Cissie. And anyhow I can't look Pa Britling in the face any more.... And I want to see those trenches--close. I reckon they're a thing it will be interesting to talk about some day.... So I'm going," said Mr. Direck. "But chiefly--it's Cissie. See?"

Look, I don’t buy a single thing Cissie’s ever said about America—not one word. You don’t get what kind of project America’s working toward, right? It’s the New World, where all those old lines between races and nations don’t count anymore. It’s the Melting Pot, the place we’re gonna forge a whole better kind of society out of. I’ve always believed that—even with a thousand tiny frustrations that make you second-guess for a split second, I still believe it now. I’m not backing down on any of that. And I’m not heading off to fight as an American, either. I’m doing this just as me… And for the record, I’m not fighting for England, either. Don’t even start thinking that. I don’t think I’m even that fond of a whole lot of British customs to go that far for them. I can’t imagine anyone being a huge fan of your whole Empire setup, with its half-dead stuffy royal court, its sleazy backstabbing politicians, all its lords and ladies and obnoxious snobs, the way you brutalize the Irish and exploit India, everyone passing the buck and lying through their teeth about your working class people. I’m not fighting for England. I’m fighting for Cissie—and justice, and Belgium, and all that other high-minded stuff—but mostly for Cissie. Plus, I can’t look Pa Britling in the eye anymore, to be honest… And I wanna see those trenches, up close. I figure they’re the kind of thing you’ll have a hell of a good time telling stories about later, right?… So I’m going,” Mr. Direck said. “But the main reason? It’s Cissie. Got it?

Had Juliet so seen her love tokens dishonoured the sooner would she have sought the lethean herbs of the good apothecary.

Had Juliet watched her love tokens so dishonored, she would have sought the good apothecary’s lethean herbs all the sooner.

While common in the evaluation of language models, cross-entropy evaluation has been eschewed in machine translation research since (i) not all MT models are probabilistic and (ii) we are often interested in measuring the quality of the candidate translation our model actually produces, e.g. under approximate decoding. However, an information-theoretic evaluation is much more suitable for measuring the more abstract notion of which language pairs are hardest to translate to and from, which is our purpose here.

metadata.title: It's Easier to Translate out of English than into it: Measuring Neural Translation Difficulty by Cross-Mutual Information

text: As N → ∞, eq. (2) approaches eq. (1). Crucially, this assessment is independent of language tokenization as long as the model q MT does not rely on it (Mielke, 2019). Although cross-entropy is widely adopted for evaluating language models, it has been largely avoided in machine translation because (i) not every MT model defines a probability distribution and (ii) the focus is typically on the quality of the actual translation produced, for instance under approximate decoding. In our setting, however, an information-theoretic metric is far better suited to capturing the more abstract question of which language pairs are most difficult to translate into and out of, which is precisely our goal.

My splendors are menagerie;
But their competeless show
Will entertain the centuries
When I am, long ago,
An island in dishonored grass,
Whom none but daisies know.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
My glories are a carnival of beasts,
Yet their unrivaled display
Will charm the ages still to come
When I have passed away,
A mound in unremembered weeds,
Known only to the clover and the day.

“And as such,” quoth Mr. Lorry, whom the counsel learned in the law had now shouldered back into the group, just as he had previously shouldered him out of it--“as such I will appeal to Doctor Manette, to break up this conference and order us all to our homes. Miss Lucie looks ill, Mr. Darnay has had a terrible day, we are worn out.”

“Therefore,” murmured Mr. Lorry, now nudged back into the circle by the very barrister who had earlier edged him aside—“therefore I shall petition Doctor Manette to dissolve this gathering and bid us each return to our dwellings. Miss Lucie appears unwell, Mr. Darnay has endured a harrowing day, and we are all spent.”

“He is tall,” said the girl, “and a strongly made man, but not stout; he has a lurking walk; and as he walks, constantly looks over his shoulder, first on one side, and then on the other. Don’t forget that, for his eyes are sunk in his head so much deeper than any other man’s, that you might almost tell him by that alone. His face is dark, like his hair and eyes; and, although he can’t be more than six or eight and twenty, withered and haggard. His lips are often discoloured and disfigured with the marks of teeth; for he has desperate fits, and sometimes even bites his hands and covers them with wounds—why did you start?” said the girl, stopping suddenly.

“He’s tall,” the girl said, “solidly built, but not heavy. He walks like he’s trying not to draw attention, always glancing over his shoulder as he moves, first one side, then the other. Don’t miss that detail—his eyes are set so much deeper in his skull than most people’s, you could practically spot him by that feature alone. He’s dark-skinned, same as his hair and eyes, and even though he can’t be more than twenty-six or twenty-eight, his face looks worn and gaunt. His lips are usually discolored and chewed raw with tooth marks, too; he gets these terrible, desperate fits, and sometimes he even bites his own hands so hard they’re covered in wounds—why did you start?” the girl asked, cutting herself off abruptly.

She left me, and I continued some time walking up and down the passages of the house and inspecting every corner that might afford a retreat to my adversary. But I discovered no trace of him and was beginning to conjecture that some fortunate chance had intervened to prevent the execution of his menaces when suddenly I heard a shrill and dreadful scream. It came from the room into which Elizabeth had retired. As I heard it, the whole truth rushed into my mind, my arms dropped, the motion of every muscle and fibre was suspended; I could feel the blood trickling in my veins and tingling in the extremities of my limbs. This state lasted but for an instant; the scream was repeated, and I rushed into the room.

After she went inside, I spent some time pacing the hallways of the house, searching every corner that might offer a hiding place for my enemy. Finding no sign of him, I began to hope that some stroke of luck had prevented him from carrying out his threats, when suddenly a sharp, terrifying shriek rang out. It came from the room where Elizabeth had gone to rest. The moment I heard it, the full horror of the situation crashed down on me; my arms fell limp, and every muscle in my body froze. I could feel the blood crawling through my veins, a cold tingling sensation reaching my very fingertips. This paralysis lasted only a second; the scream echoed again, and I bolted toward the room.

Models trained with BPE-dropout better learn compositionality of words and the meaning of subwords, which suggests that these models have to be more robust to noise. We verify this by measuring the translation quality of models on a test set augmented with synthetic misspellings. We augment the source side of a test set by modifying each word with the probability of 10% by applying one of the predefined operations. The operations we consider are (1) removal of one character from a word, (2) insertion of a random character into a word, (3) substitution of a character in a word with a random one. This augmentation produces words with the edit distance of 1 from the unmodified words. Edit distance is commonly used to model misspellings (Brill and Moore, 2000; Ahmad and Kondrak, 2005; Pinter et al., 2017) . Table 5 shows the translation quality of the models trained on WMT 14 dataset when given the original source and augmented with misspellings. We deliberately chose large datasets, where improvements from using BPE-dropout are smaller. We can see that while for the original test sets the improvements from using BPE-dropout are usually modest, for misspelled test set the improvements are a lot larger: 1.6-2.3 BLEU. This is especially interesting since models have not been exposed to misspellings during training. Therefore, even for large datasets using BPE-dropout can result in substantially better quality for practical applications where input is likely to be noisy.

metadata.title: BPE-Dropout: Simple and Effective Subword Regularization

text: Models trained with BPE-dropout exhibit a stronger grasp of word compositionality and subword semantics, indicating that they are compelled to become more resilient to noise. We substantiate this by evaluating translation performance on a test set artificially corrupted with misspellings. Specifically, we perturb the source side of a test set by applying, with a 10% probability per word, one of several predetermined operations: (1) deleting a single character from a word, (2) inserting a random character into a word, or (3) replacing a character with a randomly chosen one. This procedure yields words whose edit distance from the original is exactly 1—a standard proxy for modeling misspellings (Brill and Moore, 2000; Ahmad and Kondrak, 2005; Pinter et al., 2017). Table 5 reports the translation quality of models trained on the WMT 14 dataset when fed either the clean source or the misspelling-augmented variant. We intentionally employ large-scale datasets, where the gains attributable to BPE-dropout tend to be more modest. The results reveal that while BPE-dropout yields only slight improvements on the original test sets, the benefits become substantially more pronounced on the misspelled test set: 1.6–2.3 BLEU points. This finding is particularly noteworthy given that the models were never exposed to misspellings during training. Consequently, even in large-data regimes, BPE-dropout can deliver markedly better quality for real-world applications where noisy inputs are common.

About noon next day, when the Dodger and Master Bates had gone out to pursue their customary avocations, Mr. Fagin took the opportunity of reading Oliver a long lecture on the crying sin of ingratitude; of which he clearly demonstrated he had been guilty, to no ordinary extent, in wilfully absenting himself from the society of his anxious friends; and, still more, in endeavouring to escape from them after so much trouble and expense had been incurred in his recovery. Mr. Fagin laid great stress on the fact of his having taken Oliver in, and cherished him, when, without his timely aid, he might have perished with hunger; and he related the dismal and affecting history of a young lad whom, in his philanthropy, he had succoured under parallel circumstances, but who, proving unworthy of his confidence and evincing a desire to communicate with the police, had unfortunately come to be hanged at the Old Bailey one morning. Mr. Fagin did not seek to conceal his share in the catastrophe, but lamented with tears in his eyes that the wrong-headed and treacherous behaviour of the young person in question, had rendered it necessary that he should become the victim of certain evidence for the crown: which, if it were not precisely true, was indispensably necessary for the safety of him (Mr. Fagin) and a few select friends. Mr. Fagin concluded by drawing a rather disagreeable picture of the discomforts of hanging; and, with great friendliness and politeness of manner, expressed his anxious hopes that he might never be obliged to submit Oliver Twist to that unpleasant operation.

Around noon the following day, once the Dodger and Master Bates had departed to attend to their usual trades, Mr. Fagin seized the chance to deliver Oliver a lengthy sermon on the grievous vice of ingratitude. He made it abundantly plain that the boy had been guilty of no small measure of it—first by deliberately withdrawing from the company of his solicitous friends, and worse, by attempting to flee them after they had gone to such pains and expense to reclaim him. Fagin placed particular emphasis on the fact that he had taken Oliver in and cared for him when, without that timely intervention, the child might well have starved; and he recounted the pitiful, moving tale of another young lad whom, out of pure benevolence, he had aided in nearly identical straits, but who—proving unworthy of his trust and showing a regrettable inclination to parley with the police—had unhappily ended up hanged at the Old Bailey one morning. Fagin made no effort to disguise his own part in that catastrophe, yet lamented, with tears standing in his eyes, that the perverse and treacherous conduct of the youth in question had forced the necessity of his becoming the subject of certain Crown evidence—which, if not strictly accurate, was absolutely essential to the security of himself (Mr. Fagin) and a small circle of select companions. He closed by painting a rather disagreeable portrait of the unpleasantness of hanging, and with great cordiality and courteousness of manner expressed his fervent hope that he might never be obliged to subject Oliver Twist to that disagreeable procedure.

The untaught peasant beheld the elements around him and was acquainted with their practical uses. The most learned philosopher knew little more. He had partially unveiled the face of Nature, but her immortal lineaments were still a wonder and a mystery. He might dissect, anatomise, and give names; but, not to speak of a final cause, causes in their secondary and tertiary grades were utterly unknown to him. I had gazed upon the fortifications and impediments that seemed to keep human beings from entering the citadel of nature, and rashly and ignorantly I had repined.

The unschooled homesteader observed the natural forces surrounding his small plot and knew their practical functions intimately. The most erudite natural scholar understood barely anything beyond that. He had pulled back a corner of the wild world’s veil, but its unchanging, ageless features remained as much a marvel and enigma as they were to the farmer. He could cut apart specimens, map their internal structures, and assign taxonomical labels to every piece; yet even setting aside the question of ultimate purpose, the second and third order forces driving those systems were entirely beyond his grasp. I had stared for years at the ramparts and barriers that seemed to bar mortal minds from stepping into nature’s innermost stronghold, and foolhardy and unknowing as I was, I had railed against them.

The little village perches high among the hills and woods, and at its very centre is the inn and the linden tree and--Adam Meyer. Or at least Adam Meyer _was_ there. Whether he is there now, only the spirit of change can tell; if he live to be a hundred no friendly English will ever again come tramping along by the track of the Blaue Breiecke or the Weisse Streiche to enjoy his hospitality; there are rivers of blood between, and a thousand memories of hate....

High upon the folded hills, cradled in forest, the hamlet clings, and at its heart stand the tavern and the lime tree and—Konrad Voss. Or at least Konrad Voss once stood there. Whether he remains now, only the shifting wind of time could say; should he live to see a century, no kindly English step will ever again come trudging down the path of the Blaue Breiecke or the Weisse Streiche to taste his welcome; between them flow torrents of blood, and a thousand bitter recollections....

metadata.title: Poems and Songs of Robert Burns

text:
Three high-spirited friends, with hearts free of stain,
Craigdarroch, where wit, worth, and legal skill reign;
Trusty Glenriddel, who knows every ancient-mint coin,
And brave Robert, whom cellars of vintage conjoin.

“And there’s another fellow—Parry—an Australian, a statistician and a sporting encyclopaedia. Ask him the grain output of Paraguay for 1903, or the English importation of sheetings into China for 1890, or at what weight Jimmy Britt fought Battling Nelson, or who was welter-weight champion of the United States in ’68, and you’ll get the correct answer with the automatic celerity of a slot-machine. And there’s Andy, a stone-mason, has ideas on everything, a good chess-player; and another fellow, Harry, a baker, red hot socialist and strong union man. By the way, you remember Cooks’ and Waiters’ strike—Hamilton was the chap who organized that union and precipitated the strike—planned it all out in advance, right here in Kreis’s rooms. Did it just for the fun of it, but was too lazy to stay by the union. Yet he could have risen high if he wanted to. There’s no end to the possibilities in that man—if he weren’t so insuperably lazy.”

“And then there’s another bloke—Kael, an Australian, a statistician and walking almanac of all things sports. Ask him the maize yield of Argentina for 1911, or British cotton yarn exports to Japan in 1897, what weight Joe Gans fought Battling Jim Johnson at, or who took the U.S. middleweight champion title in 1872, and you’ll get the right answer with the automatic quickness of a gumball machine spitting out a treat. Then there’s Javi, a stonemason who has opinions on every subject under the sun, and a wicked good chess player to boot; and another guy, Moe, a baker, fiery committed socialist and staunch union man. Oh, by the way, you remember that restaurant workers’ strike a few years back? Elias was the fellow who built that union from scratch and kicked off the strike, mapped every last step of it out beforehand, right here in Rainer’s rooms. He did it just for the rush of pulling it off, but was too lazy to stick with the union once it was up and running. He could have gone far in the labor movement if he’d ever wanted to put in the effort. There’s no end to what that man could do—if he weren’t so insurmountably, unshakably lazy.”

With these and other ejaculations of the same kind, Mr. Toby Crackit swept up his winnings, and crammed them into his waistcoat pocket with a haughty air, as though such small pieces of silver were wholly beneath the consideration of a man of his figure; this done, he swaggered out of the room, with so much elegance and gentility, that Mr. Chitling, bestowing numerous admiring glances on his legs and boots till they were out of sight, assured the company that he considered his acquaintance cheap at fifteen sixpences an interview, and that he didn’t value his losses the snap of his little finger.

Accompanied by several similar boasts, Mr. Toby Crackit gathered his winnings and thrust them into his vest pocket with a disdainful look, as if such trifling silver coins were far too petty for a gentleman of his stature. This done, he sauntered from the room with such a show of grace and refinement that Mr. Chitling—who watched the man's retreating legs and boots with rapt fascination until they vanished—assured the company that the privilege of Toby’s acquaintance was a steal at fifteen sixpences a visit, and that he didn't value his own losses at the snap of a finger.

Then Bathsheba said to herself that others were assured in their opinion; strange that she should not be. A strange reflection occurred to her, causing her face to flush. Suppose that Troy had followed Fanny into another world. Had he done this intentionally, yet contrived to make his death appear like an accident? Nevertheless, this thought of how the apparent might differ from the real—made vivid by her bygone jealousy of Fanny, and the remorse he had shown that night—did not blind her to the perception of a likelier difference, less tragic, but to herself far more disastrous.

Bathsheba reflected on how convinced everyone else seemed, finding it peculiar that she could not share their certainty. A startling thought struck her, bringing a sudden heat to her face. What if Troy had pursued Fanny into the next life? Could he have planned his own end, yet managed to make it look like a mere accident? However, this vision of how reality might diverge from appearances—intensified by her old resentment toward Fanny and the profound regret he had shown that night—did not prevent her from seeing a more probable truth. It was a possibility less tragic in nature, but for her, one that would be far more ruinous.

Little lamb, I’ll tell thee;
Little lamb, I’ll tell thee:
He is callèd by thy name,
For He calls Himself a Lamb.
He is meek, and He is mild,
He became a little child.
I a child, and thou a lamb,
We are callèd by His name.
Little lamb, God bless thee!
Little lamb, God bless thee!

metadata.title: Songs of Innocence and of Experience

text:
Gentle lamb, I'll speak to you;
gentle lamb, I'll speak to you:
You share the name He chose to bear,
for He became a lamb so fair.
He is tender, He is mild,
He came down as a little child.
I'm a child, and you a lamb,
both of us bear His gentle name.
Gentle lamb, may God keep you;
gentle lamb, may God keep you.

It was a long walk to North Oakland, but it was not until he went up the steps and entered his room that he knew he had walked it. He found himself sitting on the edge of the bed and staring about him like an awakened somnambulist. He noticed “Overdue” lying on the table and drew up his chair and reached for his pen. There was in his nature a logical compulsion toward completeness. Here was something undone. It had been deferred against the completion of something else. Now that something else had been finished, and he would apply himself to this task until it was finished. What he would do next he did not know. All that he did know was that a climacteric in his life had been attained. A period had been reached, and he was rounding it off in workman-like fashion. He was not curious about the future. He would soon enough find out what it held in store for him. Whatever it was, it did not matter. Nothing seemed to matter.

The road to North Berkeley had been a long one, but it was not until he climbed the stairs and let himself into his room that he was aware he had traversed it. He found himself seated on the edge of the bed, gazing about him with the blank wonder of a sleepwalker suddenly roused. On the table he saw “Past Due,” and he drew his chair close and took up his pen. There was in him a strict, almost mathematical need for wholeness. Something remained unfinished. It had been put aside until another matter should be brought to an end. Now that other thing was done, and he would give himself to this remaining labor until it too was done. What he should do afterward he did not know. Only this he knew: that a turning point in his life had been reached. A sentence had ended, and he was setting the final stop upon it with careful, workmanlike hands. He felt no curiosity concerning the future. In due time he would learn what awaited him there. Whatever it might be, it no longer seemed to signify. Nothing seemed to signify.

The having originated a precaution which was already in course of execution, was a great relief to Miss Pross. The necessity of composing her appearance so that it should attract no special notice in the streets, was another relief. She looked at her watch, and it was twenty minutes past two. She had no time to lose, but must get ready at once.

Devising a protective measure that was already being carried out had lifted a great weight from Miss Elspeth Rowe’s shoulders. The need to smooth out her disheveled appearance so she would draw no unusual stares while out on the public streets offered a second, smaller comfort. She flicked a glance at her scuffed brass pocket watch: it was twenty minutes past two. There was not a moment to spare, and she had to make her final preparations at once.

Having followed him this distance on his journey back and concluded the necessary arrangements for the old woman’s burial, let us now look into the fate of young Oliver Twist to see if he remains in the ditch where Toby Crackit abandoned him.

Think not thou canst sigh a sigh,
And thy Maker is not by:
Think not thou canst weep a tear,
And thy Maker is not near.

metadata.title: Songs of Innocence and of Experience

text:
Don’t fool yourself you can heave one soft sigh
And your Maker is not close by.
Don’t believe you can let fall one stray tear
And your Maker is not standing near.

Oliver turned homeward, thinking on the many kindnesses he had received from the young lady, and wishing that the time could come again, that he might never cease showing her how grateful and attached he was. He had no cause for self-reproach on the score of neglect, or want of thought, for he had been devoted to her service; and yet a hundred little occasions rose up before him, on which he fancied he might have been more zealous, and more earnest, and wished he had been. We need be careful how we deal with those about us, when every death carries to some small circle of survivors, thoughts of so much omitted, and so little done—of so many things forgotten, and so many more which might have been repaired! There is no remorse so deep as that which is unavailing; if we would be spared its tortures, let us remember this, in time.

Julian turned back toward home, musing on the many acts of goodness he had received from the young woman, and longing that the hour might return when he could never tire of proving how grateful and how warmly attached he was. He had no ground for accusing himself of negligence, or of a want of feeling, for he had been faithful in her service; and yet a hundred small occasions came crowding upon him, in which he imagined he might have shown a greater ardour, a deeper concern, and wished heartily that he had done so. We should be wary in our dealings with those around us, when every death brings to some little circle of mourners thoughts of so much left undone, and so little performed—of so many things forgotten, and so many more that might still have been mended! There is no sorrow so piercing as that which cannot be helped; if we would be saved its anguish, let us bear this in mind, while there is yet time.

We build EVIDENCEMINER, a web-based system for textual evidence discovery for life sciences. The retrieved evidence sentences can be easily located in the background corpora for better visualization. EVIDENCEMINER also includes analytic functionalities such as the most frequent entity and relation summarization. We incorporated another corpus on COVID-19 in EVIDENCEMINER to help boost the scientific discoveries (Wang et al., 2020b,a) . We are further developing EVIDENCEMINER to be a more intelligent system that can assist in more efficient and in-depth scientific discoveries.

metadata.title: EVIDENCEMINER: Textual Evidence Discovery for Life Sciences

text: We develop EVIDENCEMINER, a web-based system for textual evidence discovery in the life sciences. The retrieved evidence sentences can be readily located within the background corpora for improved visualization. EVIDENCEMINER also provides analytical functions, including summarization of the most frequent entities and relations. To help accelerate scientific discoveries, we integrated an additional COVID-19 corpus into EVIDENCEMINER (Wang et al., 2020b,a). We are further extending EVIDENCEMINER into a more intelligent system that can support more efficient and in-depth scientific discovery.

"Another thing that appeals to the sunny humour of the trenches as a really delicious practical joke is the trick of the fuses. We have two kinds of fuse, a slow-burning fuse such as is used for hand-grenades and such-like things, a sort of yard-a-minute fuse, and a rapid fuse that goes a hundred yards a second--for firing mines and so on. The latter is carefully distinguished from the former by a conspicuous red thread. Also, as you know, it is the habit of the enemy and ourselves when the trenches are near enough, to enliven each other by the casting of homely but effective hand-grenades made out of tins. When a grenade drops in a British trench somebody seizes it instantly and throws it back. To hoist the German with his own petard is particularly sweet to the British mind. When a grenade drops into a German trench everybody runs. (At least that is what I am told happens by the men from our trenches; though possibly each side has its exceptions.) If the bomb explodes, it explodes. If it doesn't, Hans and Fritz presently come creeping back to see what has happened. Sometimes the fuse hasn't caught properly, it has been thrown by a nervous man; or it hasn't burnt properly. Then Hans or Fritz puts in a new fuse and sends it back with loving care. To hoist the Briton with his own petard is particularly sweet to the German mind.... But here it is that military genius comes in. Some gifted spirit on our side procured (probably by larceny) a length of mine fuse, the rapid sort, and spent a laborious day removing the red thread and making it into the likeness of its slow brother. Then bits of it were attached to tin-bombs and shied--unlit of course--into the German trenches. A long but happy pause followed. I can see the chaps holding themselves in. Hans and Fritz were understood to be creeping back, to be examining the unlit fuse, to be applying a light thereunto, in order to restore it to its maker after their custom....

There's a man arrived at the Cecil who saw them marching into Brussels. He sat at table with me at lunch yesterday. All day it went on, a vast unending river of men in grey. Endless waggons, endless guns, the whole manhood of a nation and all its stuff, marching....

“Liege was only a scuff on the varnish,” said Mr. Vardon. “A few thousand killed, a few score thousand killed, what does it signify? Not one red cent to them. There’s a fellow at the Savoy who saw them go tramping into Brussels. He lunched with me yesterday at the same table. All day long it kept up, a huge unbroken stream of men in grey. Wagons without end, guns without end, the whole male strength of a nation and all its gear, moving on....

It was cruel, cruel!), it is a curious thing that she is going to marry the nephew of Monsieur the Marquis, for whom Gaspard was exalted to that height of so many feet; in other words, the present Marquis. But he lives unknown in England, he is no Marquis there; he is Mr. Charles Darnay. D’Aulnais is the name of his mother’s family.”

Indeed, Mademoiselle Beaumont is to be married. But her choice has not fallen upon an Englishman; she weds a countryman, one who, like herself, is French by birth. And touching upon the fate of poor Baptiste—ah, the miserable soul! it was a savage, savage business—it is a most singular irony that she is to join herself to the nephew of that very Seigneur for whose murder Baptiste was swung so many cubits into the air; in other words, the current Seigneur himself. But he lives in shadow in London, renouncing his rank; there, he is known only as Mr. Julian Vane. Valeray is the name of his mother’s family, which he uses to mask his station.

metadata.title: Span-ConveRT: Few-shot Span Extraction for Dialog with Pretrained Conversational Representations

text: New Evaluation Data Set: RESTAURANTS-8K. Data sets for task-oriented dialog systems typically annotate slots using only categorical labels (Budzianowski et al., 2018). Although some data sets, such as SNIPS (Coucke et al., 2018) and ATIS (Tür et al., 2010), include spans of slot values, to our knowledge there was no restaurant-related data set with span annotations before the release of our new evaluation set, RESTAURANTS-8K. This data set consists of conversations from a commercial restaurant reservation system and covers five slots that are essential for the booking task: date, time, people, first name, and last name. The data statistics are reported in Table 1. DSTC8 Data Sets. The Schema-Guided Dialog Dataset (SGDD) (Rastogi et al., 2019), released for DSTC8, contains span annotations for a subset of slots. We extract span-annotated data sets from SGDD in four domains chosen for their broad range of slots: (1) bus and coach booking (labeled Buses_1), (2) buying tickets for events (Events_1), (3) property viewing (Homes_1), and renting cars (RentalCars_1). A detailed description of the data extraction protocol and the statistics of these data sets, which are also released with this paper, are provided in Appendix A.

"All this on account of a carpet. A palace carpet. Over which I had no control. I spoke my mind. He knew I knew of it. I never concealed it. So I was hunted. For years he had meditated revenge. Now he has it. But at what a cost! And they call him Emperor. Emperor!

For a rug. A court rug. One I could not command. I said what I thought. He understood I was aware. I hid nothing. So they pursued me. Through long seasons he had nursed his vengeance. At last it is his. Yet at such a price! And men name him Sovereign. Sovereign!

Secondly, these missionaries would gradually, and without creating suspicion or exciting alarm, introduce a rudimentary cleanliness among the nobility, and from them it would work down to the people, if the priests could be kept quiet. This would undermine the Church. I mean would be a step toward that. Next, education--next, freedom --and then she would begin to crumble. It being my conviction that any Established Church is an established crime, an established slave-pen, I had no scruples, but was willing to assail it in any way or with any weapon that promised to hurt it. Why, in my own former day--in remote centuries not yet stirring in the womb of time--there were old Englishmen who imagined that they had been born in a free country: a “free” country with the Corporation Act and the Test still in force in it--timbers propped against men's liberties and dishonored consciences to shore up an Established Anachronism with.

For another thing, these missionaries would slowly introduce basic standards of cleanliness among the nobility, no suspicion or alarm roused in the process, and the practice would seep down to the general population if the priests could be kept from interfering. That would undermine the Church’s foundations— or rather, it would be a critical first step toward that end. Next would come widespread education, then liberty, and at that point the institution would start to crack and crumble. I hold the unshakable conviction that any state-established Church is state-sanctioned crime, an official, institutionalized slave pen, so I had no moral scruples about the work; I was ready to assail it by any method, with any weapon that promised to do real damage. Why, in my own former lifetime—those distant centuries that have not yet stirred in the womb of unwritten time—there were old Englishmen who genuinely believed they had been born in a free country: a “free” nation where the Corporation Act and Test Act remained fully in force, heavy timbers propped against men’s liberties and dishonored consciences, all to shore up that state-endorsed anachronism.

The young man glanced at the clock again and frowned darkly. When his gaze strayed from the glowing horologue of time it rested intently upon a four-story red brick house in the row of dwellings opposite to where he sat. The shades were drawn, and the lights in many rooms shone dimly through them.

The young man looked at the clock once more, his brow furrowing with a deep scowl. As his eyes drifted from the luminous timepiece, they fixed themselves keenly on a four-storey red-brick house among the row of residences across from where he sat. The blinds were lowered, and through them the lights in several rooms glowed faintly.

I cannot describe to you my sensations on the near prospect of my undertaking. It is impossible to communicate to you a conception of the trembling sensation, half pleasurable and half fearful, with which I am preparing to depart. I am going to unexplored regions, to “the land of mist and snow,” but I shall kill no albatross; therefore do not be alarmed for my safety or if I should come back to you as worn and woeful as the “Ancient Mariner.” You will smile at my allusion, but I will disclose a secret. I have often attributed my attachment to, my passionate enthusiasm for, the dangerous mysteries of ocean to that production of the most imaginative of modern poets. There is something at work in my soul which I do not understand. I am practically industrious—painstaking, a workman to execute with perseverance and labour—but besides this there is a love for the marvellous, a belief in the marvellous, intertwined in all my projects, which hurries me out of the common pathways of men, even to the wild sea and unvisited regions I am about to explore.

I can scarce put into words the feelings that overtake me as my expedition draws close at hand. There is no way to convey to you the flutter of emotion I carry as I ready to leave—equal parts bright thrill and sharp unease. I head for uncharted territories, for that “land of mist and snow,” but I will bring no harm to any albatross, so you need not fear for my safety, nor fret if I return to you as haggard and heavy-hearted as the Ancient Mariner of the poem. You may smile at this reference, but I will share a secret with you. I have long traced my deep, fervent obsession with the ocean’s dangerous, hidden wonders back to that very work by the most visionary of modern poets. There is a force at work in my core I cannot fully explain. I am, by nature, methodical and diligent—meticulous, a laborer who sees tasks through with steady grit and effort—but tangled up in every one of my plans is a fierce love of the extraordinary, a wholehearted belief in its existence, that pushes me beyond the well-worn paths of ordinary men, all the way to the wild, unvisited seas I am soon to traverse.

The driver of one of them went and routed out Jerry asleep in his cab. He swore deeply, climbed to the captain’s bridge and steered his craft to the pier. His fare entered, and the cab whirled into the cool fastnesses of the park along the shortest homeward cuts.

The driver of one of them went off and roused Ned, who was dozing in his cab. He swore bitterly, mounted the captain’s perch, and brought his vehicle round to the pier. His fare got in, and the cab swept away into the cool depths of the park by the shortest homeward turns.

My dear Clerval," I cried, "you have been so incredibly good to me. You spent this entire winter cooped up in my sickroom instead of focusing on your studies as you had planned. I truly don't know how I will ever make it up to you. I feel such deep regret for being the cause of your disappointment, but I know you will forgive me.

I’ve never seed such splendid large knobs of fat before in the days of my life—they never used to be bigger then a horse-bean. And there was a great black crock upon the brandish with his legs a-sticking out, but I don’t know what was in within.”

And there was a great big black crock perched on the cooking stand with its legs sticking out, though I’ve no notion what was inside it.”

Twoscore of manuscripts were travelling the endless round of the magazines. How did the others do it? He spent long hours in the free reading-room, going over what others had written, studying their work eagerly and critically, comparing it with his own, and wondering, wondering, about the secret trick they had discovered which enabled them to sell their work.

“Well, now, you’d hardly believe it, but that man—our Miss Everdene’s father—was one of the ficklest husbands alive, after a while. Understand? ’a didn’t want to be fickle, but he couldn’t help it. The pore feller were faithful and true enough to her in his wish, but his heart would rove, do what he would. He spoke to me in real tribulation about it once. ‘Coggan,’ he said, ‘I could never wish for a handsomer woman than I’ve got, but feeling she’s ticketed as my lawful wife, I can’t help my wicked heart wandering, do what I will.’ But at last I believe he cured it by making her take off her wedding-ring and calling her by her maiden name as they sat together after the shop was shut, and so ’a would get to fancy she was only his sweetheart, and not married to him at all. And as soon as he could thoroughly fancy he was doing wrong and committing the seventh, ’a got to like her as well as ever, and they lived on a perfect picture of mutel love.”

Oh, you’d scarce believe it, but that man—our Miss Everdene’s own father—turned out to be one of the most fickle husbands walking the earth, after a while. Mark this: he never meant to be unsteady, he just couldn’t help himself. Poor fellow, every part of him wanted to be loyal and true to his wife, but his heart would wander no matter how hard he tried to rein it in. He poured out his trouble to me once, proper wretched over the whole mess. “Coggan,” he said, “I could never wish for a handsomer woman than the one I’ve got, but the second I remember she’s my lawfully wedded wife, my wicked heart drifts off, no matter what I do to stop it.” But I think he cured himself of it in the end, you see: he had her take off her wedding ring, and he’d call her by her maiden name every night once the shop was shut and they were sitting together alone. That way, he could fool himself into thinking she was only his sweetheart, not his spouse at all. The minute he could fully believe he was doing wrong and breaking the seventh commandment, he grew as fond of her as he’d ever been, and they went on to live as perfect a picture of quiet, devoted love as you could ever find.

Another part of the room soon riveted her gaze. At the other side of the chimney stood the settle, which is the necessary supplement to a fire so open that nothing less than a strong breeze will carry up the smoke. It is, to the hearths of old-fashioned cavernous fireplaces, what the east belt of trees is to the exposed country estate, or the north wall to the garden. Outside the settle candles gutter, locks of hair wave, young women shiver, and old men sneeze. Inside is Paradise. Not a symptom of a draught disturbs the air; the sitters’ backs are as warm as their faces, and songs and old tales are drawn from the occupants by the comfortable heat, like fruit from melon plants in a frame.

Her attention was soon seized by another corner of the room. Opposite the chimney stood the high-backed settle, that indispensable companion to a hearth so wide and open that only a stiff wind can draw the smoke upward. It is to the fireplaces of old, cavernous rooms what a sheltering belt of trees is to an exposed estate, or a north wall to a garden. Beyond the settle, candles flicker, strands of hair lift and stir, young women tremble, and old men catch cold. Within it lies Paradise. Not the faintest whisper of a draught troubles the air; the sitters’ backs bask as warmly as their faces, and the snug heat coaxes songs and old stories from them as gently as a frame coaxes melons from the vine.

Joe Larrabee came out of the post-oak flats of the Middle West pulsing with a genius for pictorial art. At six he drew a picture of the town pump with a prominent citizen passing it hastily. This effort was framed and hung in the drug store window by the side of the ear of corn with an uneven number of rows. At twenty he left for New York with a flowing necktie and a capital tied up somewhat closer.

Joe Larrabee hailed from the post-oak flatlands of the Midwest, bursting with innate genius for visual art from the moment he left those rural stretches. At six years old, he drew a sketch of the town pump, with one of the community’s most prominent residents hurrying past it in a rush. The piece was framed and hung in the local drugstore window, right alongside the ear of corn that had an odd, uneven number of rows. When he turned twenty, he set off for New York, wearing a loose, flowing necktie and carrying a small pool of savings he’d stowed away far more tightly than that casual neckwear was knotted.

“Of course, this is all about her future. You might not realize that another man has already made it plain he’s eager to marry Thomasin. While I haven’t given him any encouragement so far, I can’t in good conscience keep denying him a fair shot any longer. I don’t want to come off as curt with you, but I have to be fair to both him and her.”

He had no chance to utter a single word, or even form a coherent stray thought, until he was well clear of the City Sessions Hall; for the throng poured out behind him with such ferocious force it nearly swept him off his feet, and a raucous droning buzz spilled out onto the street as if a swarm of thwarted carrion flies had dispersed to hunt for fresh rotting quarry.

He returned across the heath to his van. The fire was not yet out, and everything remained as he had left it. Venn now bethought himself of his clothes, which were saturated with water to the weight of lead. He changed them, spread them before the fire, and lay down to sleep. But it was more than he could do to rest here while excited by a vivid imagination of the turmoil they were in at the house he had quitted, and, blaming himself for coming away, he dressed in another suit, locked up the door, and again hastened across to the inn. Rain was still falling heavily when he entered the kitchen. A bright fire was shining from the hearth, and two women were bustling about, one of whom was Olly Dowden.

He made his way back across the open heath to his van. The fire had not yet burned out, and every item sat exactly where he had left it. Venn suddenly thought of his clothes, which were so soaked through with rain they felt as heavy as lead. He changed out of the damp garments, spread them flat in front of the fire to dry, and stretched out to sleep. But rest was out of the question: his mind buzzed with sharp, vivid images of the chaos unfolding at the house he had just left. Chiding himself for departing in the first place, he pulled on a different suit, locked the van door, and hurried back across the heath toward the inn. Rain was still pouring heavily when he stepped into the kitchen. A bright, warm fire blazed in the hearth, and two women bustled about the space, one of whom was Olly Dowden.

He began to recapitulate items in the catalogue of his escapades, and the details of his automobile misadventures mixed themselves up with the story of his heart steering. For example there was that tremendous Siddons affair. He had been taking the corner of a girlish friendship and he had taken it altogether too far. What a frightful mess that had been! When once one is off the road anything may happen, from a crumpled mud-guard to the car on the top of you. And there was his forty miles an hour spurt with the great and gifted Delphine Marquise--for whom he was to have written a play and been a perfect Annunzio. Until Willersley appeared--very like the motor-cyclist--buzzing in the opposite direction. And then had ensued angers, humiliations....

He began going back over the list of his adventures, and the details of his automobile mishaps mingled with the story of his sentimental steering. There was, for instance, that colossal Siddons business. He had been taking a bend in a girlish friendship and had taken it altogether too fast. What a hideous tangle that had been! Once one is off the road, anything can happen, from a bent mudguard to the car landing on top of you. And then there had been his forty-miles-an-hour burst with the brilliant and gifted Delphine Marquise—for whom he was meant to write a play and become a perfect Annunzio. Until Willersley turned up—very like the motorcyclist—whirring toward him from the other direction. And after that came rage, humiliation....

“I was sitting on the churchyard wall when they came up, one from one way, the other from the other; and Miss Vye was walking thereabouts, looking at the headstones. As soon as they had gone in I went to the door, feeling I should like to see it, as I knew her so well. I pulled off my boots because they were so noisy, and went up into the gallery. I saw then that the parson and clerk were already there.”

I was perched on the churchyard boundary wall when the pair of them turned up, one coming from one end of the lane, the other from the opposite side; Miss Vye was strolling around the grounds not far off, pausing to study the inscriptions on the headstones. As soon as the two had stepped inside the church, I made my way over to the door, struck by a sudden urge to see what was happening inside, given how well I knew her. I pulled off my boots first because their hard soles would make far too much noise on the old floorboards, then crept up the stairs to the gallery. When I reached the top, I saw the parson and the parish clerk were already there.

“Ye mean well, and ye speak not unwisely. But it were shame that a king should know fear, and shame that belted knight should withhold his hand where be such as need succor. Peace, I will not go. It is you who must go. The Church's ban is not upon me, but it forbiddeth you to be here, and she will deal with you with a heavy hand an word come to her of your trespass.”

“Your heart is kind, and your counsel is not without wisdom. Yet it would be disgrace for a sovereign to cower, and disgrace for a girded warrior to stay his blade when souls cry out for aid. Enough. I shall not depart. It is you who must take leave. The anathema of the Church rests not on my head, but it bars you from this ground, and she will strike with a stern rod should whisper of your trespass reach her ear.”

Next, I sent an engineer and forty men to a point just beyond our lines on the south, to turn a mountain brook that was there, and bring it within our lines and under our command, arranging it in such a way that I could make instant use of it in an emergency. The forty men were divided into two shifts of twenty each, and were to relieve each other every two hours. In ten hours the work was accomplished.

After that, I dispatched a senior construction specialist and forty laborers to a spot just beyond our southern defensive lines, to redirect the small mountain stream that cut through that stretch, channeling it inside our perimeter and under our full control, with its routing calibrated so I could deploy its flow immediately if an emergency arose. The forty workers were split into two alternating shifts of twenty apiece, scheduled to relieve one another every two hours to keep progress steady. Ten hours after the first shovel hit dirt, the entire project was complete.

We implement three different models based on Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) using the implementation of . In the first scenario, we train an off-the-shelf Transformer encoder-decoder model for each direction, referred to as Transformer. Second, we use a strong pretrained English language model, RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) , to initialize the encoder of the NMT model for English to foreign translation. Finally, for foreign to English translation, we concatenate the translation features extracted from the last Transformer decoder layer, with the language modeling features extracted from RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) , before mapping the vectors to the output vocabulary. These models are denoted as Trans-former+RoBERTa.

metadata.title: PuzzLing Machines: A Challenge on Learning From Small Data

text: We implement three Transformer-based models (Vaswani et al., 2017) using the implementation provided by . In the first configuration, we train a standard Transformer encoder-decoder model for each translation direction; this model is referred to as Transformer. In the second, we initialize the encoder of the English-to-foreign NMT model with a powerful pretrained English language model, RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019). In the third configuration, for foreign-to-English translation, we concatenate the translation features obtained from the final Transformer decoder layer with the language modeling features extracted from RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019) before projecting the resulting vectors onto the output vocabulary. These models are designated as Transformer+RoBERTa.

So he must have sat, and presently he lay on some rough bedding and suffered, untended, in infinite discomfort; lay motionless and thought at times, it may be, of Matching's Easy and wondered what Hugh and Teddy were doing. Then he became fevered, and the world grew bright-coloured and fantastic and ugly for him. Until one day an infinite weakness laid hold of him, and his pain grew faint and all his thoughts and memories grew faint--and still fainter....

Thus he would have remained seated, until at last he stretched out upon coarse coverlets and endured, alone, a vast unease; lay still and now and then, perhaps, recalled Matching’s Easy and mused what Hugh and Teddy might be about. Then a fever took him, and the world turned garish and grotesque and monstrous in his sight. Until a day came when a boundless frailty seized him, and his suffering dimmed, and all his recollections and fancies dimmed—and dimmer still….

In due time we were challenged by the warders, from the castle walls, and after a parley admitted. I have nothing pleasant to tell about that visit. But it was not a disappointment, for I knew Mrs. le Fay by reputation, and was not expecting anything pleasant. She was held in awe by the whole realm, for she had made everybody believe she was a great sorceress. All her ways were wicked, all her instincts devilish. She was loaded to the eyelids with cold malice. All her history was black with crime; and among her crimes murder was common. I was most curious to see her; as curious as I could have been to see Satan. To my surprise she was beautiful; black thoughts had failed to make her expression repulsive, age had failed to wrinkle her satin skin or mar its bloomy freshness. She could have passed for old Uriens' granddaughter, she could have been mistaken for sister to her own son.

Before long, the guards posted on the castle ramparts called out to halt us, and we were let inside only after a short exchange. I don’t have a single nice thing to say about that visit, but that didn’t come as a letdown. I’d heard all about Mrs. le Fay’s reputation long before we arrived, so I’d never gone in anticipating a pleasant experience anyway. The entire kingdom feared her, because she’d convinced every last person she was a powerful sorceress. Every choice she made was cruel, every instinct she had was twisted. She brimmed with unfeeling, spiteful malice from head to toe. Her entire past was stained with cruelty, and murder was far from an uncommon offense for her. I’d been desperate to meet her, as curious about her as I would have been to lay eyes on the devil himself. I was shocked to find she was stunningly beautiful: the darkness of her mind hadn’t twisted her face into something ugly, and the years hadn’t etched a single wrinkle into her smooth, silken skin, or dimmed its soft, dewy glow. She could easily have passed for old Uriens’ granddaughter, and anyone would have mistaken her for her own son’s sister.

Regarding the generation quality, we observe that baseline models cannot achieve the diversity achieved by GPT-2 models in terms of RR -both for NICHE and CROWD (4.89 vs 3.23, and 8.93 vs. 5.89). Moreover, GPT-2 provides an impressive boost in novelty (0.04 vs 0.46 and 0.10 vs 0.70). Among the GPT-2 models, the quality scores (in terms of RR and novelty) of the CNs generated by GPT niche are more than double in comparison to those generated with GPT crowd .

Moreover, GPT-2 yields a substantial increase in novelty (0.04 vs. 0.46 and 0.10 vs. 0.70). Among the GPT-2 models, the quality scores, in terms of RR and novelty, of the CNs generated by GPT niche are more than twice those generated by GPT crowd.

In a dim light, and with a slight rearrangement of her hair, her general figure might have stood for that of either of the higher female deities. The new moon behind her head, an old helmet upon it, a diadem of accidental dewdrops round her brow, would have been adjuncts sufficient to strike the note of Artemis, Athena, or Hera respectively, with as close an approximation to the antique as that which passes muster on many respected canvases.

She evoked the scent of damask blooms, the fire of garnets, and the stillness of an equatorial dusk; her temper suggested the dreams of the Lethe and the gravity of a processional hymn; her movements, the rhythmic pulse of the tide; her voice, the deep wood of a cello. In the fading light, with a mere shifting of her tresses, her form might have served as a model for the supreme queens of Olympus. A silver crescent poised behind her, a rusted helm upon her brow, or a circlet of stray dewdrops would have been ornaments enough to summon the presence of Diana, Minerva, or Juno, offering a resemblance to the ancient world as faithful as those portrayals found upon the walls of the most esteemed galleries.

My father was enraptured on finding me freed from the vexations of a criminal charge, that I was again allowed to breathe the fresh atmosphere and permitted to return to my native country. I did not participate in these feelings, for to me the walls of a dungeon or a palace were alike hateful. The cup of life was poisoned for ever, and although the sun shone upon me, as upon the happy and gay of heart, I saw around me nothing but a dense and frightful darkness, penetrated by no light but the glimmer of two eyes that glared upon me. Sometimes they were the expressive eyes of Henry, languishing in death, the dark orbs nearly covered by the lids and the long black lashes that fringed them; sometimes it was the watery, clouded eyes of the monster, as I first saw them in my chamber at Ingolstadt.

My father was overjoyed to see me cleared of the torments of a criminal accusation, free once more to breathe the open air and allowed to return to my homeland. I could not share his delight, for to me a prison’s walls and a palace’s chambers were equally loathsome. The draught of life had been forever tainted, and though the sun fell on me as it does on the light-hearted and the joyful, I perceived nothing around me but a thick and terrible darkness, broken by no gleam save the glint of two eyes that stared back at me. At times they were Henry’s speaking eyes, fading in death, their dark pupils almost veiled by the lids and the long black lashes that edged them; at other times they were the clouded, watery eyes of the creature, just as I had first beheld them in my room at Ingolstadt.

So they passed inside, and the heavy canvas, to a spectator in the field, became distended with a thousand small knots like those on a bag of grain, pushed out by the frantic press of heads, shoulders, and joints within.

I watched my fifty-two boys narrowly; watched their faces, their walk, their unconscious attitudes: for all these are a language --a language given us purposely that it may betray us in times of emergency, when we have secrets which we want to keep. I knew that that thought would keep saying itself over and over again in their minds and hearts, _All England is marching against us!_ and ever more strenuously imploring attention with each repetition, ever more sharply realizing itself to their imaginations, until even in their sleep they would find no rest from it, but hear the vague and flitting creatures of the dreams say, _All England_ --ALL ENGLAND!--_is marching against you_! I knew all this would happen; I knew that ultimately the pressure would become so great that it would compel utterance; therefore, I must be ready with an answer at that time--an answer well chosen and tranquilizing.

I watched my fifty-two boys closely, studying their faces, the way they moved, their unconscious postures; for all these things are a kind of language—a language deliberately given to us so that it may betray us in moments of crisis, when we are trying to hide our secrets. I knew that one thought would go on repeating itself in their minds and hearts: All England is marching against us! And with every repetition it would demand attention more urgently, and take sharper shape in their imaginations, until even in sleep they would find no relief from it, but hear the faint, shifting creatures of dreams saying, All England—ALL ENGLAND—is marching against you! I knew that all this would come to pass; I knew that in the end the strain would grow so intense that it would force itself into words. So I had to be ready then with an answer—an answer carefully chosen and meant to calm them.

text:
Did he have any wishes at all?
Only his breath, shaped into a sigh,
Would have spoken clearly to me.
And did he stay sure of himself until
Misfortune spilled into an endless deep?

It was still the beaming time of evening, though night was stealthily making itself visible low down upon the ground, the western lines of light raking the earth without alighting upon it to any extent, or illuminating the dead levels at all. The sun had crept round the tree as a last effort before death, and then began to sink, the shearers’ lower parts becoming steeped in embrowning twilight, whilst their heads and shoulders were still enjoying day, touched with a yellow of self-sustained brilliancy that seemed inherent rather than acquired.

The evening still held its bright, warm glow, even as night crept in quiet and unassuming low to the ground. Slanted western rays skimmed the earth’s surface but barely settled there, doing nothing at all to illuminate the flat, featureless stretches of plain. The sun had slid around the tree trunk in one final, fading push before dipping out of sight, and as it sank lower, the shearers’ legs and lower torsos were swallowed up by deepening, bronze-tinged twilight. Their heads and shoulders, though, still basked in the last of the daylight, gilded with a bright honey-yellow glow that looked as if it emanated from their own skin rather than being cast down by the fading sun.

After several relapses into business-absorption, Mr. Lorry had become the Doctor’s friend, and the quiet street-corner was the sunny part of his life.

Mr. Jarvis Lorry strolled along the sun-warmed streets from his home in Clerkenwell, bound for dinner with the doctor. Though he’d slipped back into being fully consumed by his work on several occasions, Lorry had grown into a close friend of the doctor’s, and that quiet street corner had become the brightest, most welcoming part of his daily life.

Oh! How unlike it was to the blue seasons of the south! Covered with ice, it was only to be distinguished from land by its superior wildness and ruggedness. The Greeks wept for joy when they beheld the Mediterranean from the hills of Asia, and hailed with rapture the boundary of their toils. I did not weep, but I knelt down and with a full heart thanked my guiding spirit for conducting me in safety to the place where I hoped, notwithstanding my adversary’s gibe, to meet and grapple with him.

My resolve and endurance were steeled by those mocking taunts; I vowed not to falter in my quest, and beseeching Providence to sustain me, I pressed on with undiminished zeal across boundless wastes, till the sea glimmered afar and marked the final rim of the world. Ah! How alien it was to the azure summers of the south! Sheathed in frost, it could scarcely be told from the shore save by its greater savagery and desolation. The Hellenes shed glad tears when they glimpsed the Aegean from the Asian highlands, and acclaimed with ecstasy the end of their labours. Our query generation methods are inexpensive and fast, which enables rapid experimentation. Examples of R and T are shown in Figure 2, and the queries generated from R are shown in Figure 3. In the example, we have two documents, D1 and D2, each containing two sentences, S1 and S2. In sentence mode for query generation, each of the four sentences in R is used as a query; in unique-terms mode, the six vocabulary words are extracted as queries.

Two varieties only of humanity could have entered the paddock with the ghostlike glide of that mysterious figure. They were a woman and a gipsy man. A woman was out of the question in such an occupation at this hour, and the comer could be no less than a thief, who might probably have known the weakness of the household on this particular night, and have chosen it on that account for his daring attempt. Moreover, to raise suspicion to conviction itself, there were gipsies in Weatherbury Bottom.

Only two types of people could have drifted into the enclosure with the spectral grace of that shadowy figure: a woman or a traveler. Given the late hour and the nature of the task, a woman was out of the question; therefore, the intruder could be nothing but a thief. He had likely recognized the household's vulnerability on this specific night and chosen it for his bold intrusion. To turn this suspicion into a certainty, a group of travelers was currently known to be camped down in Weatherbury Bottom.
