Of her outward appearance I know no more than that she was like her mother. The other had that likeness too--as you have--but was not the same. Can you follow me, Lucie? Hardly, I think? I doubt you must have been a solitary prisoner to understand these perplexed distinctions.”

“No. That was a different matter altogether. It rose before the troubled reach of my sight, yet it never stirred. The image my thoughts went after was another child, and one more substantial than the first. Of her outward look I know nothing beyond this—that she was like her mother. The other possessed that same resemblance as well, as you do, yet she was not the same. Do you follow me, Clara? Hardly, I think? I fear one must have lived alone in a prison to make sense of such tangled distinctions.”

“By degrees I made a discovery of still greater moment. I found that these people possessed a method of communicating their experience and feelings to one another by articulate sounds. I perceived that the words they spoke sometimes produced pleasure or pain, smiles or sadness, in the minds and countenances of the hearers. This was indeed a godlike science, and I ardently desired to become acquainted with it. But I was baffled in every attempt I made for this purpose. Their pronunciation was quick, and the words they uttered, not having any apparent connection with visible objects, I was unable to discover any clue by which I could unravel the mystery of their reference. By great application, however, and after having remained during the space of several revolutions of the moon in my hovel, I discovered the names that were given to some of the most familiar objects of discourse; I learned and applied the words, _fire, milk, bread,_ and _wood._ I learned also the names of the cottagers themselves. The youth and his companion had each of them several names, but the old man had only one, which was _father._ The girl was called _sister_ or _Agatha,_ and the youth _Felix, brother,_ or _son_. I cannot describe the delight I felt when I learned the ideas appropriated to each of these sounds and was able to pronounce them. I distinguished several other words without being able as yet to understand or apply them, such as _good, dearest, unhappy._

Little by little, I stumbled on a far more important revelation. I realized the people I was watching had a way to share their experiences and emotions with each other using clear, spoken sounds. I noticed that the words they said could spark joy or hurt, grins or sorrow, both in the listeners’ faces and their inner thoughts. This felt like a skill almost divine in its power, and I ached to master it for myself. But every effort I made to learn it left me totally stumped at first. They spoke so fast, and most of the words they said had no obvious link to anything I could see around me, so I couldn’t find a single thread to pull that would untangle what they were referring to. Slowly, though, through sheer relentless effort, after spending several full lunar cycles hidden away in my small shelter, I figured out the labels they used for some of the most common items they talked about. I memorized and started using the words for fire, milk, bread, and wood. I also picked up what the cottage dwellers called each other. The young man and the woman he lived with went by multiple names, but the elderly man only had one: father. The young woman was called either sister or Agatha, and the young man Felix, brother, or son. I can’t put into words how overjoyed I was when I understood what each of those sounds meant, and could say them out loud myself. I picked out a handful of other words too, though I couldn’t yet figure out their meaning or use them correctly—words like good, dearest, and unhappy.

He dared not go near Ruth’s neighborhood in the daytime, but night found him lurking like a thief around the Morse home, stealing glimpses at the windows and loving the very walls that sheltered her. Several times he barely escaped being caught by her brothers, and once he trailed Mr. Morse down town and studied his face in the lighted streets, longing all the while for some quick danger of death to threaten so that he might spring in and save her father. On another night, his vigil was rewarded by a glimpse of Ruth through a second-story window. He saw only her head and shoulders, and her arms raised as she fixed her hair before a mirror. It was only for a moment, but it was a long moment to him, during which his blood turned to wine and sang through his veins. Then she pulled down the shade. But it was her room—he had learned that; and thereafter he strayed there often, hiding under a dark tree on the opposite side of the street and smoking countless cigarettes. One afternoon he saw her mother coming out of a bank, and received another proof of the enormous distance that separated Ruth from him. She was of the class that dealt with banks. He had never been inside a bank in his life, and he had an idea that such institutions were frequented only by the very rich and the very powerful.

He did not venture near Ruth’s neighborhood during the day, but at night he lingered around the Morse house like a prowler, watching the windows and feeling a sense of reverence for the very walls that protected her. He narrowly avoided being spotted by her brothers on several occasions, and once he even followed Mr. Morse into the city, studying the man’s face beneath the streetlamps while secretly hoping for some fatal accident to occur so that he might rush in and save her father’s life. One night, his persistence was rewarded when he caught a glimpse of Ruth through a second-story window. He saw only her head and shoulders, her arms lifted as she styled her hair in front of a mirror. It lasted only a moment, yet for him, that moment stretched on as his blood turned to wine and raced through his heart. Then she lowered the shade. Now that he knew which room was hers, he returned often, hiding beneath a dark tree on the far side of the street and smoking one cigarette after another. One afternoon, he happened to see her mother leaving a bank, which served as another reminder of the vast social gulf between them. She belonged to the class of people who used banks; he had never once entered such a building, believing they were reserved solely for the most affluent and powerful members of society.

Neural Machine Translation (NMT) directly models the conditional distribution of the translated sentence y = (y 1 , ..., y ) given a source sentence x = (x 1 , ..., x ) 2 . The conditional probability density function p(y|x) is parameterized by an encoder-decoder neural network: The encoder encodes the source sentence into a sequence of hidden representations H(x) = (h 1 , ..., h n ), and the decoder generates target sentence one token at a time using these intermediate representations.

metadata.title: Multi-Domain Neural Machine Translation with Word-Level Adaptive Layer-wise Domain Mixing

text: Neural Machine Translation (NMT) models the conditional distribution of a translated sentence y = (y1, ..., y2) given a source sentence x = (x1, ..., xn) directly. The conditional probability density p(y|x) is parameterized by an encoder-decoder neural network: the encoder maps the source sentence into a sequence of hidden representations H(x) = (h1, ..., hn), and the decoder produces the target sentence token by token conditioned on these intermediate representations.

He got up abruptly and tried to see himself in the dirty looking-glass over the wash-stand. He passed a towel over it and looked again, long and carefully. It was the first time he had ever really seen himself. His eyes were made for seeing, but up to that moment they had been filled with the ever changing panorama of the world, at which he had been too busy gazing, ever to gaze at himself. He saw the head and face of a young fellow of twenty, but, being unused to such appraisement, he did not know how to value it. Above a square-domed forehead he saw a mop of brown hair, nut-brown, with a wave to it and hints of curls that were a delight to any woman, making hands tingle to stroke it and fingers tingle to pass caresses through it. But he passed it by as without merit, in Her eyes, and dwelt long and thoughtfully on the high, square forehead,—striving to penetrate it and learn the quality of its content. What kind of a brain lay behind there? was his insistent interrogation. What was it capable of? How far would it take him? Would it take him to her?

He rose suddenly and tried to catch his reflection in the grimy mirror above the washstand. He wiped it with a towel and studied his image again, long and intently. It was the first time he had truly looked at himself. His eyes were designed for observation, but until now they had been absorbed by the shifting spectacle of the world; he had been too preoccupied gazing outward to ever examine his own face. He saw the head and features of a young man of twenty, but, unaccustomed to such scrutiny, he had no idea how to judge them. Above a broad, squared forehead, he noticed a thick shock of brown hair—nut-brown, with a gentle wave and subtle curls that any woman would find captivating, her hands itching to smooth it, her fingers longing to weave through it. Yet he dismissed it as worthless in her eyes and lingered instead, deep in thought, on the tall, squared forehead—trying to see through it and discern the nature of what lay within. What kind of mind resided there? was his persistent question. What could it achieve? How far could it carry him? Could it carry him to her?

“It’s enough for you,” retorted Mr. Cruncher, “to be the wife of a honest tradesman, and not to occupy your female mind with calculations when he took to his trade or when he didn’t. A honouring and obeying wife would let his trade alone altogether. Call yourself a religious woman? If you’re a religious woman, give me a irreligious one! You have no more nat’ral sense of duty than the bed of this here Thames river has of a pile, and similarly it must be knocked into you.”

It should be enough for you," Mr. Cruncher snapped back, "to be the wife of a hard-working man, without cluttering your head with questions about when I’m working or when I’m not. A wife who truly honors and obeys would stay out of her husband’s business entirely. You call yourself a woman of faith? If you’re what passes for religious, I’d much rather have a sinner! You have no more natural sense of duty than the bottom of the Thames has for a pier post, and just like that riverbed, it’ll have to be hammered into you.

I had no idea of coming in here; but there’s nothing so refreshing as a cup of tea, though I should never have got one if you hadn’t helped me.”

I would have departed two hours ago if it weren’t for him. I hadn’t planned on coming inside at all, but there is nothing quite so restorative as a cup of tea, though I never would have had one without your help.”

The day was done, and the merrymakers had nearly all departed. But as Martin and Lizzie emerged from the trees they found the gang waiting for them. Martin knew immediately the meaning of it. Trouble was brewing. The gang was his body-guard. They passed out through the gates of the park with, straggling in the rear, a second gang, the friends that Lizzie’s young man had collected to avenge the loss of his lady. Several constables and special police officers, anticipating trouble, trailed along to prevent it, and herded the two gangs separately aboard the train for San Francisco. Martin told Jimmy that he would get off at Sixteenth Street Station and catch the electric car into Oakland. Lizzie was very quiet and without interest in what was impending. The train pulled in to Sixteenth Street Station, and the waiting electric car could be seen, the conductor of which was impatiently clanging the gong.

The day was over, and most of the revelers had already gone. But when Martin and Lizzie came out of the trees, they found the gang waiting for them. Martin understood at once what it meant. Trouble was in the air. The gang was there to protect him. They moved out through the park gates with a second gang straggling behind—the friends Lizzie’s young man had gathered to avenge the loss of his sweetheart. Several constables and special officers, expecting trouble, followed at a distance to stop it, and they shepherded the two gangs onto the train to San Francisco, keeping them apart. Martin told Jimmy that he would get off at Sixteenth Street Station and take the electric car to Oakland. Lizzie was very quiet and seemed unconcerned with what was about to happen. The train drew into Sixteenth Street Station, and the waiting electric car was visible, its conductor ringing the gong impatiently.

In cool, fresh weather Mrs. Yeobright would have found no inconvenience in walking to Alderworth, but the present torrid attack made the journey a heavy undertaking for a woman past middle age; and at the end of the third mile she wished that she had hired Fairway to drive her a portion at least of the distance. But from the point at which she had arrived it was as easy to reach Clym’s house as to get home again. So she went on, the air around her pulsating silently, and oppressing the earth with lassitude. She looked at the sky overhead, and saw that the sapphirine hue of the zenith in spring and early summer had been replaced by a metallic violet.

In cool, fresh weather Mrs. Yeobright would have found the walk to Alderworth no trouble at all, but this fierce spell of heat made the journey a serious effort for a woman well past middle age; and by the end of the third mile she wished she had engaged Fairway to drive her at least part of the way. Yet from where she now stood, Clym’s house was no farther to reach than her own home. So she kept on, while the air about her throbbed in stillness and burdened the earth with a weary heaviness. She glanced up at the sky and saw that the sapphire tint which the zenith wore in spring and early summer had given way to a metallic violet.

And as it was with birds, so had it been with everything. His ignorant and unprepared attempts at philosophy had been fruitless. The medieval metaphysics of Kant had given him the key to nothing, and had served the sole purpose of making him doubt his own intellectual powers. In similar manner his attempt to study evolution had been confined to a hopelessly technical volume by Romanes. He had understood nothing, and the only idea he had gathered was that evolution was a dry-as-dust theory, of a lot of little men possessed of huge and unintelligible vocabularies. And now he learned that evolution was no mere theory but an accepted process of development; that scientists no longer disagreed about it, their only differences being over the method of evolution.

And as it had been with the birds, so it had been with all else. His foolish, ill-armed ventures into philosophy had come to nothing. The scholastic metaphysics of Kant had unlocked nothing for him; they had served only to make him mistrust his own powers of thought. In much the same way, his attempt to study evolution had been reduced to a desperately technical treatise by a writer named Romanes. He had understood not a word, and the one impression he carried away was that evolution was a dry, dusty doctrine, held by a swarm of little men with immense and incomprehensible vocabularies. And now he discovered that evolution was no mere theory at all, but an accepted process of growth; that scientists no longer quarrelled over its existence, but only over the manner of its working.

The Cross-lingual Language Model (XLM) is based on BERT. It differs from BERT in that it does not use NSP objective, has more layers (16 vs 12), more attention heads (16 vs 12), larger hidden layers size (1280 vs 768) and a larger vocabulary (200k vs 110k) . Moreover, the vocabulary is learned on a corpus for which the most popular languages were undersampled to balance the number of tokens between high-and low-resource languages. We use the XLM-17 model, which was trained on Wikipedia for 17 languages (including Polish).

metadata.title: KLEJ: Comprehensive Benchmark for Polish Language Understanding

text: The Cross-lingual Language Model (XLM) builds upon BERT but departs from it in several respects: it omits the next-sentence prediction (NSP) objective, incorporates more layers (16 versus 12), more attention heads (16 versus 12), a larger hidden dimension (1280 versus 768), and a larger vocabulary (200k versus 110k). Furthermore, the vocabulary is derived from a corpus in which the most prevalent languages were deliberately undersampled to balance token counts between high- and low-resource languages. We employ the XLM-17 variant, which was trained on Wikipedia data spanning 17 languages, including Polish.

Explicit Semantic Features. N-gram matching mechanism is applied to capture explicit semantic features. We use disease names (D) to sampling on the text (T). First, move the sliding window on the disease name d l ∈ D to get a n-gram substring. Then, calculate the frequency of each n-gram substring in the free-text. The sum of frequencies of gram with same length n (denoted as gram n ) has reflected the emergence of disease names in the text, nevertheless some grams have their unique particularity. For example, given a 2-gram string, "糖尿" (Diabetes) is more representative than "慢 性"(Chronic) though they have the same length.

metadata.title: Clinical-Coder: Assigning Interpretable ICD-10 Codes to Chinese Clinical Notes

text: Explicit Semantic Features. An n-gram matching mechanism is employed to capture explicit semantic features. We use disease names (D) to sample the text (T). First, a sliding window is moved over the disease name \(d_l \in D\) to obtain an n-gram substring. Then, the frequency of each n-gram substring in the free text is calculated. The sum of the frequencies of grams with the same length \(n\) (denoted as gram\(_n\)) reflects the appearance of disease names in the text; however, some grams exhibit unique specificity. For example, given a 2-gram string, "糖尿" (Diabetes) is more representative than "慢 性" (Chronic) even though they have the same length.

Of all the terrific yells that ever fell on mortal ears, none could exceed the cry of the infuriated throng. Some shouted to those who were nearest to set the house on fire; others roared to the officers to shoot him dead. Among them all, none showed such fury as the man on horseback, who, throwing himself out of the saddle, and bursting through the crowd as if he were parting water, cried, beneath the window, in a voice that rose above all others, “Twenty guineas to the man who brings a ladder!”

Of all the dreadful cries that ever struck mortal ears, none could surpass the shout of the enraged mob. Some called on those nearest at hand to set fire to the house; others bellowed to the officers to shoot him dead. Yet none among them displayed such savage fury as the man on horseback, who flung himself from the saddle and forced his way through the crowd as though parting water, crying beneath the window, in a voice louder than all the rest, “Twenty guineas to the man who brings a ladder!”

Based on the analysis in the previous section, we can consider two strategies to improve the task setting: using full-text articles as source documents instead of leading sentences; and removing nonentailment instances from the dataset. Although the former strategy reduces the ratio of non-entailment pair to 7.2% (English Gigaword) and 5.8% (JA-MUL), we must consider the trade-off: the use of full-text articles increases the cost for training, and may decrease the quality of headlines because of longer inputs to encoder-decoder models. Furthermore, JNC does not provide full-text articles but only lead three sentences. Therefore, we take the latter strategy, removing non-entailment pairs from the supervision data for headline generation.

metadata.title: Improving Truthfulness of Headline Generation

text: Based on the analysis in the preceding section, we can identify two possible ways to improve the task formulation: replacing leading sentences with full-text articles as source documents, and excluding non-entailment instances from the dataset. Although the former approach lowers the proportion of non-entailment pairs to 7.2% for English Gigaword and 5.8% for JA-MUL, it entails a clear trade-off: full-text articles increase training cost and may also degrade headline quality due to the longer inputs required by encoder-decoder models. In addition, JNC does not provide full-text articles, but only the leading three sentences. For this reason, we adopt the latter approach, namely removing non-entailment pairs from the supervision data for headline generation.

It was eventually known that Gabriel, though paid a fixed wage by Bathsheba independent of the fluctuations of agricultural profits, had made an engagement with Boldwood by which Oak was to receive a share of the receipts—a small share certainly, yet it was money of a higher quality than mere wages, and capable of expansion in a way that wages were not. Some were beginning to consider Oak a “near” man, for though his condition had thus far improved, he lived in no better style than before, occupying the same cottage, paring his own potatoes, mending his stockings, and sometimes even making his bed with his own hands. But as Oak was not only provokingly indifferent to public opinion, but a man who clung persistently to old habits and usages, simply because they were old, there was room for doubt as to his motives.

It came to light in time that Gabriel, though Bathsheba paid him a steady sum regardless of the harvest’s yield, had struck a compact with Boldwood entitling Oak to a portion of the takings—a modest portion, to be sure, but it was coin of a purer stamp than common wages, and bore within it a germ of increase that wages could never foster. A few now began to murmur that Oak was a “close” fellow, for while his fortunes had plainly risen, he kept to no grander mode of living than before, dwelling in the same humble rooms, peeling his own potatoes, darning his own hose, and sometimes even smoothing his own bedclothes with his own hands. Yet since Oak was not only vexingly careless of the world’s gaze, but a creature who held fast to ancient ways and customs merely because they were ancient, his true intentions remained open to question.

It was very gratifying. We were progressing, that was sure.

Our new coinage was not merely passing hands in fine style, but its vocabulary had already slipped quite readily into common speech; that is to say, the old denominations had been forgotten, and people now talked of things as costing so many dollars, or cents, or mills, or milrays. Gabriel was already among the turgid, prostrate forms. He had flung off his coat, rolled up his shirt-sleeves, and taken from his pocket the instrument of salvation. It was a small tube or trochar, with a lance passing down the inside; and Gabriel began to use it with a dexterity that would have graced a hospital surgeon. Passing his hand over the sheep’s left flank, and selecting the proper point, he punctured the skin and rumen with the lance as it stood in the tube; then he suddenly withdrew the lance, retaining the tube in its place. A current of air rushed up the tube, forcible enough to have extinguished a candle held at the orifice.

Gabriel was already moving among the swollen, prostrate sheep. He’d flung his coat aside, rolled his shirtsleeves up to his elbows, and drawn the life-saving implement from his pocket. It was a small trocar: a hollow tube fitted with a sharp lance running through its center, and Gabriel wielded it with the precise dexterity that would do a seasoned hospital surgeon proud. He ran his hand over the sheep’s left flank to find the correct spot, then drove the lance, still housed in the tube, through the skin and distended rumen. He withdrew the lance in a single sharp movement, leaving the tube fixed in place. A forceful stream of air rushed up through the tube, powerful enough to snuff out a candle held directly at its opening.

Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness,
Close bosom-friend of the maturing sun;
Conspiring with him how to load and bless
With fruit the vines that round the thatch-eves run;
To bend with apples the moss'd cottage-trees,
And fill all fruit with ripeness to the core;
To swell the gourd, and plump the hazel shells
With a sweet kernel; to set budding more,
And still more, later flowers for the bees,
Until they think warm days will never cease,
For Summer has o'er-brimm'd their clammy cells.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
Time of soft haze and honeyed harvest glow,
Dear heart-companion to the aging sun;
You plot together how to overflow
And grace with grapes the vines by thatch-eaves spun;
To weight with apples every mossy bough,
And steep each fruit in sweetness to its soul;
To fatten gourds, and fill the hazel’s hull
With ripened meat; to bid new buds bestow
Still more late blooms to draw the drowsy bees,
Till they believe warm hours will never flee,
For Summer’s drenched their sticky treasuries.

There was a few minutes’ interval, and then the key turned in the lock, the door opened, and Eustacia herself confronted him.

The latter gentleman had a large staff, a large head, large features, and large half-boots; and he looked as if he had been taking a proportionate allowance of ale—as indeed he had.

Gathered in that informal lower chamber of the household’s own domestic parliament were the female servants, Mr. Brittles, Mr. Giles, the tinker (who’d received a special invitation to feast and make himself at home for the rest of the day as a reward for his work), and the local constable. This last gentleman carried a thick truncheon, had a broad, heavy head, oversized facial features, and bulky half-boots; he looked as if he’d drunk a perfectly proportional share of ale—which, as it turned out, he most certainly had.

Two other passengers, besides the one, were plodding up the hill by the side of the mail. All three were wrapped to the cheekbones and over the ears, and wore jack-boots. Not one of the three could have said, from anything he saw, what either of the other two was like; and each was hidden under almost as many wrappers from the eyes of the mind, as from the eyes of the body, of his two companions. In those days, travellers were very shy of being confidential on a short notice, for anybody on the road might be a robber or in league with robbers. As to the latter, when every posting-house and ale-house could produce somebody in “the Captain’s” pay, ranging from the landlord to the lowest stable non-descript, it was the likeliest thing upon the cards. So the guard of the Dover mail thought to himself, that Friday night in November, one thousand seven hundred and seventy-five, lumbering up Shooter’s Hill, as he stood on his own particular perch behind the mail, beating his feet, and keeping an eye and a hand on the arm-chest before him, where a loaded blunderbuss lay at the top of six or eight loaded horse-pistols, deposited on a substratum of cutlass.

Beside the lone one, two more passengers were trudging up the hill alongside the mail-coach. All three were muffled to the cheekbones and over their ears, and all wore jack-boots. None of the three could have told, from anything visible, what either of the other two looked like; each, to the eyes of the mind as much as to the eyes of the body, was hidden from his companions by nearly as many wrappings. In those days, travellers were very cautious about becoming confidential at short notice, since anyone on the road might prove to be a robber, or in collusion with robbers. And as for the latter, when every posting-house and ale-house could furnish someone in “the Captain’s” pay, from the landlord down to the most insignificant stable-hand, that seemed the likeliest possibility of all. So the guard on the Dover mail reflected, on that Friday night in November of 1775, as he laboured up Shooter’s Hill, standing on his own special perch behind the coach, stamping his feet and keeping both eye and hand on the arm-chest in front of him, where a loaded blunderbuss lay atop six or eight loaded horse-pistols, all resting on a base of cutlass.

The GPT-2 models trained and evaluated in this paper use a sub-word vocabulary learned by byte-pair encoding (BPE; Sennrich et al., 2016) to represent their inputs, while all other models represent and compute over word-level inputs. This byte-pair encoding was taken from the pre-trained GPT-2 model trained on a much larger corpus. The results reported for these models thus conflate a choice of model class (a deep Transformer architecture) and preprocessing standard (sub-word tokenization computed on a larger corpus). Some preliminary work suggests that sub-word tokenization is indeed responsible for much of the larger GPT-2 models' success: we find that GPT-2 models trained on word-level representations of BLLIP-LG and BLLIP-MD achieve good perplexity measures, but degrade sharply in SG score. Peculiarities of the GPT-2 training regime may be responsible for its particularly bad performance on the smaller corpora. Its sub-word vocabulary was held constant across training corpora, meaning that the model vocabulary size also remained constant across corpora, unlike the other models tested. The poor performance of GPT-2 models trained on smaller corpora may thus be due to overparameterization, and not due to fundamental problems with the model architecture at small data scales. We leave a thorough investigation of the role of sub-word tokenization to future work.

metadata.title: A Systematic Assessment of Syntactic Generalization in Neural Language Models

text: The GPT-2 models trained and evaluated in this paper employ a sub-word vocabulary learned through byte-pair encoding (BPE; Sennrich et al., 2016) to represent their inputs, whereas all other models use and compute over word-level inputs. This byte-pair encoding was adopted from the pre-trained GPT-2 model trained on a substantially larger corpus. The reported results for these models therefore conflate a model-class choice (a deep Transformer architecture) with a preprocessing standard (sub-word tokenization learned from a larger corpus). Preliminary evidence suggests that sub-word tokenization is indeed a major contributor to the larger GPT-2 models’ success: we find that GPT-2 models trained on word-level representations of BLLIP-LG and BLLIP-MD obtain good perplexity scores, but deteriorate sharply in SG score. Idiosyncrasies of the GPT-2 training regime may explain its especially poor performance on the smaller corpora. Its sub-word vocabulary was fixed across training corpora, so the model vocabulary size also remained constant across corpora, unlike the other models evaluated. The weak performance of GPT-2 models trained on smaller corpora may thus reflect overparameterization rather than any fundamental limitation of the architecture at small data scales. A fuller investigation of the role of sub-word tokenization is left for future work.

text: After his prayer they put the noose around the young girl's neck, and they had great trouble to adjust the knot under her ear, because she was devouring the baby all the time, wildly kissing it, and snatching it to her face and her breast, and drenching it with tears, and half moaning, half shrieking all the while, and the baby crowing, and laughing, and kicking its feet with delight over what it took for romp and play. Even the hangman couldn't stand it, but turned away. When all was ready the priest gently pulled and tugged and forced the child out of the mother's arms, and stepped quickly out of her reach; but she clasped her hands, and made a wild spring toward him, with a shriek; but the rope--and the under-sheriff--held her short. Then she went on her knees and stretched out her hands and cried:

That night was an eventful one to Eustacia’s brain, and one which she hardly ever forgot. She dreamt a dream; and few human beings, from Nebuchadnezzar to the Swaffham tinker, ever dreamt a more remarkable one. Such an elaborately developed, perplexing, exciting dream was certainly never dreamed by a girl in Eustacia’s situation before. It had as many ramifications as the Cretan labyrinth, as many fluctuations as the northern lights, as much colour as a parterre in June, and was as crowded with figures as a coronation. To Queen Scheherazade the dream might have seemed not far removed from commonplace; and to a girl just returned from all the courts of Europe it might have seemed not more than interesting. But amid the circumstances of Eustacia’s life it was as wonderful as a dream could be.

That night seared itself into Eustacia’s mind so deeply she never truly shook it off, packed as it was with more vivid, unshakable happenings than most nights she’d ever lived through. She had a dream that night, one more extraordinary than nearly any dream ever dreamed by anyone, from the ancient king Nebuchadnezzar all the way down to the humble Swaffham tinker of folk lore. No girl living the kind of constrained, quiet life Eustacia led had ever had a dream so layered, disorienting, and pulse-pounding as this one. It held as many winding, tangled offshoots as the Cretan labyrinth, shifted and flickered as unpredictably as the northern lights, blazed with as much rich, bright hue as a formal June parterre, and teemed with as many distinct, bustling characters as a royal coronation. To someone like Scheherazade, who’d spun a thousand and one tales of magic and intrigue, the dream would have felt almost ordinary; to a young woman fresh off touring every royal court across Europe, it might have registered as little more than mildly diverting. But for Eustacia, bounded by the small, unchanging rhythms of her daily existence, it was as wondrous a dream as could ever exist for someone in her position.

It was the old crowd in which he found himself—the old crowd, with here and there a gap, and here and there a new face. The fellows were not bricklayers, but, as in the old days, they attended all Sunday picnics for the dancing, and the fighting, and the fun. Martin drank with them, and began to feel really human once more. He was a fool to have ever left them, he thought; and he was very certain that his sum of happiness would have been greater had he remained with them and let alone the books and the people who sat in the high places. Yet the beer seemed not so good as of yore. It didn’t taste as it used to taste. Brissenden had spoiled him for steam beer, he concluded, and wondered if, after all, the books had spoiled him for companionship with these friends of his youth. He resolved that he would not be so spoiled, and he went on to the dancing pavilion. Jimmy, the plumber, he met there, in the company of a tall, blond girl who promptly forsook him for Martin.

He found himself surrounded by the familiar old crew—faces he’d known for half his life, dotted with empty spots where old companions used to be, and the occasional unfamiliar newcomer. None of them were stonemasons, but just like they’d done back in their younger years, they showed up to every Sunday riverside picnic for the foot-stomping dancing, the scrappy backlot fights, and the rowdy, unplanned joy of it all. Leo drank round after round with them, and for the first time in months, he felt like a real, flesh-and-blood person instead of a ghost haunting library stacks. He’d been an idiot to ever walk away, he thought; he was certain his total share of happiness would have been far greater if he’d stayed right here, never touching the stuffy leather-bound books or simpering at the pretentious wealthy folks who spent their nights in gilded, high-up drawing rooms. Still, the cheap draft beer didn’t hit the way it used to. It was flat, strangely bitter, nothing like the crisp cold pints he remembered from years back. Elias had ruined him for low-grade bar beer, he concluded, and a cold little niggling thought wondered if all those books had ruined him for the company of the friends he’d grown up with. He shook the thought off, determined not to let that kind of distance take root, and wandered over to the open-air dance pavilion. He ran into Mickey the pipefitter there, accompanied by a lanky, ash-blonde girl who immediately left Mickey’s side to make her way straight over to Leo.

The noise at night would have been annoying to me ordinarily, but I didn't mind it in the present circumstances, because it kept me from hearing the quacks detaching legs and arms from the day's cripples. They ruined an uncommon good old cross-cut saw for me, and broke the saw-buck, too, but I let it pass. And as for my axe--well, I made up my mind that the next time I lent an axe to a surgeon I would pick my century.

Ordinarily, the racket after dark would have irritated me, but under the circumstances I could not say I objected to it, since it spared me the sound of the medicos unshipping legs and arms from the day’s cripples. They spoiled for me a remarkably fine old cross-cut saw, and broke the sawhorse into the bargain, though I let that go. As for my axe—well, I resolved that the next time I loaned one to a surgeon, I would choose my century first.

"You don't begin to realise in England what you are up against. You have no idea what it means to be in a country where everybody, the women, the elderly people, the steady middle-aged men, are taking war as seriously as business. They haven't the slightest compunction. I don't know what Germany was like before the war, I had hardly gotten out of my train before the war began; but Germany to-day is one big armed camp. It's all crawling with soldiers. And every soldier has his uniform and his boots and his arms and his kit.

You can’t even start to grasp in England the scale of what you’re facing. You’ve no notion what it’s like to live in a land where everyone—women, the old, the settled middle-aged—treats war with the same gravity as commerce. They feel not a shred of remorse. I can’t speak for Germany before the war, since I’d barely stepped off my train when it broke out; but the Germany of now is a single vast military encampment. The place is swarming with troops. And each soldier carries his uniform, his boots, his weapons, and his gear.

Our Arthur the king--”

Open-ended generation -the task of generating text that forms a natural continuation from the input text -requires the model to hallucinate text; hence the focus has been to ensure that the model learns to generate text that is more human-like (i.e., less repetitive or dull with more content-related words) (Holtzman et al., 2020; Welleck et al., 2020; See et al., 2019) . In contrast, tasks such as document summarization (Nenkova and McKeown, 2011; See et al., 2017; Paulus et al., 2018) and data-to-text generation (Lebret et al., 2016; Wiseman et al., 2017) which are not open-ended, require models to be factual and/or faithful to the source text.

metadata.title: On Faithfulness and Factuality in Abstractive Summarization

text: Open-ended generation—the task of producing text that constitutes a natural continuation of a given input—requires the model to generate content beyond what is explicitly provided; consequently, research has concentrated on enabling models to produce more human-like output, characterized by reduced repetitiveness, greater lexical diversity, and richer content words (Holtzman et al., 2020; Welleck et al., 2020; See et al., 2019). By contrast, tasks such as document summarization (Nenkova and McKeown, 2011; See et al., 2017; Paulus et al., 2018) and data-to-text generation (Lebret et al., 2016; Wiseman et al., 2017), which are not open-ended, demand that models remain factual and faithful to the source text.

He caught a Telegraph Avenue car that was going to Berkeley. It was crowded with youths and young men who were singing songs and ever and again barking out college yells. He studied them curiously. They were university boys. They went to the same university that she did, were in her class socially, could know her, could see her every day if they wanted to. He wondered that they did not want to, that they had been out having a good time instead of being with her that evening, talking with her, sitting around her in a worshipful and adoring circle. His thoughts wandered on. He noticed one with narrow-slitted eyes and a loose-lipped mouth. That fellow was vicious, he decided. On shipboard he would be a sneak, a whiner, a tattler. He, Martin Eden, was a better man than that fellow. The thought cheered him. It seemed to draw him nearer to Her. He began comparing himself with the students. He grew conscious of the muscled mechanism of his body and felt confident that he was physically their master. But their heads were filled with knowledge that enabled them to talk her talk,—the thought depressed him. But what was a brain for? he demanded passionately. What they had done, he could do. They had been studying about life from the books while he had been busy living life. His brain was just as full of knowledge as theirs, though it was a different kind of knowledge. How many of them could tie a lanyard knot, or take a wheel or a lookout? His life spread out before him in a series of pictures of danger and daring, hardship and toil. He remembered his failures and scrapes in the process of learning. He was that much to the good, anyway. Later on they would have to begin living life and going through the mill as he had gone. Very well. While they were busy with that, he could be learning the other side of life from the books.

He boarded a Shattuck Avenue tram bound for the Berkeley hills. The car brimmed with young men and boys, all of them chanting college anthems and now and then loosing sharp fraternity cries. He watched them with a detached, searching gaze. They were students of the university—her university. They moved in her social sphere, shared her classrooms, might speak with her, might glimpse her any ordinary day if the whim took them. It puzzled him that the whim did not take them, that they had chosen an evening’s carousal instead of her company, her conversation, the chance to gather round her in reverent, devoted attention. His mind drifted. He marked one with thin-slitted eyes and a mouth too slack at the corners. That one, he judged, was mean-hearted. On a ship he would prove a skulker, a complainer, a tale-bearer. He, Martin Eden, stood above that breed. The thought braced him. It seemed to lessen the distance between himself and Her. He fell to measuring his own worth against the students. He became aware of his body’s corded machinery and felt a sure pride that he could master any of them in physical things. Yet their heads held the learning that let them speak her language—the reflection weighed him down. But what, after all, was a brain meant for? he asked with sudden heat. Whatever they had done, he could do. They had been pulling life from printed pages while he had been wrestling life itself. His mind stored just as much knowledge as theirs, only of another grain. How many of them could whip a lanyard knot, or stand a wheel, or keep a lookout watch? His past unrolled before him like a moving panorama of risk and mettle, privation and labor. He recalled his stumbles and narrow escapes while he learned. That much, at least, he had gained. In time they would have to start living life and grinding through the mill as he had ground. So be it. While they were taken up with that, he could be mastering the other side of life from books.

Language Model BLEU scores often fail to reflect the fluency level of translated texts (Edunov et al., 2019) . Hence, we use the language model (LM) of the target language to regularize the crosslingual semantic similarity metrics, by coupling our cross-lingual similarity scores with a GPT language model of the target language (Radford et al., 2018) . We expect the language model to penalize translationese, i.e., unnatural word-by-word translations and boost the performance of our metrics. 5 judgments are based on comparing human references and system predictions. We will discuss this discrepancy in §5.3.

metadata.title: On the Limitations of Cross-lingual Encoders as Exposed by Reference-Free Machine Translation Evaluation

text: Language model BLEU scores often do not accurately capture the fluency of translated text (Edunov et al., 2019). Accordingly, we regularize the crosslingual semantic similarity metrics with a target-language language model (LM), coupling our cross-lingual similarity scores with a GPT language model for the target language (Radford et al., 2018). We expect this LM to penalize translationese, that is, unnatural word-for-word translations, and thereby improve the performance of our metrics. Five judgments are obtained by comparing human references with system predictions. We discuss this discrepancy in §5.3.

Thomasin took the child and ran. When she came to the covered car the horse, though fresh from the stable, was standing perfectly still, as if conscious of misfortune. She saw for the first time whose it was. She nearly fainted, and would have been unable to proceed another step but that the necessity of preserving the little girl from harm nerved her to an amazing self-control. In this agony of suspense she entered the house, put the baby in a place of safety, woke the lad and the female domestic, and ran out to give the alarm at the nearest cottage.

Thomasin seized the child and fled. When she reached the covered cart, the horse, though newly out of the stable, stood motionless, as if it too were aware of the disaster. For the first time she recognized whose vehicle it was. She nearly swooned, and would have been unable to take another step had not the urgent need to protect the little girl steeled her into extraordinary self-command. In this torment of suspense she entered the house, placed the baby somewhere safe, roused the boy and the maid, and then hurried out to give the alarm at the nearest cottage.

We use a simple method to select the target length for NAR generation at test time Li et al., 2019) , where we set the target length to be T = T + C, where C is a constant term estimated from the parallel data and T is the length of the source sentence. We then create a list of candidate target lengths ranging from [T − B, T + B] where B is the half-width of the interval. For example, if T = 5, C = 1 and we used a half-width of B = 2, then we would generate NAR translations of length [4, 5, 6, 7, 8] , for a total of 5 candidates. These translation candidates would then be ranked by the AR teacher to select the one with the highest probability. This is referred to as length-parallel decoding in Wei et al. (2019) .

metadata.title: Improving Non-autoregressive Neural Machine Translation with Monolingual Data

text: We adopt a simple procedure for selecting the target length for NAR generation at test time (Li et al., 2019), in which the target length is set to T = T + C, where C is a constant estimated from the parallel data and T denotes the source-sentence length. We then construct a set of candidate target lengths spanning [T − B, T + B], where B is the half-width of the interval. For instance, if T = 5, C = 1, and the half-width is set to B = 2, we would generate NAR translations with lengths [4, 5, 6, 7, 8], yielding five candidates in total. These translation candidates are then ranked by the AR teacher, and the candidate with the highest probability is selected. Wei et al. (2019) refer to this approach as length-parallel decoding.

metadata.title: Zero-Shot Transfer Learning with Synthesized Data for Multi-Domain Dialogue State Tracking

text: A pervasive bottleneck for task-oriented dialogue research stems from two core annotation-related limitations: not only is upfront data procurement prohibitively costly, but producing accurate dialogue annotations has proven uniquely challenging, with common pitfalls including annotator error, pipeline delays, inconsistent labeling conventions, and cross-utterance normalization issues (Zhou and Small, 2019). Figure 1 provides an example of a dialogue synthesized using our template framework, where 'U:' marks user utterances, 'A:' marks agent utterances, and 'S:' indicates the dialogue state recorded at each conversational turn. Even widely adopted benchmark datasets such as MultiWOZ exhibit notable annotation inconsistencies, despite being constructed through multiple iterative rounds of expert annotation (Budzianowski et al., 2018; Zhou and Small, 2019).

Thus strangely are our souls constructed, and by such slight ligaments are we bound to prosperity or ruin. When I look back, it seems to me as if this almost miraculous change of inclination and will was the immediate suggestion of the guardian angel of my life—the last effort made by the spirit of preservation to avert the storm that was even then hanging in the stars and ready to envelop me. Her victory was announced by an unusual tranquillity and gladness of soul which followed the relinquishing of my ancient and latterly tormenting studies. It was thus that I was to be taught to associate evil with their prosecution, happiness with their disregard.

How strangely our souls are fashioned, and by what frail threads we are tethered to fortune or disaster. Looking back, I feel as though this nearly wondrous shift in desire and resolve came as a direct whisper from the guardian spirit of my existence—a final effort by the preserving force to turn aside the tempest that already loomed in the constellations, poised to swallow me. Its triumph was heralded by a rare stillness and lightness of heart that arrived the moment I abandoned my long-accursed studies. So I was schooled to link misery with their pursuit, and joy with their release.

Martin recollected his blank-verse tragedy, and sent it instead. He read it over before mailing, and was particularly impressed by its sophomoric amateurishness and general worthlessness. But he sent it; and it was published, to the everlasting regret of the editor. The public was indignant and incredulous. It was too far a cry from Martin Eden’s high standard to that serious bosh. It was asserted that he had never written it, that the magazine had faked it very clumsily, or that Martin Eden was emulating the elder Dumas and at the height of success was hiring his writing done for him. But when he explained that the tragedy was an early effort of his literary childhood, and that the magazine had refused to be happy unless it got it, a great laugh went up at the magazine’s expense and a change in the editorship followed. The tragedy was never brought out in book-form, though Martin pocketed the advance royalties that had been paid.

Martin remembered his blank-verse tragedy and sent that instead. Before mailing it, he read it over, and was struck especially by its awkward, boyish amateurishness and its all-around lack of merit. Nevertheless, he sent it off; and it was published, much to the editor’s lasting regret. The public responded with anger and disbelief. It was too great a departure from Martin Eden’s lofty reputation to be credited to that solemn nonsense. People said he had never written it at all, that the magazine had forged it in a very clumsy way, or that Martin Eden, like the elder Dumas, was so successful he had others do his writing for him. But when he explained that the tragedy had been an early work from his literary childhood, and that the magazine had not been content until it had secured it, there was a loud laugh at the magazine’s expense and a change in editors followed. The tragedy was never issued in book form, though Martin kept the advance royalties that had already been paid.

He waited at the bottom of a steep hill till a stage-coach came up, and then begged of the outside passengers; but there were very few who took any notice of him: and even those told him to wait till they got to the top of the hill, and then let them see how far he could run for a halfpenny. Poor Oliver tried to keep up with the coach a little way, but was unable to do it, by reason of his fatigue and sore feet. When the outsides saw this, they put their halfpence back into their pockets again, declaring that he was an idle young dog, and didn’t deserve anything; and the coach rattled away and left only a cloud of dust behind.

He lingered at the foot of a sharp incline until a mail-coach approached, and then entreated the travelers perched on its roof; yet scarcely any regarded him at all, and those who did bade him bide until they reached the crest, and then show them what distance he could dash for a farthing. Wretched Oliver strove to match the coach’s pace a short way, but could not sustain it, owing to his weariness and blistered feet. When the roof-riders observed this, they slipped their farthings back into their purses, pronouncing him a worthless young scamp who merited no charity; and the coach clattered onward, abandoning nothing but a swirl of dust behind.

The members of this board were very sage, deep, philosophical men; and when they came to turn their attention to the workhouse, they found out at once, what ordinary folks would never have discovered—the poor people liked it! It was a regular place of public entertainment for the poorer classes; a tavern where there was nothing to pay; a public breakfast, dinner, tea, and supper all the year round; a brick and mortar elysium, where it was all play and no work. “Oho!” said the board, looking very knowing; “we are the fellows to set this to rights; we’ll stop it all, in no time.” So, they established the rule, that all poor people should have the alternative (for they would compel nobody, not they), of being starved by a gradual process in the house, or by a quick one out of it. With this view, they contracted with the water-works to lay on an unlimited supply of water; and with a corn-factory to supply periodically small quantities of oatmeal; and issued three meals of thin gruel a day, with an onion twice a week, and half a roll of Sundays. They made a great many other wise and humane regulations, having reference to the ladies, which it is not necessary to repeat; kindly undertook to divorce poor married people, in consequence of the great expense of a suit in Doctors’ Commons; and, instead of compelling a man to support his family, as they had theretofore done, took his family away from him, and made him a bachelor! There is no saying how many applicants for relief, under these last two heads, might have started up in all classes of society, if it had not been coupled with the workhouse; but the board were long-headed men, and had provided for this difficulty. The relief was inseparable from the workhouse and the gruel; and that frightened people.

The members of this board were very wise, far-seeing, philosophical men; and when they turned their attention to the workhouse, they immediately discovered what ordinary people would never have guessed—the poor actually liked it! It had become a sort of public amusement for the lower classes; a tavern where nothing had to be paid; a public breakfast, dinner, tea, and supper all year long; a brick-and-mortar paradise, where there was nothing but leisure and no labor at all. “Ha!” said the board, with a knowing air; “we are the people to put this right; we’ll put a stop to it in no time.” So they laid down the rule that every poor person should be given the choice (for they would force no one, certainly not they) of being slowly starved inside the house, or quickly starved outside it. With this in mind, they made a contract with the waterworks to provide an unlimited supply of water; and with a corn mill to furnish, at intervals, tiny amounts of oatmeal; and they served three meals a day of weak gruel, with an onion twice a week and half a roll on Sundays. They also introduced many other sensible and benevolent rules, concerning the women, which it is not necessary to repeat; graciously undertook to separate poor married couples, on account of the enormous cost of a suit in Doctors’ Commons; and, instead of making a man keep his family, as had previously been done, took his family away from him and turned him into a bachelor! It is impossible to say how many claimants for relief under these last two arrangements might have appeared in every rank of society, had it not been linked to the workhouse; but the board were shrewd men and had foreseen this problem. Relief could not be had apart from the workhouse and the gruel; and that terrified people.

Step 2: Expert Annotation. Given the aligned groups of sentences in professional and consumer MSD, we develop an annotation platform to facilitate expert annotations. We hire three doctors to select sentences from each version of group to annotate pairs of sentences that have the same meaning but are written in different styles. The hired doctors are formally medically trained, and are qualified to understand the semantics of the medical texts. To avoid subjective judgments in the annotations, they are not allowed to change the content. Particularly, the doctors are Chinese who also know English as a second language. Thus, we provide the English content accompanied with a Chinese translation as assistance, which helps to increase the annotation speed while ensuring quality. We also conduct verification on each pair of parallel sentences with the help of another doctor. Note that each pairing may contain multiple professional and consumer sentences; i.e., multiple alignment is possible, the alignments are not necessarily oneto-one. The strict procedure also discards many aligned groups, leading to 675 annotations for testing, with distribution of medical PCIO elements as shown in Figure 3 . Step 3: Knowledge Incorporation. To facilitate knowledge-aware analysis, we can utilize information extraction techniques (Cao et al., 2018a (Cao et al., , 2019 to identify medical concepts in each sentence. Here, we use QuickUMLS (Soldaini and Goharian, 2016) to automatically link entity mentions to Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) (Bodenreider, 2004) . Note that each mention may refer to multiple concepts, each for which we align to the highest ranked one. As shown in Table 2 : Statistics of MSD and SimpWiki. One annotation may contain multiple sentences, and MSD Train has no parallel annotations due to expensive expert cost. The ratio of layman to expert according to each metric denotes the gap between the two styles, and a higher value implies smaller differences except that for #Sentence.

metadata.title: Expertise Style Transfer: A New Task Towards Better Communication between Experts and Laymen

text: Step 2: Expert Annotation. Utilizing the aligned sentence groups from the professional and consumer versions of the MSD, we developed a specialized platform to facilitate expert annotation. Three medically trained physicians were tasked with selecting sentence pairs from these groups that share identical semantic meaning but differ in style. These experts possess formal medical training, qualifying them to accurately interpret medical semantics. To prevent subjective bias, annotators were not permitted to modify the text. Since the doctors are native Chinese speakers with English as a second language, we provided the English text alongside Chinese translations to improve annotation speed while maintaining high quality. Furthermore, an additional physician verified each pair of parallel sentences. Notably, these alignments are not restricted to one-to-one mappings; a single pairing may involve multiple professional or consumer sentences. This rigorous selection process led to the exclusion of many aligned groups, resulting in a final set of 675 test annotations, the medical PCIO element distribution of which is illustrated in Figure 3. Step 3: Knowledge Incorporation. To support knowledge-aware analysis, we utilize information extraction methods (Cao et al., 2018a, 2019) to identify medical concepts in each sentence. Specifically, we employ QuickUMLS (Soldaini and Goharian, 2016) to automatically link entity mentions to the Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) (Bodenreider, 2004). For mentions referring to multiple concepts, we align them with the highest-ranked candidate. As indicated in the statistics for MSD and SimpWiki in Table 2, an individual annotation may comprise several sentences. Due to the high cost of expert labor, the MSD training set does not include parallel annotations. The layman-to-expert ratio for each metric represents the stylistic gap, where higher values indicate smaller differences, except in the case of sentence count (#Sentence).

“They are,” Mr. Lorry whispered the words, glancing fearfully round at the locked room, “murdering the prisoners. If you are sure of what you say; if you really have the power you think you have--as I believe you have--make yourself known to these devils, and get taken to La Force. It may be too late, I don’t know, but let it not be a minute later!”

“They are,” Mr. Lorry breathed, casting a terrified look about the sealed chamber, “slaughtering the captives. If you are certain of your claim; if you truly possess the influence you imagine—and I trust you do—reveal yourself to these fiends, and arrange to be carried to La Force. It might already be too late, I cannot tell, but do not let another moment slip!”

"Meanwhile I've got something to tell you. I've been through a fight, a big fight, and I haven't got a scratch. I've taken two prisoners with my lily hand. Men were shot close to me. I didn't mind that a bit. It was as exciting as one of those bitter fights we used to have round the hockey goal. I didn't mind anything till afterwards. Then when I was in the trench in the evening I trod on something slippery--pah! And after it was all over one of my chums got it--sort of unfairly. And I keep on thinking of those two things so much that all the early part is just dreamlike. It's more like something I've read in a book, or seen in the _Illustrated London News_ than actually been through. One had been thinking so often, how will it feel? how shall I behave? that when it came it had an effect of being flat and ordinary.

In the meantime, I have news for you. I’ve survived a major engagement, a real battle, and I haven't sustained a single scratch. I even managed to capture two prisoners with my own bare hands. Men were being cut down right beside me, but I didn't find it troubling at all. It was every bit as exhilarating as those fierce contests we used to have around the hockey goal. I wasn't bothered by anything until later. But that evening in the trench, I stepped on something slick—the memory still revolts me. And then, after the worst was over, one of my mates was killed—it seemed so senseless, so unjust. I find myself dwelling on those two moments so much that the earlier combat feels like a dream. It feels more like a story I’ve read or a sketch in the *Illustrated London News* than something I actually experienced. I had wondered so often how it would feel and how I would hold up that, when it finally happened, the whole thing felt strangely flat and ordinary.

There are also some proposals to evaluate the quality of translations with the help of extrinsic proxy tasks. Berka et al. (2011) collected short English documents from various domains and created yes and no questions in Czech. They then translated the English documents into Czech and evaluated the quality of the MT systems based on human performances on the documents and questions in Czech. Scarton and Specia (2016) translated a dataset of German reading comprehension tests into English with various MT systems such as Google Translate and Bing Translate and judged the quality of translations based on human performances on the translated reading comprehension datasets. Unfortunately, these external tasks suffer from the same scalability and consistency issues as manual evaluation.

metadata.title: CLIReval: Evaluating Machine Translation as a Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval Task

text: Alternative approaches suggest assessing translation quality through the use of extrinsic proxy tasks. For instance, Berka et al. (2011) compiled brief English texts spanning multiple domains and formulated corresponding yes/no questions in Czech. After translating the source documents into Czech, they gauged machine translation (MT) system performance by measuring how effectively human participants could answer the questions using the translated material. Similarly, Scarton and Specia (2016) utilized various MT engines, including Google Translate and Bing Translate, to render German reading comprehension assessments into English, determining translation quality according to human scores on these translated tests. Regrettably, these extrinsic methodologies are plagued by the same limitations regarding scalability and consistency that characterize direct manual evaluation.
