Oliver puzzled over how a game of pickpocketing could possibly lead to a future of greatness. Yet, assuming the older man must know best given his years, he followed him silently to the table and was soon fully immersed in his peculiar new education.

The lonely inn was not yet closed, though scarcely an individual was there, the business done being chiefly with travellers who passed the inn on long journeys, and these had now gone on their way. Venn went to the public room, called for a mug of ale, and inquired of the maid in an indifferent tone if Mr. Wildeve was at home.

The solitary inn was still open, though there was hardly a soul within, its trade having been mostly with wayfarers on distant journeys, and these had now continued on their road. Venn entered the common room, ordered a mug of ale, and, with an air of carelessness, asked the maid whether Mr. Harlowe was at home.

France, less favoured on the whole as to matters spiritual than her sister of the shield and trident, rolled with exceeding smoothness down hill, making paper money and spending it. Under the guidance of her Christian pastors, she entertained herself, besides, with such humane achievements as sentencing a youth to have his hands cut off, his tongue torn out with pincers, and his body burned alive, because he had not kneeled down in the rain to do honour to a dirty procession of monks which passed within his view, at a distance of some fifty or sixty yards. It is likely enough that, rooted in the woods of France and Norway, there were growing trees, when that sufferer was put to death, already marked by the Woodman, Fate, to come down and be sawn into boards, to make a certain movable framework with a sack and a knife in it, terrible in history. It is likely enough that in the rough outhouses of some tillers of the heavy lands adjacent to Paris, there were sheltered from the weather that very day, rude carts, bespattered with rustic mire, snuffed about by pigs, and roosted in by poultry, which the Farmer, Death, had already set apart to be his tumbrils of the Revolution. But that Woodman and that Farmer, though they work unceasingly, work silently, and no one heard them as they went about with muffled tread: the rather, forasmuch as to entertain any suspicion that they were awake, was to be atheistical and traitorous.

France, not quite as blessed in spiritual matters as her naval and military counterpart, coasted downhill with remarkable ease, churning out paper currency and squandering it. Under the direction of her Christian shepherds, she also amused herself with such compassionate deeds as condemning a young man to have his hands severed, his tongue ripped out with pincers, and his body consumed by flames, all because he failed to kneel in the rain to pay respect to a grimy parade of monks that crossed his line of sight, some fifty or sixty yards away. It is quite possible that, deep in the forests of France and Norway, trees stood already marked by the Woodman, Fate, to be felled and cut into planks for constructing a certain portable device with a sack and a blade, notorious in history, at the very moment that victim was executed. It is quite possible that in the crude sheds of farmers working the heavy soil near Paris, there rested that same day rough wagons, splattered with country filth, sniffed at by swine, and used as roosts by chickens, which the Farmer, Death, had already designated to serve as his revolutionary tumbrils. But that Woodman and that Farmer, though they labor endlessly, labor in silence, and nobody caught the sound of their soft-footed work—especially since entertaining any notion that they were stirring was considered both godless and treasonous.

The two went round and ascended the crooked stair in darkness till Clym’s sitting-room on the upper floor was reached, where he lit a candle, Charley entering gently behind. Yeobright searched his desk, and taking out a sheet of tissue-paper unfolded from it two or three undulating locks of raven hair, which fell over the paper like black streams. From these he selected one, wrapped it up, and gave it to the lad, whose eyes had filled with tears. He kissed the packet, put it in his pocket, and said in a voice of emotion, “O, Mr. Clym, how good you are to me!”

The pair circled around and climbed the wobbly winding staircase in total darkness until they reached Clym’s sitting room on the upper floor, where he lit a candle while Charley stepped in gently behind him. Yeobright rummaged through his desk, pulled out a sheet of tissue paper, and unfolded it to reveal two or three wavy locks of jet-black hair that spilled across the paper like dark trickling streams. He picked one lock from the group, wrapped it up neatly, and handed it to the boy, whose eyes had already welled up with tears. Charley kissed the small wrapped packet, tucked it into his pocket, and said in a thick, emotional voice, “Oh, Mr. Clym, how good you are to me!”

Mr. Britling, from his praise of the enterprise that had at last brought a car he could drive within his reach, went on to that favourite topic of all intelligent Englishmen, the adverse criticism of things British. He pointed out that the central position of the brake and gear levers in his automobile made it extremely easy for the American manufacturer to turn it out either as a left-handed or a right-handed car, and so adapt it either to the Continental or to the British rule of the road. No English cars were so adaptable. We British suffered much from our insular rule of the road, just as we suffered much from our insular weights and measures. But we took a perverse pride in such disadvantages. The irruption of American cars into England was a recent phenomenon, it was another triumph for the tremendous organising ability of the American mind. They were doing with the automobile what they had done with clocks and watches and rifles, they had standardised and machined wholesale, while the British were still making the things one by one. It was an extraordinary thing that England, which was the originator of the industrial system and the original developer of the division of labour, should have so fallen away from systematic manufacturing. He believed this was largely due to the influence of Oxford and the Established Church....

Having finished his eulogy for the mechanical foresight that had finally put a motor within his grasp, Mr. Sterling drifted toward that most cherished preoccupation of the educated Briton: the systematic condemnation of his own country. He observed that the central arrangement of the levers allowed the Yankee firm to assemble the machine for either side of the highway with effortless indifference, catering with equal ease to the habits of the Continent or the eccentricities of the Isles. No home-grown carriage offered such flexibility. The English public, he argued, were victims of their own parochial traffic laws and antiquated measurements, yet they clung to these burdens with a stubborn, almost masochistic, satisfaction. 

The sudden flood of American imports was a modern marvel, another victory for the vast, coordinating intellect of the States. They were applying to the engine the same principles of uniformity they had once imposed upon the clock and the musket, achieving a wholesale precision while the British artisan still labored over individual parts. It remained a profound paradox that the very nation which had birthed the steam age and the specialization of toil should have retreated so far from the path of industrial order. This decline, he maintained, was the inevitable consequence of the classical air of the universities and the stagnant weight of the Anglican pulpit.

Before that unjust Tribunal, there was little or no order of procedure, ensuring to any accused person any reasonable hearing. There could have been no such Revolution, if all laws, forms, and ceremonies, had not first been so monstrously abused, that the suicidal vengeance of the Revolution was to scatter them all to the winds.

Before that partisan Revolutionary Court, procedural safeguards were all but abandoned, with no accused person afforded so much as a basic, reasoned hearing. Such a searing, all-consuming uprising could never have come to pass, had every preexisting law, official custom, and state ceremony not first been so monstrously perverted that the revolution’s own suicidal, retributive fury scattered every last trace of them to the winds.

When I had concluded my narration, I said, “This is the being whom I accuse and for whose seizure and punishment I call upon you to exert your whole power. It is your duty as a magistrate, and I believe and hope that your feelings as a man will not revolt from the execution of those functions on this occasion.”

Once I’d reached the end of my account, I leaned forward slightly and spoke sharply, “This is the creature I hold accountable for every atrocity I’ve just recounted, and I demand you marshal every resource at your disposal to seize it and see it punished. You are bound to this by your oath as a justice, and I trust, even hope, that your own moral compass as a private person will not rebel against fulfilling that office where this matter is concerned.”

We perform one-to-many (English-X) and manyto-many (English-X ∪ X-English) translation on OPUS-100 (|T | is 100). We apply byte pair encoding (BPE) (Sennrich et al., 2016b; Kudo and Richardson, 2018) to handle multilingual words with a joint vocabulary size of 64k. We randomly shuffle the training set to mix instances of different language pairs. We adopt BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) for translation evaluation with the toolkit SacreBLEU (Post, 2018) 8 . We employ the langdetect library 9 to detect the language of translations, and measure the translation-language accuracy for zero-shot cases. Rather than providing numbers for each language pair, we report average BLEU over all 94 language pairs with test sets (BLEU 94 ). We also show the win ratio (WR), counting the proportion where our approach outperforms its baseline.

metadata.title: Improving Massively Multilingual Neural Machine Translation and Zero-Shot Translation

text: We conduct one-to-many (English-X) and many-to-many (English-X ∪ X-English) translation on OPUS-100 (|T| = 100). We use byte pair encoding (BPE) (Sennrich et al., 2016b; Kudo and Richardson, 2018) to process multilingual words, employing a shared vocabulary of 64k tokens. The training set is randomly shuffled to intermix instances from different language pairs. Translation quality is evaluated with BLEU (Papineni et al., 2002) via the SacreBLEU toolkit (Post, 2018)⁸. We apply the langdetect library⁹ to identify the language of translations and assess translation-language accuracy for zero-shot settings. Instead of reporting per-pair scores, we present the average BLEU across all 94 language pairs for which test sets exist (BLEU₉₄). We also report the win ratio (WR), defined as the proportion of cases where our method surpasses its baseline.

"You were so good as to ask me, Mr. Britling, a little while ago, what my first impression of England was. Well, Mr. Britling, my first impression of England that seems to me to matter in the least is this: that it looks and feels more like the traditional Old England than any one could possibly have believed, and that in reality it is less like the traditional Old England than any one would ever possibly have imagined."

A little while ago, Mr. Britling, you were kind enough to ask me what my first impression of England was. Well, Mr. Britling, the only first impression of the country that feels even remotely worth mentioning is this: it looks and feels far more like that iconic traditional Old England than anyone could ever have believed, but in reality, it’s less like that long-held vision of Old England than anyone would possibly be able to imagine.

“O, that’s no inconvenience,” said Clym, smiling rather sadly. And then the party drove off and vanished in the night shades, and Yeobright entered the house. The ticking of the clock was the only sound that greeted him, for not a soul remained; Christian, who acted as cook, valet, and gardener to Clym, sleeping at his father’s house. Yeobright sat down in one of the vacant chairs, and remained in thought a long time. His mother’s old chair was opposite; it had been sat in that evening by those who had scarcely remembered that it ever was hers. But to Clym she was almost a presence there, now as always. Whatever she was in other people’s memories, in his she was the sublime saint whose radiance even his tenderness for Eustacia could not obscure. But his heart was heavy, that Mother had _not_ crowned him in the day of his espousals and in the day of the gladness of his heart. And events had borne out the accuracy of her judgment, and proved the devotedness of her care. He should have heeded her for Eustacia’s sake even more than for his own. “It was all my fault,” he whispered. “O, my mother, my mother! would to God that I could live my life again, and endure for you what you endured for me!”

“Oh, that is of no consequence,” said Adrian, with a smile that was more sorrowful than gay. Then the little party set off, and was soon swallowed by the darkness; and Valen crossed the threshold and went in. The house was met only by the measured ticking of the clock, for no living creature remained within. Mercer, who served him as cook, manservant, and gardener, had gone to sleep at his father’s house. Valen sank into one of the empty chairs and sat there long, lost in thought. Opposite him stood his mother’s old chair; that very evening others had occupied it, and scarcely remembered that it had once belonged to her. Yet to Valen she was almost a visible presence in that room, as she had been always. Whatever place she held in other men’s recollection, in his mind she was the lofty saint whose brightness even his love for Lena could not quite eclipse. Still his heart was oppressed because his mother had not crowned him on the day of his marriage, on the day that should have been the gladness of his soul. And all that had happened since had justified her discernment and shown the depth of her devotion. He ought to have listened to her, for Lena’s sake no less than his own. “It was all my doing,” he murmured. “Oh, my mother, my mother! if only I might live my life once more, and bear for you what you bore for me!”

The night was bitter cold. The snow lay on the ground, frozen into a hard thick crust, so that only the heaps that had drifted into byways and corners were affected by the sharp wind that howled abroad: which, as if expending increased fury on such prey as it found, caught it savagely up in clouds, and, whirling it into a thousand misty eddies, scattered it in air. Bleak, dark, and piercing cold, it was a night for the well-housed and fed to draw round the bright fire and thank God they were at home; and for the homeless, starving wretch to lay him down and die. Many hunger-worn outcasts close their eyes in our bare streets, at such times, who, let their crimes have been what they may, can hardly open them in a more bitter world.

The evening was punishingly cold. A thick, frozen crust had formed over the snow, so that only the loose mounds gathered in corners and side streets were vulnerable to the shrieking wind. As if venting its fury on whatever prey it could find, the gale snatched up these drifts, spinning them into a thousand misty spirals before scattering them into the dark air. It was a bleak, piercing night that drove the fortunate to gather by a warm fire and give thanks for their sanctuary, while the starving and homeless were left with no choice but to lie down and die. Many exhausted outcasts close their eyes on the desolate streets at such times; regardless of their past sins, they are unlikely to wake in a world more cruel than the one they have left behind.

: SyntaxGym allows linguists to easily design and run controlled experiments on the syntactic knowledge of language models, and allows NLP experts to test their own models against these standards. Users submit targeted syntactic evaluation experiments to the site, and they are automatically evaluated on language models available in the Gym. SyntaxGym analyzes and visualizes these evaluation results. deploy large-scale neural network language models, and b) the linguistic knowledge of language scientists necessary to design controlled, theoretically interesting psycholinguistic experiments.

metadata.title: SyntaxGym: An Online Platform for Targeted Evaluation of Language Models

text: SyntaxGym facilitates the seamless construction and execution of controlled experiments regarding the syntactic proficiency of language models for linguists, while simultaneously enabling NLP researchers to benchmark their models against established standards. Upon submitting targeted syntactic evaluation suites to the platform, users receive automated assessments based on the language models integrated within the Gym. The platform further processes and graphically represents these evaluative outcomes. It serves to bridge the technical requirements of (a) deploying large-scale neural network language models with (b) the specialized linguistic expertise of researchers necessary for designing rigorous, theoretically substantive psycholinguistic experiments.

The skewness, noisiness and insensitivity of the self-reported Likert scale rating make it a suboptimal dialog evaluation metric. In practice, we find that directly training a classifier (even for pre-trained BERT-based model) on the noisy selfreported Likert scale ratings suffers from underfitting. One of the Alexa Price Challenge team, Alana (Papaioannou et al., 2017 ) train a binaryclassifier between successful dialogs (human rating 4 or 5) and unsuccessful dialogs (rating 1 or 2) with heavy hand-engineered features. They reach 69.40% accuracy on this binary classification problem, which is far from usable in real-world settings.

metadata.title: Beyond User Self-Reported Likert Scale Ratings: A Comparison Model for Automatic Dialog Evaluation

text: The skewness, noise, and insensitivity of self-reported Likert scale ratings make them a suboptimal metric for dialog evaluation. In practice, we find that directly training a classifier—even a pre-trained BERT-based model—on noisy self-reported Likert scale ratings leads to underfitting. One of the Alexa Prize Challenge teams, Alana (Papaioannou et al., 2017), trained a binary classifier to distinguish successful dialogs (human ratings of 4 or 5) from unsuccessful dialogs (ratings of 1 or 2) using heavily hand-engineered features. They achieved 69.40% accuracy on this binary classification task, which is far from usable in real-world settings.

One line of research focuses on instance weighting, which assigns domain related weights to different samples during training. For example, Wang et al. (2017) consider sentence weighting and domain weighting for NMT. The sentence weight is determined by the bilingual cross-entropy of each sentence pair based on the language model of each domain. The domain weight can be modified by changing the number of sentences from that domain in a mini-batch. propose a cost weighting method, where the weight of each pair of sentences is evaluated by the output probability of a domain classifier on the encoder embedding. propose a dynamic training method to adjust the sentence selection and weighting during training. We remark that many of these methods are complementary to our proposed model, and can be applied to improve the training of our model.

metadata.title: Multi-Domain Neural Machine Translation with Word-Level Adaptive Layer-wise Domain Mixing

text: A prominent strand of existing research centers on instance weighting, a technique that assigns domain-specific weights to distinct samples during model training. For example, Wang et al. (2017) integrate both sentence-level weighting and domain-level weighting for NMT tasks. Their sentence-level weight is computed from the bilingual cross-entropy of each sentence pair, calculated against the language model associated with each respective domain. Domain-level weights, in turn, can be adjusted by altering the count of sentences drawn from a given domain within each training mini-batch. Other work proposes a cost weighting method, where the weight assigned to each sentence pair is measured via the output probability of a domain classifier applied to encoder embeddings. Additional studies introduce a dynamic training approach to modulate both sentence selection and weight assignment over the course of training. We note that many of these existing methods are complementary to the model we propose, and can be deployed to improve the training performance of our framework.

"And," said Mr. Britling, in very much the tones in which a preacher might say "Sixthly," "it is just all this Northern tendency that this world struggle is going to release. This war is pounding through Europe, smashing up homes, dispersing and mixing homes, setting Madame Van der Pant playing hockey, and André climbing trees with my young ruffians; it is killing young men by the million, altering the proportions of the sexes for a generation, bringing women into business and office and industry, destroying the accumulated wealth that kept so many of them in refined idleness, flooding the world with strange doubts and novel ideas...."

And," Mr. Britling continued, adopting the exact cadence a clergyman might use for his sixth sermon point, "it is exactly this Northern inclination that this global upheaval is set to liberate. This conflict is battering its way through Europe, shattering domestic lives, scattering and mingling households, leading Madame Van der Pant to take up hockey and sending André to scale trees with my young hoodlums; it is annihilating young men by the millions, warping the ratio of the sexes for an entire generation, drawing women into the spheres of trade and industry, liquidating the generational wealth that sustained so many in their elegant lethargy, and saturating the earth with peculiar misgivings and radical thoughts.

I was gradually coming to have a mysterious and shuddery reverence for this girl; nowadays whenever she pulled out from the station and got her train fairly started on one of those horizonless transcontinental sentences of hers, it was borne in upon me that I was standing in the awful presence of the Mother of the German Language. I was so impressed with this, that sometimes when she began to empty one of these sentences on me I unconsciously took the very attitude of reverence, and stood uncovered; and if words had been water, I had been drowned, sure. She had exactly the German way; whatever was in her mind to be delivered, whether a mere remark, or a sermon, or a cyclopedia, or the history of a war, she would get it into a single sentence or die. Whenever the literary German dives into a sentence, that is the last you are going to see of him till he emerges on the other side of his Atlantic with his verb in his mouth.

Little by little, I’d developed an uncanny, awestruck regard for this young woman. These days, the second she pulled out of the station and got her verbal train chugging full speed down the tracks of one of those endless, cross-continental sentences she favored, it struck me with full force that I stood in the dread, hallowed presence of the very Mother of the German language. I was so thoroughly struck by the thought that sometimes when she started pouring one of these sprawling sentences out in my direction, I slipped unthinkingly into a pose of deep reverence, baring my head without even noticing; and if her words had been water, I would have drowned for certain, no question about it. She had that quintessentially German knack for speech: no matter what she intended to say, whether it was a passing offhand comment, a full sermon, a whole encyclopedia entry, or a complete war history, she would cram the whole thing into one single sentence or perish trying. When a literary German speaker dives headfirst into a sentence, that’s the last you’ll catch sight of him until he pops up on the far side of his personal Atlantic Ocean, his verb clamped firmly between his teeth.

“And do you dream?” said the dæmon. “Do you think that I was then dead to agony and remorse? He,” he continued, pointing to the corpse, “he suffered not in the consummation of the deed. Oh! Not the ten-thousandth portion of the anguish that was mine during the lingering detail of its execution. A frightful selfishness hurried me on, while my heart was poisoned with remorse. Think you that the groans of Clerval were music to my ears? My heart was fashioned to be susceptible of love and sympathy, and when wrenched by misery to vice and hatred, it did not endure the violence of the change without torture such as you cannot even imagine.

“And do you imagine,” the creature asked, “that I was then numb to pain and regret? He,” he continued, gesturing toward the corpse, “did not suffer in the final completion of the act—not a fraction of the agony that was mine during the slow, grueling process of its fulfillment. A ghastly selfishness drove me onward, even while my soul was poisoned by remorse. Do you truly think that Clerval’s cries were like music to my ears? My heart was designed for love and empathy, and when it was twisted by wretchedness into malice and hate, it did not endure that transformation without a level of torture you cannot even conceive.”

Madame Defarge poured it out for him, turned back to her knitting, and hummed a soft little tune as she worked her needles.

As though seized by a flash of foresight, Bathsheba choked out, “No, it isn’t true; it can’t be true!” After that, she neither spoke nor perceived anything more. The frozen composure that had lately formed around her shattered, and a flood of emotion broke loose and swept her away. A blackness clouded her vision, and she dropped to the ground.

Well," he murmured, returning to her side after shutting the gate with deliberate care. "What I truly wished for was a chance to mention something that might matter to you—if by chance it does matter to you.... I expect I should state it as plainly as I can.... In plain truth.... I'm utterly and completely in love with you.... I felt I wanted you to know....

Because a plain blunt man, who has never been taught concealment, speaks out his mind without exactly intending it, he’s to be snapped off like the son of a sinner.”

“Well,” Troy continued, “I’ll admit there’s such a thing as praise that lands as rudeness, and I’ll own that might be my misstep here. All the same, there’s a kind of treatment that’s pure injustice, and that’s what you’re guilty of right now. Just because a plain, blunt man who never learned to mask his thoughts speaks his mind without meaning any offense, he’s to be sharply cut off and dressed down like he’s no better than a good-for-nothing scoundrel?”

Though Wildeve’s fevered feeling had not been elaborated to real poetical compass, it was of the standard sort. His might have been called the Rousseau of Egdon.

To long for what was hard to reach, and to grow tired of what lay ready to hand; to prize the distant and turn from the immediate—such had always been Wildeve’s nature. This is the true sign of the man of sentiment. Though Wildeve’s heated emotion had never developed into anything of real poetic range, it was of the familiar kind. He might have been called the Rousseau of Egdon.

The people directly behind Madame Defarge, explaining the reason for her satisfaction to those behind them, and those in turn to others, and so on, soon set the neighbouring streets ringing with applause. He felt that he must see the sea. He would follow the road beyond the Rodwell villages, and then turn up to the crest of Eastonbury Hill. And thither he went and saw in the gap of the low hills beyond a V-shaped level of moonlit water that glittered and yet lay still. He stopped his car by the roadside, and sat for a long time looking at this and musing. And once it seemed to him three little shapes like short black needles passed in line ahead across the molten silver.

He sensed an urgent need to look upon the ocean. He decided to drive past the Rodwell hamlets, then climb toward the summit of Eastonbury Hill. There he arrived and gazed through a cleft in the lower hills at a wedge of moonlit sea, shimmering yet motionless. He pulled his car to the verge and remained seated for a great while, watching the water and lost in thought. At one moment, he thought he saw three tiny forms, like stubby dark needles, gliding in single file across the liquid silver.

Too long had British life been corrupted by the fictions of loyalty to an uninspiring and alien Court, of national piety in an official Church, of freedom in a politician-rigged State, of justice in an economic system where the advertiser, the sweater and usurer had a hundred advantages over the producer and artisan, to maintain itself now steadily at any high pitch of heroic endeavour. It had bought its comfort with the demoralisation of its servants. It had no completely honest organs; its spirit was clogged by its accumulated insincerities. Brought at last face to face with a bitter hostility and a powerful and unscrupulous enemy, an enemy socialistic, scientific and efficient to an unexampled degree, it seemed indeed to be inspired for a time by an unwonted energy and unanimity. Youth and the common people shone. The sons of every class went out to fight and die, full of a splendid dream of this war. Easy-going vanished from the foreground of the picture. But only to creep back again as the first inspiration passed. Presently the older men, the seasoned politicians, the owners and hucksters, the charming women and the habitual consumers, began to recover from this blaze of moral exaltation. Old habits of mind and procedure reasserted themselves. The war which had begun so dramatically missed its climax; there was neither heroic swift defeat nor heroic swift victory. There was indecision; the most trying test of all for an undisciplined people. There were great spaces of uneventful fatigue. Before the Battle of the Yser had fully developed the dramatic quality had gone out of the war. It had ceased to be either a tragedy or a triumph; for both sides it became a monstrous strain and wasting. It had become a wearisome thrusting against a pressure of evils....

For too long, the British character had been hollowed out by myths: a feigned devotion to a distant, unmoving monarchy, a hollow religiosity within the state church, and a charade of liberty in a government controlled by party hacks. Economic justice was a farce in a system where the promoter, the exploiter, and the loan shark held every advantage over the laborer and the craftsman; such a society could not long sustain a truly heroic fervor. It had purchased its ease at the cost of its people’s integrity. Every institution was tainted, the national soul stifled by a mountain of pretenses. Yet, when finally confronted by a ruthless and formidable foe—an adversary characterized by an unprecedented level of social organization and scientific precision—the nation seemed briefly galvanized by a rare spirit of purpose. The common folk and the young were radiant. Men of every rank marched away to perish, fueled by a magnificent vision of the conflict. The old lethargy retreated from view. But as that first wave of passion ebbed, the complacency returned. Soon, the elder statesmen, the merchants, the socialites, and the idle wealthy began to shake off their brief moral awakening. The familiar patterns of thought and conduct resumed. The war, which had opened with such theatrical force, failed to reach a resolution; there was no swift, glorious triumph and no sudden, noble collapse. Instead came the agony of stalemate, the harshest trial for a people lacking discipline. Long stretches of monotonous exhaustion set in. Even before the struggle at the Yser reached its peak, the war’s sense of narrative had dissolved. It was no longer a story of victory or defeat, but a colossal burden of attrition. It had devolved into a grueling, stagnant resistance against an overwhelming tide of misery.

He awoke next morning from rosy scenes of dream to a steamy atmosphere that smelled of soapsuds and dirty clothes, and that was vibrant with the jar and jangle of tormented life. As he came out of his room he heard the slosh of water, a sharp exclamation, and a resounding smack as his sister visited her irritation upon one of her numerous progeny. The squall of the child went through him like a knife. He was aware that the whole thing, the very air he breathed, was repulsive and mean. How different, he thought, from the atmosphere of beauty and repose of the house wherein Ruth dwelt. There it was all spiritual. Here it was all material, and meanly material.

Julian emerged from the amber remnants of sleep into a stifling mist that reeked of lye and sodden wool, a space vibrating with the discordant clamor of a desperate existence. Crossing the threshold, he encountered the rhythmic splashing of water and a sudden, sharp rebuke, punctuated by the heavy thud of a palm against skin as his sister discharged her resentment upon a whimpering child. The boy’s subsequent shriek lanced through him like a jagged blade. He felt a profound revulsion for the entire spectacle; the very air he inhaled seemed thick with a base and grinding misery. His mind drifted toward the serene elegance of the manor where Beatrice lived. There, life was an affair of the spirit, whereas here, it was a wretchedly corporeal struggle, devoid of any higher grace.

“And once he’d done with the pressing, he set to washing the fleeces,” she would later recount. “He said to me, ‘Maria, you’re the biggest simpleton. Let me teach you how to scour the wool,’ and he taught me, truly. In ten minutes he’d fashioned the contraption—a single cask, a hub from a cartwheel, two rods, as simple as that.”

He now wished that he had driven up the circuitous road to Mistover, a plan not adopted because of the enormous length of the route in proportion to that of the pedestrian’s path down the open hillside, and the consequent increase of labour for the horse.

After a long stretch of quiet, he turned toward the lamp and glanced at his watch. To his astonishment, it was almost a quarter past midnight. He now wished he’d elected to drive the winding road up to Mistover, a plan he’d discarded earlier because that route was vastly longer than the footpath leading down the open hillside, and would have added significantly to the horse’s burden.

We formulate AMR parsing as a series of dual graph-sequence decisions and design an iterative inference approach to tackle each of them. It is sort of analogous to the cognition procedure of a person, who might first notice part of the important information in one side (graph or sequence), then try to confirm her decision at the other side, which could just refute her former hypothesis and propose a new one, and finally converge to a conclusion after multiple rounds of reasoning.

metadata.title: AMR Parsing via Graph Sequence Iterative Inference *

text: We argue that the mutual causalities outlined above facilitate action disambiguation and harmonious decision-making, ultimately yielding more accurate parses. We cast AMR parsing as a sequence of dual graph–sequence decisions and devise an iterative inference procedure to address each decision. This process loosely mirrors human cognition: a person may initially attend to salient information on one side (graph or sequence), then seek confirmation on the other side, where the evidence may refute the earlier hypothesis and prompt a new one, eventually converging on a conclusion after several rounds of reasoning.

“There, you mistake,” he had rushed on. “All persons in society, all cliques in society—or, rather, nearly all persons and cliques—ape their betters. Now, who are the best betters? The idlers, the wealthy idlers. They do not know, as a rule, the things known by the persons who are doing something in the world. To listen to conversation about such things would mean to be bored, wherefore the idlers decree that such things are shop and must not be talked about. Likewise they decree the things that are not shop and which may be talked about, and those things are the latest operas, latest novels, cards, billiards, cocktails, automobiles, horse shows, trout fishing, tuna-fishing, big-game shooting, yacht sailing, and so forth—and mark you, these are the things the idlers know. In all truth, they constitute the shop-talk of the idlers. And the funniest part of it is that many of the clever people, and all the would-be clever people, allow the idlers so to impose upon them. As for me, I want the best a man’s got in him, call it shop vulgarity or anything you please.”

“There you err,” he had plunged ahead. “Every circle in society, every coterie—or, rather, nearly every person and set—mimics its superiors. And who, pray, are the foremost superiors? The leisured, the moneyed idlers. They remain ignorant, by and large, of the matters familiar to those who truly accomplish something in the world. To endure talk of such matters would be tedium, so the idlers pronounce those subjects shop and forbid their discussion. Equally they ordain what is not shop and may be spoken of, and those topics are the freshest operas, freshest novels, cards, billiards, cocktails, motorcars, horse shows, trout angling, tuna angling, big-game stalking, yacht cruising, and the like—and observe, these are the very things the idlers know. In sober fact, they constitute the shop-talk of the idlers. And the most amusing stroke is that many clever people, and all the would-be clever people, permit the idlers to foist this upon them. For my part, I demand the best a man possesses, call it shop coarseness or what you will.”

Thus she scored; but to Terry O’Sullivan the honours of the evening fell thick and fast. He shook his curls; he smiled and went easily through the seven motions for acquiring grace in your own room before an open window ten minutes each day. He danced like a faun; he introduced manner and style and atmosphere; his words came trippingly upon his tongue, and—he waltzed twice in succession with the paper-box girl that Dempsey Donovan brought.

So she had her point; but for Terry O’Sullivan the evening’s honours came one after another in quick succession. He tossed back his curls, smiled, and moved with effortless ease through the seven exercises for gaining grace in your own room before an open window, ten minutes a day. He danced like a faun; he brought in elegance and style and atmosphere; the words danced lightly on his tongue, and—he waltzed twice in a row with the paper-box girl that Dempsey Donovan had brought.

Nevertheless, she did not move. The truth was that Bathsheba was beyond the pale of activity—and yet not in a swoon. She was in a state of mental _gutta serena_; her mind was for the minute totally deprived of light at the same time no obscuration was apparent from without.

Nevertheless, she stayed perfectly still. In truth, Bathsheba existed beyond the reach of action—yet she had not fainted. Her mind was caught in a kind of inner blindness; for that moment, all inward illumination had vanished, though outwardly nothing seemed dimmed at all.

He had never felt quite so sure as most people about the stability of the modern financial system. He did not, he felt, understand the working of this moratorium, or the peculiar advantage of prolonging the bank holidays. It meant, he supposed, a stoppage of payment all round, and a cutting off of the supply of ready money. And Hickson the grocer, according to Mrs. Faber, was already looking askance at cheques.

Unlike most people he knew, he had never fully bought into the claim that the modern financial system was reliably stable. He could not wrap his head around how this moratorium functioned, or what specific benefit was meant to come from extending the bank holidays. As far as he could tell, the policy only amounted to a universal halt on all payouts and a full cutoff of access to ready cash. And according to Mrs. Faber, Hickson the grocer was already eyeing cheques with clear suspicion.

It was very early the next morning—a time of sun and dew. The confused beginnings of many birds’ songs spread into the healthy air, and the wan blue of the heaven was here and there coated with thin webs of incorporeal cloud which were of no effect in obscuring day. All the lights in the scene were yellow as to colour, and all the shadows were attenuated as to form. The creeping plants about the old manor-house were bowed with rows of heavy water drops, which had upon objects behind them the effect of minute lenses of high magnifying power.

It was very early the next morning, in that hour of sun and dew. The broken first notes of many birds widened through the fresh air, and the pale blue sky was here and there veiled by thin webs of cloud so insubstantial they did nothing to dim the day. Every light in the scene had a yellow cast, and every shadow was drawn out and slight. The climbing plants around the old manor-house bent under rows of heavy droplets, each one acting on the objects behind it like a tiny lens of great magnifying power.

And then out I came. But I didn't get any shout. There was a wondering and eloquent silence for a moment, then a great wave of laughter began to sweep along that human sea, but a warning bugle-blast cut its career short. I was in the simplest and comfortablest of gymnast costumes--flesh-colored tights from neck to heel, with blue silk puffings about my loins, and bareheaded. My horse was not above medium size, but he was alert, slender-limbed, muscled with watchsprings, and just a greyhound to go. He was a beauty, glossy as silk, and naked as he was when he was born, except for bridle and ranger-saddle.

I stepped out then. But no roar of welcome met me. Instead, a stunned and expressive silence lingered for a beat, followed by a massive wave of laughter that began to roll through the sea of people, only to be cut dead by a sharp bugle blast. I was dressed in the most basic and practical of athletic outfits—flesh-toned tights from my throat to my heels, with blue silk ruffles around my waist, and nothing on my head. My horse was only average-sized, but he was sharp and lean, his muscles like coiled springs, and he was a pure racer. He was a gorgeous creature, his coat as glossy as satin, and he was as unadorned as the day he was born, except for his bridle and a ranger-saddle.

Ambiguity In our tests, knowing the correct diacritics reduces the full-morphological-analysis ambiguity from 9.1 to 2.4 average analyses per word form, while knowing the full-morphologicalanalysis reduces the diacritization ambiguity from 6.2 to 1.4 average options per word form. Note that these numbers reflect fine-grained morphological tagging. If we utilize coarse-grained tagging, sufficing with the part of speech for each word, then knowing the correct diacritization reduces the average morphological ambiguity from 3.2 options to 1.97, while knowing the correct POS tag reduces the average diacritization ambiguity from 6.2 options to 2.75. Thus, the need for an automated diacritization utility is particularly crucial in order to properly disambiguate a Hebrew text.

metadata.title: Nakdan: Professional Hebrew Diacritizer

text: Ambiguity. In our experiments, access to the correct diacritics reduces full morphological analysis ambiguity from an average of 9.1 analyses per word form to 2.4, whereas access to the full morphological analysis lowers diacritization ambiguity from 6.2 to 1.4 candidate options per word form. We emphasize that these figures correspond to fine-grained morphological tagging. When coarse-grained tagging is used, with only part-of-speech information per word, knowing the correct diacritization reduces average morphological ambiguity from 3.2 options to 1.97, while knowing the correct POS tag decreases average diacritization ambiguity from 6.2 options to 2.75. These results indicate that an automated diacritization component is especially important for properly disambiguating Hebrew text.

At fifty tables sat princes and queens clad in all the silks and gems of the world. And now and then one of them would look curiously at Jerry’s fare. They saw a plain figure dressed in a pink silk of the kind that is tempered by the word “foulard,” and a plain face that wore a look of love of life that the queens envied.

Half a hundred tables were crowded with royalty, draped in the finest fabrics and most precious stones the world could offer. Occasionally, one of them would glance inquisitively toward Jerry. They beheld an ordinary woman in a simple pink foulard silk, her face unexceptional but for a vibrant joy for living that the queens themselves secretly coveted.

But it was pure, earnest, joyful reality. They drove straight to the door of the chief hotel (which Oliver used to stare up at, with awe, and think a mighty palace, but which had somehow fallen off in grandeur and size); and here was Mr. Grimwig all ready to receive them, kissing the young lady, and the old one too, when they got out of the coach, as if he were the grandfather of the whole party, all smiles and kindness, and not offering to eat his head—no, not once; not even when he contradicted a very old postboy about the nearest road to London, and maintained he knew it best, though he had only come that way once, and that time fast asleep. There was dinner prepared, and there were bedrooms ready, and everything was arranged as if by magic.

Yet it was a sincere, vivid, and joyful reality. They pulled up at the entrance of the town’s principal hotel—a place Oliver had once viewed with such awe that he had mistaken it for a vast palace, though it now appeared curiously shrunken and less imposing. Mr. Grimwig was there to welcome them, greeting both the young lady and the elder woman with a grandfatherly affection as they stepped from the coach. He was the very picture of kindness, never once threatening to eat his own head; he remained remarkably pleasant even while stubbornly correcting an ancient postboy regarding the quickest route to London, insisting he knew the way best despite having only traveled it once while soundly asleep. Dinner was waiting, the bedrooms were ready, and every comfort seemed to have been arranged as if by magic.

Her gratitude spilled out in broken phrases, like someone acknowledging an unexpected kindness. Only after she went back to her place in North Oakland, spoke with her own people, and sent the Portuguese shopkeeper to look into the matter, did the truth settle upon her—that the modest home she had inhabited for years, paying rent month after month, now belonged to her.

We proposed to explore NMT model uncertainty to generate additional hypotheses for MT evaluation. We showed that by exploiting similarities in the space of translation hypotheses generated by the model, along with methods to effectively combine information from these multiple hypotheses, we can achieve more accurate estimation on the quality of MT output than standard referencebased comparison, including cases with multiple references. This suggests that model uncertainty alone can be more reliable for assessing MT quality than standard reference-based evaluation. This work can be extended in numerous ways. First, we plan to test whether similar observations will hold for more language pairs and text domains. Second, the score combination strategies could be improved by learning weights for each component. Finally, we would like to test this approach for comparing different MT systems.

metadata.title: Multi-Hypothesis Machine Translation Evaluation

text: We investigate the use of neural machine translation model uncertainty to produce supplementary hypotheses for MT evaluation. We demonstrate that leveraging similarities within the hypothesis space generated by the model, together with techniques for effectively aggregating information across these multiple hypotheses, yields more accurate estimates of translation quality than conventional reference-based evaluation, even in settings with multiple references. This finding indicates that model uncertainty alone can offer a more dependable signal for assessing MT quality than standard reference-based comparisons. Several directions for future work emerge from this study. First, we intend to examine whether similar patterns generalize across additional language pairs and textual domains. Second, the strategy for combining scores could be enhanced by learning component-specific weights. Finally, we aim to apply this approach to the comparison of different MT systems.

In her desire to make atonement she took flowers from a vase by the window, and began laying them around the dead girl’s head. Bathsheba knew no other way of showing kindness to persons departed than by giving them flowers. She knew not how long she remained engaged thus. She forgot time, life, where she was, what she was doing. A slamming together of the coach-house doors in the yard brought her to herself again. An instant after, the front door opened and closed, steps crossed the hall, and her husband appeared at the entrance to the room, looking in upon her.

Driven by a need for penance, she gathered blossoms from a vase near the window and started arranging them around the head of the deceased girl. Bathsheba could conceive of no other gesture of compassion for the dead save offering them flowers. She had no sense of how long she lingered at this task. Time, existence, her surroundings, her own actions—all slipped from her awareness. The sharp clap of the coach-house doors banging shut in the yard jolted her back to herself. A moment later, the front door swung open and then closed, footsteps crossed the hall, and her husband stood in the doorway, gazing in at her.

“Well, yes. The fact is, these autumn races are the ruin of everybody. Never did I see such a day as ’twas! ’Tis a wild open place, just out of Budmouth, and a drab sea rolled in towards us like liquid misery. Wind and rain—good Lord! Dark? Why, ’twas as black as my hat before the last race was run. ’Twas five o’clock, and you couldn’t see the horses till they were almost in, leave alone colours. The ground was as heavy as lead, and all judgment from a fellow’s experience went for nothing. Horses, riders, people, were all blown about like ships at sea. Three booths were blown over, and the wretched folk inside crawled out upon their hands and knees; and in the next field were as many as a dozen hats at one time. Ay, Pimpernel regularly stuck fast, when about sixty yards off, and when I saw Policy stepping on, it did knock my heart against the lining of my ribs, I assure you, my love!”

“Yes, indeed. The truth is, these autumn meets ruin everyone. I’ve never witnessed a day like that one! It’s a bleak, exposed spot just outside Budmouth, and a dull gray sea surged toward us like liquid despair. Wind and rain—good heavens! Darkness? Why, it was pitch-black before the final race ended. It was five o’clock, and you couldn’t spot the horses until they were practically upon you, never mind their silks. The turf was heavy as iron, and all a man’s seasoned judgment counted for nothing. Horses, jockeys, spectators—all were tossed about like vessels in a storm. Three tents collapsed, and the poor souls inside scrambled out on hands and knees; in the next field, a dozen hats were aloft at once. Yes, Pimpernel got utterly bogged down about sixty yards out, and when I saw Policy striding forward, it sent my heart slamming against my ribs, I tell you, my dear!”

But there would be sundry gentlemen there with large gold fob chains and black cigars; and somebody would tell a funny story, and then Dempsey would go back and work half an hour with the six-pound dumbbells. So, doing a tight-rope act on a wire stretched across Niagara was a safe terpsichorean performance compared with waltzing twice with Dempsey Donovan’s paper-box girl. At 10 o’clock the jolly round face of “Big Mike” O’Sullivan shone at the door for five minutes upon the scene. He always looked in for five minutes, smiled at the girls and handed out real perfectos to the delighted boys.

A few choice spirits lingered there, sporting heavy golden Albert chains and nursing stout cheroots; after some wag shared a bawdy tale, Sully Vance would retreat for a brisk half-hour’s exertion with the ten-pound iron weights. Truly, a funambulist’s feat across a jagged chasm was a trifle of no consequence when weighed against the hazard of a second waltz with Sully’s shop-girl flame. Precisely at ten, the luminous, florid countenance of “Hoss” McGinnis appeared in the doorway for its nightly vigil. He remained but five minutes, casting a benevolent wink at the ladies and dispensing prime Havanas to the eager young men.
