For instance, if we have decided to render the word "not" to the graph, then we will consider adding an edge labeled as polarity, and finally determine its attachment to the existing event go-2 (rather than an edge labeled ARG0 to the same event go-2, though it is also present in the golden graph). On the other hand, if we have decided to find the subject (ARG0 relation) of the action go-02, we are confident to locate the word "boy" instead of function words like "not" or "must", thus unambiguously predict the right concept boy. Another possible circumstance is that we may make a mistake trying to ask something that is not present in the sentence (e.g., the destination of the go-02 action). This attempt will be rejected by a review of the sentence. The rationale is that literally we cannot find the destination information in the sentence. Similarly, if we mistakenly propose to abstract some parts of the sentence that are not ready for construction yet, the proposal will be rejected by another inspection on the graph since that there is nowhere to place such a new concept.

metadata.title: AMR Parsing via Graph Sequence Iterative Inference *

text: The boy wants the girl to believe him. Figure 2 provides an overview of dual graph-sequence iterative inference for AMR parsing. Given the current graph \(G_i\) and input sequence \(W\), inference begins with an initial concept decision \(x_0\) and proceeds along an inference chain. In Figure 1(b), by contrast, a polarity attribute of the event go-2 is constructed, triggered by the word “not” in the sentence. We note that the answer to one question can help answer the other. For example, if we have decided to render the word “not” in the graph, we will consider adding an edge labeled polarity and then determine its attachment to the existing event go-2, rather than adding an edge labeled ARG0 to the same event go-2, although such an edge is also present in the gold graph. Conversely, if we have decided to identify the subject (the ARG0 relation) of the action go-02, we can confidently locate the word “boy” instead of function words such as “not” or “must,” thereby unambiguously predicting the correct concept boy. Another possible case is that we may mistakenly ask for something that is not present in the sentence, such as the destination of the go-02 action. Such an attempt will be rejected after reviewing the sentence, since, literally, the destination information cannot be found there. Likewise, if we wrongly propose to abstract parts of the sentence that are not yet ready for construction, the proposal will be rejected by another inspection of the graph, because there is nowhere to attach such a new concept.

“There’s one fellow—Stevens—a theosophist. Makes a pretty tangle when he gets going. Just now he’s dish-washer in a restaurant. Likes a good cigar. I’ve seen him eat in a ten-cent hash-house and pay fifty cents for the cigar he smoked afterward. I’ve got a couple in my pocket for him, if he shows up.”

There’s one fellow, Stevens, a theosophist, who rambles into the most tangled, convoluted tangents once he gets going on a topic. Just now he’s working as a dishwasher in a local restaurant, and he’s deeply partial to a high-quality cigar. I once saw him eat his fill at a grubby ten-cent hash house, then pay fifty cents for the cigar he lit up and smoked right after finishing his meal. I’ve got a couple of nice ones tucked in my pocket for him, if he happens to show up.

We asked NLP researchers via social network, e.g. Twitter, to try out Talk to Paper and we are able to collect 3137 queries in roughly two weeks. The logged query data and its annotation will soon be made publicly available). Table 1 shows example queries where the system is able to find relevant answers to real user queries. Analysis shows that the most frequent query type were asking about the objectives or the meaning of terms (e.g., what are pretraining objectives, what is LSTM?). Another popular question type is to ask about the state-ofthe-art method to solve a particular problem, e.g. What is the best system for NLI?. We also found that the generated questions that are presented as auto-completion and FAQs are particularly popular. About 51.7% of queries were from the suggested questions. This results is inline with research work in human-computer interaction that utilizes machine intelligent systems to assist human users to better discover knowledge (Lee et al., 2019b) .

metadata.title: Talk to Papers: Bringing Neural Question Answering to Academic Search

text: We recruited NLP researchers through social media platforms such as Twitter to experiment with Talk to Paper, yielding a collection of 3,137 queries over approximately two weeks. The recorded query data and associated annotations will be publicly released in the near future. Table 1 presents sample queries for which the system successfully retrieves pertinent answers in response to authentic user inputs. Our examination reveals that the most prevalent query category involves inquiries about research goals or terminology definitions (e.g., what are pretraining objectives, what is LSTM?). Another frequently occurring type concerns state-of-the-art approaches for specific tasks, such as “What is the best system for NLI?”. We further observe that auto-completion suggestions and frequently asked questions displayed to users attract substantial engagement: roughly 51.7% of all queries originated from these suggested prompts. This finding aligns with prior work in human–computer interaction demonstrating that intelligent machine systems can facilitate knowledge discovery for human users (Lee et al., 2019b).

The Enron corpus (Klimt and Yang, 2004) consists of a large set of email conversations exchanged by the employees of the Enron corporation. Emails serve as a medium for exchange of requests, serving as an ideal application for politeness transfer. We begin by pre-processing the raw Enron corpus following Shetty and Adibi (2004) . The first set of pre-processing 1 steps and de-duplication yielded a corpus of roughly 2.5 million sentences. Further pruning 2 led to a cleaned corpus of over 1.39 million sentences. Finally, we use a politeness classifier (Niu and Bansal, 2018) to assign politeness scores to these sentences and filter them into ten buckets based on the score (P 0 -P 9 ; Fig. 1 ). All the buckets are further divided into train, test, and dev splits (in a 80:10:10 ratio).

metadata.title: Politeness Transfer: A Tag and Generate Approach

text: The Enron corpus (Klimt and Yang, 2004) comprises an extensive collection of email exchanges among employees of the Enron corporation. Because emails function as a medium for conveying requests, they represent an ideal domain for politeness transfer. We initially pre-process the raw Enron corpus according to the procedures described by Shetty and Adibi (2004). The initial pre-processing and de-duplication produced a corpus of approximately 2.5 million sentences. Subsequent pruning yielded a cleaned corpus containing over 1.39 million sentences. We then apply a politeness classifier (Niu and Bansal, 2018) to assign politeness scores to these sentences and partition them into ten buckets based on the score (P0–P9; Fig. 1). All buckets are subsequently split into training, test, and development sets in an 80:10:10 ratio.

He walked along towards home without attending to paths. If anyone knew the heath well it was Clym. He was permeated with its scenes, with its substance, and with its odours. He might be said to be its product. His eyes had first opened thereon; with its appearance all the first images of his memory were mingled, his estimate of life had been coloured by it: his toys had been the flint knives and arrow-heads which he found there, wondering why stones should “grow” to such odd shapes; his flowers, the purple bells and yellow furze: his animal kingdom, the snakes and croppers; his society, its human haunters. Take all the varying hates felt by Eustacia Vye towards the heath, and translate them into loves, and you have the heart of Clym. He gazed upon the wide prospect as he walked, and was glad.

He cut across the heath toward home, not bothering to stick to any marked footpath. Nobody knew this stretch of moorland better than Clym, that much was certain. Every sweeping vista, every crumb of its peaty soil, every sharp, earthy scent had seeped into his very bones. You could fairly call him the heath’s own creation. He’d first opened his eyes to its skyline; every earliest memory he held was tangled up with its rough contours, and it had dyed every belief he held about life its own earthy hue. As a small boy, his playthings were the flint blades and arrowheads he dug up in its turf, marveling that stones could “grow” into such strange, sharp shapes; his favorite flowers were its purple heather bells and bright yellow gorse; his idea of the entire animal kingdom was its slithering snakes and plump game birds; his first sense of community came from the other drifters and wanderers who haunted its expanse. If you took every layered, bitter hatred Eustacia Vye held for the heath and flipped every last one into fierce, abiding affection, you’d have a perfect blueprint of Clym’s heart. He kept his gaze fixed on the wide, open spread of land stretching out ahead as he walked, and felt a quiet, unshakable gladness settle over him.

He steered along the narrow road by the black dog-rose hedge, and so into the high road towards the village. The village was alight at several windows but almost deserted. Out beyond, a coruscation of lights burnt like a group of topaz and rubies set in the silver shield of the night. The festivities of the Flower Show were still in full progress, and the reduction of the entrance fee after seven had drawn in every lingering outsider. The roundabouts churned out their relentless music, and the bottle-shooting galleries popped and crashed. The well-patronised ostriches and motorcars flickered round in a pulsing rhythm; black, black, black, before the naphtha flares.

He navigated the narrow lane by the dark dog-rose hedge, emerging onto the main road toward the village. Though several windows were illuminated, the streets were nearly deserted. In the distance, a cluster of lights glittered like rubies and topaz set against the silver shield of the night. The Flower Show remained in full swing, the late-hour discount having drawn in every remaining outsider.

“Well, you know we haven't got time for this sort of thing. Don't you see, we could distribute these people around the earth in less time than it is going to take you to explain that we can't. We mustn't talk now, we must act. You want to be careful; you mustn't let your mill get the start of you that way, at a time like this. To business now--and sharp's the word. Who is to take the aristocracy home?”

“Well, you understand we can’t afford to linger over this. Can’t you grasp that we could scatter these souls across the globe faster than it’ll take you to justify why we can’t? We’ve no time for debate—we must move. You need to watch yourself; don’t let your mind run ahead of you like that, not at a moment such as this. Now, to the task at hand—and swiftly. Who will escort the nobility home?”

The friendly go-between in Norway had written to tell Mr. Britling that Herr Heinrich also was dead; he had died a wounded prisoner in Russia some months ago. He had been wounded and captured, after undergoing great hardships, during the great Russian attack upon the passes of the Carpathians in the early spring, and his wound had mortified. He had recovered partially for a time, and then he had been beaten and injured again in some struggle between German and Croatian prisoners, and he had sickened and died. Before he died he had written to his parents, and once again he had asked that the fiddle he had left in Mr. Britling's care should if possible be returned to them. It was manifest that both for him and them now it had become a symbol with many associations.

The amiable Norwegian intermediary had written to inform Mr. Britling that Herr Heinrich was also dead; he had passed away months prior while held as a wounded prisoner of war in Russia. He had sustained injuries and been captured, after enduring severe hardship, during the massive early spring Russian offensive against the Carpathian mountain passes, and his wound had become gangrenous. He rallied partially for a time, only to be beaten and harmed again during a scuffle between German and Croatian prisoners, after which he grew gravely ill and died. Before his death, he had written to his parents, and once more asked that the fiddle he had left in Mr. Britling’s keeping be returned to them if at all possible. It was evident that, for both him and his family by that point, the instrument had become a symbol weighted with countless layered associations.

A close examination of the ground hereabout, even by the wan starlight only, revealed how a portion of what would have been casually called a wild slope had been appropriated by Farmer Oak for his great purpose this winter. Detached hurdles thatched with straw were stuck into the ground at various scattered points, amid and under which the whitish forms of his meek ewes moved and rustled. The ring of the sheep-bell, which had been silent during his absence, recommenced, in tones that had more mellowness than clearness, owing to an increasing growth of surrounding wool. This continued till Oak withdrew again from the flock. He returned to the hut, bringing in his arms a new-born lamb, consisting of four legs large enough for a full-grown sheep, united by a seemingly inconsiderable membrane about half the substance of the legs collectively, which constituted the animal’s entire body just at present.

Even under the faint glimmer of starlight, a closer look at the ground nearby showed how part of what might loosely be termed a wild hillside had been claimed by Farmer Oak for his chief winter undertaking. Here and there, freestanding wattles padded with straw had been driven into the earth, and among them, beneath them, the pale shapes of his gentle ewes shifted and whispered. The sheep-bell, quiet while he was away, began to sound again, its notes more mellow than distinct, muffled by the thickening fleece around it. It rang on until Oak moved off from the flock once more. He went back to the hut, cradling a newborn lamb whose four legs—each big enough for a full-grown sheep—were joined by a thin membrane that seemed almost negligible, yet for now formed the creature’s entire body.

It was a profounder remark than Mr. Lorry had looked for. “True,” said he, “and fearful to reflect upon. Yet, a doubt lurks in my mind, Miss Pross, whether it is good for Doctor Manette to have that suppression always shut up within him. Indeed, it is this doubt and the uneasiness it sometimes causes me that has led me to our present confidence.”

The observation was more insightful than Mr. Lorry had anticipated. "You’re right," he admitted, "and it is a terrifying thing to consider. Still, Miss Pross, I can’t help but wonder if it is healthy for Doctor Manette to keep that trauma so tightly bottled up inside. In fact, it is this very concern and the anxiety it often brings me that has prompted me to speak so candidly with you now."

Leaving her and Oliver to compare notes at leisure, Mr. Brownlow led the way into another room; and there, heard from Rose a full narration of her interview with Nancy, which occasioned him no little surprise and perplexity. Rose also explained her reasons for not confiding in her friend Mr. Losberne in the first instance. The old gentleman considered that she had acted prudently, and readily undertook to hold solemn conference with the worthy doctor himself. To afford him an early opportunity for the execution of this design, it was arranged that he should call at the hotel at eight o’clock that evening, and that in the meantime Mrs. Maylie should be cautiously informed of all that had occurred. These preliminaries adjusted, Rose and Oliver returned home.

After Rose and Oliver were left to share their thoughts freely, Mr. Brownlow went ahead into another room. There, Rose gave him a complete account of her meeting with Nancy, which left him deeply surprised and puzzled. She also told him why she had not at first trusted her friend Mr. Losberne with the matter. The old man judged that she had behaved wisely and promptly agreed to have a serious talk with the good doctor himself. To give him an early chance to carry out this plan, they decided he would visit the hotel at eight that evening, and in the meanwhile Mrs. Maylie would be carefully told everything that had happened. Once these details were settled, Rose and Oliver went home.

Among the methods evaluated, DATEWISE consistently outperforms all other methods on all tested datasets in the alignment-based ROUGE metrics. The Date-F1 metric for this method is close to other methods, and not always better, which shows that the advantage of DATEWISE is due to the sentence selection (based on our heuristic date vectors) and summarization. Note that the date selection method is identical to TRAN2013. We conclude from these results that the expensive combinatorial optimization used in MARTSCHAT2018 is not necessary to achieve high accuracy for news TLS. CLUST performs worse than DATEWISE and MARTSCHAT2018, except on ENTITIES, where it outperforms MARTSCHAT2018. We find that for the other two datasets, CLUST often merges articles from close dates together that would belong to separate events on ground-truth timelines, which may suggest that a different granularity of clusters is required depending on the task.

metadata.title: Examining the State-of-the-Art in News Timeline Summarization

text: DATEWISE consistently surpasses all other approaches across every tested dataset in terms of alignment-based ROUGE scores. Its Date-F1 performance remains comparable to—and not consistently superior to—that of other methods, indicating that DATEWISE’s gains stem primarily from its sentence selection (guided by our heuristic date vectors) and subsequent summarization, given that its date selection procedure is identical to that of TRAN2013. These findings suggest that the computationally costly combinatorial optimization employed in MARTSCHAT2018 is unnecessary for attaining high accuracy in news timeline summarization. CLUST underperforms both DATEWISE and MARTSCHAT2018 on all datasets except ENTITIES, where it exceeds MARTSCHAT2018. On the remaining two datasets, CLUST frequently groups together articles with proximate dates that correspond to distinct events in the ground-truth timelines, implying that task-dependent cluster granularity may be required.

“I was taken rather by surprise. But, nevertheless, I am very glad to hear such news. I shall approve, of course, dear Tamsie. Who can it be? I am quite at a loss to guess. No I am not—’tis the old doctor!—not that I mean to call him old, for he is not very old after all. Ah—I noticed when he attended you last time!”

The revelation caught me entirely unawares; yet, for all that, I am truly heartened by the intelligence. You have my full blessing, naturally, my sweet Beatrice. I wonder who the gentleman might be? I am quite at a loss for a name. And yet—no—it is the parish surgeon! Not that I intend to name him elderly, for his years are not so very advanced, after all. Truly, I observed a certain tenderness in his manner when he last attended your sickbed!

I felt a clearing in my mind
As if my brain had split;
I tried to match it, seam by seam,
But could not make them fit.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
A sudden rift broke through my consciousness,
As if my brain had sheared in two;
I tried to bind the jagged edges,
But they would not align.

Norton was sensitive and excitable, though he never lost his head, while Kreis and Hamilton were like a pair of cold-blooded savages, seeking out tender places to prod and poke. As the evening grew late, Norton, smarting under the repeated charges of being a metaphysician, clutching his chair to keep from jumping to his feet, his gray eyes snapping and his girlish face grown harsh and sure, made a grand attack upon their position.

Norton was sensitive and easily stirred, though he never lost his cool, while Kreis and Hamilton were a pair of cold, ruthless brutes, always hunting for vulnerable spots to nudge and prod for sport. As the evening stretched late, smarting under their repeated accusations that he was a mere metaphysician, Norton clamped his hands to the arms of his chair to stop himself from springing to his feet, his gray eyes snapping bright, his typically soft, girlish face turned harsh and unyielding as he launched a full, fierce counterattack on their core position.

As for Wildeve, his feelings are easy to guess. Obstacles were a ripening sun to his love, and he was at this moment in a delirium of exquisite misery. To clasp as his for five minutes what was another man’s through all the rest of the year was a kind of thing he of all men could appreciate. He had long since begun to sigh again for Eustacia; indeed, it may be asserted that signing the marriage register with Thomasin was the natural signal to his heart to return to its first quarters, and that the extra complication of Eustacia’s marriage was the one addition required to make that return compulsory.

Wildeve’s internal state was not difficult to discern. To him, impediments served as a nourishing sun that brought his passion to full bloom, and he was currently submerged in a feverish, beautiful agony. The chance to claim for a brief moment what belonged to another man for the rest of the year was a thrill he was uniquely suited to enjoy. He had long ago resumed his pining for Eustacia; indeed, the very act of signing the marriage register with Thomasin seemed to be the instinctive signal for his heart to retreat to its original obsession. The added complication of Eustacia’s own wedding was the final element required to make that return unavoidable.

“You dogs!” said the Marquis, but smoothly, and with an unchanged front, except as to the spots on his nose: “I would ride over any of you very willingly, and exterminate you from the earth. If I knew which rascal threw at the carriage, and if that brigand were sufficiently near it, he should be crushed under the wheels.”

“You curs!” said Count Valerius, his tone silken, his pale, sharp face set save for the faint throbbing flush that pricked at the edge of his high cheekbone: “I would gallop over every last one of you without a flicker of remorse, and wipe you all from the face of this earth. If I knew which scoundrel hurled that object at my coach, and if that cutthroat were close enough to reach, he would be crushed to pulp beneath my horses’ hooves before he could draw another breath.”

As I said this I suddenly beheld the figure of a man, at some distance, advancing towards me with superhuman speed. He bounded over the crevices in the ice, among which I had walked with caution; his stature, also, as he approached, seemed to exceed that of man. I was troubled; a mist came over my eyes, and I felt a faintness seize me, but I was quickly restored by the cold gale of the mountains. I perceived, as the shape came nearer (sight tremendous and abhorred!) that it was the wretch whom I had created. I trembled with rage and horror, resolving to wait his approach and then close with him in mortal combat. He approached; his countenance bespoke bitter anguish, combined with disdain and malignity, while its unearthly ugliness rendered it almost too horrible for human eyes. But I scarcely observed this; rage and hatred had at first deprived me of utterance, and I recovered only to overwhelm him with words expressive of furious detestation and contempt.

Just as I spoke, I suddenly glimpsed a figure in the distance, rushing toward me with a speed that defied nature. He leaped effortlessly over the deep cracks in the ice where I had trodden so tentatively; as he neared, his towering height seemed to far surpass that of any mortal. I felt a surge of unease; my vision clouded and a sudden lightheadedness took hold of me, yet the freezing mountain air quickly revived my spirit. As the shape drew closer—a sight both magnificent and repulsive—I recognized the miserable creature I had fashioned. I shuddered with a combination of fury and dread, determined to wait for him and then grapple in a fight to the finish. He reached me; his expression revealed a profound, bitter suffering joined with scorn and ill-will, while his ghastly, inhuman features were almost too revolting for the human eye to endure. But I took little note of this; my intense hatred had at first stolen my breath, and I found my voice only to assault him with a torrent of words filled with violent loathing and disdain.

Oliver reached the stile at which the by-path terminated; and once more gained the high-road. It was eight o’clock now. Though he was nearly five miles away from the town, he ran, and hid behind the hedges, by turns, till noon, fearing that he might be pursued and overtaken. Then he sat down to rest by the side of the milestone, and began to think, for the first time, where he had better go and try to live.

Oliver made it to the stile marking the end of the narrow side path, and stepped back onto the main public road. It was already eight o’clock by that point. Even though he was nearly five miles outside the town limits, he alternated between sprinting as fast as he could and ducking out of sight behind the thick hedgerows all the way until noon, frozen with fear that someone was on his trail and would overtake him any moment. Only when he finally sank down to rest beside the weathered milestone did he start to think, for the very first time, where he ought to head next to try and build some kind of life for himself.

A key distinct feature for MT-DNN is that it provides out-of-box adversarial training, multi-task learning, and knowledge distillation. Users can train a set of related tasks jointly to amplify each other. They can also invoke adversarial training (Miyato et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020) , which helps improve model robustness and generalizability. For production deployment where large model size becomes a practical obstacle, users can use MT-DNN to compress the original models into substantially smaller ones, even using a completely different architecture (e.g., compressed BERT or other Transformer-based text encoders into LSTMs (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997) ). The distillation step can similarly leverage multitask learning and adversarial training. Users can also conduct pretraining from scratch using the masked language model objective in MT-DNN. Moreover, in the fine-tuning step, users can incorporate this as an auxiliary task on the training text, which has been shown to improve performance. MT-DNN provides a comprehensive list of stateof-the-art pre-trained NLU models, together with step-by-step tutorials for using such models in general and biomedical applications.

metadata.title: The Microsoft Toolkit of Multi-Task Deep Neural Networks for Natural Language Understanding

text: A distinctive feature of MT-DNN is its out-of-the-box support for adversarial training, multi-task learning, and knowledge distillation. This framework allows users to train related tasks jointly, facilitating mutual performance gains through cross-task synergy. Users can also implement adversarial training (Miyato et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020) to enhance both model robustness and generalizability. To overcome the practical challenges of large-scale production deployment, MT-DNN enables the compression of original models into significantly smaller versions, even when utilizing different architectures—such as distilling BERT or other Transformer-based encoders into LSTMs (Hochreiter and Schmidhuber, 1997). This distillation process can itself leverage multi-task learning and adversarial training. Additionally, users can perform pretraining from scratch using the masked language modeling objective. During fine-tuning, this objective can be integrated as an auxiliary task on the training text, a method shown to improve performance. Finally, MT-DNN offers a comprehensive suite of state-of-the-art pre-trained NLU models, along with step-by-step tutorials for their application in general and biomedical domains.

text: He tried to imagine he hadn't heard all that he had heard, but Mrs. Harrowdean had a nimble pen and nimbler afterthoughts, and once her mind had got to work upon the topic she developed her offensive in half-a-dozen brilliant letters.... On the other hand she professed a steadily increasing passion for Mr. Britling. And to profess passion for Mr. Britling was to put him under a sense of profound obligation--because indeed he was a modest man. He found himself in an emotional quandary.

He endeavored to persuade himself that he had not heard what he had heard; but Mrs. Vale possessed a lively pen and an even livelier faculty for afterthought, and once her mind had fastened upon the matter she carried her attack forward in half a dozen dazzling letters.... Yet, on the other hand, she avowed an ever-growing devotion to Mr. Letham. And to avow devotion to Mr. Letham was to place him beneath a deep sense of obligation—since, in truth, he was a modest man. Thus he discovered himself in a very perplexing state of feeling.

The room was illuminated by two gas-lights; the glare of which was prevented by the barred shutters, and closely-drawn curtains of faded red, from being visible outside. The ceiling was blackened, to prevent its colour from being injured by the flaring of the lamps; and the place was so full of dense tobacco smoke, that at first it was scarcely possible to discern anything more. By degrees, however, as some of it cleared away through the open door, an assemblage of heads, as confused as the noises that greeted the ear, might be made out; and as the eye grew more accustomed to the scene, the spectator gradually became aware of the presence of a numerous company, male and female, crowded round a long table: at the upper end of which, sat a chairman with a hammer of office in his hand; while a professional gentleman with a bluish nose, and his face tied up for the benefit of a toothache, presided at a jingling piano in a remote corner.

The room was lit by two gas lamps, their glare kept from showing outside by barred shutters and tightly drawn curtains of faded red. The ceiling had been blackened so that the flames would not stain it with smoke, and the place was so thick with tobacco smoke that at first it was hardly possible to make out anything at all. Gradually, though, as some of the haze drifted out through the open door, one could distinguish a crowd of heads, as confused and indistinct as the noises that reached the ear; and as the eye grew used to the scene, the observer slowly became aware of a large company of men and women gathered round a long table. At its head sat a chairman, holding the hammer of office in his hand, while a professional gentleman with a bluish nose and his face wrapped up for a toothache kept the jingling piano going in a distant corner.

Eustacia remained within the bank looking at the fire, intending to go indoors, yet lingering still. Had she not by her situation been inclined to hold in indifference all things honoured of the gods and of men she would probably have come away. But her state was so hopeless that she could play with it. To have lost is less disturbing than to wonder if we may possibly have won; and Eustacia could now, like other people at such a stage, take a standing-point outside herself, observe herself as a disinterested spectator, and think what a sport for Heaven this woman Eustacia was.

Eustacia stayed inside the bank, gazing at the fire and meaning to go back indoors, yet still lingering there. Had her circumstances not already disposed her to regard with indifference everything revered by gods and men, she would likely have left at once. But her condition was so utterly desperate that she could almost toy with it. Loss is less troubling than the question of whether we may perhaps have won after all; and now Eustacia, like others in such a state, could stand apart from herself, watch herself as though she were a detached observer, and wonder what a jest she made for Heaven, this woman Eustacia.

At the roadside hamlet called Roy-Town, just beyond this wood, was the old inn Buck’s Head. It was about a mile and a half from Weatherbury, and in the meridian times of stage-coach travelling had been the place where many coaches changed and kept their relays of horses. All the old stabling was now pulled down, and little remained besides the habitable inn itself, which, standing a little way back from the road, signified its existence to people far up and down the highway by a sign hanging from the horizontal bough of an elm on the opposite side of the way.

Tucked in Roy-Town, the small roadside hamlet just beyond this stretch of woods, stood the aging Buck’s Head inn. Roughly a mile and a half from Weatherbury, the establishment had been a busy stagecoach stop during the golden age of horse-drawn travel, where countless lines swapped out exhausted teams and kept their relay horses stabled on site. All that old stabling has long since been torn down, however, and little remains besides the still-habitable inn itself, set a short distance back from the road. It signals its presence to passersby far up and down the highway via a sign hung from a thick horizontal bough of an elm tree growing directly across the way.

“Oh, heart of gold, now I see it! The bitter law takes the convicted man's estate and beggars his widow and his orphans. They could torture you to death, but without conviction or confession they could not rob your wife and baby. You stood by them like a man; and _you_--true wife and the woman that you are--you would have bought him release from torture at cost to yourself of slow starvation and death--well, it humbles a body to think what your sex can do when it comes to self-sacrifice. I'll book you both for my colony; you'll like it there; it's a Factory where I'm going to turn groping and grubbing automata into _men_.”

“Ah, brave soul, now I understand it! The cruel statute seizes the condemned man’s goods and leaves his widow and little ones destitute. They might have racked you to death, but without a verdict or a confession they could not have stripped your wife and child. You stood fast beside them like a man; and you—faithful wife, and woman indeed—would have purchased his freedom from torment at the price of your own slow hunger and death. Well, it brings a body low to reckon what your sex can do in the way of self-sacrifice. I’ll enter you both for my settlement; you’ll be suited to it there. It is a Factory where I mean to turn fumbling, scrambling automata into men.”

It was a second to remember another phase of the matter. The sheep were not insured. All the savings of a frugal life had been dispersed at a blow; his hopes of being an independent farmer were laid low—possibly for ever. Gabriel’s energies, patience, and industry had been so severely taxed during the years of his life between eighteen and eight-and-twenty, to reach his present stage of progress that no more seemed to be left in him. He leant down upon a rail, and covered his face with his hands.

In the space of a heartbeat, he registered the other cruel detail of the disaster. The flock had no insurance. Every penny he had scraped together over a lifetime of frugality was wiped out in a single stroke; his dream of working as an independent farmer was shattered, likely for good. Gabriel had stretched his energy, patience, and relentless industry to their absolute limits over the decade between his eighteenth and twenty-eighth birthdays to reach his current standing, and he had no more reserves left to draw on. He sagged against a fence rail, and buried his face in his hands.

Main Results The performance of AMR parsing is conventionally evaluated by SMATCH (F1) metric . The left block of Table 1 shows the SMATCH scores on the AMR 2.0 test set of our models against the previous best approaches and recent competitors. On AMR 2.0, we outperform the latest push from Zhang et al. (2019b) by 3.2% and, for the first time, obtain a parser with over 80% SMATCH score. Note that even without BERT, our model still outperforms the previous state-of-the-art approaches using BERT (Zhang et al., 2019b,a) with 77.3%. This is particularly remarkable since running BERT is computationally expensive. As shown in Table 2 , on AMR 1.0 where the training instances are only around 10K, we improve the best-reported results by 4.1% and reach at 75.4%, which is already higher than most models trained on AMR 2.0. The even more substantial performance gain on the smaller dataset suggests that our method is both effective and dataefficient. Besides, again, our model without BERT already surpasses previous state-of-the-art results using BERT. For ablated models, it can be observed that our models yield the best results in all settings if there are any competitors, indicating BERT and graph re-categorization are not the exclusive key for our superior performance.

metadata.title: AMR Parsing via Graph Sequence Iterative Inference *

text: The evaluation of AMR parsing performance conventionally relies on the SMATCH (F1) metric. The left panel of Table 1 reports SMATCH scores on the AMR 2.0 test set for our models alongside the previous best methods and recent competitive systems. On AMR 2.0, we surpass the most recent advance by Zhang et al. (2019b) by 3.2% and, for the first time, achieve a parser exceeding 80% SMATCH. Notably, even without BERT, our model still outperforms prior state-of-the-art BERT-based approaches (Zhang et al., 2019b,a) with a score of 77.3%. This finding is particularly striking given the high computational cost of running BERT. As presented in Table 2, on AMR 1.0—where training instances number only about 10K—we improve upon the best-reported results by 4.1% and reach 75.4%, a level already higher than most models trained on AMR 2.0. The larger performance gain on the smaller dataset indicates that our method is both effective and data-efficient. Moreover, our model without BERT again exceeds previous state-of-the-art results that employ BERT. In ablation studies, our models achieve the best results across all settings where competitors exist, demonstrating that neither BERT nor graph re-categorization alone accounts for our superior performance.

To many persons this Egdon was a place which had slipped out of its century generations ago, to intrude as an uncouth object into this. It was an obsolete thing, and few cared to study it. How could this be otherwise in the days of square fields, plashed hedges, and meadows watered on a plan so rectangular that on a fine day they looked like silver gridirons? The farmer, in his ride, who could smile at artificial grasses, look with solicitude at the coming corn, and sigh with sadness at the fly-eaten turnips, bestowed upon the distant upland of heath nothing better than a frown. But as for Yeobright, when he looked from the heights on his way he could not help indulging in a barbarous satisfaction at observing that, in some of the attempts at reclamation from the waste, tillage, after holding on for a year or two, had receded again in despair, the ferns and furze-tufts stubbornly reasserting themselves.

To many, this tract of Egdon seemed a relic stranded from a bygone age, thrust incongruously into the present. It was an anachronism, and scarcely anyone cared to examine it. How could it be otherwise in an era of squared fields, trimmed hedgerows, and meadows irrigated with such rigid geometry that on bright days they gleamed like silver grates? The farmer, riding out, who could smile at cultivated grasses, eye the swelling grain with concern, and sigh mournfully over fly-blighted turnips, offered the far upland heath no more than a scowl. Yet Yeobright, gazing down from the ridges along his route, could not suppress a fierce, primitive pleasure at seeing how, in certain patches where men had tried to reclaim the wild, the ploughed ground, after clinging on for a year or two, had faltered and retreated in defeat, the bracken and gorse stubbornly surging back.

“Wait a minute!” said the girl: “I wouldn’t hurry by, if it was you that was coming out to be hung, the next time eight o’clock struck, Bill. I’d walk round and round the place till I dropped, if the snow was on the ground, and I hadn’t a shawl to cover me.”

“Just a minute!” said the girl. “I wouldn’t be in such a rush if it were you coming out to be hanged the next time eight o’clock struck, Bill. I’d keep walking round and round the place till I dropped, if there were snow on the ground and I didn’t have a shawl to wrap myself in.”

One becomes a 'bayoneteer.' As I go bounding forward I imagine fat men, succulent men ahead, and I am filled with the desire to do them in neatly. This sort of thing--"

What is your money-making now? what can it do now?
What is your respectability now?
What are your theology, tuition, society, traditions, statute-books, now?
Where are your jibes of being now?
Where are your cavils about the soul now?

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
What does your wealth shape now? what can it purchase now?
What is your standing worth now?
What are your doctrines, your schooling, your customs, your laws, now?
Where are your taunts of existence now?
Where are your doubts about the spirit now?

In this paper, we revisit the idea of treating word ordering as a TSP, but unlike the common bagof-words scenario, the words have an underlying syntactic structure. We demonstrate that with the Tree-LSTM encoder, the biaffine scoring model, the divide-and-conquer strategy, and a transitionbased sorting system, we can linearize a dependency tree with high speed and quality and without the projectivity restriction. We show with various ablation experiments that all of the components are crucial for the success of the TSP-based linearizer.

metadata.title: Fast and Accurate Non-Projective Dependency Tree Linearization

text: In this paper, we revisit the formulation of word ordering as a TSP, but unlike the standard bag-of-words setting, the words are endowed with an underlying syntactic structure. We show that, by combining a Tree-LSTM encoder, a biaffine scoring model, a divide-and-conquer strategy, and a transition-based sorting system, it is possible to linearize a dependency tree with both high speed and high quality, while avoiding the projectivity restriction. Through a series of ablation experiments, we further demonstrate that each of these components is essential to the effectiveness of the TSP-based linearizer.

Notably he would not recognise at first the extreme bitterness of this war. He would not believe that the attack upon Britain and Western Europe generally expressed the concentrated emotion of a whole nation. He thought that the Allies were in conflict with a system and not with a national will. He fought against the persuasion that the whole mass of a great civilised nation could be inspired by a genuine and sustained hatred. Hostility was an uncongenial thing to him; he would not recognise that the greater proportion of human beings are more readily hostile than friendly. He did his best to believe--in his "And Now War Ends" he did his best to make other people believe--that this war was the perverse exploit of a small group of people, of limited but powerful influences, an outrage upon the general geniality of mankind. The cruelty, mischief, and futility of war were so obvious to him that he was almost apologetic in asserting them. He believed that war had but to begin and demonstrate its quality among the Western nations in order to unify them all against its repetition. They would exclaim: "But we can't do things like this to one another!" He saw the aggressive imperialism of Germany called to account even by its own people; a struggle, a collapse, a liberal-minded conference of world powers, and a universal resumption of amiability upon a more assured basis of security. He believed--and many people in England believed with him--that a great section of the Germans would welcome triumphant Allies as their liberators from intolerable political obsessions.

At first he would not admit the full savagery of this war. He could not bring himself to believe that the assault on Britain and on Western Europe at large sprang from the gathered feeling of an entire people. To him the Allies were contending with a machine, not with a national temper. He resisted the notion that the mass of a highly civilised nation could be moved by a true and enduring hatred. Ill will was something foreign to his nature; he would not concede that most men are more easily turned to enmity than to friendship. He tried as hard as he could to believe—and in his “When War is Over” he tried just as hard to persuade others—that this conflict was the distorted venture of a small circle, of narrow yet potent forces, an insult to the prevailing good humour of mankind. The cruelty, waste, and absurdity of war were so plain to him that he almost begged pardon for insisting on them. He was convinced that once war had actually begun and disclosed its character among the Western peoples, they would unite at once to prevent its ever recurring. They would cry out, “But we cannot do such things to one another!” He imagined German aggressiveness being judged even by Germans themselves; a reckoning, a breakdown, a conference of liberal statesmen from the world’s powers, and then a general return to good will on a firmer foundation of safety. He believed—and many in England believed with him—that a large part of the German people would greet a victorious Allied army as deliverers from unbearable political delusions.

For instance, it takes your woman 42 days to earn her gown, at 2 mills a day--7 weeks' work; but ours earns hers in forty days--two days _short_ of 7 weeks. Your woman has a gown, and her whole seven weeks wages are gone; ours has a gown, and two days' wages left, to buy something else with. There--_now_ you understand it!”

“Hold on! There, you see, it’s perfectly plain; this time you’ll grasp it. Say your wife needs 42 days to pay for her dress, at 2 mills per day—seven full weeks of labor; but mine earns hers in forty days—two days shy of seven weeks. Your wife has a dress, and her entire seven weeks’ pay is spent; mine has a dress, and two days’ wages remaining, to put toward something else. There—now you’ve got it!”

At a distance of half a mile she could discern the pale white fence; yet he did not come into view. She returned home with something very near to sickness of heart, and with a humiliating sense of her own weakness. She determined to seek the Parisian no more.

Finally, we compared our model against Quagga, the state-of-the-art neural segmentation model by Repke and Krestel and a re-implementation of Tang et al.'s SVM email cleaning approach. Unfortunately, a training routine was missing from Quagga's source code, so we re-implemented this part as closely to the original as possible with one notable exception. We changed the way the model handles quotations. The original model did not have a quotation class and was instead trained to ignore quotation indicators so as to predict normal content segments within quotations also. This is very different from how our model handles quotations and it renders the reconstruction of a conversation from the segments alone impossible. We prefer our approach to classify quotations as a separate segment, which retains the structure of emails and one can simply strip the quotation indicators and then apply the model recursively. We trained our own Quagga on all 16 classes for 20 epochs (the model started overfitting after more epochs). Although the original model was trained and tested on only five classes, the extended and retrained model performs only slightly worse than ours with 94% accuracy overall and very similar scores for most of the frequent classes. The degradation on the Enron corpus appears to be worse than in our model (with the exception of the log data class). In conclusion, we can say that both models perform equally well, though our model achieves overall better generalization. In terms of training speed, we found our approach to be faster and more efficient, since it relies on a 2D context window instead of a vertical RNN for sequences of lines.

metadata.title: Crawling and Preprocessing Mailing Lists At Scale for Dialog Analysis

text: Finally, we evaluated our model against Quagga, the state-of-the-art neural segmentation framework by Repke and Krestel, alongside a re-implementation of the SVM-based email cleaning method by Tang et al. Due to the absence of a training routine in the Quagga source code, we re-implemented this component as faithfully as possible, with the notable exception of quotation handling. Whereas the original model lacked a quotation class and ignored markers to predict content within quoted text—making conversation reconstruction from segments alone impossible—our approach classifies quotations as distinct segments. This preserves the structural integrity of emails, allowing for the removal of quotation indicators and subsequent recursive model application. We trained our implementation of Quagga on all 16 classes for 20 epochs, as further training led to overfitting. Although the original Quagga was designed for only five classes, our extended version reached 94% accuracy, performing only slightly worse than our model with comparable scores for most frequent classes. However, the performance degradation on the Enron corpus was more significant for Quagga than for our model, except within the log data class. Ultimately, both models perform well, yet our approach achieves better overall generalization. Furthermore, our model is faster and more efficient to train, as it utilizes a 2D context window rather than a vertical RNN for processing line sequences.

Hence, when his name was casually mentioned by neighbouring yeomen, the listener said, “Ah, Clym Yeobright—what is he doing now?” When the instinctive question about a person is, What is he doing? it is felt that he will be found to be, like most of us, doing nothing in particular. There is an indefinite sense that he must be invading some region of singularity, good or bad. The devout hope is that he is doing well. The secret faith is that he is making a mess of it. Half a dozen comfortable market-men, who were habitual callers at the Quiet Woman as they passed by in their carts, were partial to the topic. In fact, though they were not Egdon men, they could hardly avoid it while they sucked their long clay tubes and regarded the heath through the window. Clym had been so inwoven with the heath in his boyhood that hardly anybody could look upon it without thinking of him. So the subject recurred: if he were making a fortune and a name, so much the better for him; if he were making a tragical figure in the world, so much the better for a narrative.

And so, when his name cropped up unprompted among nearby smallholders, whoever was listening would ask, “Oh, Kael Thorne—what’s he gotten up to these days?” When the first question that springs to mind about someone is what he’s been up to, there’s an unspoken assumption he’s likely drifting through life the same as most of us, with no grand purpose to speak of. There’s a vague hunch he’s wandered into some out-of-the-ordinary path, for better or for worse. Everyone outwardly wishes he’s thriving. Privately, most are convinced he’s making a thorough hash of things. Half a dozen well-off peddlers who regularly stopped at the Crooked Hearth on their cart routes loved to chew over this very topic. Even though they were not native to Bramble Moor, they could scarce keep from returning to it as they puffed on their long clay pipes and stared out the window at the rolling moorland. Kael had been so tied to the moor through all his boyhood that scarcely anyone could set eyes on its rolling slopes without him coming to mind. And so the conversation circled back time and again: if he was building a fortune and a reputation for himself, all the better for his sake; if he was cutting a tragic, ruined figure out in the wider world, all the better for a good story.

“I can cure it, I think, and without drugs,” was Martin’s answer. “I am not sure, of course, but I’d like to try. It’s simply massage. I learned the trick first from the Japanese. They are a race of masseurs, you know. Then I learned it all over again with variations from the Hawaiians. They call it _lomi-lomi_. It can accomplish most of the things drugs accomplish and a few things that drugs can’t.”

“I believe I can remedy this without resorting to medicine,” Martin replied. “I can’t be certain, naturally, but I’m eager to give it a go. It is a matter of massage, really. I first picked up the technique from the Japanese—they are a people gifted in the art of bodywork, you know. Later, I refined the practice through the Hawaiians, who refer to it as *lomi-lomi*. It achieves much of what pharmaceuticals can do, along with several things they simply cannot.”

Meanwhile, Oliver Twist, little dreaming that he was within so very short a distance of the merry old gentleman, was on his way to the book-stall. When he got into Clerkenwell, he accidently turned down a by-street which was not exactly in his way; but not discovering his mistake until he had got half-way down it, and knowing it must lead in the right direction, he did not think it worth while to turn back; and so marched on, as quickly as he could, with the books under his arm.

All the while, Oliver Twist had not the faintest inkling he was mere steps away from the jovial old gentleman, and was making his steady way toward the bookstall. When he reached Clerkenwell, he veered accidentally down a side lane that was not quite on his intended route; he only caught his mistake when he was already halfway along it, but since he knew the street would eventually lead in the correct direction, he saw no point in doubling back. So he pressed onward at as brisk a pace as he could manage, the stack of books tucked firmly under his arm.

He took the hint at once, for the fist had been too often impressed upon his body not to be deeply impressed upon his recollection.

Oliver was just about to declare his eager willingness to go with anyone at all when, looking up, he spotted Mrs. Mann. She had positioned herself behind the beadle’s chair and was brandishing her fist at him with a look of pure rage. He understood the warning immediately, for that fist had been pressed against his ribs too often not to be permanently etched into his memory.

Who sang full many a day,
But when the woods were painted
He, too, did fly away.
Time brought me other robins, --
Their ballads were the same, --
Still for my missing troubadour
I kept the 'house at hame.'

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
I had a scarlet robin  
Who sang through many a day,  
But when the woods turned golden,  
He, too, flew off away.  
Time brought me other robins,  
Their songs were just the same,  
Yet for my lost troubadour  
I kept the house at home.

"My one consolation," he said, "in this storm is a talk I had last month with a young Irishwoman in Meath. She was a young person of twelve, and she took a fancy to me--I think because I went with her in an alleged dangerous canoe she was forbidden to navigate alone. All day the eternal Irish Question had banged about over her observant head. When we were out on the water she suddenly decided to set me right upon a disregarded essential. 'You English,' she said, 'are just a bit disposed to take all this trouble seriously. Don't you fret yourself about it... Half the time we're just laffing at you. You'd best leave us all alone....'"

My only comfort in the midst of this upheaval," he remarked, "is a conversation I had last month with a young Irish girl in Meath. She was only twelve years old, and she took a liking to me—mostly, I suspect, because I joined her in a supposedly hazardous canoe she was forbidden from rowing alone. All through the day, the incessant arguments regarding the Irish Question had been swirling around her observant head. Once we were out on the water, she decided to enlighten me on a crucial point that everyone else seemed to ignore. 'You English,' she said, 'have a tendency to take all this conflict far too seriously. Don't let it trouble you... Half the time we’re just laughing at you. You really ought to just leave us be.'

“He’s laying low to avoid showing up here before dark, but he’ll turn up soon enough,” Chitling replied. “There’s nowhere else he can go right now: everyone at the Cripples is in custody, and the bar of the den—I went up there and saw it for myself—is crammed full of traps.”

Sound the flute!
Now it’s mute!
Birds delight,
Day and night,
Nightingale,
In the dale,
Lark in sky,—
Merrily,
Merrily, merrily to welcome in the year.

text:
Strike the flute—  
its voice has gone still.  
Birds revel  
in sun and starlight,  
nightingale  
in the hollow,  
lark overhead—  
gaily,  
gaily, gaily to greet the turning year.

Afraid, in her extreme perturbation, of the loneliness of the deserted rooms, and of half-imagined faces peeping from behind every open door in them, Miss Pross got a basin of cold water and began laving her eyes, which were swollen and red. Haunted by her feverish apprehensions, she could not bear to have her sight obscured for a minute at a time by the dripping water, but constantly paused and looked round to see that there was no one watching her. In one of those pauses she recoiled and cried out, for she saw a figure standing in the room.

Terrified by her overwhelming distress and the emptiness of the abandoned chambers—where she half-fancied faces lurking behind each ajar door—Miss Pross fetched a bowl of cool water and started bathing her puffy, crimson eyes. Tormented by restless dread, she couldn’t stand even a moment’s blindness from the trickling water, and kept stopping to glance about, checking that no one observed her. During one such halt, she flinched and shrieked, for she spotted a silhouette standing within the room.

It is well for the unfortunate to be resigned, but for the guilty there is no peace. The agonies of remorse poison the luxury there is otherwise sometimes found in indulging the excess of grief.

She wept with me and for me. When reason came back, she would gently reprove me and strive to teach me resignation. His wilful hands and feet began to beat and churn about, spasmodically and feebly. But he had fooled them and the will to live that made them beat and churn. He was too deep down. They could never bring him to the surface. He seemed floating languidly in a sea of dreamy vision. Colors and radiances surrounded him and bathed him and pervaded him. What was that? It seemed a lighthouse; but it was inside his brain—a flashing, bright white light. It flashed swifter and swifter. There was a long rumble of sound, and it seemed to him that he was falling down a vast and interminable stairway. And somewhere at the bottom he fell into darkness. That much he knew. He had fallen into darkness. And at the instant he knew, he ceased to know.

His rebellious limbs thrashed weakly, twitching in erratic spasms. Yet he had outwitted them, and the stubborn life force that drove their flailing. He had sunk too far. They could never haul him back up. He drifted as if suspended in a drowsy sea of hallucinatory light. Hues and luminous glows wrapped around him, soaked through him, saturated his being. What was that? It looked like a lighthouse, but it pulsed inside his skull—a flickering, brilliant white beam. It blinked faster and faster. A long, rolling thunder of noise swelled, and he felt himself tumbling down an immense, never-ending staircase. And somewhere at its base he plunged into blackness. That much he grasped. He had plunged into blackness. And the moment he grasped it, he grasped nothing at all.

The light of the candle had by this time attracted heath-flies, moths, and other winged creatures of night, which floated round the lantern, flew into the flame, or beat about the faces of the two players.

The match tugged back and forth, tipping first to one opponent, then the next, with neither ever pulling far enough ahead to claim a clear upper hand. Close to twenty minutes slipped by in this even, tense rhythm. By now, the guttering candle glow had lured moor gnats, miller moths, and all manner of small winged night creatures, which drifted in lazy loops around the glass lantern, dove straight into the licking flame, or bumped and skittered across the faces of the two competitors.

They who were in a hurry restrained their impatience for the joy of merely gazing upon her swiftly moving, graceful figure. They who had finished eating ate more that they might continue in the light of her smiles. Every man there—and they were mostly men—tried to make his impression upon her.

The patrons at Marden’s were her captives. She could attend to six tables at a time. Those who were pressed for time held back their impatience for the pleasure of merely watching her quick, graceful figure glide among them. Those who had finished their meals ordered more, so they might linger longer in the warmth of her smiles. Every man in the room—and men made up the greater part of the company—endeavored to leave some mark upon her.

This study takes a first step towards computationally modeling the MWF of English derivatives. We present a derivational graph auto-encoder (DGA) that combines semantic and syntactic information with associative information from the mental lexicon, achieving very good results on MWF prediction and performing on par with a character-based LSTM at a fraction of the number of trainable parameters. The model produces embeddings that capture information about the compatibility of affixes and stems in derivation and can be used as pretrained input to other NLP applications. 1 2 Derivational Morphology

text: Abruptly and shockingly, this malignity of warfare, which had been so far only a festering cluster of reports and stories and rumours and suspicions, stretched out its arm into Essex and struck a barb of grotesque cruelty into the very heart of Mr. Britling. Late one afternoon came a telegram from Filmington-on-Sea, where Aunt Wilshire had been recovering her temper in a boarding-house after a round of visits in Yorkshire and the moorlands. And she had been "very seriously injured" by an overnight German air raid. It was a raid that had not been even mentioned in the morning's papers. She had asked to see him.

Suddenly and with a jarring force, the malice of the war—which until then had been merely a stagnant collection of rumors, reports, and vague suspicions—reached into the quiet of Essex to strike a blow of senseless cruelty into the very soul of Mr. Britling. In the late afternoon, a telegram arrived from Filmington-on-Sea, where Aunt Wilshire had been nursing her grievances in a boarding-house after traveling through the Yorkshire moors. She had been "gravely wounded" during a German air raid the previous night. The morning papers had not uttered a word about the attack. She was asking for him.

Among these heavy yeomen a feminine figure glided, the single one of her sex that the room contained. She was prettily and even daintily dressed. She moved between them as a chaise between carts, was heard after them as a romance after sermons, was felt among them like a breeze among furnaces. It had required a little determination—far more than she had at first imagined—to take up a position here, for at her first entry the lumbering dialogues had ceased, nearly every face had been turned towards her, and those that were already turned rigidly fixed there.

Amid these burly countrymen there drifted a womanly presence, the sole one of her kind that the chamber held. She was dressed with a neatness that was almost delicate. She passed among them as a light carriage among drays, was heard after them as a ballad after a homily, was felt in their midst like a fresh wind among furnaces. It had demanded a little resolution—much more, indeed, than she had at first supposed—to establish herself in such a place, for when she first entered the heavy talk had fallen silent, almost every countenance had swung toward her, and those already turned remained fixed there with a kind of stern attention.

While he waved his hand and muttered that he had done nothing at all, he was obeying her behest by trying to get into a chair. He found time to admire the ease with which she sat down, then lurched toward a chair facing her, overwhelmed with consciousness of the awkward figure he was cutting. This was a new experience for him. All his life, up to then, he had been unaware of being either graceful or awkward. Such thoughts of self had never entered his mind. He sat down gingerly on the edge of the chair, greatly worried by his hands. They were in the way wherever he put them. Arthur was leaving the room, and Martin Eden followed his exit with longing eyes. He felt lost, alone there in the room with that pale spirit of a woman. There was no bar-keeper upon whom to call for drinks, no small boy to send around the corner for a can of beer and by means of that social fluid start the amenities of friendship flowing.

As he dismissed her gratitude with a clumsy gesture and a few swallowed words, Elias obeyed her silent instruction to sit. He paused to marvel at the effortless fluidity of her movements before lurching toward a seat across from her, suddenly and painfully aware of his own bulk. This sensation was entirely novel. Throughout his years, he had never once considered whether he moved with elegance or ineptitude; such vanity had never breached his consciousness. He lowered himself tentatively onto the cushion's edge, tormented by the sudden prominence of his hands. No matter where he rested them, they felt intrusive. Julian was withdrawing from the study, and Elias tracked his retreat with a look of profound longing. He felt abandoned, cast away in this room with a woman who seemed more a ghost than a person. There was no publican here to provide a dram, no lad to fetch a flagon of porter—none of the common spirits that typically washed away the friction of a first meeting.

across the sun-struck fields, the long roads unwinding,

You’re a great deal too fond of poking your nose into things that don’t concern you, making everybody in the house laugh, the moment your back is turned, and making yourself look like a fool every hour in the day. Be off; come!”

“Let me make this perfectly clear, Mr. Bumble,” his wife shot back. “We’ve got no use at all for your meddling here. You’re far too keen on sticking your nose into matters that are none of your business, making everyone in the house laugh at you the second your back is turned, and making a total fool of yourself every single hour of the day. Get moving, now!”

“Pooh! pooh! nonsense!” said the gentleman in the white waistcoat. “He’d be cheap with nothing at all, as a premium. Take him, you silly fellow! He’s just the boy for you. He wants the stick, now and then: it’ll do him good; and his board needn’t come very expensive, for he hasn’t been overfed since he was born. Ha! ha! ha!”

“Pshaw! pshaw! absolute rubbish!” said the stout functionary in the starched off-white waistcoat. “He’d be a bargain even if you paid not a farthing in premium. Take him, you daft fellow! He’s just the lad for you. He needs a good strike of the cane every so often: it’ll do him nothing but good; and his board won’t run you much at all, for he’s never been overfed a day since he was born. Ho! ho! ho!”

His lonely daughter, bereft of her final hope and reliance, appealed to them both too strongly. Again, as if by agreement, they looked at one another with one meaning in their faces.
