This assertion, however, was hardly borne out by the event. Again the instruments ended the tune; again they recommenced with as much fire and pathos as if it were the first strain. The air was now that one without any particular beginning, middle, or end, which perhaps, among all the dances which throng an inspired fiddler’s fancy, best conveys the idea of the interminable—the celebrated “Devil’s Dream.” The fury of personal movement that was kindled by the fury of the notes could be approximately imagined by these outsiders under the moon, from the occasional kicks of toes and heels against the door, whenever the whirl round had been of more than customary velocity.

That declaration, however, was scarcely justified by what followed. Once more the instruments brought the air to its close; once more they struck up again with all the same ardor and emotion, as though it were only their opening phrase. It was now that sort of tune with no discernible first act, middle, or finish, which, among all the dances that crowd a gifted fiddler’s imagination, perhaps most nearly suggests the endless—the much-vaunted “Old Nick’s Reverie.” The wildness of bodily motion awakened by the wildness of the music could be faintly divined by those observers in the moonlight from the rare thuds of toe and heel against the door, whenever the circling had grown faster than usual.

Yeobright had, in fact, found his vocation in the career of an itinerant open-air preacher and lecturer on morally unimpeachable subjects; and from this day he laboured incessantly in that office, speaking not only in simple language on Rainbarrow and in the hamlets round, but in a more cultivated strain elsewhere—from the steps and porticoes of town halls, from market-crosses, from conduits, on esplanades and on wharves, from the parapets of bridges, in barns and outhouses, and all other such places in the neighbouring Wessex towns and villages. He left alone creeds and systems of philosophy, finding enough and more than enough to occupy his tongue in the opinions and actions common to all good men. Some believed him, and some believed not; some said that his words were commonplace, others complained of his want of theological doctrine; while others again remarked that it was well enough for a man to take to preaching who could not see to do anything else. But everywhere he was kindly received, for the story of his life had become generally known.

The truth was, Yeobright had found his true calling working as a traveling open-air preacher and lecturer focusing on unassailably ethical topics. From that day forward, he threw himself into the work without pause: he spoke in plain, straightforward language at Rainbarrow and in the small surrounding hamlets, and switched to a more refined, educated tone for other engagements all across neighboring Wessex towns and villages—from the steps and porticos of town halls, at market crosses and public conduits, on seaside esplanades and dock wharves, from the low stone parapets of bridges, inside empty barns and outbuildings, and every other similar public spot he could access. He chose not to wade into debates over religious creeds or abstract philosophical systems, figuring there was more than enough material to fill his speeches in the shared values and everyday choices common to all people of good character. Audiences were split on his work: some took his words to heart, others dismissed them entirely; some wrote off his messaging as unoriginal, others grumbled that he neglected to include traditional theological doctrine, and still others remarked that preaching was a fine enough pursuit for a man who wasn’t capable of holding down any other line of work. Even so, he was greeted warmly everywhere he went, as the full story of his life had spread widely across the region.

I knew I had him. Hot Scotches he took, sitting at a table. For an hour he kept the Campbells coming. I sat by his side rapping for the waiter with my tail, and eating free lunch such as mamma in her flat never equalled with her homemade truck bought at a delicatessen store eight minutes before papa comes home.

I knew I had him then. He was taking Hot Scotches, seated at a table, and for a full hour he kept the Campbells coming. I sat at his elbow, thumping with my tail for the waiter, and making free with the lunch, such as mamma in her flat never matched, even with all her homemade truck, bought at the delicatessen store eight minutes before papa came home.

Martin’s hand instinctively closed on the piece of gold. In the same instant he knew he ought not to accept, and found himself struggling in the throes of indecision. That bit of gold meant food, life, and light in his body and brain, power to go on writing, and—who was to say?—maybe to write something that would bring in many pieces of gold. Clear on his vision burned the manuscripts of two essays he had just completed. He saw them under the table on top of the heap of returned manuscripts for which he had no stamps, and he saw their titles, just as he had typed them—“The High Priests of Mystery,” and “The Cradle of Beauty.” He had never submitted them anywhere. They were as good as anything he had done in that line. If only he had stamps for them! Then the certitude of his ultimate success rose up in him, an able ally of hunger, and with a quick movement he slipped the coin into his pocket.

Martin’s fingers reflexively tightened around the gold coin. Even as he did so, he knew he should refuse it, and he felt himself caught in a painful surge of indecision. That small piece of metal represented more than money; it was nourishment, vitality, and the fuel his mind required to continue his craft. It held the potential for him to create something that might eventually earn him a fortune. In his mind, he could clearly see the two essays he had just finished. They lay beneath his table, resting on a stack of rejected manuscripts he couldn't afford to mail. He envisioned their titles exactly as he had typed them: “The High Priests of Mystery” and “The Cradle of Beauty.” He hadn't sent them out yet, though they were among his finest work. If only he had the postage! A sudden, deep conviction in his future success rose to join his hunger, and with a swift motion, he slid the coin into his pocket.

Among these terrors, and the brood belonging to them, the Doctor walked with a steady head: confident in his power, cautiously persistent in his end, never doubting that he would save Lucie’s husband at last. Yet the current of the time swept by, so strong and deep, and carried the time away so fiercely, that Charles had lain in prison one year and three months when the Doctor was thus steady and confident. So much more wicked and distracted had the Revolution grown in that December month, that the rivers of the South were encumbered with the bodies of the violently drowned by night, and prisoners were shot in lines and squares under the southern wintry sun. Still, the Doctor walked among the terrors with a steady head. No man better known than he, in Paris at that day; no man in a stranger situation. Silent, humane, indispensable in hospital and prison, using his art equally among assassins and victims, he was a man apart. In the exercise of his skill, the appearance and the story of the Bastille Captive removed him from all other men. He was not suspected or brought in question, any more than if he had indeed been recalled to life some eighteen years before, or were a Spirit moving among mortals.

Amidst these monstrosities and the foul progeny they sired, the Healer moved with a fixed purpose: certain of his authority, patient in his design, never faltering in the conviction that he would ultimately rescue his son-in-law. Yet the deluge of the era surged past, so potent and bottomless, and dragged the days away with such violence, that Julian had languished in his cell for fifteen months even as the Healer stood firm in his hope. To such a degree of malice and frenzy had the Insurrection swelled by that bleak December, that the southern currents were clogged with the remains of those cast into the depths by night, and rows of captives were mowed down under the cold glare of the winter sun. Still, the Healer navigated the carnage with a tranquil spirit. No man was more familiar to the city in those dark hours; no man inhabited a more singular position. Muted, compassionate, and vital within both clinic and keep, bestowing his skill upon the slayer and the slain, he remained a figure isolated. Through the ministry of his art, the aura and the history of the Citadel Captive severed him from the rest of humanity. He was neither doubted nor challenged, appearing as one who had been summoned back from the void after twenty years of death, or as a specter gliding through the world of men.

Our analysis yields several interesting findings. (i) We show that, unlike in the monolingual reference-based setup, metrics that operate on contextualized representations generally do not outperform symbolic matching metrics like BLEU, which operate in the reference-based environment. (ii) We identify two reasons for this failure: (a) firstly, cross-lingual semantic mismatch, especially for multi-lingual BERT (M-BERT), which construes a shared multilingual space in an unsupervised fashion, without any direct bilingual signal; (b) secondly, the inability of the state-of-the-art crosslingual metrics based on multilingual encoders to adequately capture and punish "translationese", i.e., literal word-by-word translations of the source sentence-as translationese is an especially persistent property of MT systems, this problem is particularly troubling in our context of referencefree MT evaluation. (iii) We show that by executing an additional weakly-supervised cross-lingual re-mapping step, we can to some extent alleviate both previous issues. (iv) Finally, we show that the combination of cross-lingual reference-free metrics and language modeling on the target side (which is able to detect "translationese"), surpasses the performance of reference-based baselines.

metadata.title: On the Limitations of Cross-lingual Encoders as Exposed by Reference-Free Machine Translation Evaluation

text: Our analysis yields several notable findings. (i) We show that, unlike in the monolingual reference-based setting, metrics that operate on contextualized representations generally do not outperform symbolic matching metrics such as BLEU in the reference-based environment. (ii) We identify two reasons for this failure: (a) first, cross-lingual semantic mismatch, especially for multilingual BERT (M-BERT), which constructs a shared multilingual space in an unsupervised manner, without any direct bilingual signal; and (b) second, the inability of state-of-the-art crosslingual metrics based on multilingual encoders to adequately capture and penalize “translationese,” that is, literal word-for-word translations of the source sentence. Since translationese is an especially persistent property of MT systems, this problem is particularly troubling in our context of reference-free MT evaluation. (iii) We show that by introducing an additional weakly supervised cross-lingual remapping step, we can partially alleviate both of the preceding issues. (iv) Finally, we show that combining cross-lingual reference-free metrics with target-side language modeling, which can detect “translationese,” surpasses the performance of reference-based baselines.

“But you will just think—in kindness and condescension think—if you cannot bear with me as a husband! I fear I am too old for you, but believe me I will take more care of you than would many a man of your own age. I will protect and cherish you with all my strength—I will indeed! You shall have no cares—be worried by no household affairs, and live quite at ease, Miss Everdene. The dairy superintendence shall be done by a man—I can afford it well—you shall never have so much as to look out of doors at haymaking time, or to think of weather in the harvest. I rather cling to the chaise, because it is the same my poor father and mother drove, but if you don’t like it I will sell it, and you shall have a pony-carriage of your own. I cannot say how far above every other idea and object on earth you seem to me—nobody knows—God only knows—how much you are to me!”

“But you will only think—think of it kindly, and with forbearance—if you cannot bear with me as a husband! I am afraid I am too old for you; yet believe me, I would care for you more tenderly than many a man of your own years. I would shield and cherish you with all my power—I would indeed! You should have no burdens, be troubled by no domestic matters, and live in perfect comfort, Miss Everdene. The management of the dairy should be left to a man—I can well afford it—you should never have to look out of doors at haymaking time, nor give a thought to the weather at harvest. I am somewhat attached to the chaise, because it is the very one my poor father and mother used to drive; but if you dislike it, I will sell it, and you shall have a pony-carriage of your own. I cannot tell how far above every other thought and thing in the world you seem to me—no one knows—God alone knows how much you are to me!”

Miss Pross recalled soon afterwards, and to the end of her life remembered, that as she pressed her hands on Sydney’s arm and looked up in his face, imploring him to do no hurt to Solomon, there was a braced purpose in the arm and a kind of inspiration in the eyes, which not only contradicted his light manner, but changed and raised the man. She was too much occupied then with fears for the brother who so little deserved her affection, and with Sydney’s friendly reassurances, adequately to heed what she observed.

Miss Pross remembered afterward, and remembered to the end of her life, that as she laid her hands upon Sydney’s arm and looked up into his face, begging him not to injure Solomon, there was a resolute strength in the arm and a kind of inspiration in the eyes which not only belied his easy manner, but altered and ennobled the man. At the time, she was too much taken up with anxiety for the brother who so ill deserved her love, and with Sydney’s kindly assurances, to give full heed to what she saw.

text: When she reached the more wealthy quarter of the town, the streets were comparatively deserted; and here her headlong progress excited a still greater curiosity in the stragglers whom she hurried past. Some quickened their pace behind, as though to see whither she was hastening at such an unusual rate; and a few made head upon her, and looked back, surprised at her undiminished speed; but they fell off one by one; and when she neared her place of destination, she was alone.

When she came into the richer part of the town, the streets were for the most part empty; and there her breathless haste awakened a yet livelier wonder in the few idlers she swept by. Some quickened their steps behind her, as if to discover whither she could be bound at so extraordinary a pace; and a handful turned to meet her, then glanced after her, astonished at the unbroken swiftness of her course. But one after another they dropped away; and by the time she drew near the place she sought, she was alone.
