metadata.title: Torch-Struct: Deep Structured Prediction Library

text: Structured prediction has played a central role in the history of natural language processing. Representative methods include techniques for sequence labeling and segmentation (Lafferty et al., 2001; Sarawagi and Cohen, 2005), discriminative dependency and constituency parsing (Finkel et al., 2008; McDonald et al., 2005), unsupervised learning for labeling and alignment (Vogel et al., 1996; Goldwater and Griffiths, 2007), approximate translation decoding with beam search (Tillmann and Ney, 2003), among many others. Figure 1: Distribution of binary trees over a 1000-token sequence. Coloring indicates the marginal probabilities of each span. Torch-Struct is an optimized collection of common CRF distributions used in NLP, designed to integrate with deep learning frameworks.

Martin gathered together a number of carbon copies of his short stories, hesitated a moment, then added his “Sea Lyrics.” They mounted their wheels on a late June afternoon and rode for the hills. It was the second time he had been out with her alone, and as they rode along through the balmy warmth, just chilled by she sea-breeze to refreshing coolness, he was profoundly impressed by the fact that it was a very beautiful and well-ordered world and that it was good to be alive and to love. They left their wheels by the roadside and climbed to the brown top of an open knoll where the sunburnt grass breathed a harvest breath of dry sweetness and content.

Edgar collected a sheaf of carbon copies of his short tales, paused for a breath, and then slipped in his “Tide Songs.” In the waning light of a June evening they took to their bicycles and rode away toward the hills. It was the second time he had been alone with her, and as they moved on through the soft warmth, tempered now by the sea air into a pleasant coolness, he was deeply moved by the sense that the world was a fair and orderly place, and that to live and to love were good. They left their bicycles by the roadside and climbed to the brown crest of an open knoll, where the sun-parched grass seemed to exhale a dry, ripe sweetness and a kind of quiet content.

An explanation for why M-DiCoh outperforms ASeq and EAGrid on DailyDialog but not on SwitchBoard might be that the ASeq and EAGrid models explicitly use gold DA labels during evaluation but M-DiCoh does not; and the DA labels in SwitchBoard are about 10 times higher finegrained than those in DailyDialog (see Table 2 ). This interpretation becomes more concrete by observing a considerable reduction in the performance of ASeq and EAGrid when they are evaluated on DailyDialog compared with when they are evaluated on SwitchBoard. In contrast, our M-DiCoh, which uses DAs only during training to obtain better utterance vectors, performs almost evenly on both corpora. Since our model does not need DA labels during evaluations, it is more suitable than the examined models for evaluating dialogue coherence in real scenarios.

metadata.title: Dialogue Coherence Assessment Without Explicit Dialogue Act Labels

text: One possible reason why M-DiCoh outperforms ASeq and EAGrid on DailyDialog but not on SwitchBoard is that ASeq and EAGrid rely on gold dialogue act labels at evaluation time, whereas M-DiCoh does not; moreover, the dialogue act labels in SwitchBoard are approximately ten times more fine-grained than those in DailyDialog (see Table 2). This interpretation gains support from the marked drop in performance that ASeq and EAGrid exhibit when evaluated on DailyDialog relative to SwitchBoard. By contrast, our M-DiCoh, which uses dialogue acts solely during training to learn better utterance representations, performs comparably across the two corpora. Because our model does not require dialogue act labels at evaluation, it is better suited than the examined models for assessing dialogue coherence in real-world settings.

Clym took the hand which was already bared for him—it was a favourite way with them to walk bare hand in bare hand—and led her through the ferns. They formed a very comely picture of love at full flush, as they walked along the valley that late afternoon, the sun sloping down on their right, and throwing their thin spectral shadows, tall as poplar trees, far out across the furze and fern. Eustacia went with her head thrown back fancifully, a certain glad and voluptuous air of triumph pervading her eyes at having won by her own unaided self a man who was her perfect complement in attainment, appearance, and age. On the young man’s part, the paleness of face which he had brought with him from Paris, and the incipient marks of time and thought, were less perceptible than when he returned, the healthful and energetic sturdiness which was his by nature having partially recovered its original proportions. They wandered onward till they reached the nether margin of the heath, where it became marshy and merged in moorland.

Kael curled his fingers around the hand she had already slipped free of its glove to offer him—bare palm to bare palm was their preferred way of strolling, after all—and guided her through the thick drifts of ferns. They made a strikingly tender portrait of love in its full, glowing bloom as they picked their way down the valley that soft late afternoon, the sun sinking low on their right side, stretching their thin, ghostly silhouettes as high as white poplars to reach far across the gorse and fern fronds at their feet. Liora held her head tilted back in a dreamy, unburdened arc, a bright, giddy, almost luxurious edge of triumph lighting her eyes: she had won this man entirely on her own merit, a partner who matched her perfectly in intellect, bearing, and years. As for the young man, the faint pallor he had carried home with him from Lyon, and the first faint lines of weariness and deep thinking etched into his face, were far less visible than they had been in the first weeks of his return; the hearty, vigorous ruggedness that was his birthright had slowly reclaimed much of its old hold on his features. They meandered further on until they came to the lowermost edge of the heath, where the ground grew soft and waterlogged, bleeding seamlessly into the wide, boggy moor beyond.

“Very well, then,” said the parson. “And I will arrange that the funeral shall take place early to-morrow. Perhaps Mrs. Troy is right in feeling that we cannot treat a dead fellow-creature too thoughtfully. We must remember that though she may have erred grievously in leaving her home, she is still our sister: and it is to be believed that God’s uncovenanted mercies are extended towards her, and that she is a member of the flock of Christ.”

“Very well, then,” said the parson. “I will see that the funeral is held early tomorrow. Perhaps Mrs. Troy is right in thinking that we cannot be too considerate toward a dead fellow-creature. We must not forget that, although she may have sinned grievously in leaving her home, she is still our sister; and we may trust that God’s uncovenanted mercies are extended to her, and that she belongs to the flock of Christ.”

“Rot you!” murmured the man, in a horrible passion; between his clenched teeth; “if I had only had the courage to say the word, I might have been free of you in a night. Curses on your head, and black death on your heart, you imp! What are you doing here?”

“Damn you!” the fellow hissed, his fury barely contained behind locked jaws; “had I but possessed the spine to speak it aloud, I could have shed your company ere dawn. A blight upon your brow, and a slow rot upon your soul, you wretch! Why do you linger here?”

On reaching Casterbridge he left the horse and trap at an inn, and at five minutes before ten came back to the bridge at the lower end of the town, and sat himself upon the parapet. The clocks struck the hour, and no Fanny appeared. In fact, at that moment she was being robed in her grave-clothes by two attendants at the Union poorhouse—the first and last tiring-women the gentle creature had ever been honoured with. The quarter went, the half hour. A rush of recollection came upon Troy as he waited: this was the second time she had broken a serious engagement with him. In anger he vowed it should be the last, and at eleven o’clock, when he had lingered and watched the stone of the bridge till he knew every lichen upon their face and heard the chink of the ripples underneath till they oppressed him, he jumped from his seat, went to the inn for his gig, and in a bitter mood of indifference concerning the past, and recklessness about the future, drove on to Budmouth races.

When he reached Casterbridge, he left the horse and trap at an inn, then, five minutes before ten, returned to the bridge at the lower end of the town and took a seat on the parapet. The clocks struck the hour, and Fanny did not appear. At that very moment, in fact, she was being dressed in her grave-clothes by two attendants at the Union poorhouse—the first and last dressers the gentle girl had ever been privileged to have. The quarter hour passed, then the half hour. As Troy waited, a flood of memory came over him: this was the second time she had failed to keep a serious appointment with him. In anger, he swore it would be the last. By eleven o’clock, after he had sat there long enough to study the bridge stones until he knew every patch of lichen on their faces and to hear the ring of the ripples beneath them until the sound became unbearable, he sprang up, went back to the inn for his gig, and, in a bitter mood of indifference toward the past and carelessness about the future, drove on to the Budmouth races.

They turned into no house at Shepperton, as the weary boy had expected; but still kept walking on, in mud and darkness, through gloomy lanes and over cold open wastes, until they came within sight of the lights of a town at no great distance. On looking intently forward, Oliver saw that the water was just below them, and that they were coming to the foot of a bridge.

They did not stop at any cottage in Shepperton, though the exhausted child had hoped they would; instead they trudged onward, through mire and shadow, along sunken byways and across bleak, wind-scoured heaths, till at last the glimmer of a town rose before them, not far off. Peering hard into the distance, Oliver made out the dark gleam of water beneath their path and understood that they were nearing the base of a bridge.

Farmer Oak went towards the plantation and pushed through its lower boughs to the windy side. A dim mass under the slope reminded him that a shed occupied a place here, the site being a cutting into the slope of the hill, so that at its back part the roof was almost level with the ground. In front it was formed of board nailed to posts and covered with tar as a preservative. Through crevices in the roof and side spread streaks and dots of light, a combination of which made the radiance that had attracted him. Oak stepped up behind, where, leaning down upon the roof and putting his eye close to a hole, he could see into the interior clearly.

Farmer Oak approached the grove and maneuvered through the low branches until he reached the windward side. A shadowy form nestled against the hill signaled the location of a shed, built into an excavation in the rise so that the rear of its roof rested nearly even with the soil. The front consisted of wooden planks fastened to posts and treated with tar to withstand the elements. Rays and specks of light filtered through gaps in the walls and ceiling, forming the collective glow that had drawn his attention. Oak moved to the back, where he leaned over the roof and peered through a small hole, gaining a clear view of the scene inside.

“You should ’a’ ben up at Riley’s barn last night,” Jim announced inconsequently. “A lot of the fellers put on the gloves. There was a peach from West Oakland. They called ’m ‘The Rat.’ Slick as silk. No one could touch ’m. We was all wishin’ you was there. Where was you anyway?”

“You ought to have been up at Riley’s barn last night,” Jim said abruptly. “A bunch of the boys put on the gloves. There was a peach from West Oakland. They called him ‘The Rat.’ Slick as silk. Nobody could lay a hand on him. We were all wishing you’d been there. Where were you, anyway?”

“Not dead! He feared us so much--and with reason--that he caused himself to be represented as dead, and had a grand mock-funeral. But they have found him alive, hiding in the country, and have brought him in. I have seen him but now, on his way to the Hotel de Ville, a prisoner. I have said that he had reason to fear us. Say all! _Had_ he reason?”

He is not dead! He was so terrified of us—and with good cause—that he staged his own death and held a grand, fake funeral. But they found him alive, hiding in the provinces, and have dragged him back. I just saw him myself, a captive being led toward the Hotel de Ville. I told you he had reason to tremble before us. Speak up, everyone! Was his fear not justified?

“Well, my sweet,” said Miss Pross, nodding her head emphatically, “the short and the long of it is, that I am a subject of His Most Gracious Majesty King George the Third;” Miss Pross curtseyed at the name; “and as such, my maxim is, Confound their politics, Frustrate their knavish tricks, On him our hopes we fix, God save the King!”

“Well, my dear,” said Miss Pross, with a firm nod, “the plain truth is, I am a loyal subject of His Most Gracious Majesty King George the Third.” At the name, she dipped in a curtsey. “And as such, my motto is: Blast their schemes, Thwart their deceitful games, In him our trust remains, God save the King!”

As the resting man looked at the barrow he became aware that its summit, hitherto the highest object in the whole prospect round, was surmounted by something higher. It rose from the semiglobular mound like a spike from a helmet. The first instinct of an imaginative stranger might have been to suppose it the person of one of the Celts who built the barrow, so far had all of modern date withdrawn from the scene. It seemed a sort of last man among them, musing for a moment before dropping into eternal night with the rest of his race.

Watching the ancient mound, the reclining man saw that its summit, formerly the highest mark on the horizon, was crowned by something taller. It projected from the rounded earth like a crest upon a casque. A dreamer might have mistaken the silhouette for one of the prehistoric builders, so far had the modern world retreated from the view. He seemed a final sentinel of his people, lingering a moment in thought before falling into the same eternal shadow as the rest of his vanished kin.

“‘They are kind—they are the most excellent creatures in the world; but, unfortunately, they are prejudiced against me. I have good dispositions; my life has been hitherto harmless and in some degree beneficial; but a fatal prejudice clouds their eyes, and where they ought to see a feeling and kind friend, they behold only a detestable monster.’

“They’re kind, really—some of the best people you could ever come across. But unfortunately, they’re dead set against me for no reason other than blind prejudice. I’ve always had a gentle, generous nature; I’ve never hurt a soul up to this point, and I’ve even done my share of good for others when I had the chance. But that terrible, unshakable bias clouds their vision entirely. Where they ought to recognize a warm, caring friend, all they can make out is a loathsome, repulsive monster.”

Martin had ascended from pitch to pitch of intellectual living, and here he was at a higher pitch than ever. All the hidden things were laying their secrets bare. He was drunken with comprehension. At night, asleep, he lived with the gods in colossal nightmare; and awake, in the day, he went around like a somnambulist, with absent stare, gazing upon the world he had just discovered. At table he failed to hear the conversation about petty and ignoble things, his eager mind seeking out and following cause and effect in everything before him. In the meat on the platter he saw the shining sun and traced its energy back through all its transformations to its source a hundred million miles away, or traced its energy ahead to the moving muscles in his arms that enabled him to cut the meat, and to the brain wherewith he willed the muscles to move to cut the meat, until, with inward gaze, he saw the same sun shining in his brain. He was entranced by illumination, and did not hear the “Bughouse,” whispered by Jim, nor see the anxiety on his sister’s face, nor notice the rotary motion of Bernard Higginbotham’s finger, whereby he imparted the suggestion of wheels revolving in his brother-in-law’s head.

Martin had climbed rung after rung of intellectual elevation, and now he stood at a height he had never reached before. Every long-concealed truth was unfurling its hidden core to him. He was giddy, drunk on the sheer force of his new understanding. When he slept at night, he wandered beside gods in vast, thrumming nightmares; waking by day, he moved through the world like a sleepwalker, his gaze distant and unmoored, fixed on the startling new version of reality he had only just uncovered. Sitting at the dinner table, he did not register the chatter of small, mean concerns around him, his restless mind racing to map the lines of cause and effect woven into every object in his line of sight. Looking at the cut of meat on his plate, he could see the bright glow of the sun, tracing its energy backward through every step of transformation to its source a hundred million miles out in space, or following it forward to the flexing muscles in his arm that let him slice the meat, to the brain that sent the command for those muscles to move—until, with his mind’s eye, he could see that very same sun glowing inside his own skull. He was so lost in this flood of revelation that he did not catch Jim’s quiet, sneering whisper of “Bughouse,” nor spot the worried furrow of his sister’s brow, nor catch Bernard Higginbotham twirling a finger next to his temple, wordlessly insinuating that his brother-in-law’s wits had come unmoored.

She trembled, turned, and said “Good morning.” His tone was so utterly removed from all she had expected as a beginning. It was lowness and quiet accentuated: an emphasis of deep meanings, their form, at the same time, being scarcely expressed. Silence has sometimes a remarkable power of showing itself as the disembodied soul of feeling wandering without its carcase, and it is then more impressive than speech. In the same way, to say a little is often to tell more than to say a great deal. Boldwood told everything in that word.

She shivered, turned, and murmured a greeting. His voice was so completely different from the start she had imagined. It was a quality of profound, hushed stillness—an emphasis on deep significance that remained barely articulated. Silence sometimes possesses a striking ability to manifest as the naked spirit of emotion, wandering free from its physical shell, and it is then more powerful than any spoken word. In this manner, brevity often reveals more than an abundance of speech. Boldwood had conveyed everything in that single greeting.

Fagin put several other questions: all with the same drift of ascertaining whether the girl had profited by his unguarded hints; but, she answered them so readily, and was withal so utterly unmoved by his searching looks, that his original impression of her being more than a trifle in liquor, was confirmed. Nancy, indeed, was not exempt from a failing which was very common among the Jew’s female pupils; and in which, in their tenderer years, they were rather encouraged than checked. Her disordered appearance, and a wholesale perfume of Geneva which pervaded the apartment, afforded strong confirmatory evidence of the justice of the Jew’s supposition; and when, after indulging in the temporary display of violence above described, she subsided, first into dullness, and afterwards into a compound of feelings: under the influence of which she shed tears one minute, and in the next gave utterance to various exclamations of “Never say die!” and divers calculations as to what might be the amount of the odds so long as a lady or gentleman was happy, Mr. Fagin, who had had considerable experience of such matters in his time, saw, with great satisfaction, that she was very far gone indeed.

The old man pressed her with further inquiries, each designed to probe whether his careless disclosures had taken root; yet she parried them with such swift composure, and remained so wholly indifferent beneath his probing gaze, that his first suspicion—that she was rather more than slightly drunk—settled into certainty. Nancy, in truth, shared a frailty common among the master’s female wards, one that in their younger days was quietly fostered rather than suppressed. Her rumpled dress, and a thick reek of gin that saturated the close room, lent weighty corroboration to the old man’s judgment; and when, after that brief storm of temper already recounted, she sank first into a sullen stupor, and then into a muddled tide of emotion—weeping one moment, and the next crying out scraps like “Keep your spirits up!” and hazarding various reckonings on what the chances might be so long as a lady or gentleman stayed cheerful—Mr. Fagin, long seasoned in such scenes, observed with deep contentment that she was thoroughly gone.

The ultimate and saddest singularity of woman’s effort and invention was reached when, with a quickened breathing, she rose to a stooping posture, and, resting her two little arms upon the shoulders of the dog, leant firmly thereon, and murmured stimulating words. Whilst she sorrowed in her heart she cheered with her voice, and what was stranger than that the strong should need encouragement from the weak was that cheerfulness should be so well stimulated by such utter dejection. Her friend moved forward slowly, and she with small mincing steps moved forward beside him, half her weight being thrown upon the animal. Sometimes she sank as she had sunk from walking erect, from the crutches, from the rails. The dog, who now thoroughly understood her desire and her incapacity, was frantic in his distress on these occasions; he would tug at her dress and run forward. She always called him back, and it was now to be observed that the woman listened for human sounds only to avoid them. It was evident that she had an object in keeping her presence on the road and her forlorn state unknown.

The most grievous, singular apex of Elara’s desperate ingenuity arrived when, chest rising and falling fast, she pushed herself up into a hunched stance, rested her two slight forearms on the hound’s broad shoulders, leaned her weight steadily against him, and murmured soft, urging words under her breath. Though her chest ached with unspoken grief, her tone stayed bright and buoyant, and there was no stranger paradox than that a creature so sturdy should draw courage from one so frail, or that warm hope could be kindled by a spirit so crushed by despair. Her companion paced forward at a crawl, and she picked her way beside him in tiny, shuffling steps, half her mass bearing down on his broad frame. Every now and then her knees buckled, just as they had when she’d tried walking unassisted, when she’d relied on crutches, when she’d clung to roadside fence posts for support. The hound, who now fully grasped what she needed and how little she could manage on her own, grew nearly frantic with worry when she stumbled; he would yank at the hem of her skirt and dart a few paces ahead, as if trying to pull her onward. She always called him back to her side, and anyone watching might have noticed she now strained to catch the sound of other people only to veer out of their way. It was clear she was determined to keep her presence on the lonely lane and her wretched condition hidden from all passersby.

He found himself thinking of young Heinrich in the very manner, if with a lesser intensity, in which he thought about his own son, as of hopes senselessly destroyed. His mind took no note of the fact that Heinrich was an enemy, that by the reckoning of a "war of attrition" his death was balance and compensation for the death of Hugh. He went straight to the root fact that they had been gallant and kindly beings, and that the same thing had killed them both....

He caught himself reflecting on young Heinrich much as he did on his own boy, though with a shade less anguish—both of them, he felt, were bright futures pointlessly cut short. His thoughts brushed aside the reality that Heinrich was a foe, that in the cold arithmetic of a grinding war his death evened the scales for Hugh’s. He cut directly to the core truth: they had been brave and gentle souls, and the same force had taken them both.

When we arrived at that hut at mid-afternoon, we saw no signs of life about it. The field near by had been denuded of its crop some time before, and had a skinned look, so exhaustively had it been harvested and gleaned. Fences, sheds, everything had a ruined look, and were eloquent of poverty. No animal was around anywhere, no living thing in sight. The stillness was awful, it was like the stillness of death. The cabin was a one-story one, whose thatch was black with age, and ragged from lack of repair.

When we reached the hut in mid-afternoon, there was no sign of life around it. The nearby field had long since been stripped of its crop and wore a flayed, exhausted look, so thoroughly had it been reaped and gleaned. The fences, the sheds, everything had a broken-down appearance, speaking plainly of poverty. There was not a single animal anywhere, not a living thing in sight. The silence was dreadful, like the silence of death. The cabin was a one-story place, its thatch blackened by age and tattered from want of repair.

Data filtering tools are important to reduce the noise fed into machine learning algorithms such as the ones used in neural machine translation. This is especially true for data sets with suspicious sources like unrestricted web crawls or data sets that are automatically extracted from complex data formats such as PDF or HTML with all their different flavors and implementations. Cleaning parallel corpora is a special case in which not only the raw data but also the quality of alignment between source and target language needs to checked. The aligned translations drive the mapping from input to the output language as a strong supervision during the training steps, and the amount of noise will have a decisive impact on the adequacy of the translations. The effect is especially severe for low resource settings, in which little data is available, and each mistake might directly influence the end result.

metadata.title: OpusFilter: A Configurable Parallel Corpus Filtering Toolbox

text: Data filtering tools are important for reducing the noise fed into machine learning algorithms such as those used in neural machine translation. This is especially true for datasets with questionable sources, such as unrestricted web crawls, or datasets that are automatically extracted from complex data formats such as PDF or HTML, with all their varied flavors and implementations. Cleaning parallel corpora is a special case in which not only the raw data but also the quality of the alignment between source and target languages must be checked. The aligned translations drive the mapping from input to output language as a form of strong supervision during training, and the amount of noise has a decisive impact on translation adequacy. The effect is especially severe in low-resource settings, where little data is available and each mistake may directly influence the final result.

Mr. Bumble emerged at early morning from the workhouse-gate, and walked with portly carriage and commanding steps, up the High Street. He was in the full bloom and pride of beadlehood; his cocked hat and coat were dazzling in the morning sun; he clutched his cane with the vigorous tenacity of health and power. Mr. Bumble always carried his head high; but this morning it was higher than usual. There was an abstraction in his eye, an elevation in his air, which might have warned an observant stranger that thoughts were passing in the beadle’s mind, too great for utterance.

Mr. Bumble stepped out through the workhouse gate at dawn and strode up the High Street with a stately bearing and authoritative tread. He stood at the very peak of his beadle’s glory; his tricorne hat and coat gleamed brilliantly in the early sunlight, and he gripped his walking stick with the firm vigor of robust vitality. Mr. Bumble habitually held his chin aloft, but this morning it rose even higher than normal. A faraway look in his gaze and a loftiness in his demeanor might have signaled to any perceptive passerby that ideas were stirring in the beadle’s head—thoughts far too momentous to be spoken aloud.

“You have given me a glimpse of fairyland,” Martin said on the ferry-boat. “It makes life worth while to meet people like that. My mind is all worked up. I never appreciated idealism before. Yet I can’t accept it. I know that I shall always be a realist. I am so made, I guess. But I’d like to have made a reply to Kreis and Hamilton, and I think I’d have had a word or two for Norton. I didn’t see that Spencer was damaged any. I’m as excited as a child on its first visit to the circus. I see I must read up some more. I’m going to get hold of Saleeby. I still think Spencer is unassailable, and next time I’m going to take a hand myself.”

“You’ve let me peer into an enchanted realm,” Martin said as the ferry churned across the water. “Meeting people like that makes existence feel worthwhile. My thoughts are spinning. I’d never truly grasped idealism before. Yet I can’t embrace it. I know I’ll always be a realist—it’s simply how I’m built, I suppose. Still, I wish I’d answered Kreis and Hamilton, and I believe I’d have had a few things to say to Norton as well. I didn’t see Spencer suffer any real blow. I’m as thrilled as a child stepping into a circus for the first time. Clearly, I need to read more. I’m going to find Saleeby. I remain convinced Spencer is beyond reproach, and next time I intend to join the fray myself.”

Fair Isabel, poor simple Isabel!
Lorenzo, a young palmer in Love's eye!
They could not in the self-same mansion dwell
Without some stir of heart, some malady;
They could not sit at meals but feel how well
It soothed each to be the other by;
They could not, sure, beneath the same roof sleep
But to each other dream, and nightly weep.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
Sweet Isabel, so innocent and mild!
Lorenzo, Love's young pilgrim, pure and true!
Within one shared abode they could not bide
Without a heart's quick ache, a fevered hue;
They could not break their bread but felt inside
How nearness brought a solace fresh and new;
They could not, surely, rest through night's deep sweep
But dream each other's form, and nightly weep.

Eustacia again remained in a sort of stupefied silence. What curious feeling was this coming over her? Was it really possible that her interest in Wildeve had been so entirely the result of antagonism that the glory and the dream departed from the man with the first sound that he was no longer coveted by her rival? She was, then, secure of him at last. Thomasin no longer required him. What a humiliating victory! He loved her best, she thought; and yet—dared she to murmur such treacherous criticism ever so softly?—what was the man worth whom a woman inferior to herself did not value? The sentiment which lurks more or less in all animate nature—that of not desiring the undesired of others—was lively as a passion in the supersubtle, epicurean heart of Eustacia. Her social superiority over him, which hitherto had scarcely ever impressed her, became unpleasantly insistent, and for the first time she felt that she had stooped in loving him.

Eustacia stayed quiet once more, her mind foggy and unmoored. What was this strange, unnameable sensation creeping through her? Could her entire fixation on Wildeve have actually been nothing more than a product of rivalry, so that the magic and fantasy around him vanished the second she heard he was no longer her rival’s most desired prize? So she’d finally won him, no strings attached. Thomasin didn’t want him anymore. What a cheap, embarrassing win that felt like. He cared for her more than anyone, she told herself; and yet—could she even admit this quiet, disloyal thought to herself, let alone say it out loud?—what good was a man that someone she considered beneath her didn’t even think was worth keeping? That impulse that hums in almost every living thing, the reflex to not want what no one else is fighting for, burned as bright as any full-blown passion in Eustacia’s sharp, pleasure-seeking, over-sensitive soul. The gap between her social standing and his, which she’d barely ever noticed before, now pressed on her unpleasantly, impossible to ignore, and for the first time it hit her that she’d lowered herself by caring for him at all.

Mrs. Mann ushered the beadle into a small parlour with a brick floor; placed a seat for him; and officiously deposited his cocked hat and cane on the table before him. Mr. Bumble wiped from his forehead the perspiration which his walk had engendered, glanced complacently at the cocked hat, and smiled. Yes, he smiled. Beadles are but men: and Mr. Bumble smiled.

Mrs. Craik guided the parish officer into a snug sitting room paved with red tiles; she drew out a chair for his comfort, then, with bustling attention, laid his tricorn and walking stick upon the nearby sideboard. Mr. Grimes dabbed from his brow the moisture that the journey had summoned, cast a satisfied look at the tricorn, and allowed himself a grin. Indeed, he grinned. Parish officers are merely human: and Mr. Grimes grinned.

As I purpose to show in the sequel whether the white waistcoated gentleman was right or not, I should perhaps mar the interest of this narrative (supposing it to possess any at all), if I ventured to hint just yet, whether the life of Oliver Twist had this violent termination or no.

Whether the gentleman in the white waistcoat was correct is something I mean to reveal later in this account, and so—should this story hold even a scrap of interest—I would likely spoil it if I dared suggest, at this early point, whether Oliver Twist’s life came to such a brutal end or not.

“Sire, I have been misunderstood. I will explain. There are two kinds of prophecy. One is the gift to foretell things that are but a little way off, the other is the gift to foretell things that are whole ages and centuries away. Which is the mightier gift, do you think?”

“My Sovereign, I have been misread. Let me clarify. Foresight is of a dual nature. One form is the knack for discerning what is nearly at hand; the other is the power to perceive occurrences lost in the mists of remote centuries. Which of these talents, in your judgment, is the more potent?”

This cell was in shape and size something like an area cellar, only not so light. It was most intolerably dirty; for it was Monday morning; and it had been tenanted by six drunken people, who had been locked up, elsewhere, since Saturday night. But this is little. In our station-houses, men and women are every night confined on the most trivial charges—the word is worth noting—in dungeons, compared with which, those in Newgate, occupied by the most atrocious felons, tried, found guilty, and under sentence of death, are palaces. Let any one who doubts this, compare the two.

The chamber’s dimensions evoked a subterranean scullery, though it was deprived of even that modest light. An insufferable grime pervaded the space, for it was now Monday, and the room had served as a pen for six intoxicated souls since their apprehension on Saturday evening. Yet this is a trifling matter. In these local watch-houses, citizens are nightly immured for the most petty transgressions—a word deserving of particular attention—within pits so dismal that the cells of Newgate, inhabited by the most monstrous of condemned convicts, would seem like grand estates in contrast. Should any man harbor doubts, let him merely set the two side by side.

“Silence!” cried Mr. Bumble, sternly. “Take yourself downstairs, ma’am. Noah, you shut up the shop; say another word till your master comes home, at your peril; and, when he does come home, tell him that Mr. Bumble said he was to send a old woman’s shell after breakfast tomorrow morning. Do you hear sir? Kissing!” cried Mr. Bumble, holding up his hands. “The sin and wickedness of the lower orders in this porochial district is frightful! If Parliament don’t take their abominable courses under consideration, this country’s ruined, and the character of the peasantry gone for ever!” With these words, the beadle strode, with a lofty and gloomy air, from the undertaker’s premises.

“Quiet!” thundered Master Grimshaw, his voice like a lash. “Remove yourself to the cellar, woman. Silas, bar the door of the workshop; breathe no syllable until your employer returns, if you value your hide; and the moment he sets foot inside, inform him that Master Grimshaw commands the delivery of a pauper’s coffin directly after sunrise. Are you attending, boy? Embracing!” cried Master Grimshaw, flinging his arms aloft. “The depravity and corruption among the rabble of this parish beggars belief! Unless the Crown takes their scandalous habits in hand, the realm is doomed, and the very soul of the labouring class extinguished forever!” And with that, the parish officer swept, with a grand and baleful stride, out of the coffin-maker’s shop.

In fact, Mr. Fagin had so well humoured his young friend’s eccentric disposition, that Master Bates, who had at first been disposed to consider the imprisoned Dodger rather in the light of a victim, now looked upon him as the chief actor in a scene of most uncommon and exquisite humour, and felt quite impatient for the arrival of the time when his old companion should have so favourable an opportunity of displaying his abilities.

Indeed, the elder had so deftly manipulated the lad’s capricious humours that young Tom, who had previously been inclined to see the incarcerated Stripling as a mere object of pity, now perceived him as the protagonist of a most rare and hilarious spectacle. He grew quite eager for the day when his old comrade should be afforded so grand a stage for the exercise of his particular gifts.

In the short time he had had to collect his senses, the boy had firmly resolved that, whether he died in the attempt or not, he would make one effort to dart upstairs from the hall, and alarm the family. Filled with this idea, he advanced at once, but stealthily.

In the fleeting interval permitted him to master his terror, the lad had forged a grim determination that, regardless of the mortal peril, he would stake everything on a single rush for the staircase to awaken the household. Possessed by this desperate intent, he stole forward on the instant, treading with the silent caution of a shadow.

I met a Lady in the Meads
Full beautiful, a faery's child,
Her hair was long, her foot was light
And her eyes were wild.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
I met a lady in the meads,  
So lovely, like a fairy’s child;  
Her hair was long, her step was light,  
And in her eyes there burned a wild.

Training. On the Universal Dependencies treebanks, we tuned all hyper-parameters on several large treebanks and applied them to all other treebanks. We used the word2vec embeddings released as part of the 2018 UD Shared Task (Zeman et al., 2018) , or the fastText embeddings (Bojanowski et al., 2017) whenever word2vec is not available. For the character-level language models in the NER component, we pretrained them on a mix of the Common Crawl and Wikipedia dumps, and the news corpora released by the WMT19 Shared Task (Barrault et al., 2019) , except for English and Chinese, for which we pretrained on the Google One Billion Word (Chelba et al., 2013) and the Chi-nese Gigaword corpora 5 , respectively. We again applied the same hyper-parameters to models for all languages.

metadata.title: Sta n z a : A Python Natural Language Processing Toolkit for Many Human Languages

text: For training on the Universal Dependencies treebanks, we tuned all hyper-parameters on several large treebanks and then applied them across all remaining treebanks. We employed the word2vec embeddings provided for the 2018 UD Shared Task (Zeman et al., 2018), or, when these were unavailable, the fastText embeddings (Bojanowski et al., 2017). For the character-level language models within the NER component, we pretrained on a combination of Common Crawl and Wikipedia dumps, together with the news corpora from the WMT19 Shared Task (Barrault et al., 2019), except for English and Chinese. For English, we pretrained on the Google One Billion Word corpus (Chelba et al., 2013), and for Chinese, on the Chinese Gigaword corpus. We again applied the same hyper-parameters to the models for all languages.

“Well, well,” said the beadle, evidently gratified with the compliment; “perhaps I may be. Perhaps I may be, Mrs. Mann.” He finished the gin-and-water, and added, “Oliver being now too old to remain here, the board have determined to have him back into the house. I have come out myself to take him there. So let me see him at once.”

Quite so," the beadle remarked, clearly flattered by the praise. "I suppose I may be, Mrs. Mann." He drained the remainder of his gin and water before adding, "Since Oliver is now too old to stay on here, the board has decided he must return to the workhouse. I have come personally to escort him back. So, bring him out to me at once.

“Gabriel,” she said, automatically, when he entered, turning up a face of which only the well-known lines remained to tell him it was hers, all else in the picture having faded quite. “Ride to Casterbridge instantly for a surgeon. It is, I believe, useless, but go. Mr. Boldwood has shot my husband.”

“Elias,” she murmured mechanically as he crossed the threshold, tilting up a visage so hollowed that only its familiar architecture remained to mark her identity, all other color having vanished from the portrait. “Ride to Blackwood this instant for a physician. It is, I fear, a futile errand, but go. Mr. Sterling has shot my husband.”

They had spoken about the Canadians who had come up and relieved the Essex men after the fight in which Teddy had been captured. And then it was manifest that Mr. Direck was talking of his regiment. "I'm not the only American who has gone Canadian--for the duration of the war."

The conversation had shifted to the battalions from the North who had marched up to replace the Kentish ranks following the skirmish in which Julian was taken. It soon grew apparent that Mr. Thorne was speaking of his own brothers-in-arms. "I am hardly the solitary American to have pledged himself to the Dominion—for the length of the struggle."

“See, here, Wiggle-and-Skip,” I says, “you know that it ain’t the nature of a real man to play dry nurse to a dog in public. I never saw one leashed to a bow-wow yet that didn’t look like he’d like to lick every other man that looked at him. But your boss comes in every day as perky and set up as an amateur prestidigitator doing the egg trick. How does he do it? Don’t tell me he likes it.”

“Look, Wiggle-and-Skip,” I said, “you’re well aware that no genuine man would be caught dead babysitting a dog out in the open. Every fellow I’ve ever seen tethered to a mutt had the expression of someone itching to take a swing at anyone who so much as glanced his way. Yet your employer strolls in daily, as chipper and self-assured as a novice magician pulling off the egg illusion. What’s his secret? Don’t try to convince me he enjoys it.”

The tenderest and softest phases of Bathsheba’s nature were prominent now—advanced impulsively for his acceptance, without any of the disguises and defences which the wariness of her character when she was cool too frequently threw over them. Few men could have resisted the arch yet dignified entreaty of the beautiful face, thrown a little back and sideways in the well known attitude that expresses more than the words it accompanies, and which seems to have been designed for these special occasions. Had the woman not been his wife, Troy would have succumbed instantly; as it was, he thought he would not deceive her longer.

Now the gentlest and most yielding aspects of Bathsheba’s heart came to the fore—offered freely for his taking, stripped of the guardedness and pretense that her cooler prudence so often cast over them. Few men could have withstood that lovely face, tilted slightly back and aside in the familiar pose that says more than the words it frames, a look seemingly made for just such moments. Had she not been his wife, Troy would have yielded at once; as things stood, he resolved he would deceive her no more.

With the same perversity of feeling and irresolution that had fastened upon him, despite himself, all day, the murderer, finding that he was not followed, and that they most probably considered him some drunken sullen fellow, turned back up the town, and getting out of the glare of the lamps of a stage-coach that was standing in the street, was walking past, when he recognised the mail from London, and saw that it was standing at the little post-office. He almost knew what was to come; but he crossed over, and listened.

The murderer had been gripped all day by a strange, stubborn irresolution that clung to him against his will. Realizing no one pursued him and that the passersby likely dismissed him as some sullen drunk, he retraced his steps through the town. Steering clear of the bright lamps of a stage-coach waiting in the street, he moved past it—then halted. He recognized the London mail, standing at the little post-office. Some presentiment of what would follow stirred in him, yet he crossed over and listened.

Bang! One saddle empty. Bang! another one. Bang--bang, and I bagged two. Well, it was nip and tuck with us, and I knew it. If I spent the eleventh shot without convincing these people, the twelfth man would kill me, sure. And so I never did feel so happy as I did when my ninth downed its man and I detected the wavering in the crowd which is premonitory of panic. An instant lost now could knock out my last chance. But I didn't lose it. I raised both revolvers and pointed them--the halted host stood their ground just about one good square moment, then broke and fled.

Crack! One rider tumbled from his saddle. Crack! a second followed. Crack—crack, and I had dropped two more. The contest was balanced on a knife's edge, and I was fully aware of it. If I fired my eleventh round without breaking their resolve, the twelfth man would surely be the death of me. So you can imagine the rush of relief when my ninth shot brought down its target and I caught that subtle tremor in the group that signals the onset of panic. A single wasted second now could forfeit my final opportunity. But I didn't waste it. I leveled both pistols and took aim—the stalled assembly held firm for just about one solid, square instant, then shattered and scattered in flight.

Mr. Giles walked into the corner with much importance, and some wonder, and was honoured with a short whispering conference with the doctor, on the termination of which, he made a great many bows, and retired with steps of unusual stateliness. The subject matter of this conference was not disclosed in the parlour, but the kitchen was speedily enlightened concerning it; for Mr. Giles walked straight thither, and having called for a mug of ale, announced, with an air of majesty, which was highly effective, that it had pleased his mistress, in consideration of his gallant behaviour on the occasion of that attempted robbery, to deposit, in the local savings-bank, the sum of five-and-twenty pounds, for his sole use and benefit. At this, the two women-servants lifted up their hands and eyes, and supposed that Mr. Giles, pulling out his shirt-frill, replied, “No, no”; and that if they observed that he was at all haughty to his inferiors, he would thank them to tell him so. And then he made a great many other remarks, no less illustrative of his humility, which were received with equal favour and applause, and were, withal, as original and as much to the purpose, as the remarks of great men commonly are.

Mr. Giles approached the corner with significant gravity and a touch of intrigue, where he was favored with a brief, whispered dialogue with the doctor. Once the exchange concluded, he performed a series of elaborate bows and departed with a stride of remarkable stateliness. Though the nature of this conversation was kept secret from those in the parlor, the kitchen was quickly informed of its contents. Mr. Giles headed there at once, and after calling for a mug of ale, declared with an effective air of grandeur that his mistress, moved by his brave defense during the attempted burglary, had deposited twenty-five pounds into the local savings bank for his exclusive use. At this news, the two maidservants raised their hands in wonder. Mr. Giles, smoothing his shirt-front, demurred with a "No, no," requesting that they inform him if he ever appeared too arrogant toward his social inferiors. He then offered several other comments intended to showcase his humility; these were received with great acclaim and were, in truth, about as insightful and relevant as the pronouncements of great men typically tend to be.

These results call into question the van Schijndel and and Prasad et al. (2019) syntactic priming paradigm's ability to distinguish models which represent syntax from those which rely on shallow phenomena by achieving a positive result with two non-syntactic baseline models. First, success in the priming paradigm is measured by whether or not adaptation reduces surprisal, but not by how much, so even though both baseline models tested here reduce surprisal by less than PvSL's models on average, they still pass the success criterion. To put it another way, PvSL report quantitative results but do not actually establish what would constitute a meaningful effect size. Even though the effect sizes of both our baseline replications were smaller, PvSL could have reported the results from our baseline models instead of their actual model and drawn the same conclusions.

metadata.title: Overestimation of Syntactic Representation in Neural Language Models

text: These findings challenge the ability of the syntactic priming paradigm introduced by van Schijndel and Prasad et al. (2019) to differentiate models that encode syntax from those that depend on superficial cues, given that two non-syntactic baseline models also yield a positive result. First, success in the priming paradigm is defined solely by whether adaptation lowers surprisal, not by the magnitude of that reduction; consequently, although both baseline models examined here reduce surprisal by less than the models in PvSL on average, they nonetheless meet the success criterion. In other words, PvSL present quantitative outcomes but never specify what would count as a meaningful effect size. Even though the effect sizes from our baseline replications were smaller, PvSL could have substituted the results from our baseline models for those of their actual model and still reached the same conclusions.

The main goal of this paper is to predict the MWF of morphological derivatives (i.e., how likely is a word to be formed as an extension of a lexical cluster) by directly leveraging associative information. Since links in the mental lexicon reflect semantic and formal similarities of various sorts, many of which are not morphological (Tamariz, 2008) , we want to create a distilled model of the mental lexicon that only contains derivational information. One way to achieve this is by means of a derivational projection of the mental lexicon, a network that we call the Derivational Graph (DG).

metadata.title: A Graph Auto-encoder Model of Derivational Morphology

text: The primary objective of this paper is to predict the morphological word frequency (MWF) of derivatives—specifically, the probability that a word will emerge as an extension of a lexical cluster—by directly utilizing associative information. Given that connections within the mental lexicon encompass various semantic and formal similarities, many of which are not morphological (Tamariz, 2008), we aim to construct a distilled model of the mental lexicon restricted to derivational information. This is achieved through a derivational projection of the mental lexicon, a network architecture we designate as the Derivational Graph (DG).

We trained our DIALOGPT model on the basis of the GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2018) architecture.The GPT-2 transformer model adopts the generic transformer language model (Vaswani et al., 2017) and leverages a stack of masked multi-head selfattention layers to train on massive web-text data. The text generated either from scratch or based on a user-specific prompt is realistic-looking. The success of GPT-2 demonstrates that a transformer language model is able to characterize human language data distributions at a fine-grained level, presumably due to large large model capacity and superior efficiency.

metadata.title: DIALOGPT : Large-Scale Generative Pre-training for Conversational Response Generation

text: We built our DIALOGPT model upon the GPT-2 architecture (Radford et al., 2018). The GPT-2 transformer employs a standard transformer language model design (Vaswani et al., 2017) and uses a series of masked multi-head self-attention layers, trained on extensive web-text corpora. Text produced by the model—whether generated from scratch or conditioned on a user-provided prompt—appears convincingly natural. The performance of GPT-2 indicates that a transformer-based language model can capture the fine-grained distributional properties of human language, likely owing to its large model capacity and high representational efficiency.

At eleven o’clock he went to bed himself, smoked for some time in his bedroom, put out his light at half-past eleven, and then, as was his invariable custom, pulled up the blind before getting into bed, that he might see which way the wind blew on opening his eyes in the morning, his bedroom window commanding a view of the flagstaff and vane. Just as he had lain down he was surprised to observe the white pole of the staff flash into existence like a streak of phosphorus drawn downwards across the shade of night without. Only one explanation met this—a light had been suddenly thrown upon the pole from the direction of the house. As everybody had retired to rest the old man felt it necessary to get out of bed, open the window softly, and look to the right and left. Eustacia’s bedroom was lighted up, and it was the shine from her window which had lighted the pole. Wondering what had aroused her, he remained undecided at the window, and was thinking of fetching the letter to slip it under her door, when he heard a slight brushing of garments on the partition dividing his room from the passage.

He headed to bed at eleven, smoking for a while before turning out his lamp at eleven-thirty. Following his nightly routine, he raised the blind before climbing under the covers so he could check the wind's direction upon waking, as his window looked directly out at the flagstaff and its weather vane. He had just settled in when he was startled to see the white staff suddenly flare into view, appearing like a stroke of phosphorus painted against the dark night. There was only one possible cause: a light had suddenly been cast onto the pole from somewhere in the house. Since everyone else had already turned in for the night, the old man felt compelled to get back up, ease the window open, and peer outside. Eustacia's room was illuminated, and it was the glow from her window that had lit up the mast. Puzzled by what might have woken her, he stood there hesitating, considering whether to get the letter and slide it under her door, when he detected the faint rustle of clothing against the wall in the hallway.

“Don’t make any mistake, Dell,” he said, “about me. I don’t think there’s anything in the way of a haircut or a shave or a shampoo that could make me like my girl any less. But if you’ll unwrap that package you may see why you had me going a while at first.”

“Let’s be clear, Dell,” he said, “about where I stand. Nothing as simple as a trim or a shave or a wash could ever dim how I feel about my girl. But go on and open that package—then you’ll understand why you nearly had me fooled at the start.”

Mr. Fagin saw, with delight, that this tribute to his powers was no mere compliment, but that he had really impressed his recruit with a sense of his wily genius, which it was most important that he should entertain in the outset of their acquaintance. To strengthen an impression so desirable and useful, he followed up the blow by acquainting him, in some detail, with the magnitude and extent of his operations; blending truth and fiction together, as best served his purpose; and bringing both to bear, with so much art, that Mr. Bolter’s respect visibly increased, and became tempered, at the same time, with a degree of wholesome fear, which it was highly desirable to awaken.

Mr. Fenwick perceived, with great satisfaction, that this homage to his abilities was no empty civility, but that he had in truth impressed his new associate with a due sense of his crafty powers—a conviction it was of the first importance to plant at the very beginning of their acquaintance. To confirm an impression so profitable and so agreeable, he pursued the advantage by setting forth, with considerable particularity, the scale and compass of his undertakings; mingling fact with invention as best suited his ends, and employing both with such dexterity that Mr. Haskett’s admiration plainly deepened, while it acquired, at the same time, a salutary touch of apprehension, which it was very desirable to inspire.

He dropped his arms upon the table and buried his face in them. His throat was aching, and he wanted to cry. It reminded him of his first fight, when he was six years old, when he punched away with the tears running down his cheeks while the other boy, two years his elder, had beaten and pounded him into exhaustion. He saw the ring of boys, howling like barbarians as he went down at last, writhing in the throes of nausea, the blood streaming from his nose and the tears from his bruised eyes.

He let his arms fall onto the table and pressed his face into them. A raw ache filled his throat, and tears threatened to come. The sensation carried him back to his first brawl at six years old—swinging his fists blindly while tears streaked his cheeks, as a boy two years older battered him until he could barely stand. He could still see the circle of boys, shrieking like savages when he finally collapsed, twisting in waves of sickness, blood pouring from his nose and tears spilling from his swollen eyes.

Additional information Roughly half of the puzzles contain remarks on individual characters and diacritics in the inventory of the foreign language, e.g. "In the following orthography a colon (:) marks a long vowel, and the P symbol marks a glottal stop (like the sound in the middle of uh-oh)". In many cases, the instructions state that these are pronunciation notes, i.e. they are made available only to allow the participants to vocalize the words they see on the page. On some occasions, however, they might introduce a character that is not present in the training data, but is relevant to the translation of the test sentences, e.g. the voiceless counterpart of a voiced consonant in a language with voice assimilation. As this kind of information cannot be mapped to the parallel data format, we include it in a separate field in the JSON files, directly as it Source balan waymin bambun baNgu jugaNgu jamiman.

metadata.title: PuzzLing Machines: A Challenge on Learning From Small Data

text: Approximately half of the puzzles include commentary on specific characters and diacritics found in the foreign language’s inventory, for example: “In the following orthography a colon (:) marks a long vowel, and the P symbol marks a glottal stop (like the sound in the middle of uh-oh).” In many instances, the instructions clarify that these serve as pronunciation notes—that is, they are provided solely to enable participants to vocalize the words they encounter on the page. Occasionally, however, such notes may introduce a character absent from the training data yet pertinent to translating the test sentences, such as the voiceless counterpart of a voiced consonant in a language exhibiting voice assimilation. Because this type of information cannot be accommodated within the parallel data format, we store it in a distinct field in the JSON files, presented exactly as it Source balan waymin bambun baNgu jugaNgu jamiman.

At this time of the year it was pleasanter to walk by night than by day, and when Clym had been absent about an hour she suddenly resolved to go out in the direction of Blooms-End, on the chance of meeting him on his return. When she reached the garden gate she heard wheels approaching, and looking round beheld her grandfather coming up in his car.

At this season, strolling under the stars felt far more agreeable than in the harsh light of day, and after Clym had been gone for roughly an hour, she abruptly made up her mind to venture toward Blooms-End, hoping she might encounter him on his way back. Just as she arrived at the garden gate, the sound of wheels reached her ears, and turning, she saw her grandfather approaching in his carriage.

When I came to again, I was sitting under an oak tree, on the grass, with a whole beautiful and broad country landscape all to myself--nearly. Not entirely; for there was a fellow on a horse, looking down at me--a fellow fresh out of a picture-book. He was in old-time iron armor from head to heel, with a helmet on his head the shape of a nail-keg with slits in it; and he had a shield, and a sword, and a prodigious spear; and his horse had armor on, too, and a steel horn projecting from his forehead, and gorgeous red and green silk trappings that hung down all around him like a bedquilt, nearly to the ground.

When I came to myself again, I was seated on the grass beneath an oak tree, with a whole broad, lovely stretch of country spread out before me—almost to myself. Not quite, for there was a man on horseback looking down at me, a man who seemed to have stepped straight out of a storybook. He was clad from head to foot in ancient iron armor, with a helmet upon his head shaped like a nail keg and slit for sight; he carried a shield, a sword, and a monstrous spear, and even his horse wore armor, with a steel horn jutting from its forehead and splendid red-and-green silk trappings hanging all about it like a bedspread, almost to the ground.

“Why,” said he, “a ‘Man About Town’ is something between a ‘rounder’ and a ‘clubman.’ He isn’t exactly—well, he fits in between Mrs. Fish’s receptions and private boxing bouts. He doesn’t—well, he doesn’t belong either to the Lotos Club or to the Jerry McGeogheghan Galvanised Iron Workers’ Apprentices’ Left Hook Chowder Association. I don’t exactly know how to describe him to you. You’ll see him everywhere there’s anything doing. Yes, I suppose he’s a type. Dress clothes every evening; knows the ropes; calls every policeman and waiter in town by their first names. No; he never travels with the hydrogen derivatives. You generally see him alone or with another man.”

“Well,” he said, “a ‘Man About Town’ is a cross between a professional carouser and a high-society regular. He doesn’t quite—well, he exists somewhere between elite gala receptions and gritty underground prize fights. He doesn’t belong to the most prestigious private clubs, but he isn’t a member of some local blue-collar workers’ union either. I’m not entirely sure how to define him for you. You’ll find him wherever there’s something happening. I suppose he is a specific type. He’s in formal wear every night, knows the city's inner workings, and is on a first-name basis with every police officer and waiter in town. No, he doesn't hang around with the flashy, superficial crowd. You’ll generally see him on his own or with another man.”

Meanwhile a little drama had been acted outside which saved Eustacia from all possibility of compromising herself that evening at least. Whilst Wildeve had been preparing his moth-signal another person had come behind him up to the gate. This man, who carried a gun in his hand, looked on for a moment at the other’s operation by the window, walked up to the house, knocked at the door, and then vanished round the corner and over the hedge.

In the meantime, a brief drama unfolded outside that spared Eustacia from any risk of compromising herself that night. While Wildeve was busy arranging his moth-signal, another figure approached the gate behind him. This man, carrying a gun, watched the other’s activities at the window for a moment, then stepped up to the house and knocked. He quickly slipped around the corner and vanished over the hedge.

“I’ll tell you,” said Yeobright with unexpected earnestness. “I am not sorry to have the opportunity. I’ve come home because, all things considered, I can be a trifle less useless here than anywhere else. But I have only lately found this out. When I first got away from home I thought this place was not worth troubling about. I thought our life here was contemptible. To oil your boots instead of blacking them, to dust your coat with a switch instead of a brush—was there ever anything more ridiculous? I said.”

“I’ll explain,” Yeobright said with a sudden, intense sincerity. “I’m actually glad for the chance to say this. I’ve moved back because, looking at the big picture, I might be slightly more useful here than in any other place. But that’s a realization I’ve only come to recently. When I first left home, I believed this area wasn't worth a second thought. I viewed our way of life here as beneath me. Conditioning your boots with oil instead of polish, or beating the dust off your jacket with a twig rather than a proper brush—I used to ask myself if anything could be more absurd.”

How Mr. Brownlow went on, from day to day, filling the mind of his adopted child with stores of knowledge, and becoming attached to him, more and more, as his nature developed itself, and showed the thriving seeds of all he wished him to become—how he traced in him new traits of his early friend, that awakened in his own bosom old remembrances, melancholy and yet sweet and soothing—how the two orphans, tried by adversity, remembered its lessons in mercy to others, and mutual love, and fervent thanks to Him who had protected and preserved them—these are all matters which need not to be told. I have said that they were truly happy; and without strong affection and humanity of heart, and gratitude to that Being whose code is Mercy, and whose great attribute is Benevolence to all things that breathe, happiness can never be attained.

How Mr. Harcourt passed from day to day, storing the mind of his adopted boy with knowledge, and growing more and more fond of him as his character unfolded itself, and revealed the flourishing seeds of all he would fain have him become; how he discerned in him fresh lineaments of his earliest friend, and these recalled to his own breast memories long past, sad indeed, yet gentle and consoling; how the two children of misfortune, taught by suffering, remembered its lesson in kindness to others, in tenderness for one another, and in grateful praise to Him who had watched over and preserved them—these are matters there is no need to tell. I have said that they were truly happy; and without deep affection and a humane heart, and thankfulness to that Being whose law is Mercy, and whose chief attribute is goodness toward all that lives and breathes, happiness can never be won.

The jailer lit the small heap, and it flared up fiercely, bright and warm. Bending once more to pass through the low, curved doorway, they abandoned the fire as it burned and made their way back to the yard; their hearing seemed to return gradually as they descended, until they stood again amid the roaring deluge.

“Of course they can,” replied Sikes. “It was Bartlemy time when I was shopped; and there warn’t a penny trumpet in the fair, as I couldn’t hear the squeaking on. Arter I was locked up for the night, the row and din outside made the thundering old jail so silent, that I could almost have beat my brains out against the iron plates of the door.”

“Right enough, they do,” growled Thorne. “It was the week of the Great Fair when I was pinched; and there wasn’t a brass horn in the stalls whose thin bleat didn’t reach me. Once I was barred in for the dark hours, the roar and ruckus beyond the walls made that grim dungeon so stiflingly quiet that I could have cracked my skull against the iron-plated door for the madness of it.”

They sat down, and Mr. Direck opened his case. He was shy and clumsy about opening it, because he had been thinking dreadfully hard about it, and he hated to seem heavy or profound or anything but artless and spontaneous to Cecily. And he felt even when he did open his case that the effect of it was platitudinous and disappointing. Yet when he had thought it out it had seemed very profound and altogether living.

They settled into their seats, and Mr. Direck began to lay out his argument. He fumbled with the task, self-conscious and awkward, for he had been brooding over it with painful intensity, and he dreaded appearing ponderous or overly philosophical—anything other than natural and unguarded in Cecily’s eyes. Even once he had presented his case, he sensed that it came across as trite and underwhelming. And yet, when he had first worked through it in his mind, it had struck him as deeply meaningful and vividly alive.

text: The Weatherbury Farms, however, were no such long distance from the hill, and those arrangements were not necessary in their case. But the large united flocks of Bathsheba and Farmer Boldwood formed a valuable and imposing multitude which demanded much attention, and on this account Gabriel, in addition to Boldwood’s shepherd and Cain Ball, accompanied them along the way, through the decayed old town of Kingsbere, and upward to the plateau,—old George the dog of course behind them.

The Weatherbury estates were not a great distance from the hill, rendering such complex arrangements unnecessary for their journey. Yet the combined flocks of Bathsheba and Farmer Boldwood comprised a vast and precious assembly that required considerable care; for this reason, Gabriel, accompanied by Boldwood’s shepherd and Cain Ball, escorted them through the ancient, fading town of Kingsbere and up toward the plateau, with old George the dog following steadily in their wake.

It was a soft, reposeful summer landscape, as lovely as a dream, and as lonesome as Sunday. The air was full of the smell of flowers, and the buzzing of insects, and the twittering of birds, and there were no people, no wagons, there was no stir of life, nothing going on. The road was mainly a winding path with hoof-prints in it, and now and then a faint trace of wheels on either side in the grass--wheels that apparently had a tire as broad as one's hand.

It was a gentle, hushed summer scene, lovely as a vision and lonely as a Sabbath morning. The air was laden with the fragrance of blossoms, the hum of insects, and the light chirping of birds; yet there were no people, no carts, no movement of life at all, nothing stirring anywhere. The road was little more than a meandering track marked by hoofprints, with here and there a dim wheel-rut showing in the grass on either side—ruts made by wheels that seemed to wear tires as broad as a hand.

He nodded, twice or thrice, to a passing friend; and, resisting as many invitations to take a morning dram, pressed steadily onward, until they were clear of the turmoil, and had made their way through Hosier Lane into Holborn.

Mr. Sikes hauled Oliver along, shoving a path through the densest part of the throng, and paid scant regard to the many spectacles and noises that held the boy in such wonder. He gave a brief nod now and then to acquaintances who passed by and, declining several offers to stop for a morning drink, pushed on without pause until they broke free of the chaos and emerged from Hosier Lane into Holborn.

“I’ll bring him at once,” murmured Mrs. Grimes, stepping out to do just that. By now, the outermost crust of grime that had caked Oliver’s cheeks and fingers had been scoured away—as much, at least, as a single scrubbing could strip—and his kindly guardian guided him into the chamber.

Nonsense, sir!--And, my dear Charles,” said Mr. Lorry, glancing at the House again, “you are to remember, that getting things out of Paris at this present time, no matter what things, is next to an impossibility. Papers and precious matters were this very day brought to us here (I speak in strict confidence; it is not business-like to whisper it, even to you), by the strangest bearers you can imagine, every one of whom had his head hanging on by a single hair as he passed the Barriers. At another time, our parcels would come and go, as easily as in business-like Old England; but now, everything is stopped.”

Papers and valuable things were brought to us here this very day—I speak in strict confidence; it is not business-like even to whisper it to you—by the strangest bearers you can imagine, each one of whom had his head hanging by a single hair as he went through the Barriers. At another time our parcels would pass to and fro as freely as in practical Old England; but now, everything is at a standstill.”

Natural language understanding (NLU) and natural language generation (NLG) are two fundamental tasks in building task-oriented dialogue systems. In a modern dialogue system, an NLU module first converts a user utterance, provided by an automatic speech recognition model, into a formal representation. The representation is then consumed by a downstream dialogue state tracker to update a belief state which represents an aggregated user goal. Based on the current belief state, a policy network decides the formal representation of the system response. This is finally used by an NLG module to generate the system response (Young et al., 2010) .

metadata.title: A Generative Model for Joint Natural Language Understanding and Generation

text: Natural language understanding (NLU) and natural language generation (NLG) constitute two core foundational tasks underpinning the development of task-oriented dialogue systems. Within contemporary dialogue system architectures, the NLU module first transforms a user utterance output by an automatic speech recognition model into a structured formal representation. This structured representation is subsequently processed by a downstream dialogue state tracker to revise the system’s belief state, a construct that encodes the cumulative inferred user goal. Using the current belief state as input, a policy network determines the formal representation of the intended system response. This formal response representation is ultimately fed to the NLG module, which produces the final natural language system response (Young et al., 2010).

“The things is well enough in their way,” observed Mr. Sikes: a little soothed as he glanced over the table; “but what have you got to say for yourself, why you should leave me here, down in the mouth, health, blunt, and everything else; and take no more notice of me, all this mortal time, than if I was that ’ere dog.—Drive him down, Charley!”

“The spread’s decent, I’ll grant you,” said Mr. Sikes, his temper easing a fraction as his eyes swept the table. “But what excuse can you offer for deserting me here, low in spirits, sick, short of cash, the whole lot—and paying me no more mind all this everlasting while than you would that dog there. Send him off, Charley!”

Your proffer o’ luve’s an airle-penny,
My tocher’s the bargain ye wad buy;
But an ye be crafty, I am cunnin’,
Sae ye wi anither your fortune may try.
Ye’re like to the timmer o’ yon rotten wood,
Ye’re like to the bark o’ yon rotten tree,
Ye’ll slip frae me like a knotless thread,
And ye’ll crack your credit wi’ mae nor me.

text:
Your offer o’ love is but a bawbee,
My tocher’s the price ye’d fainly hae;
But if ye’re sly, I’m doubly wary,
Sae wi’ anither ye may bide your day.
Ye’re like the timber of yon rotted wood,
Ye’re like the rind o’ yon decayed tree,
Ye’ll slide frae me like a thread without a knot,
And break your honour wi’ mair than me.

The most thoroughgoing ascetic could feel that he had a natural right to wander on Egdon—he was keeping within the line of legitimate indulgence when he laid himself open to influences such as these. Colours and beauties so far subdued were, at least, the birthright of all. Only in summer days of highest feather did its mood touch the level of gaiety. Intensity was more usually reached by way of the solemn than by way of the brilliant, and such a sort of intensity was often arrived at during winter darkness, tempests, and mists. Then Egdon was aroused to reciprocity; for the storm was its lover, and the wind its friend. Then it became the home of strange phantoms; and it was found to be the hitherto unrecognized original of those wild regions of obscurity which are vaguely felt to be compassing us about in midnight dreams of flight and disaster, and are never thought of after the dream till revived by scenes like this.

Even the most uncompromising ascetic might feel that he had an inborn right to roam Egdon—he would still be keeping within the bounds of proper indulgence if he exposed himself to influences like these. Such subdued colors and beauties, at the very least, belonged to everyone by birth. Only on summer days at the height of its splendor did the heath’s spirit rise to something like gaiety. More often, it achieved its force through solemnity rather than brilliance, and that kind of intensity was commonly reached in winter darkness, in storms, and in mist. Then Egdon seemed to awaken in answer; the storm was its lover and the wind its friend. Then it became the dwelling place of uncanny apparitions, and proved to be the long-unrecognized original of those vast dim regions that we feel, without clearly seeing them, to surround us in midnight dreams of escape and ruin, and which are never remembered after the dream until scenes like this call them back to life.

And watered heaven with their tears,
Did He smile His work to see?
Did He who made the lamb make thee?
