“I suppose it was,” replied Oliver, “because heaven is a long way off; and they are too happy there, to come down to the bedside of a poor boy. But if she knew I was ill, she must have pitied me, even there; for she was very ill herself before she died. She can’t know anything about me though,” added Oliver after a moment’s silence. “If she had seen me hurt, it would have made her sorrowful; and her face has always looked sweet and happy, when I have dreamed of her.”

“I reckon that’s why,” Elias answered softly, “since the kingdom of heaven’s so far removed, and everyone there’s far too full of joy to travel down to the cot of a ragged, sick boy. But if she knew I was laid up and unwell, she’d feel for me even there—she was wracked with terrible sickness herself, right before she passed, after all.” He fell quiet for a long, slow beat before speaking again. “I don’t think she knows any of what’s befallen me, though. If she’d ever seen me hurt or suffering, it would have cut her to the core. Every time she comes to me in dreams, her face is only ever sweet, and peaceful, and bright.”

Our goal is to build a framework that will allow users to quickly build text generation demos using existing modeling techniques. This design allows the framework to be almost agnostic to the ongoing development of text generation techniques (Gao et al., 2019a) . Instead, we focus on the organic integration of models and the interfaces for the final demo/app. From a top-down view, our design are bounded to two markets: text processing assistant, and conversational AI, as illustrated in Fig. 2 . Two demos are present as examples in these domains: document auto-completion and Sherlock Holmes. We further breakdown these demos into several tasks, designed to be shared across different demos. We also designed several strategies to integrate multiple models to generate text. These strategies allow each model to plug-in without heavy constraints on the architecture of the models, as detailed in Section 4.

metadata.title: MIXINGBOARD: a Knowledgeable Stylized Integrated Text Generation Platform

text: Our objective is to develop a framework that enables users to rapidly construct text generation demonstrations using existing modeling techniques. This architecture makes the framework largely agnostic to the continued evolution of text generation methods (Gao et al., 2019a). Rather than centering on specific generation advances, we emphasize the seamless integration of models with the interfaces used in the final demo or application. From a high-level perspective, our design is constrained to two application areas: text processing assistants and conversational AI, as shown in Fig. 2. We present two example demos in these domains: document auto-completion and Sherlock Holmes. We further decompose these demos into a set of tasks that can be shared across different demos. In addition, we devise several strategies for combining multiple models to generate text. These strategies enable each model to be incorporated without imposing heavy architectural constraints, as discussed in Section 4.

"Nor did I. But I do now.... I have suddenly found it and seen it plain. I see it so plain that I am amazed that I have not always seen it.... It is, you see, so easy to understand that there is a God, and how complex and wonderful and brotherly He is, when one thinks of those dear boys who by the thousand, by the hundred thousand, have laid down their lives.... Ay, and there were German boys too who did the same.... The cruelties, the injustice, the brute aggression--they saw it differently. They laid down their lives--they laid down their lives.... Those dear lives, those lives of hope and sunshine....

I didn't either. But I do at this moment. It has finally revealed itself to me, and I see it with total clarity. I see it so vividly that I am stunned it wasn't obvious to me all along. You see, it becomes quite simple to believe in God—to see how intricate, magnificent, and compassionate He is—when you reflect on those young men who, by the thousands and hundreds of thousands, gave up their lives. Yes, and there were German boys who did exactly the same. The cruelty, the unfairness, the raw violence—they perceived it all differently. They sacrificed themselves; they gave everything they had. All those precious lives, so full of potential and light.

The maltster, after having lain down in his clothes for a few hours, was now sitting beside a three-legged table, breakfasting off bread and bacon. This was eaten on the plateless system, which is performed by placing a slice of bread upon the table, the meat flat upon the bread, a mustard plaster upon the meat, and a pinch of salt upon the whole, then cutting them vertically downwards with a large pocket-knife till wood is reached, when the severed lump is impaled on the knife, elevated, and sent the proper way of food.

The maltster had stretched out fully dressed for a short rest and was now seated at a rickety three-legged table, making his breakfast of bread and bacon. He followed the plateless method: laying a slice of bread directly on the wood, setting the meat flat atop it, spreading a layer of mustard over the meat, and scattering a pinch of salt across everything. Then, using a hefty pocket-knife, he sliced straight down until the blade met the table, speared the severed morsel on the knife-point, lifted it, and dispatched it along its rightful path.

This paper explores approaches to improve massively multilingual NMT, especially on zero-shot translation. We show that multilingual NMT suffers from weak capacity, and propose to enhance it by deepening the Transformer and devising language-aware neural models. We find that multilingual NMT often generates off-target translations on zero-shot directions, and propose to correct it with a random online backtranslation algorithm. We empirically demonstrate the feasibility of backtranslation in massively multilingual settings to allow for massively zero-shot translation for the first time. We release OPUS-100, a multilingual dataset from OPUS including 100 languages with around 55M sentence pairs for future study. Our experiments on this dataset show that the proposed approaches substantially increase translation performance, narrowing the performance gap with bilingual NMT models and pivot-based methods.

metadata.title: Improving Massively Multilingual Neural Machine Translation and Zero-Shot Translation

text: This paper investigates strategies for improving massively multilingual neural machine translation (NMT), with particular emphasis on zero-shot translation. We demonstrate that multilingual NMT suffers from limited model capacity and address this issue by deepening the Transformer architecture and designing language-aware neural components. We further observe that multilingual NMT frequently produces off-target translations in zero-shot directions, and we propose a random online back-translation algorithm to rectify this behavior. We empirically establish the viability of back-translation in massively multilingual settings, thereby enabling massively zero-shot translation for the first time. We release OPUS-100, a multilingual corpus derived from OPUS that covers 100 languages and contains approximately 55 million sentence pairs, to support future research. Experiments on this dataset show that the proposed methods substantially boost translation quality, reducing the performance gap relative to bilingual NMT models and pivot-based approaches.

I guess I can sell papers or lay cobblestones, and bring in a dollar or two.”

Do you think I’d ever let you hustle for pay while I dally in the world of high art? Not by the bones of Benvenuto Cellini, I wouldn’t! I’m more than able to sell newspapers or lay cobblestones, and bring in a dollar or two to boot.”

As she went back over the particulars of his looks and words—things she had then hardly noticed—it grew clear to her how much they had been governed by his awareness of this new development. “If he had been the sort of man to nurse resentment against a woman who had refused him, he would have boasted of his good fortune in triumphant tones; instead of that, he said nothing at all, out of consideration for my unhappiness, and only suggested that he still loved me, as someone above him.”

As a result, they decided that it was time to waive etiquette and send their greatest and best against me. To the astonishment of that little world, I lassoed Sir Lamorak de Galis, and after him Sir Galahad. So you see there was simply nothing to be done now, but play their right bower--bring out the superbest of the superb, the mightiest of the mighty, the great Sir Launcelot himself!

The crowd demanded a repeat, and then another, and yet another still. Once I had unhorsed five of their number, the armored ranks grew somber, pausing to hold a desperate council. They concluded that the time for mere pageantry was over and that they must deploy their most formidable legends. To the utter bewilderment of the assembly, my hempen coil brought down Sir Valerius, followed swiftly by the peerless Sir Percival. There was nothing left for them now but to reveal their ultimate gambit—to summon the paragon of paragons, the titan of the realm, the illustrious Sir Roland himself!

There was no question she belonged in the frame, so she stepped into it like she was its entire focal point, like she was the living embodiment of quintessential Englishness. There she stood, carrying all the hallmarks, the lineage, the familiar bearing you’d spot in countless longstanding Massachusetts families—cut from exactly the same stock, by all appearances, and yet she was still unmistakably different.

Giles stumbled along as best he could, with Oliver trailing behind; and within a minute or two, Mr. Losberne, who had been strolling abroad and had only just come back, pitched over the hedge in their wake, scrambled to his feet with surprising nimbleness, and set off along the same track at a pace not to be scoffed at, bellowing all the while, at the top of his lungs, to discover what on earth had happened.

The seventh affair had been very disconcerting. He had made a fool of himself with quite a young girl; he blushed to think how young; it hadn't gone very far, but it had made his nocturnal reflections so disagreeable that he had--by no means for the first time--definitely and forever given up these foolish dreams of love. And when Mrs. Harrowdean swam into his circle, she seemed just exactly what was wanted to keep his imagination out of mischief. She came bearing flattery to the pitch of adoration. She was the brightest and cleverest of young widows. She wrote quite admirably criticism in the _Scrutator_ and the _Sectarian_, and occasionally poetry in the _Right Review_--when she felt disposed to do so. She had an intermittent vein of high spirits that was almost better than humour and made her quickly popular with most of the people she met, and she was only twenty miles away in her pretty house and her absurd little jolly park.

The seventh entanglement had been deeply unsettling. He had made an ass of himself with a girl still very much in her youth, young enough to make him redden at the recollection; it had not advanced far, yet it had so soured his nights with uncomfortable meditations that he had—certainly not for the first time—resolved, once and for all, to abandon these foolish romantic fantasies. Then, when Mrs. Vale drifted into his orbit, she appeared to be precisely the safeguard required to keep his imagination from going astray. She arrived laden with adoration so intense it bordered on worship. She was the most brilliant and quick-witted of young widows. She wrote criticism, and very well too, for the Scrutinizer and the Partisan, and now and again verse for the Right Revue—whenever the mood took her. She possessed an occasional streak of high spirits that was almost better than wit, and which quickly made her a favourite with nearly everyone she encountered; besides, she was only twenty miles off, in her charming little house and her absurd, cheerful park.

Yet, Monseigneur had slowly found that vulgar embarrassments crept into his affairs, both private and public; and he had, as to both classes of affairs, allied himself perforce with a Farmer-General. As to finances public, because Monseigneur could not make anything at all of them, and must consequently let them out to somebody who could; as to finances private, because Farmer-Generals were rich, and Monseigneur, after generations of great luxury and expense, was growing poor. Hence Monseigneur had taken his sister from a convent, while there was yet time to ward off the impending veil, the cheapest garment she could wear, and had bestowed her as a prize upon a very rich Farmer-General, poor in family. Which Farmer-General, carrying an appropriate cane with a golden apple on the top of it, was now among the company in the outer rooms, much prostrated before by mankind--always excepting superior mankind of the blood of Monseigneur, who, his own wife included, looked down upon him with the loftiest contempt.

Still, le Comte de Valroux had gradually come to realize that crass, unseemly misfortunes were seeping into every corner of his dealings, both personal and state-related; and he had, of necessity, struck a partnership with a Tax-Farmer for both spheres of his life. Where public finances were concerned, the comte was entirely incapable of untangling their knotted ledgers, and so had no choice but to contract the work out to someone with the skill to make them function; as for his personal accounts, Tax-Farmers accumulated fortunes faster than any other class in the realm, and the comte, after generations of his line’s unbridled luxury and reckless spending, was sliding rapidly into crippling debt. For this reason, the comte had pulled his younger sister from the abbey while there was still time to spare her the impending nun’s veil—the only garment in the world that would cost him nothing to clothe her in—and offered her hand as a reward to a phenomenally wealthy Tax-Farmer of exceedingly common lineage. This same Tax-Farmer, who carried a polished ebony cane capped with a gilded pomegranate, was now lingering among the crowd in the outer antechambers, bowed and scraped to by nearly everyone in attendance—always excepting those of the comte’s own noble bloodline, who, his new wife included, regarded him with the most unassailable, icy scorn.

“Then I tell you again, Mr. Darnay, I am sorry for it. I am sorry to hear you putting any such extraordinary questions. Here is a fellow, who, infected by the most pestilent and blasphemous code of devilry that ever was known, abandoned his property to the vilest scum of the earth that ever did murder by wholesale, and you ask me why I am sorry that a man who instructs youth knows him? Well, but I’ll answer you. I am sorry because I believe there is contamination in such a scoundrel. That’s why.”

“Let me state this plainly again, Mr. Verrall, I am deeply aggrieved by the whole affair. I am frankly stunned you would pose such an absurd, out-of-line question. The man in question is corrupted by the most pestilent, godless doctrine of evil ever devised, who signed over all his lands and holdings to the very dregs of society, the same murderous hordes that carry out mass slaughter at a whim, and you dare ask why I am distressed that a man charged with educating young people knows him? Very well, I will give you your answer. I am distressed because I know a scoundrel like that carries corruption in every fiber of his being, ready to taint anyone who associates with him. That is why.”

Mr. Cruncher had by this time taken quite a lunch of rust off his fingers in his following of the evidence. He had now to attend while Mr. Stryver fitted the prisoner’s case on the jury, like a compact suit of clothes; showing them how the patriot, Barsad, was a hired spy and traitor, an unblushing trafficker in blood, and one of the greatest scoundrels upon earth since accursed Judas--which he certainly did look rather like. How the virtuous servant, Cly, was his friend and partner, and was worthy to be; how the watchful eyes of those forgers and false swearers had rested on the prisoner as a victim, because some family affairs in France, he being of French extraction, did require his making those passages across the Channel--though what those affairs were, a consideration for others who were near and dear to him, forbade him, even for his life, to disclose. How the evidence that had been warped and wrested from the young lady, whose anguish in giving it they had witnessed, came to nothing, involving the mere little innocent gallantries and politenesses likely to pass between any young gentleman and young lady so thrown together;--with the exception of that reference to George Washington, which was altogether too extravagant and impossible to be regarded in any other light than as a monstrous joke. How it would be a weakness in the government to break down in this attempt to practise for popularity on the lowest national antipathies and fears, and therefore Mr. Attorney-General had made the most of it; how, nevertheless, it rested upon nothing, save that vile and infamous character of evidence too often disfiguring such cases, and of which the State Trials of this country were full. But, there my Lord interposed (with as grave a face as if it had not been true), saying that he could not sit upon that Bench and suffer those allusions.

By now, Mr. Cruncher had cleaned a fair meal’s worth of rust from his fingers while tracking the testimony. He now had to listen as Mr. Stryver tailored the defendant’s case to the jury like a snug suit, demonstrating how the so-called patriot Barsad was a paid informer and traitor, a shameless dealer in blood, and one of the earth’s worst villains since the damned Judas—whom he did, in truth, somewhat resemble. How the upright servant Cly was his crony and partner, and deserved to be; how those forgers and perjurers had fixed their vigilant gaze on the accused as their prey, because certain family matters in France, since he was of French descent, obliged him to make those Channel crossings—though what those matters were, out of regard for those closest to his heart, he could not reveal, even to save his life. How the testimony twisted and extracted from the young woman, whose distress in delivering it they had seen, amounted to nothing, dealing only with the trivial, harmless flirtations and courtesies that might naturally occur between any young man and young woman cast together—except for that mention of George Washington, which was so wildly overblown and absurd it could only be taken as a monstrous jest. How it would be a failing in the government to falter in this bid to court popularity by exploiting the basest national hatreds and anxieties, and so the Attorney-General had squeezed it for all it was worth; how, regardless, it was built on nothing but that sordid and disgraceful type of testimony that too often mars such trials, and of which the State Trials of this nation were replete. But at this, my Lord cut in (with a face as stern as if it had been false), declaring he could not occupy that Bench and permit such insinuations.

Yet, finding that none among the ragged culprits answered to the name of Julian, nor offered any clue to his fate, Eliza marched boldly toward the gruff official in the embroidered vest. There, she erupted into a display of such wretched grief and piercing woe—greatly enhanced by the frantic rattling of a large house-key and the clutching of a small wicker basket—as she piteously begged for the return of her darling brother.

For users who prefer a condensed summary of the report, Xiaomingbot can provide a short gist version using a pre-trained text summarization model. We choose to use the said model instead of generating the summary directly from the table data because the former can create more general content, and can be employed to process manually written reports as well. There are two approaches to summarize a text: extractive and abstractive summarization. Extractive summarization trains a sentence selection model to pick the important sentences from an input article, while an abstractive summarization will further rephrase the sentences and explore the potential for combining multiple sentences into a simplified one. We trained two summarization models. One is a general text summarization using a BERT-based sequence labelling network. We use the TTNews dataset, a Chinese single document summarization dataset for training from NLPCC 2017 and 2018 shared tasks (Hua et al., 2017; Li and Wan, 2018) . It includes 50,000 Chinese documents with human written summaries. The article is separated into a sequence of sentences. The BERT-based summarization model output 0-1 labels for all sentences.

metadata.title: Xiaomingbot: A Multilingual Robot News Reporter

text: For users who prefer a condensed version of the report, Xiaomingbot can generate a brief gist using a pre-trained text summarization model. We chose this model rather than producing the summary directly from the table data because the former can generate more general content and can also be applied to manually written reports. There are two main approaches to text summarization: extractive and abstractive summarization. Extractive summarization trains a sentence selection model to identify the important sentences in an input article, whereas abstractive summarization further rephrases sentences and investigates the possibility of combining multiple sentences into a simplified one. We trained two summarization models. One is a general text summarization model based on a BERT-based sequence labeling network. For training, we use the TTNews dataset, a Chinese single-document summarization dataset from the NLPCC 2017 and 2018 shared tasks (Hua et al., 2017; Li and Wan, 2018). It contains 50,000 Chinese documents with human-written summaries. The article is segmented into a sequence of sentences, and the BERT-based summarization model outputs 0-1 labels for all sentences.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
A flower was offered to me,
Such a flower as May never bore;
But I said, ‘I’ve a pretty rose tree,’
And I passed the sweet flower o’er.

metadata.title: Songs of Innocence and of Experience

text:
Someone held out a bloom to me,
More radiant than spring ever grew;
But I said, “I’ve a lovely rose tree,”
And I turned from that exquisite hue.

The policeman was still looking. The persecuted young woman had but to beckon a finger and Soapy would be practically en route for his insular haven. Already he imagined he could feel the cozy warmth of the station-house. The young woman faced him and, stretching out a hand, caught Soapy’s coat sleeve.

The police officer was still lingering with his gaze fixed on the pair. All the harried young woman had to do was lift a single finger, and Soapy would effectively be on his way to the small, isolated haven he’d been chasing. He was already so caught up in imagining the cozy, warm air of the precinct that he swore he could feel it seeping into his bones. The young woman turned to face him fully, extended a hand, and curled her fingers around the sleeve of Soapy’s coat.

But I believed I saw my chance at last. I would form this crack regiment out of officers alone--not a single private. Half of it should consist of nobles, who should fill all the places up to Major-General, and serve gratis and pay their own expenses; and they would be glad to do this when they should learn that the rest of the regiment would consist exclusively of princes of the blood. These princes of the blood should range in rank from Lieutenant-General up to Field Marshal, and be gorgeously salaried and equipped and fed by the state. Moreover--and this was the master stroke --it should be decreed that these princely grandees should be always addressed by a stunningly gaudy and awe-compelling title (which I would presently invent), and they and they only in all England should be so addressed. Finally, all princes of the blood should have free choice; join that regiment, get that great title, and renounce the royal grant, or stay out and receive a grant. Neatest touch of all: unborn but imminent princes of the blood could be _born_ into the regiment, and start fair, with good wages and a permanent situation, upon due notice from the parents.

At long last, I thought I’d spotted my perfect opportunity. I would build this elite crack regiment entirely from commissioned officers—no rank-and-file privates whatsoever. Half its ranks would be made up of nobles, who would hold every post up to Major-General, serve without pay, and cover all their own costs; I knew they would be eager to do so once they learned the other half of the regiment would be reserved exclusively for princes of the blood. Those royal princes would hold ranks ranging from Lieutenant General all the way up to Field Marshal, with lavish state-funded salaries, uniforms, equipment, and rations. What’s more, this was my master stroke: I would issue a decree that these princely grandees would always be addressed by a dazzling, awe-inspiring custom title I planned to invent shortly, and they would be the only people in all of England permitted to receive that honorific. Finally, every prince of the blood would get a free choice: join the regiment, claim the prestigious title, and renounce their regular royal grant, or stay out of the corps and receive the grant as usual. My cleverest little touch of all? Even unborn, soon-to-arrive princes of the blood could be born directly into the regiment, so they would enter the world with a guaranteed well-paying, permanent post, as long as their parents submitted proper advance notice.

We perform manual error analysis on the predictions of our top two models for the Chickasaw puzzle presented in Table 1 . The predicted translations are shown in Table 4 . We also provide the predictions of the simple baselines in Appendix A.5 for comparison. Although the PBSMT model is best on average, we find that for this particular puzzle, the Transformer model did much better. PBSMT had very few hits overall: it correctly chose to include the lexical items hattak and hollo in (1), but the position and inflection of the former is incorrect. In (5) and (6) there are indications of correct lexicon induction, but the overall quality of the translations is very poor both in terms of accuracy and fluency. The Transformer model, on the other hand, predicts fluent translations in both directions. In the direction from English to Chickasaw, we see that the model correctly acquired the relevant morphological patterns: subjects take suffix at, objects take suffixã, and, importantly, that first person agency is expressed through suffix li. The translations are still not perfect, though, due to lexical confusion: the words for cat and dog have been swapped in both (1) and (2), as well as the words for love and chase in (3). In the direction from Chickasaw to English, the Transformer's predictions remain fluent, but they hardly relate to the input. Contrary to the overall results, for this puzzle translation to English appears to be more challenging for the model.

metadata.title: PuzzLing Machines: A Challenge on Learning From Small Data

text: We conduct a manual error analysis of the predictions made by our two strongest models on the Chickasaw puzzle shown in Table 1. The resulting translations are listed in Table 4, and the outputs of the simpler baselines are given in Appendix A.5 for comparison. Although PBSMT performs best on average, we observe that, for this specific puzzle, the Transformer model substantially outperforms it. PBSMT produces very few correct elements overall: it correctly includes the lexical items hattak and hollo in (1), but the placement and inflection of the former are wrong. In (5) and (6), there are signs of partial lexicon induction, yet the translations are poor in both adequacy and fluency. By contrast, the Transformer yields fluent translations in both translation directions. For English-to-Chickasaw, the model correctly learns the relevant morphological regularities: subjects take the suffix at, objects take the suffix ã, and, crucially, first-person agency is marked with the suffix li. The outputs are nevertheless not fully correct, largely because of lexical confusions: the words for cat and dog are interchanged in both (1) and (2), and the words for love and chase are also swapped in (3). For Chickasaw-to-English, the Transformer's predictions remain fluent, but they are only weakly connected to the source input. In contrast to the aggregate results, this puzzle suggests that translation into English is more difficult for the model.

Thus, for different reasons, what was to the rest an exhilarating movement was to these two a riding upon the whirlwind. The dance had come like an irresistible attack upon whatever sense of social order there was in their minds, to drive them back into old paths which were now doubly irregular. Through three dances in succession they spun their way; and then, fatigued with the incessant motion, Eustacia turned to quit the circle in which she had already remained too long. Wildeve led her to a grassy mound a few yards distant, where she sat down, her partner standing beside her. From the time that he addressed her at the beginning of the dance till now they had not exchanged a word.

So, each for their own cause, felt the night’s revel not as a glad release but as a gale that swept them off their feet. The music had descended upon their fragile restraint like a sudden onslaught, flinging them back toward familiar transgressions now grown doubly wayward. They whirled through three reels without pause; then, worn by the ceaseless turning, Clymene withdrew from the ring she had already graced too long. Marek guided her to a low turf bank a little apart, where she sank down while he remained standing at her shoulder. Since his first whispered greeting when the figures began, no further word had passed between them.

We proposed an approach to content selection for abstractive text summarization in clinical notes. We introduced our novel approach to augment standard summarization model with significant ontological terms within the source. Content selection problem is framed as a word-level sequence-tagging task. The intrinsic evaluations on two publicly available real-life clinical datasets show the efficacy of our model in terms of ROUGE metrics. Furthermore, the extrinsic evaluation by domain experts further reveals the qualities of our system-generated summaries in comparison with gold summaries.

metadata.title: Attend to Medical Ontologies: Content Selection for Clinical Abstractive Summarization

text: We proposed an approach to content selection for abstractive text summarization in clinical notes. We introduced a novel method for augmenting a standard summarization model with salient ontological terms extracted from the source text. The content selection problem is formulated as a word-level sequence tagging task. Intrinsic evaluations on two publicly available real-world clinical datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our model in terms of ROUGE metrics. Furthermore, extrinsic evaluation by domain experts further reveals the quality of the system-generated summaries in comparison with gold summaries.

We then define Corresponding Syntactic Relations (CSR) as a pair (R s , R t ) such that R s and R t are dependency paths in T s and T t , and such that the origin and endpoint of R s are in CW s and the origin and endpoint of R t are their aligned tokens in CW t according to A . If the origin or the endpoint of R s do not have a corresponding node in T t , R s does not have a corresponding relation in T t . The types of R s and R t are the sequence of labels on the edges of the paths, optionally along with their directionality in the tree (linear order is not taken into account). Without loss of generality, we assume that R s begins at the leftmost word of the pair in the En sentence, and R t by definition begins at the target word corresponding to the leftmost source word. For brevity, we only present results where directionality is not taken into account. Relations are thus written as sequences of UD edge labels separated by the '+' sign.

metadata.title: Fine-Grained Analysis of Cross-Linguistic Syntactic Divergences

text: We formalize Corresponding Syntactic Relations (CSR) as a tuple (R_s, R_t) where R_s and R_t are dependency paths in the source and target trees T_s and T_t, respectively. The start and end nodes of R_s must belong to the common word set CW_s, and the start and end nodes of R_t are their aligned counterparts in CW_t under the alignment A. If either endpoint of R_s lacks an aligned node in T_t, then R_s has no corresponding relation in T_t. The type of a relation is defined by the sequence of edge labels along the path, optionally including edge directionality within the tree, while linear order is ignored. Without loss of generality, we let R_s originate at the leftmost word of the pair in the English sentence; by construction, R_t then starts at the target token aligned to that leftmost source word. For conciseness, we report only results that disregard directionality. Relations are therefore expressed as sequences of UD edge labels concatenated by the '+' symbol.

Rounding his mouth and both his eyes, as he stepped backward from the table, the waiter shifted his napkin from his right arm to his left, dropped into a comfortable attitude, and stood surveying the guest while he ate and drank, as from an observatory or watchtower. According to the immemorial usage of waiters in all ages.

With eyes and mouth wide in an expression of surprise, the waiter backed away from the table, transferring his napkin from his right arm to his left. He settled into an easy pose and stood there monitoring the patron as he ate and drank, as if from a high vantage point or a sentry post. It was the timeless tradition of servers throughout the ages.

Non-autoregressive (NAR) models generate all the tokens of a sequence in parallel, resulting in faster generation speed compared to their autoregressive (AR) counterparts but at the cost of lower accuracy. Different techniques including knowledge distillation and source-target alignment have been proposed to bridge the gap between AR and NAR models in various tasks such as neural machine translation (NMT), automatic speech recognition (ASR), and text to speech (TTS). With the help of those techniques, NAR models can catch up with the accuracy of AR models in some tasks but not in some others. In this work, we conduct a study to understand the difficulty of NAR sequence generation and try to answer: (1) Why NAR models can catch up with AR models in some tasks but not all? (2) Why techniques like knowledge distillation and source-target alignment can help NAR models. Since the main difference between AR and NAR models is that NAR models do not use dependency among target tokens while AR models do, intuitively the difficulty of NAR sequence generation heavily depends on the strongness of dependency among target tokens. To quantify such dependency, we propose an analysis model called CoMMA to characterize the difficulty of different NAR sequence generation tasks. We have several interesting findings: 1) Among the NMT, ASR and TTS tasks, ASR has the most target-token dependency while TTS has the least. 2) Knowledge distillation reduces the target-token dependency in target sequence and thus improves the accuracy of NAR models. 3) Source-target alignment constraint encourages dependency

metadata.title: A Study of Non-autoregressive Model for Sequence Generation

The resulting values after the convolution will be in the interval [0.0, 1.0]. For each target word we select the square with the highest attention mass, encouraging a sparse distribution over source positions in A and thus effectively training the model towards strong attention values on neighboring positions. We mask the contiguity loss such that the end of sentence symbol is not considered during this procedure. text: We use a 2 × 2 kernel K ∈ R 2×2 with every entry set to 0.5. As a result, Ā ∈ R n×m contains the normalized attention mass of each 2 × 2 block in the attention matrix A. The values produced by the convolution lie in the interval [0.0, 1.0]. For each target word, we choose the block with the greatest attention mass, thereby promoting a sparse allocation over source positions in A and effectively training the model to place strong attention values on adjacent positions. We mask the contiguity loss so that the end-of-sentence symbol is excluded from this procedure. Before applying the softmax function to obtain A, we apply position-wise dropout of 0.1 to the attention logits, which proved important for avoiding trivial solutions during training. 3 Optimizing the alignment loss especially encourages diagonal and horizontal patterns 4 , as visualized in Figure 4 . These correspond well to a large portion of the patterns found in human alignment annotations, as shown in Figure 1 .

and training of models on translation memories, machine translated output has been progressively introduced as a substitute for fuzzy matches when no sufficiently "good" fuzzy match is found and proved to also increase translator productivity given appropriate post-editing environment (Plitt and Masselot, 2010) . These two technologies are entirely different in their finality -indeed, for a given source sentence, fuzzy matching is just a database retrieval and scoring technique always returning a pair of source and target segments, while machine translation is actually building an original translation. However, with Statistical Machine Translation, the two technologies are sharing the same simple idea about managing and retrieving optimal combination of longest translated n-grams and this property led to the development of several techniques like use of fuzzy matches in SMT decoding (Koehn and Senellart, 2010; Wang et al., 2013) , adaptive machine translation (Zaretskaya et al., 2015) or "fuzzy match repairing" (Ortega et al., 2016) .

metadata.title: Boosting Neural Machine Translation with Similar Translations

text: Alongside training models on translation memories, machine-translated output has been progressively adopted as a replacement for fuzzy matches when no sufficiently high-quality fuzzy match is available, and it has been shown to enhance translator productivity in suitably configured post-editing settings (Plitt and Masselot, 2010). These two technologies differ fundamentally in their purpose: for a given source sentence, fuzzy matching merely constitutes a database retrieval and scoring mechanism that invariably returns a source–target segment pair, whereas machine translation genuinely constructs a novel translation. Nevertheless, under Statistical Machine Translation, both technologies rest on the same core principle of managing and retrieving optimal combinations of the longest translated n-grams, a characteristic that has spurred the development of various approaches, including the integration of fuzzy matches into SMT decoding (Koehn and Senellart, 2010; Wang et al., 2013), adaptive machine translation (Zaretskaya et al., 2015), and “fuzzy match repairing” (Ortega et al., 2016).

Preparations were made for the event, congratulatory visits were received, and all wore a smiling appearance. I shut up, as well as I could, in my own heart the anxiety that preyed there and entered with seeming earnestness into the plans of my father, although they might only serve as the decorations of my tragedy. Through my father’s exertions a part of the inheritance of Elizabeth had been restored to her by the Austrian government. A small possession on the shores of Como belonged to her. It was agreed that, immediately after our union, we should proceed to Villa Lavenza and spend our first days of happiness beside the beautiful lake near which it stood.

Preparations for the occasion were soon underway; well-wishers arrived with congratulations, and every face beamed with good cheer. I concealed as best I could the gnawing dread within my own heart, and with feigned earnestness I entered into my father’s designs, though they seemed to me only the ornamental drapery of my impending tragedy. Through my father’s efforts, a portion of Elizabeth’s inheritance had been reclaimed for her by the Austrian government. A modest estate on the shores of Como belonged to her. It was settled that, immediately after our marriage, we would journey to Villa Lavenza and spend our first days of happiness beside the lovely lake on whose banks it lay.

“I’m Mister Noah Claypole,” said the charity-boy, “and you’re under me. Take down the shutters, yer idle young ruffian!” With this, Mr. Claypole administered a kick to Oliver, and entered the shop with a dignified air, which did him great credit. It is difficult for a large-headed, small-eyed youth, of lumbering make and heavy countenance, to look dignified under any circumstances; but it is more especially so, when superadded to these personal attractions are a red nose and yellow smalls.

“Name’s Noah Claypole—Mister Claypole, to you,” the charity ward said, “and you answer directly to me. Get those shutters pulled down, you lazy little lout!” With that, Claypole landed a sharp kick to Oliver’s leg, then marched into the shop with a lofty, dignified bearing he clearly thought suited him perfectly. It’s hard enough for a big-headed, beady-eyed, clumsily built, thick-featured teen to pull off dignity at the best of times, but the task becomes outright impossible when you add a blotchy red nose and scuffed yellow knee-breeches to that already unflattering list of traits.

As he discharged himself of this pleasantry, Mr. Bumble took his hat from a peg, and putting it on, rather rakishly, on one side, as a man might, who felt he had asserted his superiority in a becoming manner, thrust his hands into his pockets, and sauntered towards the door, with much ease and waggishness depicted in his whole appearance.

Having delivered this witticism, Mr. Thistlewick lifted his bowler from its hook and set it at a jaunty slant, the gesture of a gentleman convinced he had elegantly affirmed his own ascendancy. He then buried his fists in his coat pockets and ambled toward the exit, his entire bearing radiating an air of smug amusement.

And presently Mr. Direck found himself strolling through the rose garden with Cecily. She had the sweetest moonlight face, her white shining robe made her a thing of moonlight altogether. If Mr. Direck had not been in love with her before he was now altogether in love. Mamie Nelson, whose freakish unkindness had been rankling like a poisoned thorn in his heart all the way from Massachusetts, suddenly became Ancient History.

And before long, Mr. Alcott found himself wandering in the rose garden beside Miss Celia. She had the gentlest face for moonlight, and her pale, gleaming gown made her seem altogether a creature wrought of moonshine. If Mr. Alcott had not been in love with her before, he was now in love beyond recall. As for Mamie Kendricks, whose capricious cruelty had pricked him like a venomous thorn ever since they left Massachusetts, she suddenly slipped into the far dim category of Ancient History.

The noise subsided, and he was asked if he had anything to say why sentence of death should not be passed upon him. He had resumed his listening attitude, and looked intently at his questioner while the demand was made; but it was twice repeated before he seemed to hear it, and then he only muttered that he was an old man—an old man—and so, dropping into a whisper, was silent again.

The tumult sank away, and he was asked whether he had anything to offer why sentence of death should not be pronounced against him. He had fallen back into his posture of strained attention, and fixed his eyes steadily on the one who addressed him while the question was put; but it had to be repeated twice before he appeared to take it in, and then he only murmured that he was an old man—an old man—and, sinking into a whisper, fell silent once more.

It was all right. She had always known it was all right. (Hold close to him.) Except just for a little while. But that had been foolishness. Hadn't she always known he was alive? And here he was alive! (Hold close to him.) Only it was so good to be sure--after all her torment; to hold him, to hang about him, to feel the solid man, kissing her, weeping too, weeping together with her. "Teddy my love!"

It was all right. She had always known, deep down, that it was all right. (Keep close to him.) Only for a little while had she thought otherwise. But that had been folly. Hadn’t she always known he was alive? And there he stood, alive indeed! (Keep close to him.) Yet it was so sweet to be certain at last—after all that agony—to clasp him, to cling about him, to feel the firm body of him, kissing her, and crying too, crying with her. “Tom, my darling!”

Things ran along, a tournament nearly every week; and now and then the boys used to want me to take a hand--I mean Sir Launcelot and the rest--but I said I would by and by; no hurry yet, and too much government machinery to oil up and set to rights and start a-going.

Events moved forward, with a joust occurring almost every week. Every so often, the fellows—Lancelot and the others—would try to get me involved, but I’d tell them I’d join in eventually. There wasn't any rush, and I had far too many administrative gears to grease, fix, and set into motion.

Oliver _did_ see it in his mind’s eye as distinctly as if he had not altered his position; but he thought it better not to worry the kind old lady; so he smiled gently when she looked at him; and Mrs. Bedwin, satisfied that he felt more comfortable, salted and broke bits of toasted bread into the broth, with all the bustle befitting so solemn a preparation. Oliver got through it with extraordinary expedition. He had scarcely swallowed the last spoonful, when there came a soft rap at the door. “Come in,” said the old lady; and in walked Mr. Brownlow.

Oliver could picture it perfectly in his mind, as clearly as if he had never moved an inch; yet he decided it was kinder not to trouble the gentle old woman. So when she glanced at him, he offered a soft smile, and Mrs. Bedwin, reassured that he was more at ease, set about crumbling toasted bread into the broth with all the busy solemnity the task demanded. Oliver finished it with remarkable speed. He had barely taken the final spoonful when a light knock sounded at the door. "Come in," said the old lady, and Mr. Brownlow entered.

Towards evening Wildeve came. Since Thomasin’s marriage Mrs. Yeobright had shown him that grim friendliness which at last arises in all such cases of undesired affinity. The vision of what ought to have been is thrown aside in sheer weariness, and browbeaten human endeavour listlessly makes the best of the fact that is. Wildeve, to do him justice, had behaved very courteously to his wife’s aunt; and it was with no surprise that she saw him enter now.

Wildeve arrived as evening approached. Since Thomasin’s marriage, Mrs. Yeobright had adopted a sort of grim civility toward him—the kind of forced warmth that eventually develops in all such unwanted relationships. The dream of what might have been is cast aside out of sheer fatigue, and exhausted human effort half-heartedly accepts the reality of the situation. To give him credit, Wildeve had behaved quite politely toward his wife’s aunt, so his entrance now caused her no surprise.

There are some promotions in life, which, independent of the more substantial rewards they offer, require peculiar value and dignity from the coats and waistcoats connected with them. A field-marshal has his uniform; a bishop his silk apron; a counsellor his silk gown; a beadle his cocked hat. Strip the bishop of his apron, or the beadle of his hat and lace; what are they? Men. Mere men. Dignity, and even holiness too, sometimes, are more questions of coat and waistcoat than some people imagine.

Certain elevations in social standing possess a unique prestige that transcends their material benefits, largely derived from the specific garments they demand. A general is defined by his uniform; a bishop by his ceremonial apron; a barrister by his flowing robes; a parish beadle by his gold-laced hat. Deprive the cleric of his vestments, or the official of his tricorn and lace, and what remains? Only men. Simple, unremarkable men. Respectability, and even a sense of the sacred, frequently depend more upon the cut of a coat or the fabric of a waistcoat than many would care to admit.

He reached the house. There was no appearance of its inmates stirring at that early hour. Oliver stopped, and peeped into the garden. A child was weeding one of the little beds; as he stopped, he raised his pale face and disclosed the features of one of his former companions. Oliver felt glad to see him, before he went; for, though younger than himself, he had been his little friend and playmate. They had been beaten, and starved, and shut up together, many and many a time.

He made his way up to the house. It was still so early that none of the household’s residents showed the slightest sign of stirring. Oliver came to a stop and peered into the garden. A small boy was pulling weeds from one of the tiny planting beds, and when he caught sight of Oliver’s figure, he lifted his pale face, revealing the features of one of Oliver’s old companions. A soft rush of gladness washed over Oliver at getting to see him before he left: though the boy was a few years younger than him, he had once been his dearest little friend and playmate. More times than they could keep track of, the two of them had been beaten, half-starved, and shut up together in the same cramped, cold space.

The marriage-day was shining brightly, and they were ready outside the closed door of the Doctor’s room, where he was speaking with Charles Darnay. They were ready to go to church; the beautiful bride, Mr. Lorry, and Miss Pross--to whom the event, through a gradual process of reconcilement to the inevitable, would have been one of absolute bliss, but for the yet lingering consideration that her brother Solomon should have been the bridegroom.

The wedding morning was radiant with sunlight as the small party gathered outside the Doctor’s closed door, where he remained in private conversation with Charles Darnay. They were all prepared to depart for the church: the lovely bride, Mr. Lorry, and Miss Pross. This occasion would have filled Miss Pross with unclouded joy—she having slowly accepted the inevitability of the match—were it not for her persistent, secret conviction that her brother Solomon was the only one truly worthy of the bride’s hand.

Day by day he worked on, and day by day the postman delivered to him rejected manuscripts. He had no money for stamps, so the manuscripts accumulated in a heap under the table. Came a day when for forty hours he had not tasted food. He could not hope for a meal at Ruth’s, for she was away to San Rafael on a two weeks’ visit; and for very shame’s sake he could not go to his sister’s. To cap misfortune, the postman, in his afternoon round, brought him five returned manuscripts. Then it was that Martin wore his overcoat down into Oakland, and came back without it, but with five dollars tinkling in his pocket. He paid a dollar each on account to the four tradesmen, and in his kitchen fried steak and onions, made coffee, and stewed a large pot of prunes. And having dined, he sat down at his table-desk and completed before midnight an essay which he entitled “The Dignity of Usury.” Having typed it out, he flung it under the table, for there had been nothing left from the five dollars with which to buy stamps.

Day after day he kept at his labor, and day after day the mail carrier brought him back his manuscripts, each one refused. He had not a cent for postage, so the rejected pages piled up in a drift beneath the table. Then came a day when he had gone forty hours without food. He could not look for a meal at Clara’s, for she had gone to San Rafael for a fortnight’s stay; and, out of sheer shame, he could not bring himself to seek out his sister. As if to sharpen his bad luck, the mailman, on his afternoon round, delivered five more returned manuscripts. It was then that Adrian put on his overcoat, walked it down into Alameda, and returned without it, though with five dollars singing in his pocket. He paid a dollar apiece on account to the four merchants, and in his kitchen he fried a steak with onions, brewed coffee, and set a big pot of prunes to simmering. Then, having eaten, he went to his table-desk and, before midnight, finished an essay he called “The Majesty of Interest.” When he had typed it, he cast it beneath the table, for not a penny of the five dollars remained to buy stamps.

A factitious reply had been again upon his lips, but it was again suspended, and he looked at her with an arrested eye. The truth was, that as she now stood—excited, wild, and honest as the day—her alluring beauty bore out so fully the epithets he had bestowed upon it that he was quite startled at his temerity in advancing them as false. He said mechanically, “Ah, why?” and continued to look at her.

Another invented answer had risen once more to his lips, but it was once more checked, and he regarded her with a look suddenly arrested. The truth was that, as she stood there now—excited, untamed, and as candid as daylight itself—her irresistible beauty justified so completely the very names he had flung at it that he was almost startled by his own audacity in calling them false. He murmured, almost involuntarily, “Ah, why?” and kept his eyes upon her.

metadata.title: A Graph Auto-encoder Model of Derivational Morphology

text: Because window size shapes the type of information encoded in embeddings—larger windows tend to capture topical content while smaller windows capture morphosyntactic features (Lison and Kutuzov, 2017)—we also train the DGA using 200-dimensional embeddings formed by concatenating separate 100-dimensional embeddings trained with window sizes of 10 and 1, respectively (DGA+). Since the DGA already provides semantic information and the window‑10 embeddings contribute semantic content, the primary benefit of DGA+ should be the addition of syntactic information. CM: We adopt binary cross-entropy as the loss function. The CM is trained for 20 epochs with the Adam optimizer at a learning rate of 0.001. Both the input character embeddings and the hidden states of the bidirectional LSTMs are set to 100 dimensions. The output of the first feed-forward layer has 50 dimensions. Dropout with a rate of 0.2 is applied after the embedding layer and after the first feed-forward layer.

Statistics concerning the number of words per sentence 4 are shown on the left of Figure 1 . The majority of both training and test pairs are fairly short, but length varies considerably. This is due to the fact that some puzzles in the dataset concern the translation of individual words, some take scope over noun-modifier phrases and some, over entire sentences. English sentences are generally longer (median 4) than their translations (median 2). This is rather intuitive considering the synthetic nature of many of the foreign languages in the dataset, wherein a single long word in the foreign language may translate into 4-5 words on the English side, as in this translation from tΛckotoyatih in the Mexican language Zoque to the English only for the tooth.

metadata.title: PuzzLing Machines: A Challenge on Learning From Small Data

text: The left panel of Figure 1 presents statistics regarding sentence length. While the majority of training and test pairs are relatively brief, there is significant variation in length. This variability arises because the dataset’s puzzles range from individual word translations to noun-modifier phrases and full sentences. English sentences are generally longer (median of 4) than their translations (median of 2). This discrepancy is intuitive given the synthetic nature of many languages in the dataset, where a single complex word often corresponds to four or five English words; for instance, the Zoque term *tΛckotoyatih* translates to the four-word English phrase "only for the tooth."

All this time and at all times wait the words of true poems,
The words of true poems do not merely please,
The true poets are not followers of beauty but the august masters of beauty;
The greatness of sons is the exuding of the greatness of mothers
and fathers,
The words of true poems are the tuft and final applause of science.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

All this while, and in every while, the words of true poems stand ready,
The words of true poems are not here simply to delight,
The true poets do not chase beauty but command it, its sober sovereigns;
The scale of the children is the slow, rich breathing-out of the mothers
and fathers,
The words of true poems are the crest and last bravo of knowing.

“Stay another moment,” interposed Rose, as the girl moved hurriedly towards the door. “Think once again on your own condition, and the opportunity you have of escaping from it. You have a claim on me: not only as the voluntary bearer of this intelligence, but as a woman lost almost beyond redemption. Will you return to this gang of robbers, and to this man, when a word can save you? What fascination is it that can take you back, and make you cling to wickedness and misery? Oh! is there no chord in your heart that I can touch! Is there nothing left, to which I can appeal against this terrible infatuation!”

“Stay one moment longer,” Rose said, as the girl turned hastily toward the door. “Consider again your own situation, and the chance you have of escaping from it. You have a claim upon me—not only because you came of your own will to bring me this news, but because you are a woman almost beyond redemption, lost as you are. Will you go back to this band of robbers, and to that man, when a single word might save you? What spell is it that draws you back, and binds you to wickedness and misery? Oh, is there no string in your heart that I can touch? Is there nothing left to which I may appeal against this dreadful madness?”

By the outer margin of the Pit was an oval pond, and over it hung the attenuated skeleton of a chrome-yellow moon which had only a few days to last—the morning star dogging her on the left hand. The pool glittered like a dead man’s eye, and as the world awoke a breeze blew, shaking and elongating the reflection of the moon without breaking it, and turning the image of the star to a phosphoric streak upon the water. All this Oak saw and remembered.

Caleb lifted his gaze, and uncertain of his next act, wearily took in the landscape. Along the far rim of the Hollow lay a round tarn, and above it dangled the gaunt frame of a brass-pale moon that had but a handful of nights remaining—the dawn star trailing close at her right. The water shone like a blind man’s eye, and as the earth stirred a wind rose, trembling and stretching the moon’s likeness yet leaving it whole, and spinning the star’s shape into a luminous smear across the surface. All this Caleb witnessed and held fast.

Such were the professor’s words—rather let me say such the words of the fate—enounced to destroy me. As he went on I felt as if my soul were grappling with a palpable enemy; one by one the various keys were touched which formed the mechanism of my being; chord after chord was sounded, and soon my mind was filled with one thought, one conception, one purpose. So much has been done, exclaimed the soul of Frankenstein—more, far more, will I achieve; treading in the steps already marked, I will pioneer a new way, explore unknown powers, and unfold to the world the deepest mysteries of creation.

So fell Dr. Corvin’s words—no, better to call them the edicts of fate, spoken expressly to unmake me. As he continued speaking, I felt my soul locked in grapple with a foe I could almost touch; each turn of his lecture plucked at the hidden wires strung through the core of my being, one after another, chord after resonant chord, until my whole mind narrowed to a single thought, a single vision, a single unshakable purpose. So much has already been achieved, the soul of Elias Thorne exulted—more, far more, will I accomplish; treading the faint paths others have already marked, I will cut an untrodden way forward, probe powers no mortal has ever uncovered, and lay bare the deepest, most closely guarded mysteries of creation for all the world to witness.

Like GPT-2, DIALOGPT is formulated as an autoregressive (AR) language model, and uses the multi-layer transformer as model architecture. Unlike GPT-2, however, DIALOGPT is trained on large-scale dialogue pairs/sessions extracted from Reddit discussion chains. Our assumption is that this should enable DIALOGPT to capture the joint distribution of P (Target, Source) in conversational flow with finer granularity. In practice, this is what we observe: sentences generated by DIALOGPT are diverse and contain information specific to the source prompt, analogous what GPT-2 generates for continuous text. We have evaluated the pre-trained model on a public benchmark dataset (DSTC-7), and a new 6k multireference test dataset extracted from Reddit postings. DIALOGPT achieves state-of-the-art results in both automatic and human evaluation, lifting performance to near-human response quality.

metadata.title: DIALOGPT : Large-Scale Generative Pre-training for Conversational Response Generation

text: Similar to GPT-2, DIALOGPT is structured as an autoregressive (AR) language model, with a multi-layer transformer as its core architectural backbone. It diverges from GPT-2, however, in its training corpus, which consists of large-scale dialogue pairs and full conversational sessions extracted from public Reddit discussion threads. We hypothesize that this training data configuration allows DIALOGPT to model the joint probability distribution P(Target, Source) of turn-taking in conversational flows at a finer level of granularity. Empirical results align with this hypothesis: outputs generated by DIALOGPT are diverse, contain information specific to the input source prompt, and are analogous to the coherent outputs GPT-2 produces for long-form continuous text. We evaluated the pre-trained DIALOGPT model across two test sets: the publicly available DSTC-7 benchmark dataset, and a newly curated 6k multi-reference test set sourced from Reddit postings. DIALOGPT attains state-of-the-art results in both automatic and human evaluation, pushing overall performance to near-human conversational response quality levels.

I cannot guess how many days have passed since then, but I have endured misery which nothing but the eternal sentiment of a just retribution burning within my heart could have enabled me to support. Immense and rugged mountains of ice often barred up my passage, and I often heard the thunder of the ground sea, which threatened my destruction. But again the frost came and made the paths of the sea secure.

The count of days has long escaped me, yet I have weathered a wretchedness that only the inextinguishable thirst for a righteous vengeance could have steeled me to bear. Great, jagged ramparts of ice frequently choked my progress, and the deafening boom of the churning depths below seemed to herald my doom. Yet, the biting air would descend once more, knitting the fractured waves into a firm and traversable floor.

Mr. Bumble sat in the workhouse parlour, with his eyes moodily fixed on the cheerless grate, whence, as it was summer time, no brighter gleam proceeded, than the reflection of certain sickly rays of the sun, which were sent back from its cold and shining surface. A paper fly-cage dangled from the ceiling, to which he occasionally raised his eyes in gloomy thought; and, as the heedless insects hovered round the gaudy net-work, Mr. Bumble would heave a deep sigh, while a more gloomy shadow overspread his countenance. Mr. Bumble was meditating; it might be that the insects brought to mind, some painful passage in his own past life.

Mr. Barrow sat in the parish parlour, his eyes fixed in a sullen reverie upon the cheerless grate, from which, though it was summertime, no warmer light was returned than the faint reflection of a few wan sunbeams cast back from its cold, polished face. From the ceiling there hung a paper cage for flies, toward which he now and then lifted his glance in despondent reflection; and as the foolish creatures drifted about the showy mesh, Mr. Barrow would draw a long sigh, while a deeper cloud seemed to settle upon his brow. Mr. Barrow was in thought; perhaps the insects recalled to him some bitter chapter in his own earlier life.

I appear incognito, of course, as you may gather from my appearance. It is a fancy of mine to render aid to others whom I think worthy of it. Perhaps the matter that seems to distress you is one that would more readily yield to our mutual efforts.”

As you might infer from my dress, I am traveling incognito. It is a whim of mine to offer help to those I deem deserving. Perhaps the trouble that so plainly afflicts you might be more easily resolved if we were to face it together.”

His sight, like the wings in Rasselas, though useless to him for his grand purpose, sufficed for this strait, and he found that when a little practice should have hardened his palms against blistering he would be able to work with ease.

How to discover a solution to this riddle of death seemed a query of more importance than highest problems of the living. There was housed in his memory a vivid picture of the face of a little boy as he entered the hovel where Clym’s mother lay. The round eyes, eager gaze, the piping voice which enunciated the words, had operated like stilettos on his brain.

He remained in that same listless state throughout the afternoon. A neighbor prepared a bed for him in the house so that he would not have to leave and return the following morning; however, when he finally retired in the empty dwelling, he spent the long hours of the night wide awake, circling the same dark thoughts. Solving the enigma of this death felt more urgent than any problem belonging to the living world. Fixed in his mind was the striking image of a small boy entering the hut where Clym’s mother lay. The child’s wide eyes, his intense gaze, and the high-pitched voice that delivered those words had pierced his brain like sharp blades.

The proposed model collects weak supervision from multiple labelling functions. Each labelling function takes a text document as input and outputs a series of spans associated with NER labels. These outputs are then aggregated using a hidden Markov model (HMM) with multiple emissions (one per labelling function) whose parameters are estimated in an unsupervised manner. Finally, the aggregated labels are employed to learn a sequence labelling model. Figure 1 illustrates this process. The process is performed on documents from the target domain, e.g. a corpus of financial news.

metadata.title: Named Entity Recognition without Labelled Data: A Weak Supervision Approach

text: The proposed model gathers weak supervision from multiple labeling functions. Each labeling function takes a text document as input and outputs a sequence of spans associated with NER labels. These outputs are then combined using a hidden Markov model (HMM) with multiple emissions—one for each labeling function—whose parameters are estimated in an unsupervised manner. Finally, the aggregated labels are used to train a sequence labeling model. Figure 1 illustrates this process. The procedure is applied to documents from the target domain, for example, a corpus of financial news.

On these black nights, when the personal Mr. Britling would lie awake thinking how unsatisfactorily Mr. Britling was going on, and when the impersonal Mr. Britling would be thinking how unsatisfactorily his universe was going on, the whole mental process had a likeness to some complex piece of orchestral music wherein the organ deplored the melancholy destinies of the race while the piccolo lamented the secret trouble of Mrs. Harrowdean; the big drum thundered at the Irish politicians, and all the violins bewailed the intellectual laxity of the university system. Meanwhile the trumpets prophesied wars and disasters, the cymbals ever and again inserted a clashing jar about the fatal delay in the automobile insurance, while the triangle broke into a plangent solo on the topic of a certain rotten gate-post he always forgot in the daytime, and how in consequence the cows from the glebe farm got into the garden and ate Mrs. Britling's carnations.

In the depths of these dark nights, the individual Mr. Britling would lie awake considering how poorly his own life was proceeding, while the universal Mr. Britling weighed the failures of the cosmos. His thoughts played out like a grand, intricate piece of music, where the heavy organ mourned the tragic fate of the human race and the shrill piccolo fretted over the private complications of Mrs. Harrowdean. The bass drum thundered with indignation at the Irish politicians, and the violins wept for the intellectual decay of the universities. Meanwhile, the trumpets heralded coming wars and ruin, the cymbals periodically struck a harsh note regarding the stalled automobile insurance, and the triangle rang out a sharp, persistent solo about a decaying gate-post he neglected during the day—reminding him how, as a result, the cows from the parish farm would invade the garden and consume his wife’s prize carnations.

Helen Della Delmar (proclaimed with a flourish of trumpets and rolling of tomtoms to be the greatest woman poet in the United States) denied Brissenden a seat beside her on Pegasus and wrote voluminous letters to the public, proving that he was no poet.

Every so often he flipped through magazines and local dailies to track how badly “Transience” was being torn apart. It had blown up in the public consciousness. But what a bizarre, bruising kind of hit it was! Everyone had read it, and every social circle and street corner hummed with debates over whether it qualified as real poetry at all. Regional papers had latched onto the conversation, and each day brought columns of stuffy academic criticism, snarky facetious editorials, and earnest, lengthy letters from subscribed readers. Elara Voss—proclaimed with blaring trumpets and rolling drum fanfare to be the greatest woman poet in the United States—refused to grant Hale a seat beside her on Pegasus, and wrote reams of open letters to the public, laying out exhaustive proof that he was no true poet.

Blacklock, a poet blind from his birth, could describe visual objects with accuracy; Professor Sanderson, who was also blind, gave excellent lectures on colour, and taught others the theory of ideas which they had and he had not. In the social sphere these gifted ones are mostly women; they can watch a world which they never saw, and estimate forces of which they have only heard. We call it intuition.

Blacklock, a poet blind from birth, was able to describe the visual world with startling precision; Professor Sanderson, who was also blind, gave masterful lectures on the properties of color, teaching others about concepts they could see while he could not. In the social realm, these gifted individuals are often women; they are able to observe a world they have never set foot in and gauge the strength of forces they have only heard about. We label this gift intuition.

metadata.title: Cross-modal Language Generation using Pivot Stabilization for Web-scale Language Coverage

text: Our experiments are conducted across two distinct benchmarks. We utilize the Multi30K dataset (Elliott et al., 2016) to evaluate the performance of the PLuGS model on a standard resource widely adopted by the research community. Our focus is on Task 1 for French as described by Caglayan et al. (2019), which involves generating a French translation using an image and an English caption as inputs. The training set is composed of images from the Flickr30K training and validation partitions, paired with their associated French captions. The validation split contains the test2016 images and captions, while the test split consists of the test2017 images and captions.

“I could,” said Rose. “Stay!” she added, disengaging her hand, “why should we prolong this painful interview? Most painful to me, and yet productive of lasting happiness, notwithstanding; for it _will_ be happiness to know that I once held the high place in your regard which I now occupy, and every triumph you achieve in life will animate me with new fortitude and firmness. Farewell, Harry! As we have met today, we meet no more; but in other relations than those in which this conversation have placed us, we may be long and happily entwined; and may every blessing that the prayers of a true and earnest heart can call down from the source of all truth and sincerity, cheer and prosper you!”

“I could,” said Rose. “Wait,” she continued, pulling her hand free, “why drag out this painful conversation? It hurts me deeply, yet it will bring lasting joy all the same—for it will be a joy to know I once held the place in your esteem that I do now, and every success you achieve in life will fill me with fresh strength and resolve. Goodbye, Harry! In addition to the pain and delay attendant on a broken limb, his exposure to the wet and cold had brought on fever and ague: which hung about him for many weeks, and reduced him sadly. But, at length, he began, by slow degrees, to get better, and to be able to say sometimes, in a few tearful words, how deeply he felt the goodness of the two sweet ladies, and how ardently he hoped that when he grew strong and well again, he could do something to show his gratitude; only something, which would let them see the love and duty with which his breast was full; something, however slight, which would prove to them that their gentle kindness had not been cast away; but that the poor boy whom their charity had rescued from misery, or death, was eager to serve them with his whole heart and soul.

Elias’s maladies were no trivial, short-lived affair. Beyond the throbbing ache and slow, cumbersome healing of his fractured leg, the hours he’d spent exposed to lashing rain and bitter frost had stirred up a raging fever and shaking ague that clung to his system for weeks, wasting his small frame to a gaunt, listless shadow. But at length, he began to mend by almost imperceptible degrees, now and then able to murmur a few wobbly, tear-thick words about how deeply he cherished the kindness of the two gentle women who’d taken him in, and how ardently he hoped that once he regained his full strength and health, he might find some small way to show his gratitude. It only needed to be some small act, something that would lay bare the overflowing devotion and sense of duty swelling in his breast; no matter how slight the effort, it would prove that their soft, unhesitating goodness had not been cast aside for nothing, that the ragged, destitute boy their charity had pulled from misery, and almost certain death, ached to devote every beat of his heart and every scrap of his strength to their care.

One key idea behind the proposed IRN model is to conduct distant supervision on the actions of template copy and generation. We diagram its motivation in Figure 2 . During training, only candidate y and its reference z are given. The exact actions that convert template y to z have to be inferred from the two templates. Here we use simple rules for the inference. Firstly, the rules check if reference token z j exists in the candidate y. The output is a label d c consisting of 1s and 0s, representing whether tokens in the reference template are existent/absent in the candidate.
