text: Now ensued one of the dullest quarter-hours I had ever endured. We waited in a silent solitude enclosed by our circles of wire, and by a circle of heavy smoke outside of these. We couldn't see over the wall of smoke, and we couldn't see through it. But at last it began to shred away lazily, and by the end of another quarter-hour the land was clear and our curiosity was enabled to satisfy itself. No living creature was in sight! We now perceived that additions had been made to our defenses. The dynamite had dug a ditch more than a hundred feet wide, all around us, and cast up an embankment some twenty-five feet high on both borders of it. As to destruction of life, it was amazing. Moreover, it was beyond estimate. Of course, we could not _count_ the dead, because they did not exist as individuals, but merely as homogeneous protoplasm, with alloys of iron and buttons.

There followed one of the dreariest quarter-hours I have ever been forced to endure. We waited in a lonely stillness, shut in by our rings of wire and by a farther ring of dense smoke. We could not look beyond that wall of smoke, nor could we pierce it with our eyes. But at last it began to tear itself apart, drifting off sluggishly; and by the end of another quarter-hour the ground was once more visible, and our curiosity was at last satisfied. Not a living thing was to be seen! We now discovered that our defenses had been strengthened. The dynamite had gouged out a trench more than a hundred feet wide all around us, and thrown up on either side of it an embankment some twenty-five feet high. As for the destruction of life, it was astonishing. Indeed, it was beyond reckoning. Naturally, we could not count the dead, since they no longer existed as separate beings, but only as indistinguishable protoplasm, mixed with bits of iron and buttons.

"What makes all this so plain are the diaries the French and English have been finding on the dead. You know at the early state of the war every German soldier was expected to keep a diary. He was ordered to do it. The idea was to keep him interested in the war. Consequently, from the dead and wounded our people have got thousands.... It helps one to realise that the Germans aren't really soldiers at all. Not as our men are. They are obedient, law-abiding, intelligent people, who have been shoved into this. They have to see the war as something romantic and melodramatic, or as something moral, or as tragic fate. They have to bellow songs about 'Deutschland,' or drag in 'Gott.' They don't take to the game as our men take to the game....

The clarity of this reality is rooted in the countless journals retrieved by our forces from the pockets of the fallen and the maimed. During the war’s initial phases, every infantryman of the Kaiser was strictly enjoined to record his daily thoughts; it was a formal command designed to sustain his psychological investment in the struggle. From these thousands of recovered testimonies, a singular truth emerges: these men are not soldiers by instinct. Unlike our own troops, they are merely orderly, educated civilians propelled into the fray. They require a lens of high romance or heavy tragedy to make the violence bearable, framing the conflict as a moral imperative or a stroke of destiny. They must shout their patriotic hymns and invoke the Divine to bolster their spirits. They possess none of that effortless affinity for the sport of battle that defines our own men.

“Come now,” Mr. Bumble said, his tone a shade less self-important, since seeing the impact of his words rather pleased him. “Come, Oliver! MY DEAR EUSTACIA,—I must obey my heart without consulting my reason too closely. Will you come back to me? Do so, and the past shall never be mentioned. I was too severe; but O, Eustacia, the provocation! You don’t know, you never will know, what those words of anger cost me which you drew down upon yourself. All that an honest man can promise you I promise now, which is that from me you shall never suffer anything on this score again. After all the vows we have made, Eustacia, I think we had better pass the remainder of our lives in trying to keep them. Come to me, then, even if you reproach me. I have thought of your sufferings that morning on which I parted from you; I know they were genuine, and they are as much as you ought to bear. Our love must still continue. Such hearts as ours would never have been given us but to be concerned with each other. I could not ask you back at first, Eustacia, for I was unable to persuade myself that he who was with you was not there as a lover. But if you will come and explain distracting appearances I do not question that you can show your honesty to me. Why have you not come before? Do you think I will not listen to you? Surely not, when you remember the kisses and vows we exchanged under the summer moon. Return then, and you shall be warmly welcomed. I can no longer think of you to your prejudice—I am but too much absorbed in justifying you.—Your husband as ever,

We put up at the inn in a village just at nightfall, and when I rose next morning and looked abroad, I was ware where a knight came riding in the golden glory of the new day, and recognized him for knight of mine--Sir Ozana le Cure Hardy. He was in the gentlemen's furnishing line, and his missionarying specialty was plug hats. He was clothed all in steel, in the beautifulest armor of the time--up to where his helmet ought to have been; but he hadn't any helmet, he wore a shiny stove-pipe hat, and was ridiculous a spectacle as one might want to see. It was another of my surreptitious schemes for extinguishing knighthood by making it grotesque and absurd. Sir Ozana's saddle was hung about with leather hat boxes, and every time he overcame a wandering knight he swore him into my service and fitted him with a plug and made him wear it. I dressed and ran down to welcome Sir Ozana and get his news.

We came to a village inn just as dusk was falling, and when I rose the next morning and gazed out, I saw a knight riding through the golden blaze of dawn. I knew him at once for one of my own—Sir Ozana le Cure Hardy. His trade was gentlemen’s outfitting, and his particular missionary charge was the promotion of plug hats. He was sheathed from head to foot in steel, clad in the finest armor of the age—up to the point where his helmet should have sat; but he wore no helmet. Instead, a glossy stovepipe hat crowned his head, and a more ludicrous figure you could hardly hope to behold. This was another of my quiet stratagems to kill off knighthood by rendering it grotesque and absurd. Sir Ozana’s saddle was festooned with leather hat-boxes, and whenever he vanquished a knight-errant he made him swear fealty to me, fitted him with a plug hat, and forced him to wear it. I dressed quickly and hurried down to greet Sir Ozana and hear his tidings.

Right then, Della’s beautiful hair tumbled loose around her, rippling and glinting like a cascading fall of chestnut brown water. It hung all the way below her knees, so long it practically functioned as a second garment for her. A second later, she twisted it back up again, jittery and moving in quick, fumbling bursts. For a full minute she wavered, standing stock still, as one or two tears splattered onto the well-worn red carpet beneath her feet.

I left the house, the horrid scene of the last night’s contention, and walked on the beach of the sea, which I almost regarded as an insuperable barrier between me and my fellow creatures; nay, a wish that such should prove the fact stole across me. I desired that I might pass my life on that barren rock, wearily, it is true, but uninterrupted by any sudden shock of misery. If I returned, it was to be sacrificed or to see those whom I most loved die under the grasp of a dæmon whom I had myself created.

I left the house, the dreadful scene of the previous night’s conflict, and wandered along the shore, which I almost thought of as an impassable wall between myself and my fellow beings; indeed, the wish that this might truly be so crossed my mind. If I returned, it would be to be offered up, or to watch those I loved most die in the grasp of a demon whom I myself had made.

If sincere prayers born of profound gratitude find their way to heaven—and truly, what else could?—the blessings the boy invoked for his protectors took root in their hearts, spreading a quiet sense of serenity and contentment.

The WMT datasets contain segment-and systemlevel human judgments that we use for evaluating the quality of our reference-free metrics. The segment-level judgments assign one direct assessment (DA) score to each pair of system and human translation, while system-level judgments associate each system with a single DA score averaged across all pairs in the dataset. We initially suspected the DA scores to be biased for our setup-which compares x with y-as they are based on comparing y and y. Indeed, it is known that (especially) human professional translators "improve" y , e.g., by making it more readable, relative to the original x (Rabinovich et al., 2017) . We investigated the validity of DA scores by collecting human assessments in the cross-lingual settings (CLDA), where annotators directly compare source and translation pairs (x, y) from the WMT17 dataset. This small-scale manual analysis hints that DA scores are a valid proxy for CLDA. Therefore, we decided to treat them as reliable scores for our setup and evaluate our proposed metrics by comparing their correlation with DA scores.

metadata.title: On the Limitations of Cross-lingual Encoders as Exposed by Reference-Free Machine Translation Evaluation

text: The WMT datasets provide both segment-level and system-level human judgments, which we use to assess the quality of our reference-free metrics. At the segment level, each source–translation pair is assigned a single direct assessment (DA) score, whereas at the system level, each system is associated with one DA score obtained by averaging over all pairs in the dataset. We initially hypothesized that DA scores might be biased in our setting, which compares x with y, since they are derived from comparisons of y with y. Indeed, it is well known that human professional translators, in particular, may “improve” y—for example, by making it more readable—relative to the original x (Rabinovich et al., 2017). To examine the validity of DA scores, we collected human evaluations in the cross-lingual setting (CLDA), in which annotators directly compare source and translation pairs (x, y) from the WMT17 dataset. This small-scale manual study suggests that DA scores are a valid proxy for CLDA. We therefore treat them as reliable scores for our setting and evaluate our proposed metrics by measuring their correlation with DA scores.

She was now literally trembling and panting at this her temerity in such an errant undertaking; her breath came and went quickly, and her eyes shone with an infrequent light. Yet go she must. She reached the verge of a pit in the middle of the ferns. Troy stood in the bottom, looking up towards her.

By now she was literally trembling and breathless at her boldness in such a reckless venture; her breathing came fast and uneven, and her eyes were bright with an uncommon light. Yet go on she must. She came to the edge of a pit set among the ferns. Troy was standing in the bottom of it, looking up at her.

"There's no end of such little incidents," said Mr. Britling, cutting in without apparent effort. "You see, we get it on both sides. Our manufacturer class was, of course, originally an insurgent class. It was a class of distended craftsmen. It had the craftsman's natural enterprise and natural radicalism. As soon as it prospered and sent its boys to Oxford it was lost. Our manufacturing class was assimilated in no time to the conservative classes, whose education has always had a mandarin quality--very, very little of it, and very cold and choice. In America you have so far had no real conservative class at all. Fortunate continent! You cast out your Tories, and you were left with nothing but Whigs and Radicals. But our peculiar bad luck has been to get a sort of revolutionary who is a Tory mandarin too. Ruskin and Morris, for example, were as reactionary and anti-scientific as the dukes and the bishops. Machine haters. Science haters. Rule of Thumbites to the bone. So are our current Socialists. They've filled this country with the idea that the ideal automobile ought to be made entirely by the hand labour of traditional craftsmen, quite individually, out of beaten copper, wrought iron and seasoned oak. All this electric-starter business and this electric lighting outfit I have here, is perfectly hateful to the English mind.... It isn't that we are simply backward in these things, we are antagonistic. The British mind has never really tolerated electricity; at least, not that sort of electricity that runs through wires. Too slippery and glib for it. Associates it with Italians and fluency generally, with Volta, Galvani, Marconi and so on. The proper British electricity is that high-grade useless long-sparking stuff you get by turning round a glass machine; stuff we used to call frictional electricity. Keep it in Leyden jars.... At Claverings here they still refuse to have electric bells. There was a row when the Solomonsons, who were tenants here for a time, tried to put them in...."

These sorts of episodes are endless," Mr. Britling interjected with effortless ease. "You see, we suffer from both ends of the spectrum. Our industrialist class began as a group of rebels—essentially expanded artisans. They once possessed the innate drive and progressive spirit of the skilled worker. However, as soon as they gained wealth and enrolled their sons at Oxford, that spirit vanished. The manufacturing elite was quickly absorbed into the old aristocracy, whose schooling has always been rather elitist and detached—refined, sparse, and intentionally aloof. In America, you’ve managed to avoid a genuine conservative caste thus far. A lucky continent! By expelling your Loyalists, you were left with nothing but liberals and reformers. 

Our specific misfortune, however, is a breed of radical who is also a traditionalist snob. Figures like Ruskin and Morris, for instance, were just as regressive and hostile to science as any bishop or nobleman. They loathed machinery and despised scientific rigor, clinging to outdated intuition. Modern Socialists follow suit. They’ve convinced the public that a perfect car should be hand-forged by lone artisans using hammered copper, forged iron, and aged wood. This modern convenience of electric ignitions and lighting systems is utterly loathsome to the British psyche. We aren't merely behind the times; we are actively hostile. The English temperament has never truly accepted electricity—at least not the kind that flows through circuits. It feels too slick, too foreign. It brings to mind Italian cleverness—Volta, Galvani, Marconi, and the like. To us, respectable electricity is that dignified, impractical variety that produces long sparks from a hand-cranked glass wheel—the old frictional sort kept safely in Leyden jars. At the Claverings estate, they still won't allow electric doorbells. There was quite a scandal when the Solomonsons, during their tenancy, attempted to install them.

And often, at such times, he would abruptly see slouch in among the company a young hoodlum in square-cut coat and under a stiff-rim Stetson hat. It happened to him at the Gallina Society in Oakland one afternoon. As he rose from his chair and stepped forward across the platform, he saw stalk through the wide door at the rear of the great room the young hoodlum with the square-cut coat and stiff-rim hat. Five hundred fashionably gowned women turned their heads, so intent and steadfast was Martin’s gaze, to see what he was seeing. But they saw only the empty centre aisle. He saw the young tough lurching down that aisle and wondered if he would remove the stiff-rim which never yet had he seen him without. Straight down the aisle he came, and up the platform. Martin could have wept over that youthful shade of himself, when he thought of all that lay before him. Across the platform he swaggered, right up to Martin, and into the foreground of Martin’s consciousness disappeared. The five hundred women applauded softly with gloved hands, seeking to encourage the bashful great man who was their guest. And Martin shook the vision from his brain, smiled, and began to speak.

Often during those moments, he’d suddenly notice a young tough slouching into the gathering, wearing a boxy coat and a stiff-brimmed Stetson. It came upon him one afternoon at the Gallina Society in Oakland. As he got up from his seat and moved toward the front of the stage, he watched the young hoodlum in the boxy coat and rigid-brim hat stride through the broad doorway at the back of the hall. Five hundred elegantly dressed women turned their heads, drawn by the intensity and fixity of Martin’s stare, trying to spot what he saw. But they found only the vacant central aisle. He watched the young rough shamble down that aisle and wondered if he’d take off the stiff brim, which Martin had never yet glimpsed him without. Straight down the aisle he came, then up onto the stage. Martin felt like weeping over that spectral young self when he considered everything the boy still had to face. The figure swaggered across the platform, right up to Martin, and then faded from the foreground of his mind. The five hundred women applauded gently with gloved hands, trying to hearten the shy, celebrated man who was their guest. And Martin shook the image from his thoughts, smiled, and began his speech.

At this moment, on the ridge, up against the blazing sky, a figure was visible, like the black snuff in the midst of a candle-flame. Then it moved and began to bustle about vigorously from place to place, carrying square skeleton masses, which were riddled by the same rays. A small figure on all fours followed behind. The tall form was that of Gabriel Oak; the small one that of George; the articles in course of transit were hurdles.

At that instant, on the crest against the burning sky, a figure showed itself, like the black core of a wick in the heart of a candle-flame. Then it stirred and began to move busily from point to point, bearing square, gaunt frames through which the same shafts of light were pierced. A lesser shape crept after it on hands and knees. The taller was Elias Venn; the smaller, Tom; and the objects being carried were hurdles.

“Quite true,” said Mr. Darnel, to whom it appeared the proper moment had come for speech. “That is perhaps America’s difficulty even more than England’s. Though I have not had the paternal experience you have gone through.... I can see very plainly that a son is a matter of the gravest consequence.”

Troy entered the carrier’s house, which had been the place of his lodging on his last visit to his native place. Boldwood was moved by a sudden determination. He hastened home. In ten minutes he was back again, and made as if he were going to call upon Troy at the carrier’s. But as he approached, some one opened the door and came out. He heard this person say “Good-night” to the inmates, and the voice was Troy’s. This was strange, coming so immediately after his arrival. Boldwood, however, hastened up to him. Troy had what appeared to be a carpet-bag in his hand—the same that he had brought with him. It seemed as if he were going to leave again this very night.

Elias stepped through the door of the stagecoach operator’s cottage, the same space he’d rented a room in during his last trip back to his childhood valley. Gideon was seized by a sharp, unplanned resolve. He rushed back to his own farmstead, and returned barely ten minutes later, feigning an errand to call on Elias at the cottage. But just as he neared the porch, the door swung open and a figure stepped out. He heard the man bid the household “Good-night,” and the timbre was unmistakably Elias’s. It was a bizarrely quick departure, given he’d only just arrived minutes earlier. Even so, Gideon quickened his pace to catch up. Elias clutched what looked like a carpet satchel in his fist—the very same one he’d carried when he arrived. It seemed for all the world as if he intended to leave town again that very evening.

Consequence-based analysis would be implemented over naturalistic data rather than templates by embedding it in higher level tasks like question answering to mitigate the unnaturalness problem and demonstrate a model's practical utility. The possibility of side-channel information is already known in relation to these higher-level tasks (e.g., Poliak et al., 2018; Geva et al., 2019) , and various challenge data sets have been constructed to mitigate it in different ways (Levesque et al., 2011; Chao et al., 2017; Dua et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2019; . Uniting these with a collection of hard sentence types (e.g., Marvin and Linzen, 2018; Warstadt et al., 2019) in something like a syntax-focused QA challenge set would provide new insights into which families of models capture the practical benefits of true hierarchical syntactic representation.

metadata.title: Overestimation of Syntactic Representation in Neural Language Models

text: Consequence-based analysis will be deployed on naturalistic rather than template-generated data, integrated into high-level tasks such as question answering to address the unnaturalness limitation and empirically validate a model’s real-world utility. The risk of spurious side-channel information leakage is well-documented for this class of high-level tasks (e.g., Poliak et al., 2018; Geva et al., 2019), and a broad range of challenge datasets have been developed to target and reduce this bias across varied task contexts (Levesque et al., 2011; Chao et al., 2017; Dua et al., 2019; Lin et al., 2019). Combining these resources with a curated corpus of linguistically challenging sentence stimuli (e.g., Marvin & Linzen, 2018; Warstadt et al., 2019) to construct a syntax-centric question answering challenge benchmark would yield novel empirical insights into which model families successfully capture the functional advantages of authentic hierarchical syntactic representation.

But dismissing all this he went onward home, and came to the front of his house. The blinds of Eustacia’s bedroom were still closely drawn, for she was no early riser. All the life visible was in the shape of a solitary thrush cracking a small snail upon the door-stone for his breakfast, and his tapping seemed a loud noise in the general silence which prevailed; but on going to the door Clym found it unfastened, the young girl who attended upon Eustacia being astir in the back part of the premises. Yeobright entered and went straight to his wife’s room.

Putting these thoughts aside, he continued on his way home until he stood before his house. Eustacia’s bedroom curtains remained tightly shut, as she never woke early. The only sign of life was a lone thrush on the doorstep, smashing a tiny snail against the stone for its morning meal; the sharp rapping of its beak rang out unnaturally loud in the deep stillness all around. But when Clym reached the door, he discovered it was unlatched—the girl who waited on Eustacia was already up and moving about at the back of the house. Yeobright stepped inside and made directly for his wife’s room.

In all weathers, in the snow and frost of winter, in the bitter winds of spring, in the hot sunshine of summer, in the rains of autumn, and again in the snow and frost of winter, Lucie passed two hours of every day at this place; and every day on leaving it, she kissed the prison wall. Her husband saw her (so she learned from her father) it might be once in five or six times: it might be twice or thrice running: it might be, not for a week or a fortnight together. It was enough that he could and did see her when the chances served, and on that possibility she would have waited out the day, seven days a week.

Through biting winter frosts and drifting snows, through the raw gales of spring, under the blazing summer sun, amid the autumn downpours, and once more through the biting frosts and snows of winter, Elara kept her daily vigil of two hours at that spot; and each day, as she departed, she pressed her lips to the stone wall. Her beloved saw her (so her father told her) perhaps once in five or six visits, perhaps two or three times in succession, perhaps not at all for a week or even a fortnight together. It sufficed that he could and did behold her whenever fortune permitted, and on that slender chance she would have lingered the whole day long, every day of the week.

I suspected as much the moment you walked in," Monks replied, observing the venomous glare the lady directed at her husband. "That is all for the best; I have far less hesitation in bargaining with two people when I see that only one of them truly calls the shots. I am in earnest. Look here!

And now night drooped slowly upon the wide watery levels in front; and at no great distance from them, where the shoreline curved round, and formed a long riband of shade upon the horizon, a series of points of yellow light began to start into existence, denoting the spot to be the site of Budmouth, where the lamps were being lighted along the parade. The cluck of their oars was the only sound of any distinctness upon the sea, and as they laboured amid the thickening shades the lamp-lights grew larger, each appearing to send a flaming sword deep down into the waves before it, until there arose, among other dim shapes of the kind, the form of the vessel for which they were bound.

Evening settled slowly over the vast, watery expanse ahead. Nearby, where the coast curved to trace a dark ribbon against the horizon, a row of amber sparks began to flicker into view, signaling the location of Budmouth as the lamps were lit along the promenade. The rhythmic thud of their oars was the only distinct sound on the water, and as they rowed through the deepening shadows, the lights grew brighter, each casting a fiery blade deep into the waves. Finally, among the other hazy outlines, the silhouette of the ship they were heading for loomed into sight.

But the horrors of the unknown had passed. Vague misgivings about her future as a deserted wife were at an end. The worst had once been matter of trembling conjecture; it was now matter of reason only, a limited badness. Her chief interest, the little Eustacia, still remained. There was humility in her grief, no defiance in her attitude; and when this is the case a shaken spirit is apt to be stilled.

Yet the gnawing terror of the unknowable had lifted. The faint, persistent misgivings she’d carried about her future as an abandoned wife had faded entirely. The worst fate she’d once trembled to guess at was now nothing more than a cold, practical fact, a bounded hardship with no hidden shocks left to offer. Her dearest priority, her small daughter Lila, still remained at the center of her world. Her grief held no spark of defiance, no sharp edge of fury at her circumstances; when a sorrow wears that quiet, unresisting shape, a frayed, shaken spirit will almost always settle into gentle stillness.

"I slept thirty hours after I got back here. And now I am slowly digesting these experiences. Most of our fellows are. My mind and nerves have been rather bumped and bruised by the shelling, but not so much as you might think. I feel as though I'd presently not think very much of it. Some of our men have got the stun of it a lot more than I have. It gets at the older men more. Everybody says that. The men of over thirty-five don't recover from a shelling for weeks. They go about--sort of hesitatingly....

I crashed for thirty straight hours the second I got back here. Right now I’m still slowly processing everything I went through out there, and most of the other fellows in my unit are doing the same. My mind and nerves are pretty frayed and rattled from all the shelling, though not half as bad as you’d probably guess. I get the sense I’ll barely even think much of the whole ordeal before too long. Plenty of our men took the shock of the barrage far worse than I did, though. It hits the older fellows hardest, everyone says that. Men over thirty-five don’t bounce back from a stretch of shelling for weeks on end. They move around… sort of halting, tentative, like they’re not sure what’s coming next.

So Martin thought, and so he spoke when Brissenden urged him to give them hell. He obeyed the mandate, walking up to the platform, as was the custom, and addressing the chairman. He began in a low voice, haltingly, forming into order the ideas which had surged in his brain while the Jew was speaking. In such meetings five minutes was the time allotted to each speaker; but when Martin’s five minutes were up, he was in full stride, his attack upon their doctrines but half completed. He had caught their interest, and the audience urged the chairman by acclamation to extend Martin’s time. They appreciated him as a foeman worthy of their intellect, and they listened intently, following every word. He spoke with fire and conviction, mincing no words in his attack upon the slaves and their morality and tactics and frankly alluding to his hearers as the slaves in question. He quoted Spencer and Malthus, and enunciated the biological law of development.

He began in a low voice, with some hesitation, setting in order the thoughts that had risen in his mind while the Jew was speaking. At such meetings each speaker was allowed five minutes; but when Martin’s five minutes had expired, he was in full stride, and his attack on their doctrines was only half finished. He had seized their attention, and the audience called on the chairman by acclamation to extend Martin’s time. They respected him as an opponent worthy of their minds, and they listened closely, catching every word. He spoke with fire and conviction, sparing no one in his assault on the slaves and their morality and methods, and openly referring to his hearers as the slaves in question. He quoted Spencer and Malthus, and stated the biological law of development.

Mr. Britling returned to the Dower House perplexed and profoundly disturbed. He had a distressful sense that things were far more serious with Teddy than he had tried to persuade Letty they were; that "wounded and missing" meant indeed a man abandoned to very sinister probabilities. He was distressed for Teddy, and still more acutely distressed for Mrs. Teddy, whose every note and gesture betrayed suppositions even more sinister than his own. And that preposterous sense of liability, because he had helped Teddy to get his commission, was more distressful than it had ever been. He was surprised that Letty had not assailed him with railing accusations.

Mr. Halloway retreated to Old Orchard, his mind clouded by a heavy, gnawing unease. He was haunted by the conviction that Leo’s situation was far more precarious than the fragile hopes he had offered Sybilla; "unaccounted for" was a designation that likely hid a brutal and lonely end. He felt a deep sorrow for the boy, yet an even sharper pang for the young woman, whose every word and movement hinted at terrors far darker than his own. Most agonizing of all was that irrational sense of debt, the realization that his own hand had smoothed the path to Leo’s enlistment. He found it remarkable, almost unnerving, that Sybilla had not yet lashed out at him with the accusations he felt he deserved.

He crossed over towards Bathsheba, who turned to greet him with a carriage of perfect ease. He spoke to her in low tones, and she instinctively modulated her own to the same pitch, and her voice ultimately even caught the inflection of his. She was far from having a wish to appear mysteriously connected with him; but woman at the impressionable age gravitates to the larger body not only in her choice of words, which is apparent every day, but even in her shades of tone and humour, when the influence is great.

He came across to Bathsheba, who turned to meet him with an air of complete composure. He addressed her quietly, and she, by instinct, lowered her own voice to match his; before long, her speech had even taken on his inflection. She was by no means eager to seem secretly linked with him; yet a woman at an impressionable age is drawn toward the stronger presence not only in her choice of words, as is seen every day, but even in the finer shades of tone and feeling, when the influence is powerful.

Human judgements were given by professional translators following the FLORES setup (Guzmán et al., 2019) which presents a form of DA judgements (Graham et al., 2013) . The annotators were asked to rate each sentence from 0-100 according to the perceived translation quality. Specifically, the 0-10 range represents an incorrect translation; 11-29, a translation with few correct keywords, but the overall meaning is different from the source; 30-50, a translation with major mistakes; 51-69, a translation which is understandable and conveys the overall meaning of the source but contains typos or grammatical errors; 70-90, a translation that closely preserves the semantics of the source sentence; and 90-100, a perfect translation. Each segment was annotated by up to 6 translators. Raw scores were converted into z-scores, i.e. standardised according to each individual annotator's overall mean and standard deviation. The scores collected for each segment were averaged to obtain the final score. The judgments were collected for the 1K segments translated by the standard Transformer model and for the 400 segments produced by four MT model variants in §3, resulting in a total of 1000 + 4 * 400 = 2600 source-MT pairs annotated with DA judgments. The distribution of DA scores for English-Czech and 1K Estonian-English datasets is shown in the Appendix A. 4

text: We also study the average number of unique keyphrase predictions per document. Duplicated keyphrases are removed. The results are shown in Table 5 . One main finding is that all the models generate an insufficient number of unique keyphrases on most datasets, especially for predicting absent keyphrases. We also observe that our methods can improve the number of unique keyphrases by a large margin, which is extremely beneficial to solve the problem of insufficient generation. Correspondingly, it also leads to overgenerate more keyphrases than the ground-truth for the cases that do not have this problem, such as the present keyphrase predictions on Krapivin and KP20k datasets. We leave solving the overgeneration of present keyphrases on Krapivin and KP20k as our future work.

metadata.title: Exclusive Hierarchical Decoding for Deep Keyphrase Generation

text: We further analyze the average number of unique keyphrase predictions per document, with duplicate keyphrases removed. The results are reported in Table 5. A key observation is that all models produce too few unique keyphrases on most datasets, particularly when predicting absent keyphrases. We also find that our methods substantially increase the number of unique keyphrase predictions, which is highly effective for alleviating the problem of undergeneration. However, this improvement also causes the models to overgenerate keyphrases beyond the ground truth in settings where this issue is not present, such as present keyphrase prediction on the Krapivin and KP20k datasets. We leave the problem of overgeneration for present keyphrases on Krapivin and KP20k to future work.

Editors wrote to him telling him to name his own terms, which he did, but it was always for work performed. He refused resolutely to pledge himself to any new thing. The thought of again setting pen to paper maddened him. He had seen Brissenden torn to pieces by the crowd, and despite the fact that him the crowd acclaimed, he could not get over the shock nor gather any respect for the crowd. His very popularity seemed a disgrace and a treason to Brissenden. It made him wince, but he made up his mind to go on and fill the money-bag.

Publishers dispatched letters urging him to dictate his own price, and he obliged, though always for labor already delivered. He would not bind himself to any fresh undertaking. The mere notion of once more lowering nib to page sent a fury through his veins. He had watched the multitude rip Carrington limb from limb, and even though that same multitude now lionized him, he could neither shake the horror nor muster a shred of reverence for them. His very renown struck him as a betrayal and a stain upon Carrington. It made him flinch, yet he steeled himself to press onward and swell the purse.

• SRL-based Semantic Graph. The task of Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) is to identify what semantic relations hold among a predicate and its associated participants and properties , including "who" did "what" to "whom", etc. For each sentence, we extract predicate-argument tuples via SRL toolkits 1 . Each tuple forms a subgraph where each tuple element (e.g., arguments, location, and temporal) is a node. We add intertuple edges between nodes from different tuples if they have an inclusive relationship or potentially mention the same entity.

metadata.title: Semantic Graphs for Generating Deep Questions

text: The Semantic Role Labeling (SRL)-based semantic graph is constructed as follows. SRL aims to detect the semantic relations that link a predicate to its associated participants and properties—capturing, for instance, “who” did “what” to “whom.” For each sentence, we extract predicate–argument tuples using established SRL toolkits.1 Each tuple gives rise to a subgraph in which every tuple element (such as arguments, locations, and temporal modifiers) is represented as a node. We then introduce intertuple edges between nodes belonging to different tuples when they stand in an inclusive relation or are likely to refer to the same entity.

"Our feeding here is typical of the whole system. It is a system invented not with any idea of getting the best results--that does not enter into the War Office philosophy--but to have a rule for everything, and avoid arguments. There is rather too generous an allowance of bread and stuff per man, and there is a very fierce but not very efficient system of weighing and checking. A rather too generous allowance is, of course, a direct incentive to waste or stealing--as any one but our silly old duffer of a War Office would know. The checking is for quantity, which any fool can understand, rather than for quality. The test for the quality of army meat is the smell. If it doesn't smell bad, it is good....

“Our provisioning here is a fair sample of the whole machine. It is a machine devised not with the least notion of securing the best outcome—that never enters the creed of the War Department—but in order that there may be a rule for every circumstance and no one need argue. The ration of bread and other matter per man is, if anything, somewhat too liberal, and over it there hangs a very savage though not very effective apparatus of weighing and inspection. An allowance a shade too ample is, naturally enough, a plain invitation to waste or pilfering—as anyone except our absurd old fool of a War Department would understand. The inspection concerns quantity, which any idiot can grasp, rather than quality. The proper test of army meat is the nose. If it does not stink, it is good....

It was a cold, dark night. The stars seemed, to the boy’s eyes, farther from the earth than he had ever seen them before; there was no wind; and the sombre shadows thrown by the trees upon the ground, looked sepulchral and death-like, from being so still. He softly reclosed the door. Having availed himself of the expiring light of the candle to tie up in a handkerchief the few articles of wearing apparel he had, sat himself down upon a bench, to wait for morning.

It was a chill, black night. To the boy, the stars appeared more remote from the earth than ever he had known them; not a breath of wind stirred; and the gloomy shadows the trees cast upon the ground wore, by reason of their motionless gloom, an air of the grave, as though death itself had lain upon them. He gently closed the door again. Then, using the dying glimmer of the candle, he tied the few garments he possessed into a handkerchief, and seated himself upon a bench to await the coming of morning.

metadata.title: Improving Massively Multilingual Neural Machine Translation and Zero-Shot Translation

text: The model yields improved language accuracy, with zero-shot accuracy gains of +7.78% and +13.81% for the 3→5 and 3→4 experimental conditions respectively in the absence of ROBT, as well as stronger zero-shot translation performance both prior to ROBT application (with zero-shot BLEU improvements of 1.22 and 0.53 for 3→5 and 3→4, no ROBT) and following ROBT integration (with corresponding zero-shot BLEU gains of 2.20 and 1.56 for 3→5 and 3→4, with ROBT). In short, increasing modeling capacity confers dual advantages for zero-shot translation, elevating both core performance and robustness. Turning to the convergence behavior of ROBT: unlike prior research (Gu et al., 2019; Lakew et al., 2019), we employ an online backtranslation framework in this work. The performance curve presented in Figure 1 confirms that ROBT is highly effective, requiring only several thousand training steps to converge, which suggests that decoding the full training corpus for each zero-shot language pair is unnecessary for effective implementation. We leave to future work the exploration of whether alternative backtranslation strategies (beyond greedy decoding) can deliver larger, sustained performance improvements when used in combination with ROBT.

We test what demographic profiles author attribute tools predict for the translations from various commercially available machine translation tools. We find that independent of the MT system and the translation quality, the predicted demographics differ systematically when translating into English. On average, translations make the author seem substantially older and more male. Translating from English into any of the other languages shows more mixed results, but similar tendencies. for Data Science and Analytics (BIDSA) and the Data and Marketing Insights (DMI) unit.

metadata.title: "You Sound Just Like Your Father" Commercial Machine Translation Systems Include Stylistic Biases

text: We examine which demographic profiles author attribution tools predict for translations produced by a range of commercially available machine translation systems. We find that, regardless of the MT system and the translation quality, the predicted demographics shift systematically when translating into English. On average, translations make the author appear substantially older and more male. Translating from English into other languages yields more mixed results, but similar tendencies are evident.

The stillness consequent on the cessation of the rumbling and labouring of the coach, added to the stillness of the night, made it very quiet indeed. The panting of the horses communicated a tremulous motion to the coach, as if it were in a state of agitation. The hearts of the passengers beat loud enough perhaps to be heard; but at any rate, the quiet pause was audibly expressive of people out of breath, and holding the breath, and having the pulses quickened by expectation.

Once the coach’s grinding, laborious rumble cut out, the hush that fell layered over the preexisting quiet of the night, wrapping everything in a profound, almost weighty silence. The horses’ heavy, ragged pants sent a faint, trembling shiver through the coach’s frame, as if the vehicle itself was thrumming with suppressed unease. The passengers’ heartbeats might well have been loud enough to carry through the stillness; in any case, the quiet lull hummed with the unmistakeable edge of people half-out of breath, holding their air, pulses thrumming sharp with taut, waiting expectation.

Yes, it was a neat thing, very neat and pretty to see. It resembled a steamboat explosion on the Mississippi; and during the next fifteen minutes we stood under a steady drizzle of microscopic fragments of knights and hardware and horse-flesh. I say we, for the king joined the audience, of course, as soon as he had got his breath again. There was a hole there which would afford steady work for all the people in that region for some years to come --in trying to explain it, I mean; as for filling it up, that service would be comparatively prompt, and would fall to the lot of a select few--peasants of that seignory; and they wouldn't get anything for it, either.

Oh, it was a fine sight, remarkably sharp and charming to take in. It looked exactly like a canal barge detonation on the Severn; and for the full quarter-hour after, we huddled under a steady fine drizzle of minuscule scraps of jousters, plate steel, and horse flesh. I say we, because the king joined the crowd of onlookers, of course, the second he’d caught his breath again. The crater left there would give every soul in that region steady work for years on end—trying to explain how it came to be, that is; as for filling it back in, that job would be over comparatively quickly, and would fall to a small, handpicked lot: the serfs tied to that lord’s estate. And they wouldn’t earn a farthing for the work, either.

I was going to step out, but the king laid his hand upon my arm. There was a moment of silence, then we heard a soft knock on the cabin door. It made me shiver. Presently the knock was repeated, and then we heard these words in a guarded voice:

The Doctor was in his best condition, and looked specially young. The resemblance between him and Lucie was very strong at such times, and as they sat side by side, she leaning on his shoulder, and he resting his arm on the back of her chair, it was very agreeable to trace the likeness.

The Doctor was at his finest, appearing almost youthful. In these moments, the likeness between him and Lucie grew remarkably vivid; as they sat close together, her head nestled against his shoulder and his arm draped along the back of her chair, it was a quiet pleasure to follow the lines of that resemblance.

Then there came a third flash. Manœuvres of a most extraordinary kind were going on in the vast firmamental hollows overhead. The lightning now was the colour of silver, and gleamed in the heavens like a mailed army. Rumbles became rattles. Gabriel from his elevated position could see over the landscape at least half-a-dozen miles in front. Every hedge, bush, and tree was distinct as in a line engraving. In a paddock in the same direction was a herd of heifers, and the forms of these were visible at this moment in the act of galloping about in the wildest and maddest confusion, flinging their heels and tails high into the air, their heads to earth. A poplar in the immediate foreground was like an ink stroke on burnished tin. Then the picture vanished, leaving the darkness so intense that Gabriel worked entirely by feeling with his hands.

A third bolt flared. Above, in the immense hollows of the sky, strange and violent maneuvers were unfolding. The lightning had turned silver now, glittering across the heavens like an armored legion. Rumbles sharpened into rattles. From his high vantage, Gabriel could survey at least six miles of the country before him. Every hedge, bush, and tree stood out with the precision of an engraving. In a paddock in that same direction, a herd of heifers was caught mid-gallop in the wildest, most frantic disorder, hooves and tails flung high, heads driven low. A poplar in the near foreground cut the scene like a stroke of ink on polished tin. Then the vision collapsed, and the darkness that followed was so absolute that Gabriel worked solely by touch.

As Martin Eden went down the steps, his hand dropped into his coat pocket. It came out with a brown rice paper and a pinch of Mexican tobacco, which were deftly rolled together into a cigarette. He drew the first whiff of smoke deep into his lungs and expelled it in a long and lingering exhalation. “By God!” he said aloud, in a voice of awe and wonder. “By God!” he repeated. And yet again he murmured, “By God!” Then his hand went to his collar, which he ripped out of the shirt and stuffed into his pocket. A cold drizzle was falling, but he bared his head to it and unbuttoned his vest, swinging along in splendid unconcern. He was only dimly aware that it was raining. He was in an ecstasy, dreaming dreams and reconstructing the scenes just past.

As Adrian Vale descended the stairs, his hand slipped into his coat pocket. It emerged with a sheet of brown rice paper and a pinch of Mexican tobacco, which he quickly and skillfully rolled together into a cigarette. He drew the first breath of smoke deep into his lungs and let it go in one long, slow exhalation. “By God!” he cried aloud, in a tone of awe and amazement. “By God!” he said again. And once more he muttered, “By God!” Then his hand went to his collar, which he tore from the shirt and thrust into his pocket. A cold drizzle was falling, but he left his head bare beneath it and unfastened his vest, striding onward in magnificent unconcern. He was only vaguely conscious that it was raining. He was in an ecstasy, dreaming and remaking in his mind the scenes of a moment before.

Once, he tried to arouse himself from his lethargy, and went forward into the forecastle with the sailors. But the breed of sailors seemed to have changed since the days he had lived in the forecastle. He could find no kinship with these stolid-faced, ox-minded bestial creatures. He was in despair. Up above nobody had wanted Martin Eden for his own sake, and he could not go back to those of his own class who had wanted him in the past. He did not want them. He could not stand them any more than he could stand the stupid first-cabin passengers and the riotous young people.

Once he made an effort to shake off his torpor and went forward into the forecastle among the sailors. But the breed of sailors seemed changed since the days when he had lived in the forecastle himself. He could find no fellowship with these stolid, ox-eyed, brutish men. Despair seized him. Above, no one had wanted Martin Eden for his own sake; and he could not return to the people of his own class who had once wanted him. He did not want them. He could no more endure them than he could endure the foolish first-cabin passengers and the boisterous young people.

And so, as kinsmen met a night,
We talked between the rooms,
Until the moss had reached our lips,
And covered up our names.

metadata.title: Poems by Emily Dickinson, Three Series, Complete

text:
Thus, like kin finding one another in the dark,
We spoke across the chamber walls,
Until the creeping moss reached our mouths
And blurred the inscriptions of our names.

Comparison to expert ratings We randomly selected 50 dialogs rated score-5 and showed these to an expert, and our expert rated 27 of them with score-4 or less. The Alexa team (Venkatesh et al., 2018) has also reported that the inter-user agreement is quite low for their internal rating analysis. Such phenomena indicate that the self-reported Likert scale ratings are extremely noisy. Using such ratings cannot localize individual bad interactions. In addition, Likert score based evaluation also suffers from insensitivity issues. As observed by the Alexa team (Venkatesh et al., 2018) in multiple internal user studies, even though users evaluated multiple dialogs with the same score, they had a clear rank order among the dialogs.

metadata.title: Beyond User Self-Reported Likert Scale Ratings: A Comparison Model for Automatic Dialog Evaluation

text: To assess alignment with expert judgments, we randomly sampled 50 dialogs that users had assigned a score of 5 and presented them to an expert evaluator; the expert rated 27 of these dialogs as 4 or lower. Similarly, the Alexa team (Venkatesh et al., 2018) documented notably low inter-user agreement in their internal rating analyses. These findings suggest that self-reported Likert-scale ratings are highly noisy. Relying on such ratings fails to pinpoint individual poor interactions. Moreover, Likert-based evaluation exhibits insensitivity: as Venkatesh et al. (2018) observed across multiple internal user studies, users who assigned identical scores to several dialogs nonetheless maintained a clear rank ordering among them.

That was all the writing. It was so much, however, to her who received it, that she turned from Defarge to his wife, and kissed one of the hands that knitted. It was a passionate, loving, thankful, womanly action, but the hand made no response--dropped cold and heavy, and took to its knitting again.

The writing ended there. Yet for the woman who received it, the words meant so much that she turned from Defarge toward his wife and kissed one of the hands that held the needles. It was a gesture of intense, heartfelt gratitude, but the hand offered no reaction—it simply fell, cold and heavy, and returned to its knitting.

“She’ll rue it—surely, surely!” he said. “Benjy Pennyways were not a true man or an honest baily—as big a betrayer as Judas Iscariot himself. But to think she can carr’ on alone!” He allowed his head to swing laterally three or four times in silence. “Never in all my creeping up—never!”

“She will regret this—most certainly!” he declared. “Benjy Pennyways was neither an honorable man nor a faithful manager—a traitor as treacherous as Judas Iscariot himself. But the idea that she thinks she can manage everything on her own!” He fell silent, letting his head sway slowly from side to side several times. “In all my long years, I have never seen the like—never!”

He drew up the other chair. The tea brightened the girl’s eyes and brought back some of her colour. She began to eat with a sort of dainty ferocity like some starved wild animal. She seemed to regard the young man’s presence and the aid he had rendered her as a natural thing—not as though she undervalued the conventions; but as one whose great stress gave her the right to put aside the artificial for the human. But gradually, with the return of strength and comfort, came also a sense of the little conventions that belong; and she began to tell him her little story. It was one of a thousand such as the city yawns at every day—the shop girl’s story of insufficient wages, further reduced by “fines” that go to swell the store’s profits; of time lost through illness; and then of lost positions, lost hope, and—the knock of the adventurer upon the green door.

He pulled the second empty chair across the scuffed linoleum and settled into it. The steaming chamomile tea gilded the young woman’s irises and painted faint rose back into her sallow, hollowed cheeks. She picked at the bread and jam first, then tore into it with a delicate, ravenous hunger, sharp as a starved wren that’s stumbled across a forgotten windowsill feeder. She treated his presence and the kindness he’d extended as something unremarkable, expected even—not that she dismissed ordinary social niceties; but when one has been stretched thin past breaking point, the unspoken rules of politeness fall away to make room for raw, unvarnished need. But as warmth seeped back into her frozen fingertips and the tight knot of hunger in her stomach unclenched, the weight of those small, unwritten social rules crept back in, and she began to haltingly share what had led her there. It was the kind of tale the great gray city swallowed by the dozen each day, no more notable than a raindrop hitting the sidewalk: a sales clerk’s account of wages too meager to cover rent, whittled even thinner by arbitrary “infraction fees” that padded the department store’s annual profits; weeks missed from work after a bout of winter flu that left her too weak to stand; then a pink termination slip, no savings left, no prospects, and finally, the desperate rap of someone with nowhere else to turn on his forest-green apartment door.

He soon found an excuse for advancing from his position in the rear, and rode close by her side. They had gone two or three miles in the moonlight, speaking desultorily across the wheel of her gig concerning the fair, farming, Oak’s usefulness to them both, and other indifferent subjects, when Boldwood said suddenly and simply—

Yeobright gave him some particulars of her illness and death, and continued: “After this no kind of pain will ever seem more than an indisposition to me. I began saying that I wanted to ask you something, but I stray from subjects like a drunken man. I am anxious to know what my mother said to you when she last saw you. You talked with her a long time, I think?”

Yeobright passed along specific details of her illness and eventual death to him, then continued speaking: “After all this, no sort of pain will ever register as anything worse than a minor ailment to me. I started out meaning to ask you a question, didn’t I? I wander off subject as easily as a man addled by drink. What I’m eager to know is what my mother said to you the last time you saw each other. You spent a good long while talking with her, if I recall correctly?”

Some have used rule-based techniques to do data cleaning (Allamanis et al., 2016; Hu et al., 2018; Fernandes et al., 2019) , and others train classifiers on hand-labeled examples that can be applied to a much larger pool of examples in order to differentiate between clean and noisy examples (Iyer et al., 2016; Yao et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2018) . Most of these approaches focus on code summarization or comment generation which only require single code-NL pairs for training and evaluation as the task entails generating a natural language summary of a given code snippet. On the contrary, our proposed task requires two code-NL pairs that are assumed to hold specific parallel relationships with one another. Namely, the relationship between C new and M new is expected to be similar to that of C old and M old . The relationship between C new and C old is expected to correlate with the relationship between M new and M old . Not only does having four moving parts in one example magnify noise, but the need to hold these relationships makes data cleaning particularly difficult. We leave building classifiers for aiding this process as future work.

metadata.title: Learning to Update Natural Language Comments Based on Code Changes

text: Certain studies have employed rule-based methods for data cleaning (Allamanis et al., 2016; Hu et al., 2018; Fernandes et al., 2019), while others have trained classifiers on manually labeled instances that can then be applied to a substantially larger corpus to distinguish clean from noisy examples (Iyer et al., 2016; Yao et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2018). The majority of these efforts concentrate on code summarization or comment generation, tasks that require only single code–natural language pairs for training and evaluation, since the objective is to produce a natural language summary of a given code snippet. In contrast, our proposed task involves two code–natural language pairs that are assumed to maintain specific parallel relationships with one another. Specifically, the relationship between C_new and M_new is expected to mirror that between C_old and M_old, and the relationship between C_new and C_old is expected to correspond to the relationship between M_new and M_old. The presence of four interdependent components in a single example not only amplifies noise but also makes data cleaning especially challenging because these relational constraints must be preserved. We defer the construction of classifiers to support this process to future work.

Firstly, while inflection is obligatory and determined by syntactic needs, the existence of derivatives is mainly driven by communicative goals, allowing to express a varied spectrum of meanings (Acquaviva, 2016) . Secondly, derivation can produce a larger number of new words than inflection since it is iterable (Haspelmath and Sims, 2010) ; derivational affixes can be combined, in some cases even recursively (e.g., post$post$modern$ism). However, morphotactic constraints restrict the ways in which affixes can be attached to stems and other affixes (Hay and Plag, 2004) ; e.g., the suffix $less can be combined with $ness (atom$less$ness) but not with $ity (atom$less$ity).

metadata.title: A Graph Auto-encoder Model of Derivational Morphology

text: Firstly, whereas inflection is obligatory and determined by syntactic requirements, the existence of derivatives is primarily driven by communicative purposes, enabling the expression of a wide range of meanings (Acquaviva, 2016). Secondly, derivation can generate a larger number of new words than inflection because it is iterable (Haspelmath and Sims, 2010); derivational affixes may be combined, in some cases even recursively (e.g., post-post-modernism). However, morphotactic constraints restrict the ways in which affixes can attach to stems and to one another (Hay and Plag, 2004); for example, the suffix -less can combine with -ness (atom-less-ness) but not with -ity (atom-less-ity).

“No,” he said, smiting the table with his fist, “I do not. The boy has a new suit of clothes on his back, a set of valuable books under his arm, and a five-pound note in his pocket. He’ll join his old friends the thieves, and laugh at you. If ever that boy returns to this house, sir, I’ll eat my head.”

“No,” he declared, slamming his fist upon the table, “I do not. The lad has fresh garments on his back, a collection of fine books beneath his arm, and a five-pound note tucked in his pocket. He’ll fall in with his old companions, the thieves, and mock you. Should that boy ever set foot in this house again, sir, I’ll devour my own head.”

“You see, it appears this way to me,” he went on. “When I was all that I am now, nobody out of my own class seemed to care for me. When my books were all written, no one who had read the manuscripts seemed to care for them. In point of fact, because of the stuff I had written they seemed to care even less for me. In writing the stuff it seemed that I had committed acts that were, to say the least, derogatory. ‘Get a job,’ everybody said.”

“Look, here’s how it struck me,” he continued. “Back when I was fully myself, not a soul beyond my own circle gave a damn about me. Once my pages were all penned, those who glanced at the drafts showed no feeling for them. Indeed, on account of the things I’d set down, they seemed to hold me in still lower regard. To have written such things felt as though I’d performed deeds that were, to put it mildly, degrading. ‘Find work,’ they all told me.”

The Echoes rarely answered to the actual tread of Sydney Carton. Some half-dozen times a year, at most, he claimed his privilege of coming in uninvited, and would sit among them through the evening, as he had once done often. He never came there heated with wine. And one other thing regarding him was whispered in the echoes, which has been whispered by all true echoes for ages and ages.

The murmurs seldom stirred at Sydney Carton’s own footfalls. At most, on five or six occasions each year, he exercised his right to appear without summons, settling among them for the night as he had habitually done in former days. He never arrived flushed with drink. And one further secret about him was breathed through the murmurs—a secret that every faithful murmur has carried across the centuries.

Out of each week’s earnings Chandler set aside $1. At the end of each ten weeks with the extra capital thus accumulated, he purchased one gentleman’s evening from the bargain counter of stingy old Father Time. He arrayed himself in the regalia of millionaires and presidents; he took himself to the quarter where life is brightest and showiest, and there dined with taste and luxury. With ten dollars a man may, for a few hours, play the wealthy idler to perfection. The sum is ample for a well-considered meal, a bottle bearing a respectable label, commensurate tips, a smoke, cab fare and the ordinary etceteras.

Every week, Chandler tucked one dollar away from his earnings. Once every ten weeks, with that small stockpile of savings, he bought himself a proper gentleman’s evening from the discount shelf of thrifty old Father Time. He dressed himself in the kind of fine garments worn by millionaires and heads of state, then headed for the city’s most glittering, ostentatious neighborhood, where he ate a meal curated for elegance and indulgence. Ten dollars is more than enough for a man to pull off the role of a carefree, wealthy idler flawlessly for a few hours. The sum covers a thoughtfully chosen meal, a bottle of reputable wine, appropriately generous tips, a smoke, cab fare, and all the usual small incidental costs that come with such an excursion.

That Ruth had little faith in his power as a writer, did not alter her nor diminish her in Martin’s eyes. In the breathing spell of the vacation he had taken, he had spent many hours in self-analysis, and thereby learned much of himself. He had discovered that he loved beauty more than fame, and that what desire he had for fame was largely for Ruth’s sake. It was for this reason that his desire for fame was strong. He wanted to be great in the world’s eyes; “to make good,” as he expressed it, in order that the woman he loved should be proud of him and deem him worthy.

The fact that Ruth doubted his talent as an author did not change Martin’s feelings or lower her in his estimation. During a brief respite from his work, he spent many hours looking inward, learning a great deal about his own character. He realized that he cherished beauty far more than he desired status, and that his ambition was mostly for Ruth’s benefit. This was the source of his powerful drive. He wanted to achieve public greatness—to "make it," as he put it—so that the woman he loved would be proud of him and consider him worthy of her.

Cramped in all kinds of dim cupboards and hutches at Tellson’s, the oldest of men carried on the business gravely. When they took a young man into Tellson’s London house, they hid him somewhere till he was old. They kept him in a dark place, like a cheese, until he had the full Tellson flavour and blue-mould upon him. Then only was he permitted to be seen, spectacularly poring over large books, and casting his breeches and gaiters into the general weight of the establishment.

In the shadowy recesses of Tellson’s, confined to cramped alcoves and narrow closets, the most ancient of clerks conducted their affairs with solemn dignity. Whenever a young man was brought into the London office, they tucked him away somewhere until age had claimed him. Like a cheese left to mature in the dark, he remained sequestered until he had absorbed the distinct Tellson essence and acquired its patina of blue mould. Only then was he allowed to emerge, conspicuously bent over enormous ledgers, his breeches and gaiters contributing their solid presence to the firm’s collective gravitas.

“Look at that,” murmured Christian Cantle. “Neighbours, how do we know but that something of the old serpent in God’s garden, that gied the apple to the young woman with no clothes, lives on in adders and snakes still? Look at his eye—for all the world like a villainous sort of black currant. ’Tis to be hoped he can’t ill-wish us! There’s folks in heath who’ve been overlooked already. I will never kill another adder as long as I live.”

“Look at that,” Christian Cantle whispered. “Friends, who’s to say some remnant of the old serpent from God’s garden—the one that gave the apple to the naked young woman—doesn’t live on in adders and snakes even now? See that eye, black and wicked as a currant. Let’s hope he can’t cast a curse on us! There are folk on the heath who’ve already been blighted by an evil glance. I’ll never kill another adder as long as I draw breath.”

One night he went to the theatre, on the blind chance that he might see her there, and from the second balcony he did see her. He saw her come down the aisle, with Arthur and a strange young man with a football mop of hair and eyeglasses, the sight of whom spurred him to instant apprehension and jealousy. He saw her take her seat in the orchestra circle, and little else than her did he see that night—a pair of slender white shoulders and a mass of pale gold hair, dim with distance. But there were others who saw, and now and again, glancing at those about him, he noted two young girls who looked back from the row in front, a dozen seats along, and who smiled at him with bold eyes. He had always been easy-going. It was not in his nature to give rebuff. In the old days he would have smiled back, and gone further and encouraged smiling. But now it was different. He did smile back, then looked away, and looked no more deliberately. But several times, forgetting the existence of the two girls, his eyes caught their smiles. He could not re-thumb himself in a day, nor could he violate the intrinsic kindliness of his nature; so, at such moments, he smiled at the girls in warm human friendliness. It was nothing new to him. He knew they were reaching out their woman’s hands to him. But it was different now. Far down there in the orchestra circle was the one woman in all the world, so different, so terrifically different, from these two girls of his class, that he could feel for them only pity and sorrow. He had it in his heart to wish that they could possess, in some small measure, her goodness and glory. And not for the world could he hurt them because of their outreaching. He was not flattered by it; he even felt a slight shame at his lowliness that permitted it. He knew, did he belong in Ruth’s class, that there would be no overtures from these girls; and with each glance of theirs he felt the fingers of his own class clutching at him to hold him down.

One evening he headed to the theater, clinging to the faint, unplanned hope he might spot her there—and sure enough, from his seat in the second balcony, he caught sight of her. He watched her make her way down the aisle, alongside Arthur and an unfamiliar young guy with a thick, unruly mop of hair and wire-rimmed glasses, and a sharp jolt of unease and jealousy hit him immediately at the sight of the stranger. He tracked her to her seat in the orchestra section, and he barely noticed anything else for the rest of the night: just the curve of her slim, pale shoulders and the cloud of soft gold hair, blurred a little by the distance between them.

Other people were watching him, though. Every so often when he glanced around at the people nearby, he caught two young women a dozen seats down in the row ahead staring back, grinning at him with unapologetically forward looks. He’d always been laid-back, the kind of person who hated snubbing anyone. A few years earlier, he would have grinned right back, even leaned into it a little to keep the flirtation going. But things were different now. He gave them a small smile back, then turned his gaze away and made a point not to look over again. But more than once, when he’d forgotten the two women were even there, his line of sight drifted back to their waiting grins.

He couldn’t rewrite his entire personality overnight, and he’d never go against the inherent kindness that was part of who he was, so in those moments he smiled back at them with gentle, harmless friendliness. This wasn’t out of the ordinary for him; he knew they were making romantic overtures his way. But none of that landed the way it used to. Far below him in the orchestra seats was the only woman that mattered to him in the whole world, so far removed, so impossibly far above the two women from his own social circle, that all he could feel for them was soft pity. He found himself quietly wishing they could have even a fraction of her warmth and grace.

He would never have dreamed of hurting them just for being interested in him, either. He didn’t take their attention as a compliment; if anything, he felt a faint twinge of shame that his lower social standing made this kind of advance acceptable to them. He knew that if he moved in Ruth’s circles, these two women would never have dared to flirt with him at all, and every time they glanced his way, he could almost feel the weight of his own background yanking at him, trying to keep him tied to the life he’d known before.

By and by we went to see one of the supremely great ones. He was a mighty celebrity; his fame had penetrated all Christendom; the noble and the renowned journeyed from the remotest lands on the globe to pay him reverence. His stand was in the center of the widest part of the valley; and it took all that space to hold his crowds.

After a while, we made our way to behold one of the truly towering figures. He was a colossal luminary, his renown spreading through every corner of the Christian world; aristocrats and illustrious souls traveled from the farthest reaches of the earth to honor him. His position lay at the heart of the valley’s broadest expanse, and even that vast area was barely enough to contain the multitudes who gathered.

“Listen up,” said Madame, “I don’t care at all about this Doctor, not me. Whether he keeps his head or loses it means absolutely nothing to me; it’s all the same. But the entire Evrémonde line is to be wiped out entirely, so the wife and child must follow their husband and father.”
