This gentleman had been located through the Court Guide, and being present in court, swore the snuff-box belonged to him and that he had noticed it gone the day before, just after freeing himself from the crowd mentioned earlier. He had also noticed a young man in the crush who was especially busy worming through the press, and that young man was the prisoner before him.

“The pleasant showers and genial warmth of spring greatly altered the aspect of the earth. Men who before this change seemed to have been hid in caves dispersed themselves and were employed in various arts of cultivation. The birds sang in more cheerful notes, and the leaves began to bud forth on the trees. Happy, happy earth! Fit habitation for gods, which, so short a time before, was bleak, damp, and unwholesome. My spirits were elevated by the enchanting appearance of nature; the past was blotted from my memory, the present was tranquil, and the future gilded by bright rays of hope and anticipations of joy.”

The gentle rains and tender warmth of spring transformed the face of the land. Figures who had earlier seemed buried in shadowy hollows now emerged and scattered across the fields, devoting themselves to the various tasks of cultivation. The birds lifted their voices in lighter, gladder strains, and fresh leaves began to unfurl upon the branches. Blessed, blessed earth! A dwelling worthy of gods, where only a brief span before all had been raw, sodden, and sickly. My heart rose with the enchanting beauty of the world around me; the past was erased from my mind, the present lay serene, and the future gleamed with bright shafts of hope and the promise of joy.

In this paper, we focus on bridging anaphora resolution where bridging anaphors and their antecedents are linked via various lexico-semantic, frame or encyclopedic relations. Following Hou et al. (2013b) and , we mainly consider "referential bridging" in which bridging anaphors are truly anaphoric and bridging relations are context-dependent. In Example 1 1 , both "her building" and "buildings with substantial damage" are plausible antecedent candidates for the bridging anaphor "residents" based on lexical semantics. In order to find the antecedent (buildings with substantial damage), we have to take the meaning of the broader discourse context into account.

metadata.title: Bridging Anaphora Resolution as Question Answering

text: This study investigates the resolution of bridging anaphora, a process in which anaphors and their antecedents are connected via diverse frame-based, lexico-semantic, or encyclopedic associations. Adopting the approach of Hou et al. (2013b), we primarily address "referential bridging," a category where the expressions are genuinely anaphoric and the bridging links are context-dependent. As shown in Example 1, lexical semantics alone may suggest that both "her building" and "buildings with substantial damage" are viable antecedent candidates for the anaphor "residents." To identify the correct antecedent—buildings with substantial damage—it is necessary to account for the semantics of the wider discourse context.

I turned with loathing from the woman who could utter so unfeeling a speech to a person just saved, on the very edge of death; but I felt languid and unable to reflect on all that had passed. The whole series of my life appeared to me as a dream; I sometimes doubted if indeed it were all true, for it never presented itself to my mind with the force of reality.

I turned away in revulsion from the woman who could utter such a cold, unfeeling remark to someone who’d just been pulled back from the very brink of death; but I was so weak and listless I could barely parse the full weight of all that had unfolded. The entire arc of my life felt like a hazy, unmoored dream; sometimes I doubted if any of it had actually been real, for none of it ever settled in my mind with the sharp, tangible force of lived truth.

Two Zeppelins had come in from over the sea, and had been fired at by an anti-aircraft gun coming on an automobile from Ipswich. The first intimation the people of the town had had of the raid was the report of this gun. Many had run out to see what was happening. It was doubtful if any one had really seen the Zeppelins, though every one testified to the sound of their engines. Then suddenly the bombs had come streaming down. Only six had made hits upon houses or people; the rest had fallen ruinously and very close together on the local golf links, and at least half had not exploded at all and did not seem to have been released to explode.

Two Zeppelins had approached from out over the sea and had been engaged by an anti-aircraft gun that came by car from Ipswich. The town’s first warning of the raid had been the crash of that gun. Many people had hurried outside to see what was going on. It was doubtful whether anyone had actually caught sight of the Zeppelins, though everyone swore they had heard the beat of their engines. Then, all at once, the bombs began to rain down. Only six struck houses or people; the rest fell in destructive clusters very near one another on the local golf course, and at least half failed to explode at all and did not appear to have been fitted to do so.

“Our friend was born in this village. This town then included the adjoining towns to the north and south. The region was then more sparsely settled, although many houses standing then have disappeared. While he was sleeping peacefully in the cradle, while he was opening on the world childhood's wide, wondering eyes, those great men whose names are our perpetual benediction were planning for freedom from a foreign yoke. While he was passing through the happy years of early-childhood, the fierce clash of arms resounded through the little strip of territory which then made up the United States. I can hardly realize that, as a child, he heard as a fresh, new, real story, of the deeds of Lexington, from the lips of men then young who had been in the fight, or listened as one of an eager group gathered about the fireside, or in the old, now deserted tavern on the turnpike, to the story of Bunker Hill.

He entered the world in this very village. In those days, the settlement stretched to include the neighboring communities both north and south. The countryside was more thinly populated back then, and many homes that once stood have since vanished. As he lay dreaming in his cradle, as his young eyes first opened wide with childhood’s wonder, the great figures whose names we still bless were already scheming to cast off foreign rule. Throughout the carefree seasons of his early youth, the clangor of battle echoed across the narrow stretch of land that then formed the United States. I can scarcely grasp that, as a boy, he heard the exploits of Lexington told as fresh, immediate news from the lips of men still young who had fought there, or sat among a breathless circle by the hearth—or in the old, now-abandoned roadside inn—listening to the tale of Bunker Hill.

Noah was a charity-boy, but not a workhouse orphan. No chance-child was he, for he could trace his genealogy all the way back to his parents, who lived hard by; his mother being a washerwoman, and his father a drunken soldier, discharged with a wooden leg, and a diurnal pension of twopence-halfpenny and an unstateable fraction. The shop-boys in the neighbourhood had long been in the habit of branding Noah in the public streets, with the ignominious epithets of “leathers,” “charity,” and the like; and Noah had borne them without reply. But, now that fortune had cast in his way a nameless orphan, at whom even the meanest could point the finger of scorn, he retorted on him with interest. This affords charming food for contemplation. It shows us what a beautiful thing human nature may be made to be; and how impartially the same amiable qualities are developed in the finest lord and the dirtiest charity-boy.

Noah attended school on charity, yet he was no foundling of the workhouse. He was no child of chance, for he could trace his lineage directly to his parents, who lived close by; his mother took in washing, and his father was a besotted soldier, discharged with a wooden leg and a daily pension of twopence-halfpenny plus some unutterable fraction. The local errand-boys had long made it their custom to brand Noah in the open streets with shameful names—“leathers,” “charity,” and the like—and Noah had swallowed these taunts without a word. But now that fortune had thrown in his path a nameless orphan, at whom the lowest could point a scornful finger, he repaid the insult upon him with compound interest. This offers a rich morsel for reflection. It shows us how lovely a thing human nature can be shaped to become, and how evenly the same pleasing traits unfold in the noblest lord and the grubbiest charity-boy.

And this double refraction of his mind by which a concentrated and individualised Britling did but present a larger impersonal Britling beneath, carried with it a duplication of his conscience and sense of responsibility. To his personal conscience he was answerable for his private honour and his debts and the Dower House he had made and so on, but to his impersonal conscience he was answerable for the whole world. The world from the latter point of view was his egg. He had a subconscious delusion that he had laid it. He had a subconscious suspicion that he had let it cool and that it was addled. He had an urgency to incubate it. The variety and interest of his talk was largely due to that persuasion, it was a perpetual attempt to spread his mental feathers over the task before him....

And this double refraction of his mind, by which the intensified and particular Britling merely revealed a larger, impersonal Britling underneath, brought with it a duplication of conscience and of responsibility. To his personal conscience he was accountable for his private honour, his debts, the Dower House he had created, and the rest; but to his impersonal conscience he was answerable for the whole world. From that point of view the world was his egg. He had a subconscious illusion that he himself had laid it. He had a subconscious fear that he had let it grow cold and that it was spoiled. He felt an urgent need to sit upon it and warm it. The richness and variety of his conversation were largely due to this conviction; it was a continual effort to spread his mental feathers over the work before him....

“But even if he has been wicked,” pursued Rose, “think how young he is; think that he may never have known a mother’s love, or the comfort of a home; that ill-usage and blows, or the want of bread, may have driven him to herd with men who have forced him to guilt. Aunt, dear aunt, for mercy’s sake, think of this, before you let them drag this sick child to a prison, which in any case must be the grave of all his chances of amendment. Oh! as you love me, and know that I have never felt the want of parents in your goodness and affection, but that I might have done so, and might have been equally helpless and unprotected with this poor child, have pity upon him before it is too late!”

“But even if he has acted wickedly,” Rose went on earnestly, “think of how young he still is. He may never have known a mother’s tender love, or the quiet comfort of a steady home; harsh mistreatment and blows, or the pangs of hunger when there was no bread to eat, may have driven him to fall in with men who forced him into wrongdoing. Aunt, dear aunt, for mercy’s sake, stop to consider this before you let them drag this ailing child off to a prison that will, no matter what, be the grave of every chance he has to mend his ways. Oh! As you love me, and know that I never felt the loss of parents thanks to your goodness and affection, but that I might well have been in his position, just as helpless and unprotected as this poor boy, have pity on him before it is too late!”

The young man took out his handkerchief and wiped his brow. It was a good handkerchief, a good brow, and the young man was good to look at. He said to the boy:

For a second or two, Oliver glanced up the street, and down the street, and over the way: impressed with the belief that the unknown, who had addressed him through the key-hole, had walked a few paces off, to warm himself; for nobody did he see but a big charity-boy, sitting on a post in front of the house, eating a slice of bread and butter: which he cut into wedges, the size of his mouth, with a clasp-knife, and then consumed with great dexterity.

Briefly, Arthur peered up the thoroughfare and down again, surveying the opposite pavement with the conviction that the invisible soul who had spoken through the lock-hole had simply wandered off to escape the frost; for he found the way deserted, save for a hulking parish-lad who sat upon a bollard before the shop, busy with a slab of buttered crust. This the fellow divided into wedges, tailored to the span of his mouth with a folding-blade, and dispatched with singular agility.

His breath went, as it were, out of his body and the cry of anguish which would have escaped him died upon his lips. During the momentary interval that elapsed before he became conscious that something must be done all sense of time and place left him, and it seemed as if he and his mother were as when he was a child with her many years ago on this heath at hours similar to the present. Then he awoke to activity; and bending yet lower he found that she still breathed, and that her breath though feeble was regular, except when disturbed by an occasional gasp.

The wind was knocked out of him, and the agonized scream that rose in his throat never made it past his lips. In the brief pause before he understood he had to do something, he lost all track of time and place. It felt as if he were a young boy again, out on this same moor with his mother just as they used to be at this time of day. Then he jolted back to the task at hand; leaning down even closer, he found that she was still breathing. Though her breath was faint, it remained steady, save for the occasional ragged gasp.

After prayers we had dinner in a great banqueting hall which was lighted by hundreds of grease-jets, and everything was as fine and lavish and rudely splendid as might become the royal degree of the hosts. At the head of the hall, on a dais, was the table of the king, queen, and their son, Prince Uwaine. Stretching down the hall from this, was the general table, on the floor. At this, above the salt, sat the visiting nobles and the grown members of their families, of both sexes,--the resident Court, in effect--sixty-one persons; below the salt sat minor officers of the household, with their principal subordinates: altogether a hundred and eighteen persons sitting, and about as many liveried servants standing behind their chairs, or serving in one capacity or another. It was a very fine show. In a gallery a band with cymbals, horns, harps, and other horrors, opened the proceedings with what seemed to be the crude first-draft or original agony of the wail known to later centuries as “In the Sweet Bye and Bye.” It was new, and ought to have been rehearsed a little more. For some reason or other the queen had the composer hanged, after dinner.

Once prayers concluded, we sat down to dinner in a vast banqueting hall lit by hundreds of tallow wicks, every detail as grand, opulent, and unpolishedly magnificent as befitted the royal station of our hosts. At the hall’s upper end, raised on a dais, stood the table reserved for the king, queen, and their son, Prince Uwaine. Stretching the full length of the hall down from this platform was the common table, set directly on the floor. At its upper end, above the salt cellar, sat visiting nobles and their adult family members of both sexes—effectively the full resident court, sixty-one people total. Below the salt were seated minor household officers and their senior subordinates: all told, one hundred and eighteen seated diners, with roughly the same number of liveried servants standing behind their chairs or tending to various serving duties. It was a thoroughly impressive sight. Up in a gallery, a band armed with cymbals, horns, harps, and other cacophonous nuisances kicked off the proceedings with what sounded like a rough first draft, or the original agonized wail, of the tune later centuries would know as “In the Sweet Bye and Bye.” The piece was brand new, and clearly ought to have been rehearsed a few more times. For some unstated reason, the queen had the composer hanged directly after dinner.

For a few seconds the wayfarer stood with that tense stillness which signifies itself to be not the end, but merely the suspension, of a previous motion. Her attitude was that of a person who listens, either to the external world of sound, or to the imagined discourse of thought. A close criticism might have detected signs proving that she was intent on the latter alternative. Moreover, as was shown by what followed, she was oddly exercising the faculty of invention upon the speciality of the clever Jacquet Droz, the designer of automatic substitutes for human limbs.

For a brief moment the traveler halted in that rigid quietude which declares itself not a conclusion, but only a pause within an earlier movement. Her posture suggested one who attends, whether to the outer realm of noise, or to the inner murmur of reverie. A sharper scrutiny might have discerned indications that she favored the second course. And, as the sequel revealed, she was curiously applying the inventive gift to the specialty of the ingenious Henri Maillardet, the creator of mechanical replicas for living appendages.

“True, drink is a pleasant delight,” said Jan, as one who repeated a truism so familiar to his brain that he hardly noticed its passage over his tongue; and, lifting the cup, Coggan tilted his head gradually backwards, with closed eyes, that his expectant soul might not be diverted for one instant from its bliss by irrelevant surroundings.

“True, drink is a pleasant delight,” Jan said, as though repeating a truth so well worn in his mind that he scarcely noticed the words as they left his tongue; and, lifting the cup, Coggan slowly tipped his head back, his eyes shut, so that his eager soul might not be distracted for even a moment from its bliss by anything around him.

text: But at that moment, with an impatient movement, Mr. Ends turned as if about to leave the room. At the same instant Martin sprang for him, clutching him by the throat with one hand in such fashion that Mr. Ends’ snow-white beard, still maintaining its immaculate trimness, pointed ceilingward at an angle of forty-five degrees. To the horror of Mr. White and Mr. Ford, they saw their business manager shaken like an Astrakhan rug.

Yet in that very breath, with a fretful snap of his frame, Mr. Sterling pivoted as though to vacate the parlor. Simultaneously, Silas lunged, his hand clamping the man’s throat with such force that Sterling’s ivory beard, still meticulously groomed, was jerked upward toward the cornices at a sharp angle. To the paralyzed horror of Mr. Blackwood and Mr. Vance, they witnessed their administrator being thrashed with the same vigor one might use on a heavy woolen rug.

“As they descend the hill like madmen running a race, he falls. They laugh and pick him up again. His face is bleeding and covered with dust, but he cannot touch it; thereupon they laugh again. They bring him into the village; all the village runs to look; they take him past the mill, and up to the prison; all the village sees the prison gate open in the darkness of the night, and swallow him--like this!”

Tumbling down the incline like maniacs in a desperate chase, the wretch collapses. His captors howl with glee as they wrench him upright. His countenance is a mask of grit and scarlet, yet his shackled hands cannot reach it; seeing his struggle, they jeer anew. They haul him into the hamlet, where the populace swarms to watch; they parade him past the grinding stones of the mill to the fortress gates. There, beneath the black cloak of night, the village sees the portal yawn open and devour him—precisely so!

His thoughts grew firmer and clearer; they went deeper and wider. His first superficial judgments were endorsed and deepened or replaced by others. He thought along the lonely lanes at night; he thought at his desk; he thought in bed; he thought in his bath; he tried over his thoughts in essays and leading articles and reviewed them and corrected them. Now and then came relaxation and lassitude, but never release. The war towered over him like a vigilant teacher, day after day, week after week, regardless of fatigue and impatience, holding a rod in its hand.

His thoughts became steadier and more lucid; they sank deeper and spread farther. His early, merely superficial judgments were confirmed and enriched, or else supplanted by others. He thought as he walked the lonely lanes at night; he thought at his desk; he thought in bed; he thought in his bath; he rehearsed his ideas in essays and leading articles, then revised and corrected them. At moments there was ease and weariness, but never any real escape. The war loomed above him like a watchful schoolmaster, day after day, week after week, unmoved by fatigue or impatience, a rod in its hand.

There was something so abnormal and startling in the childlike pain and simplicity of this appeal from a woman of Bathsheba’s calibre and independence, that Troy, loosening her tightly clasped arms from his neck, looked at her in bewilderment. It was such an unexpected revelation of all women being alike at heart, even those so different in their accessories as Fanny and this one beside him, that Troy could hardly seem to believe her to be his proud wife Bathsheba. Fanny’s own spirit seemed to be animating her frame. But this was the mood of a few instants only. When the momentary surprise had passed, his expression changed to a silencing imperious gaze.

There was something so bizarre and jarring about such a vulnerable, childlike plea coming from a woman as capable and independent as Bathsheba. As Troy unclasped her arms from around his neck, he stared at her in utter confusion. It was a startling revelation that all women were fundamentally the same at their core, even those as outwardly different as Fanny and the woman standing before him; he could hardly recognize his proud wife in this desperate figure. It was as if Fanny’s own spirit had taken possession of her body. Yet this lasted only a few seconds. Once the initial surprise faded, his expression shifted back into a cold, domineering look that commanded her silence.

The whole Britling family, who were lamenting the loss of their Belgians, welcomed the coming of the twenty-five with great enthusiasm. It made them feel that they were doing something useful once more. For three days Mrs. Britling had to feed her new lodgers--the kitchen motors had as usual gone astray--and she did so in a style that made their boastings about their billet almost insufferable to the rest of their battery. The billeting allowance at that time was ninepence a head, and Mr. Britling, ashamed of making a profit out of his country, supplied not only generous firing and lighting, but unlimited cigarettes, cards and games, illustrated newspapers, a cocoa supper with such little surprises as sprats and jam roly-poly, and a number of more incidental comforts. The men arrived fasting under the command of two very sage middle-aged corporals, and responded to Mrs. Britling's hospitalities by a number of good resolutions, many of which they kept. They never made noises after half-past ten, or at least only now and then when a singsong broke out with unusual violence; they got up and went out at five or six in the morning without a sound; they were almost inconveniently helpful with washing-up and tidying round.

The Britling household, still mourning their departed Belgian guests, greeted the twenty-five new arrivals with open arms. Their presence restored a sense of purpose. For three days Mrs. Britling—whose kitchen machinery had, predictably, malfunctioned—fed the soldiers so lavishly that their tales of the billet became nearly unbearable to the rest of their battery. The official allowance was ninepence per man, but Mr. Britling, unwilling to profit from his nation’s need, provided not only generous heat and light but endless cigarettes, cards, games, illustrated papers, a cocoa supper featuring modest treats like sprats and jam roly-poly, and countless smaller comforts. The men, who had marched in hungry under two quietly seasoned corporals, repaid Mrs. Britling’s kindness with a string of earnest promises, many of which they honored. They kept silent after half-past ten—barring the occasional sing-song that erupted with sudden fervor—rose and slipped out at five or six without a whisper, and proved almost excessively eager to help with washing-up and tidying.

“How very ridiculous!” Thomasin murmured to herself, in a tone which was intended to be satirical. “To think that a man should be so silly as to go mooning about like that for a girl’s glove! A respectable dairyman, too, and a man of money as he is now. What a pity!”

“What a preposterous display!” Margaret whispered, adopting a note of calculated derision. “To imagine that a fellow could be so witless as to go pining about like a schoolboy for a maiden’s glove! A man of substance, a wealthy orchardist with his pockets full, no less. What a shame!”

It was five in the afternoon when she came out from the house ready for her walk. There was material enough in the picture for twenty new conquests. The rebellious sadness that was rather too apparent when she sat indoors without a bonnet was cloaked and softened by her outdoor attire, which always had a sort of nebulousness about it, devoid of harsh edges anywhere; so that her face looked from its environment as from a cloud, with no noticeable lines of demarcation between flesh and clothes. The heat of the day had scarcely declined as yet, and she went along the sunny hills at a leisurely pace, there being ample time for her idle expedition. Tall ferns buried her in their leafage whenever her path lay through them, which now formed miniature forests, though not one stem of them would remain to bud the next year.

By five in the evening, she emerged from the house, fully prepared for her excursion. There was beauty enough in her presentation to win twenty new hearts. The defiant sorrow that marked her face while indoors was now tempered and obscured by her outdoor dress, which always possessed a soft, misty quality, free of any sharp outlines. Her face appeared from within it as if framed by a cloud, with no harsh transition between her complexion and the cloth. The day’s heat had hardly begun to lift, and she wandered across the bright hills at a slow pace, for there was no need to hurry her aimless wandering. Towering ferns swallowed her in their foliage whenever the path led that way, creating a landscape of miniature woods, even though not a single stalk would remain to sprout again in the coming year.

How can I call the lone night good,
Though thy sweet wishes wing its flight?
Be it not said, thought, understood—
Then it will be—GOOD night.

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
How can I name the solitary night benign,
Though your sweet wishes give its darkened hours their wings?
Let it not be spoken, thought, or known—
Then it shall be a good night indeed.

Sydney had been working double tides that night, and the night before, and the night before that, and a good many nights in succession, making a grand clearance among Mr. Stryver’s papers before the setting in of the long vacation. The clearance was effected at last; the Stryver arrears were handsomely fetched up; everything was got rid of until November should come with its fogs atmospheric, and fogs legal, and bring grist to the mill again.

Sydney had been pulling double shifts that night, the night before, the night before that, and a long stretch of consecutive nights total, powering through a complete sweep of Mr. Stryver’s paperwork before the long vacation kicked off. The sweep was finished at last; all of Stryver’s outstanding backlogs were neatly tied up, every last loose end sorted out, until November rolled around with its thick, hanging atmospheric fog and its equally tangled piles of legal casework, to bring fresh work to keep the practice busy once more.

Wildeve stood looking on the ground beside Venn’s feet, till he allowed his eyes to travel upwards over Diggory’s form, as illuminated by the candle, to his head and face. “Ha-ha! Well, I suppose I deserve it, considering how I have played with them both,” he said at last, as much to himself as to Venn. “But of all the odd things that ever I knew, the oddest is that you should so run counter to your own interests as to bring this to me.”

Wildeve kept his eyes fixed on the earth near Venn’s boots until he let his gaze climb slowly up Diggory’s figure, bathed in the candle’s glow, to rest upon his features. “Ha! Well, I suppose this is my due, given how I’ve toyed with both women,” he remarked eventually, half to himself and half to Venn. “But of all the peculiar turns I’ve witnessed, the most bizarre is that you would work so directly against your own advantage to bring this to me.”

Our final opportunity has vanished, and it was a slim one at best. Yes, it is better that he be brought to her now. But before you depart, I need you to give me your full attention for a moment. Do not question the conditions I am about to set or the pledge I am going to demand; I have my reasons, and they are sound ones.

"O Isabella, I can half perceive
That I may speak my grief into thine ear;
If thou didst ever any thing believe,
Believe how I love thee, believe how near
My soul is to its doom: I would not grieve
Thy hand by unwelcome pressing, would not fear
Thine eyes by gazing; but I cannot live
Another night, and not my passion shrive.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
O Isabella, I partly sense
That I may pour my sorrow in your ear;
If you have ever trusted evidence,
Trust now my love for you, trust how severe
My spirit nears its end: I’d not distress
Your hand with unwanted touch, nor would I fear
Your gaze by looking; yet I can’t survive
One more night without confessing my love alive.

text: GSL: Our model equipped with the GSL module achieves an F1 score of 48.91%, outperforming the baseline model. While GSL does not surpass the performance of ELMo, it delivers results that are fully comparable to those of ELMo. This observation indicates that the GSL and SFL modules are mutually reinforcing, as they utilize geometric structure information via algebraic operations. Furthermore, we leverage geometric structure cues to enhance discourse semantic understanding from a novel angle, a key difference from ELMo, which exclusively focuses on the inherent semantic information of the input text itself.

Indeed they were. Mortification at the overthrow of his notable scheme; hatred of the girl who had dared to palter with strangers; and utter distrust of the sincerity of her refusal to yield him up; bitter disappointment at the loss of his revenge on Sikes; the fear of detection, and ruin, and death; and a fierce and deadly rage kindled by all; these were the passionate considerations which, following close upon each other with rapid and ceaseless whirl, shot through the brain of Fagin, as every evil thought and blackest purpose lay working at his heart.

Indeed, they were. Shame at the collapse of his cunning design; loathing for the girl who had ventured to trifle with strangers; and a total disbelief in the truth of her refusal to surrender him; sharp disappointment at being robbed of his vengeance on Slyke; the terror of discovery, of ruin, and of death; and a fierce, consuming fury kindled by them all—these were the turbulent impulses that, one after another in swift and unbroken succession, rushed through the mind of Rendel, while every evil notion and darkest intent worked deep within his breast.

At last he reached the summit, and a wide and novel prospect burst upon him with an effect almost like that of the Pacific upon Balboa’s gaze. The broad steely sea, marked only by faint lines, which had a semblance of being etched thereon to a degree not deep enough to disturb its general evenness, stretched the whole width of his front and round to the right, where, near the town and port of Budmouth, the sun bristled down upon it, and banished all colour, to substitute in its place a clear oily polish. Nothing moved in sky, land, or sea, except a frill of milkwhite foam along the nearer angles of the shore, shreds of which licked the contiguous stones like tongues.

At last he crested the summit, and a vast, unfamiliar panorama broke upon him with an impact almost like that of the Pacific on Balboa’s eyes. The wide, metallic sea, traced only by faint streaks that seemed etched upon its surface too lightly to disturb its uniform smoothness, spanned his entire forward view and curved away to the right, where, near Budmouth town and harbor, the sun blazed down upon it, stripping away all color and replacing it with a lucent, oily sheen. Nothing stirred in sky, land, or sea, save a fringe of milk-white foam along the nearer angles of the coast, tatters of which lapped the adjacent stones like tongues.

Dig--dig--dig--until an impatient movement from one of the two passengers would admonish him to pull up the window, draw his arm securely through the leathern strap, and speculate upon the two slumbering forms, until his mind lost its hold of them, and they again slid away into the bank and the grave.

Digging, digging, digging—until a restless stir from one of his companions prompted him to slide the window shut, hook his arm firmly through the leather strap, and study the two sleeping figures. Slowly, his concentration would slip, and they would once again dissolve into the twin images of the bank and the grave.

“What!” repeated the girl. “Look before you, lady. Look at that dark water. How many times do you read of such as I who spring into the tide, and leave no living thing, to care for, or bewail them. It may be years hence, or it may be only months, but I shall come to that at last.”

“Hah!” the young woman snapped again. “Cast your eyes straight ahead, madam. Gaze on that inky, glass-dark water. How often have you read of girls just like me, who hurl themselves into the tide, and leave not a single living soul to care for them, or mourn their passing? It may be years down the line, or it may be only a few short months, but that is the end I am bound for, one way or another.”

His daily life was of a curious microscopic sort, his whole world being limited to a circuit of a few feet from his person. His familiars were creeping and winged things, and they seemed to enroll him in their band. Bees hummed around his ears with an intimate air, and tugged at the heath and furze-flowers at his side in such numbers as to weigh them down to the sod. The strange amber-coloured butterflies which Egdon produced, and which were never seen elsewhere, quivered in the breath of his lips, alighted upon his bowed back, and sported with the glittering point of his hook as he flourished it up and down. Tribes of emerald-green grasshoppers leaped over his feet, falling awkwardly on their backs, heads, or hips, like unskilful acrobats, as chance might rule; or engaged themselves in noisy flirtations under the fern-fronds with silent ones of homely hue. Huge flies, ignorant of larders and wire-netting, and quite in a savage state, buzzed about him without knowing that he was a man. In and out of the fern-dells snakes glided in their most brilliant blue and yellow guise, it being the season immediately following the shedding of their old skins, when their colours are brightest. Litters of young rabbits came out from their forms to sun themselves upon hillocks, the hot beams blazing through the delicate tissue of each thin-fleshed ear, and firing it to a blood-red transparency in which the veins could be seen. None of them feared him.

His existence had narrowed to a strangely minute scale, his entire world restricted to a small circle immediately surrounding him. The creeping and winged inhabitants of the heath were his constant associates, and they seemed to have inducted him into their society. Bees hummed with an air of intimacy near his head, crowding the heather and furze-blossoms at his side until the weight of their bodies pressed the flowers to the soil. The rare, amber-tinted butterflies found only on Egdon quivered in the very air he exhaled, alighting on his bent back and playing about the shining point of his hook as he swung it. Legions of emerald grasshoppers vaulted over his feet, landing haphazardly on their heads or backs like clumsy acrobats, or pursued noisy romances beneath the ferns with silent, drab-colored mates. Great flies, wild and unacquainted with kitchens or nets, buzzed around him without recognizing him as a human being. In the hollows of the bracken, snakes glided in vivid displays of blue and yellow, their scales gleaming with the fresh intensity that follows the shedding of a skin. Young rabbits ventured from their nests to warm themselves on the slopes, the fierce sun shining through their delicate ears until they glowed a translucent blood-red, exposing every fine vein. Not one of them felt any fear of him.

He nodded his head, regarding her thoughtfully as she gathered the torn pieces of manuscript and tucked them into the pocket of her jacket—ocular evidence of the success of her mission. She reminded him of Lizzie Connolly, though there was less of fire and gorgeous flaunting life in her than in that other girl of the working class whom he had seen twice. But they were on a par, the pair of them, in dress and carriage, and he smiled with inward amusement at the caprice of his fancy which suggested the appearance of either of them in Mrs. Morse’s drawing-room. The amusement faded, and he was aware of a great loneliness. This sister of his and the Morse drawing-room were milestones of the road he had travelled. And he had left them behind. He glanced affectionately about him at his few books. They were all the comrades left to him.

He gave a slight nod and watched her with a reflective air as she gathered up the torn scraps of the manuscript and slipped them into the pocket of her jacket—visible proof, as it were, that her errand had succeeded. She put him in mind of Nora Daly, though there was less flame and splendid, unashamed vitality in her than in that other working girl whom he had seen but twice. Yet in dress and bearing the two were much of a kind, and he smiled inwardly at the whim that could imagine either of them in Mrs. Harrow’s drawing-room. The smile soon passed, and a profound loneliness settled over him. That sister of his and the Harrow drawing-room were milestones on the road he had already travelled. He had left them behind. He looked round him with a kind of affection at his handful of books. They alone remained his companions.

“I hate talking of Paris! Well, I remember one sunny room in the Louvre which would make a fitting place for you to live in—the Galerie d’Apollon. Its windows are mainly east; and in the early morning, when the sun is bright, the whole apartment is in a perfect blaze of splendour. The rays bristle and dart from the encrustations of gilding to the magnificent inlaid coffers, from the coffers to the gold and silver plate, from the plate to the jewels and precious stones, from these to the enamels, till there is a perfect network of light which quite dazzles the eye. But now, about our marriage——”

I threw myself into the chaise that was to convey me away and indulged in the most melancholy reflections. I, who had ever been surrounded by amiable companions, continually engaged in endeavouring to bestow mutual pleasure—I was now alone. In the university whither I was going I must form my own friends and be my own protector. My life had hitherto been remarkably secluded and domestic, and this had given me invincible repugnance to new countenances. I loved my brothers, Elizabeth, and Clerval; these were “old familiar faces,” but I believed myself totally unfitted for the company of strangers. Such were my reflections as I commenced my journey; but as I proceeded, my spirits and hopes rose. I ardently desired the acquisition of knowledge. I had often, when at home, thought it hard to remain during my youth cooped up in one place and had longed to enter the world and take my station among other human beings. Now my desires were complied with, and it would, indeed, have been folly to repent.

I sank into the chaise that was to carry me away and gave myself up to the most sorrowful thoughts. I, who had always been surrounded by kind companions, forever occupied in trying to give and receive pleasure—I was now alone. At the university to which I was bound, I would have to make my own friends and serve as my own guardian. My life had so far been unusually quiet and domestic, and this had left me with an unconquerable dislike of unfamiliar faces. I loved my brothers, Elizabeth, and Clerval; these were “old familiar faces,” but I believed myself utterly ill suited to the company of strangers. Such were my thoughts as I began my journey; yet as I went on, my spirits and hopes gradually lifted. I was passionately eager to gain knowledge. At home, I had often felt it was cruel to spend my youth shut up in one place, and I had longed to go out into the world and take my place among other human beings. Now my wishes were being fulfilled, and it would truly have been foolish to regret it.

Lila’s arrival pulled the conversation in an entirely new direction; the ragged street boys, catching a sly side wink from shrewd old Silas the fence, started pressing her to help herself to the gin set out on the splintered table. She only sipped a tiny mouthful, though, turning down nearly all their offers, while Silas put on an uncharacteristically giddy air, slowly drawing the gruff Mr. Hale out of his foul temper by framing his snappish threats as playful, lighthearted ribbing. He even roared with genuine-sounding laughter at a couple of coarse jokes Hale deigned to crack once he’d tipped back the spirit bottle enough times to loosen his usual tight jaw.

“Let me talk to you instead for the little time I shall be here,” Thomasin said soothingly. “Consider what a one-sided way you have of looking at the matter, Clym. When she said that to the little boy you had not found her and taken her into your arms; and it might have been uttered in a moment of bitterness. It was rather like Aunt to say things in haste. She sometimes used to speak so to me. Though she did not come I am convinced that she thought of coming to see you. Do you suppose a man’s mother could live two or three months without one forgiving thought? She forgave me; and why should she not have forgiven you?”

“Let me just talk to you for the short time I’m here, all right?” Thomasin said in a gentle, soothing tone. “Think about how lopsided your take on this whole thing is, Clym. When she said that to the little boy, you hadn’t found her yet and pulled her into your arms, right? She could’ve just spit the words out in a split second of deep bitterness. That’s exactly the kind of hasty, unthinking remark Aunt used to make, anyway. She’d snap at me the exact same way sometimes. Even if she never ended up coming over, I’m fully convinced she thought about coming to see you. Do you honestly believe a man’s own mother could go two or three months without a single forgiving thought crossing her mind? She forgave me, after all—why wouldn’t she have forgiven you too?”

An overview of our NLP pipeline is given in Figure 2 . We first retrieve Medline-indexed articles using the Semantic Scholar API, 4 and pre-process the text to generate candidate evidence sentences (Section 3.1). We then use our DDI-detection model, a neural network classifier based on BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) and fine-tuned on labeled DDI data from Ayvaz et al. (2015) (Section 3.2), to classify sentences for the existence of an interaction. Sentences classified as positive by our model are collated and surfaced on SUPP.AI (Section 2).

metadata.title: SUPP.AI: Finding Evidence for Supplement-Drug Interactions

text: Figure 2 provides an overview of our NLP pipeline. We first retrieve Medline-indexed articles through the Semantic Scholar API, 4 and preprocess the text to produce candidate evidence sentences (Section 3.1). We then apply our DDI-detection model, a neural network classifier built on BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) and fine-tuned on labeled DDI data from Ayvaz et al. (2015) (Section 3.2), to determine whether a sentence contains an interaction. Sentences predicted as positive by our model are gathered and displayed on SUPP.AI (Section 2).

An ocean of dark and menacing waters, where waves collided in ruinous upheaval, its true depths still unplumbed and its raw power yet to be gauged. It was a pitiless tide of surging, violent figures—cries for retribution and expressions forged in the fires of agony until they were tempered against any touch of mercy.

3 Graph and Tree Transformations Goodman (2019a) described four transformations of AMR graphs and trees-namely, role canonicalization, edge reification (including dereification), attribute reification, and tree structure indication 8 -and how they could be used to improve the comparability of parser-produced AMR corpora by normalizing differences that are meaningequivalent in AMR and by allowing for partial credit when, for example, a relation has a correct role but an incorrect target value. Penman incorporates all of those transformations but it (a) depends on the semantic model to define canonical roles and reifications, whereas Goodman 2019a used hardcoded transformations; and (b) inserts layout markers for a "no-surprises" configuration that results in the expected tree. A separately-defined model allows Penman to use the same transformation methods with different versions of AMR, for different tasks, or even with non-AMR representations, by creating different models. For the implementation details of these transformations, refer to Goodman 2019a.

metadata.title: Penman: An Open-Source Library and Tool for AMR Graphs

text: 3 Graph and Tree Transformations Goodman (2019a) identified four transformations for AMR graphs and trees—namely, role canonicalization, edge reification (including dereification), attribute reification, and tree structure indication 8—and explained how they can be used to improve the comparability of AMR corpora produced by parsers by normalizing differences that are semantically equivalent in AMR and by enabling partial credit when, for instance, a relation has the correct role but an incorrect target value. Penman includes all of these transformations, but it (a) relies on the semantic model to determine canonical roles and reifications, whereas Goodman 2019a used hardcoded transformations; and (b) inserts layout markers for a "no-surprises" configuration that produces the expected tree. A separately defined model enables Penman to apply the same transformation methods to different versions of AMR, for different tasks, or even to non-AMR representations, by constructing different models. For details on how these transformations are implemented, see Goodman 2019a.

The company was assembling in the panelled hall, and made a brilliant show in the light of the acetylene candles against the dark background. Mr. Britling in a black velvet cloak and black silk tights was a deeper shade among the shadows; the high lights were Miss Corner and her sister, in glittering garments of peacock green and silver that gave a snake-like quality to their lithe bodies. They were talking to the German tutor, who had become a sort of cotton Cossack, a spectacled Cossack in buff and bright green. Mrs. Britling was dignified and beautiful in a purple djibbah, and her stepson had become a handsome still figure of black and crimson. Teddy had contrived something elaborate and effective in the Egyptian style, with a fish-basket and a cuirass of that thin matting one finds behind washstands; the small boys were brigands, with immensely baggy breeches and cummerbunds in which they had stuck a selection of paper-knives and toy pistols and similar weapons. Mr. Carmine and his young man had come provided with real Indian costumes; the feeling of the company was that Mr. Carmine was a mullah. The aunt-like lady with the noble nose stood out amidst these levities in a black silk costume with a gold chain. She refused, it seemed, to make herself absurd, though she encouraged the others to extravagance by nods and enigmatical smiles. Nevertheless she had put pink ribbons in her cap. A family of father, golden-haired mother, and two young daughters, sympathetically attired, had just arrived, and were discarding their outer wrappings with the assistance of host and hostess.

The guests were gathering in the paneled hall, where, against the dark background, the acetylene candles threw them into a dazzling display. Mr. Britling, in a black velvet cloak and black silk tights, made a darker shape among the shadows; the brightest figures were Miss Corner and her sister, glittering in peacock green and silver, their sleek bodies given a serpent-like grace by their shimmering clothes. They were talking with the German tutor, who had turned himself into a kind of cotton Cossack—a spectacled one, in buff and bright green. Mrs. Britling looked dignified and lovely in a purple djibbah, while her stepson had become a striking, motionless figure in black and crimson. Teddy had devised something elaborate and effective in the Egyptian manner, with a fish-basket and a cuirass made from that thin matting one finds behind washstands; the little boys were brigands, with hugely baggy breeches and cummerbunds into which they had tucked an assortment of paper-knives, toy pistols, and similar weapons. Mr. Carmine and his young companion had arrived in genuine Indian dress; everyone felt that Mr. Carmine looked like a mullah. The aunt-like lady with the noble nose stood apart from all this light-heartedness in a black silk gown and a gold chain. She seemed unwilling to make a fool of herself, though she encouraged the others in their extravagance with nods and cryptic smiles. Even so, she had put pink ribbons in her cap. A family consisting of a father, a fair-haired mother, and two young daughters, suitably dressed for the occasion, had just come in and were shedding their outer wraps with the help of host and hostess.

These only chanced to be heard, indeed, on account of their being very frequently repeated with great emphasis.

These fragments were only audible because they were repeated so often and with such heavy emphasis.

Liddy, elevating her feelings to the occasion from a sense of grandeur, floated off behind Bathsheba with a milder dignity not entirely free from travesty, and the door was closed.

At that, the petite magistrate rose from the table and swept out of the hall, her black silk gown catching stray bits of straw and pulling them across the floor with a rasping whisper. Lifting her mood to match the moment out of a feeling of importance, Liddy drifted after Bathsheba with a softer stateliness that bordered on parody, and the door shut behind them.

text:
The wind began to sway the grass  
With dark and rumbling sound —  
It hurled a threat against the ground,  
A threat against the clouds.

An interesting future direction is to better extract semantics from app GUIs so that the user can focus on high-level task specifications and personal preferences without dealing with low-level mundane details (e.g., "buy 2 burgers" means setting the value of the textbox below the text "quantity" and next to the text "Burger" to be "2"). Some works have made early progress in this domain (Liu et al., 2018b; Deka et al., 2016; thanks to the availability of large datasets of GUIs like RICO (Deka et al., 2017) . Recent reinforcement learning-based approaches and semantic parsing techniques have also shown promising results in learning models for navigating through GUIs for user-specified task objectives (Liu et al., 2018a; Pasupat et al., 2018) . For ITL, an interesting future challenge is to combine these user-independent domain-agnostic machine-learned models with the user's personalized instructions for a specific task. This will likely require a new kind of mixedinitiative instruction (Horvitz, 1999) where the agent is more proactive in guiding the user and takes more initiative in the dialog. This could be supported by improved background knowledge and task models, and more flexible dialog frameworks that can handle the continuous refinement and uncertainty inherent in natural language interaction, and the variations in user goals. Collecting and aggregating personal task instructions across many users also introduce important concerns on user privacy, as discussed in .

metadata.title: Interactive Task Learning from GUI-Grounded Natural Language Instructions and Demonstrations

text: A promising avenue for future work involves more effectively extracting semantic information from application GUIs, enabling users to concentrate on high-level task descriptions and personal preferences rather than on low-level, repetitive details (for instance, the instruction “buy 2 burgers” entails setting the textbox labeled “quantity” and located beside the label “Burger” to the value “2”). Early progress in this area has been made by several studies (Liu et al., 2018b; Deka et al., 2016), facilitated by the availability of large GUI datasets such as RICO (Deka et al., 2017). More recently, reinforcement learning methods and semantic parsing techniques have yielded encouraging outcomes in learning models that navigate GUIs to accomplish user-specified task goals (Liu et al., 2018a; Pasupat et al., 2018). Within the context of interactive task learning, an intriguing future challenge is to integrate these user-independent, domain-agnostic, machine-learned models with personalized instructions tailored to a specific task. This integration will likely demand a novel form of mixed-initiative instruction (Horvitz, 1999), in which the agent adopts a more proactive role in guiding the user and takes greater initiative during the dialogue. Such an approach could be underpinned by richer background knowledge and task models, along with more adaptable dialogue frameworks capable of managing the ongoing refinement and uncertainty characteristic of natural language interaction, as well as the diversity of user objectives. Moreover, collecting and aggregating personal task instructions across numerous users raises significant user privacy concerns, as examined in prior work.

When using cross-entropy based sorting of the data, cutting off 40% of the lowest scoring training pairs increased BLEU by 0.67 points when compared to using the full training set. If more than 40% of the data is removed, the BLEU score starts to decrease. Surprisingly, ROC AUC based sorting, which requires a manually annotated development set, produces worse results than cross-entropy. ROC AUC reaches a maximum gain of 0.26 BLEU points over using the whole data set when 20% of the data is truncated from the noisy end. English to Finnish translations show similar results, as illustrated in figure 3, although the BLEU scores are overall lower as it is common in systems translating into morphologically rich languages. Again, cross-entropy based models perform better than ROC AUC based ones: at 80% cutoff cross-entropy model has 0.18 point and ROC AUC model has 0.12 point improvement over using the whole data. The increases in scores are more modest in English to Finnish translations than in Finnish to English translations.

metadata.title: OpusFilter: A Configurable Parallel Corpus Filtering Toolbox

text: Removing low-quality training pairs according to cross-entropy-based sorting degrades the BLEU score at every cutoff threshold. Eliminating the bottom 40% of training pairs, as ranked by cross-entropy, yields a BLEU improvement of 0.67 points over employing the full dataset. Beyond 40% removal, the BLEU score declines. Notably, ROC‑AUC-based sorting—which relies on a manually annotated development set—performs worse than cross-entropy-based sorting, achieving a maximum gain of only 0.26 BLEU points over the complete dataset when the noisiest 20% of the data is discarded. English-to-Finnish translations exhibit comparable trends, as shown in Figure 3, although the absolute BLEU scores are lower, as is typical for systems translating into morphologically rich languages. Here, too, cross-entropy-based models outperform ROC‑AUC-based ones: at an 80% retention cutoff, the cross-entropy model yields a 0.18-point improvement and the ROC‑AUC model a 0.12-point improvement over using all data. The score increases are more modest for English-to-Finnish than for Finnish-to-English translations.

My oldest boy is just shy of seventeen," he said. "He's eager to enlist, and I'd be troubled if he weren't. Naturally, he'll join some cadet unit—he's already had a taste of that at school. Or they'll bring him into the Territorials. But before he turns nineteen, the whole business will be finished, one way or the other. I fear, poor lad, he'll feel cheated....

Martin shook his head, but gave no explanations. How could he explain? He was appalled at the awful intellectual chasm that yawned between him and his people. He could never cross it and explain to them his position,—the Nietzschean position, in regard to socialism. There were not words enough in the English language, nor in any language, to make his attitude and conduct intelligible to them. Their highest concept of right conduct, in his case, was to get a job. That was their first word and their last. It constituted their whole lexicon of ideas. Get a job! Go to work! Poor, stupid slaves, he thought, while his sister talked. Small wonder the world belonged to the strong. The slaves were obsessed by their own slavery. A job was to them a golden fetich before which they fell down and worshipped.

Martin shook his head, but offered no explanation. How could he? He was horrified by the vast intellectual gulf that yawned between himself and his people. He could never bridge it and make them understand his position—the Nietzschean position—toward socialism. There were not enough words in English, or in any language, to render his attitude and conduct intelligible to them. Their highest notion of right conduct, as it applied to him, was that he should get a job. That was their first word and their last. It made up the whole vocabulary of their thought. Get a job! Go to work! Poor, foolish slaves, he thought, as his sister went on talking. No wonder the world belonged to the strong. The slaves were forever preoccupied with their own slavery. To them a job was a golden idol before which they knelt and worshipped.

Thomasin stepped up into a fork of one of the bushes, as she had done under happier circumstances on many similar occasions, and with a small chopper that they had brought she began to lop off the heavily berried boughs.

They came at last to the hollow where the ilex trees clustered, sunken in a funnel-shaped depression so that their crowns rose only a little above the surrounding earth. Into a crotch of one of the shrubs Tamsin climbed, as she had done in brighter days on countless such errands, and with the little billhook they had fetched she set to work severing the branches freighted with crimson fruit.

Day dawned; and I directed my steps towards the town. The gates were open, and I hastened to my father’s house. My first thought was to discover what I knew of the murderer, and cause instant pursuit to be made. But I paused when I reflected on the story that I had to tell. A being whom I myself had formed, and endued with life, had met me at midnight among the precipices of an inaccessible mountain. I remembered also the nervous fever with which I had been seized just at the time that I dated my creation, and which would give an air of delirium to a tale otherwise so utterly improbable. I well knew that if any other had communicated such a relation to me, I should have looked upon it as the ravings of insanity. Besides, the strange nature of the animal would elude all pursuit, even if I were so far credited as to persuade my relatives to commence it. And then of what use would be pursuit? Who could arrest a creature capable of scaling the overhanging sides of Mont Salêve? These reflections determined me, and I resolved to remain silent.

Morning arrived, and I set out for the town. Finding the gates unbarred, I hurried toward my father’s house. My initial instinct was to disclose everything I knew about the killer and call for an immediate manhunt. However, I faltered when I considered the narrative I would have to share. A creature I had constructed and animated myself had encountered me at midnight among the jagged peaks of an unreachable mountain. I also thought of the nervous breakdown I had endured at the time of its birth, which would make an already far-fetched story sound like a lingering hallucination. I knew perfectly well that if anyone else had told me such a tale, I would have dismissed it as pure lunacy. Moreover, the creature’s bizarre capabilities would make any pursuit futile, even if I could convince my family to begin one. What point would there be in a hunt? No one could possibly apprehend a monster capable of scaling the precipitous cliffs of Mont Salêve. These considerations decided my course, and I chose to hold my tongue.

The boy nestled himself upon my shoulder and pretended to go to sleep. The old man began his tale; and presently the lad was asleep in reality; so also were the dogs, and the court, the lackeys, and the files of men-at-arms. The droning voice droned on; a soft snoring arose on all sides and supported it like a deep and subdued accompaniment of wind instruments. Some heads were bowed upon folded arms, some lay back with open mouths that issued unconscious music; the flies buzzed and bit, unmolested, the rats swarmed softly out from a hundred holes, and pattered about, and made themselves at home everywhere; and one of them sat up like a squirrel on the king's head and held a bit of cheese in its hands and nibbled it, and dribbled the crumbs in the king's face with naive and impudent irreverence. It was a tranquil scene, and restful to the weary eye and the jaded spirit.

The scrawny urchin curled into the crook of my shoulder, feigning drowsiness as the gray-haired storyteller cleared his throat to begin his far-fetched saga. Within minutes the boy had drifted off for real, as had the pair of shaggy hunting hounds at our feet, the clustered courtiers, the harried footmen, and the rigid ranks of royal guards lining the hall. The storyteller’s low, rumbling drawl droned unbroken, and soft snores rose from every corner to underscore it, deep and muted as the thrum of distant organ pipes. Some attendees had bowed their heads onto their crossed forearms, others lolled back with slack jaws spilling unselfconscious, rumbling breaths; flies buzzed and nipped at exposed skin entirely unhindered, plump grain rats slipped out of dozens of wall cracks to patter across the stone floors, making themselves comfortable on benches, sills, and even the raised dais. One bold little rat perched upright like a chipmunk atop the dozing sovereign’s crown, clutching a crumb of aged cheddar in its tiny paws to nibble, letting crumbs tumble down onto the ruler’s cheek with a guileless, cheeky disregard for all royal decorum. It was a perfectly peaceful scene, soft and soothing to fatigued eyes and frayed, overtaxed souls.

“I communicate to him that secret. I smite this bosom with these two hands as I smite it now, and I tell him, ‘Defarge, I was brought up among the fishermen of the sea-shore, and that peasant family so injured by the two Evrémonde brothers, as that Bastille paper describes, is my family. Defarge, that sister of the mortally wounded boy upon the ground was my sister, that husband was my sister’s husband, that unborn child was their child, that brother was my brother, that father was my father, those dead are my dead, and that summons to answer for those things descends to me!’ Ask him, is that so.”

“I laid that hidden truth before him. I beat upon this breast with both my hands, even as I beat it now, and said to him, ‘Monsieur Dufort, I was reared among the fishermen of the seashore, and that humble household so cruelly wronged by the two de Villiers brothers, as the Bastille record sets forth, is my own house. Monsieur Dufort, that sister of the youth lying mortally stricken upon the ground was my sister; that husband was my sister’s husband; that child unborn was their child; that brother was my brother; that father was my father; those who died are my dead, and that call to answer for such deeds has come down upon me!’ Ask him whether it was so.”

While Mr. Britling was trying to find his duty in the routine of a special constable, Mrs. Britling set to work with great energy to attend various classes and qualify herself for Red Cross work. And early in October came the great drive of the Germans towards Antwerp and the sea, the great drive that was apparently designed to reach Calais, and which swept before it multitudes of Flemish refugees. There was an exodus of all classes from Antwerp into Holland and England, and then a huge process of depopulation in Flanders and the Pas de Calais. This flood came to the eastern and southern parts of England and particularly to London, and there hastily improvised organisations distributed it to a number of local committees, each of which took a share of the refugees, hired and furnished unoccupied houses for the use of the penniless, and assisted those who had means into comfortable quarters. The Matching's Easy committee found itself with accommodation for sixty people, and with a miscellaneous bag of thirty individuals entrusted to its care, who had been part of the load of a little pirate steam-boat from Ostend. There were two Flemish peasant families, and the rest were more or less middle-class refugees from Antwerp. They were brought from the station to the Tithe barn at Claverings, and there distributed, under the personal supervision of Lady Homartyn and her agent, among those who were prepared for their entertainment. There was something like competition among the would-be hosts; everybody was glad of the chance of "doing something," and anxious to show these Belgians what England thought of their plucky little country. Mr. Britling was proud to lead off a Mr. Van der Pant, a neat little bearded man in a black tail-coat, a black bowler hat, and a knitted muffler, with a large rucksack and a conspicuously foreign-looking bicycle, to the hospitalities of Dower House. Mr. Van der Pant had escaped from Antwerp at the eleventh hour, he had caught a severe cold and, it would seem, lost his wife and family in the process; he had much to tell Mr. Britling, and in his zeal to tell it he did not at once discover that though Mr. Britling knew French quite well he did not know it very rapidly.

While Mr. Britling groped for his duty in the tedium of a special constable’s round, Mrs. Britling flung herself into a flurry of classes, determined to earn her place in the Red Cross. Then, early in October, came the great German thrust toward Antwerp and the sea—a drive plainly aimed at Calais—and before it fled a tide of Flemish refugees. All ranks poured out of Antwerp into Holland and England, and a vast emptying of Flanders and the Pas de Calais followed. This human flood washed into the eastern and southern counties, especially London, where hastily cobbled relief bodies funnelled it out to local committees. Each committee took its quota, found and furnished empty houses for the destitute, and guided those with money into decent lodgings. The Matching’s Easy committee discovered it had beds for sixty but only a mixed bag of thirty souls to mind—part of the cargo of a rakish little steamer from Ostend. Among them were two Flemish peasant families; the rest were mostly middle-class fugitives from Antwerp. They were conveyed from the station to the Tithe barn at Claverings and there apportioned, under Lady Homartyn’s eye and her agent’s, to households ready to receive them. A kind of rivalry sprang up among the would-be hosts; everyone relished the chance to “do something” and was eager to show the Belgians what England felt for their plucky little land. Mr. Britling was proud to usher a Mr. Van der Pant—a trim, bearded man in a black frock-coat, black bowler, and knitted muffler, burdened with a large rucksack and a conspicuously foreign bicycle—into the comforts of Dower House. Mr. Van der Pant had slipped out of Antwerp at the final moment, had caught a nasty cold, and, it seemed, mislaid his wife and family along the way; he had a great deal to tell Mr. Britling, and in his eagerness did not immediately notice that while Mr. Britling knew French respectably, he did not know it fast.

Without doubt some practical person had come along and mended the leak, and then had come up and told the abbot he had discovered by divination that if the sinful bath were destroyed the well would flow again. The leak had befallen again now, and these children would have prayed, and processioned, and tolled their bells for heavenly succor till they all dried up and blew away, and no innocent of them all would ever have thought to drop a fish-line into the well or go down in it and find out what was really the matter. “There is a type of ‘Man About Town’ in New York,” he answered. “The term is quite familiar to me, but I don’t think I was ever called upon to define the character before. It would be difficult to point you out an exact specimen. I would say, offhand, that it is a man who had a hopeless case of the peculiar New York disease of wanting to see and know. At 6 o’clock each day life begins with him. He follows rigidly the conventions of dress and manners; but in the business of poking his nose into places where he does not belong he could give pointers to a civet cat or a jackdaw. He is the man who has chased Bohemia about the town from rathskeller to roof garden and from Hester street to Harlem until you can’t find a place in the city where they don’t cut their spaghetti with a knife. Your ‘Man About Town’ has done that. He is always on the scent of something new. He is curiosity, impudence and omnipresence. Hansoms were made for him, and gold-banded cigars; and the curse of music at dinner. There are not so many of him; but his minority report is adopted everywhere.

“There’s a specific sort of ‘Metropolitan Roamer’ native to San Francisco,” he replied. “I know the label well enough, though no one’s ever asked me to pin down exactly what makes one before. Pointing you to a perfect example would be tricky. Off the top of my head, I’d describe him as someone incurable with that unique San Francisco fever of needing to witness and learn every little thing the city has to offer. His day doesn’t start until six in the evening. He sticks unflinchingly to all the unwritten rules of tailored dress and polished social graces, but when it comes to sticking his nose where he’s not invited, he could out-snoop a raccoon or a magpie without half trying. He’s the soul who’s chased bohemian haunts all across the city, from basement beer halls to sky-high cocktail lounges, from the Mission District out to the Richmond, until there’s not a corner left where folks don’t hack their pasta with a butter knife to save time. That’s the Metropolitan Roamer’s doing, plain and simple. He’s forever hot on the trail of some fresh novelty. He is pure nosiness, unapologetic cheek, and impossible to escape. Horse-drawn cabs were built for him, and gold-foil wrapped cigars, and that bothersome fad of hiring string quartets to play over supper. There are precious few of these men scattered across the city, but every odd little preference they pick up is adopted by everyone else before the month is out.”

At a table behind him sat the Artful Dodger, Master Charles Bates, and Mr. Chitling: all intent upon a game of whist; the Artful taking dummy against Master Bates and Mr. Chitling. The countenance of the first-named gentleman, peculiarly intelligent at all times, acquired great additional interest from his close observance of the game, and his attentive perusal of Mr. Chitling’s hand; upon which, from time to time, as occasion served, he bestowed a variety of earnest glances: wisely regulating his own play by the result of his observations upon his neighbour’s cards. It being a cold night, the Dodger wore his hat, as, indeed, was often his custom within doors. He also sustained a clay pipe between his teeth, which he only removed for a brief space when he deemed it necessary to apply for refreshment to a quart pot upon the table, which stood ready filled with gin-and-water for the accommodation of the company.

At a scrubbed pine table just over his shoulder sat Cunning Jem, Master Toby Hackett, and Mr. Griggs, all wholly absorbed in a game of whist; Jem held the dummy hand, pitted against the paired play of Hackett and Griggs. The face of the first-named young rascal, sharp and quick-witted even on the slowest of days, took on an extra air of keen absorption as he tracked every turn of the game, his gaze drifting repeatedly to study Mr. Griggs’ fanned-out hand. Whenever the moment suited, he darted a series of shrewd sidelong looks at those exposed cards, carefully calibrating his own moves to what he’d gleaned from his neighbor’s hold. The night carried a sharp bite, so Jem kept his cap pulled down on his head, a habit he indulged even indoors more often than not. A dented clay pipe jutted from between his teeth, which he only removed for a few seconds at a time when he wanted a sip of refreshment from the quart pot resting on the table, brimming full of gin-and-water for the whole party’s use.

This woman was not given to soliloquy; but extremity of feeling lessens the individuality of the weak, as it increases that of the strong. She said again in the same tone, “I’ll believe that the end lies five posts forward, and no further, and so get strength to pass them.”

She was not a woman prone to speaking her thoughts; yet a crisis of the spirit often dissolves the distinctiveness of the frail, just as it clarifies the character of the brave. In a level voice she said once more, “I shall imagine the journey ends at the fifth marker yonder, and no further, and so find the fortitude to reach it.”

There was a violent convergence of miscellaneous backs and suchlike irregulars upon the threatened goal. Mr. Britling's dozen was rapidly losing its disciplined order. One of the sidecar ladies and the gallant Indian had shifted their activities to the defensive back, and with them was a spectacled gentleman waving his stick, high above all recognised rules. Mr. Direck's captain and both Britling boys hurried to join the fray. Mr. Britling, who seemed to Mr. Direck to be for a captain rather too demagogic, also ran back to rally his forces by loud cries. "Pass outwardly!" was the burthen of his contribution.

The ball darted swiftly back and forth across the pitch as our team mounted a fierce offensive. A chaotic mass of defenders and auxiliary players swarmed toward the vulnerable goal. Mr. Britling’s twelve-man squad was quickly losing any semblance of formation. One of the women from the sidecar and the courageous Indian gentleman had retreated to bolster the defense, joined by a man in spectacles who brandished his stick in flagrant violation of the rules. Mr. Direck’s captain and both of the Britling boys raced into the middle of the scramble. Mr. Britling, who struck Mr. Direck as being far too vocal and populist for a team leader, also hurried back to marshal his players with loud exhortations. "Pass it wide!" was the primary refrain of his instructions.

She passed him by, and would have gone upstairs unnoticed, but Clym was so concerned that he immediately followed her.

She stepped over the threshold indoors, her cheeks flushed bright, and faint traces of her recent flurry of excitement still glinting in her eyes. Clym Yeobright looked up, astonished; he had never caught even a hint of this kind of unguarded, heightened state in her before. She slipped past him, ready to climb the stairs without drawing notice, but Clym was so thoroughly concerned that he rose immediately and followed close at her heels.

At first Mrs. Britling had listened to Mr. Direck with an almost undivided attention, but as he had developed his opening the feast upon the blue linen table had passed on to a fresh phase that demanded more and more of her directive intelligence. The two little boys appeared suddenly at her elbows. "Shall we take the plates and get the strawberries, Mummy?" they asked simultaneously. Then one of the neat maids in the background had to be called up and instructed in undertones, and Mr. Direck saw that for the present Robinson's illuminating experience was not for her ears. A little baffled, but quite understanding how things were, he turned to his neighbour on his left....

At the outset, Mrs. Britling had been giving nearly her full attention to Mr. Direck as he spoke, but just as he was getting into the core of his opening remarks, the meal laid out on the blue linen table moved into a new phase that demanded more and more of her active direction. The two little boys appeared out of nowhere right at her elbows. “Can we take the plates to get the strawberries, Mummy?” they asked in perfect unison. Next, she had to call over one of the crisply put-together maids lingering in the background and murmur quiet instructions to her, and Mr. Direck caught on that for the time being, she was not going to be available to listen to Robinson’s illuminating anecdote. A little thrown off, but fully understanding how these domestic interruptions go, he turned to the guest seated to his left....

When Mrs. Yeobright approached, Eustacia surveyed her with the calm stare of a stranger.
