When Mrs. Yeobright drew near, Eustacia regarded her with the cool, unblinking stare of a total stranger.

• Increasing the capacity of multilingual NMT yields large improvements and narrows the performance gap with bilingual models. Lowresource translation benefits more from the increased capacity. • Language-specific modeling and deep NMT architectures can slightly improve zero-shot translation, but fail to alleviate the off-target translation issue. • Finetuning multilingual NMT with ROBT substantially reduces the proportion of offtarget translations (by ∼50%) and delivers an improvement of ∼10 BLEU in zero-shot settings, approaching the conventional pivotbased method. We show that finetuning with ROBT converges within a few thousand steps.

metadata.title: Improving Massively Multilingual Neural Machine Translation and Zero-Shot Translation

text: Increasing the capacity of multilingual NMT leads to substantial gains and reduces the performance gap relative to bilingual systems, with low-resource translation benefiting the most from this added capacity. Language-specific modeling and deeper NMT architectures can provide modest improvements for zero-shot translation, but they do not resolve the off-target translation problem. By contrast, finetuning multilingual NMT with ROBT markedly decreases the share of off-target outputs by approximately 50% and yields an improvement of about 10 BLEU in zero-shot scenarios, bringing performance close to that of the conventional pivot-based approach. We further show that finetuning with ROBT converges after only a few thousand steps.

We use a similar idea in Cai and Lam (2019) to encode the incrementally expanding graph. Specifically, a graph is simply treated as a sequence of nodes (concepts) in the chronological order of when they are inserted into the graph. We employ multi-layer Transformer architecture with masked self-attention and source-attention, which only allows each position in the node sequence to attend to all positions up to and including that position, and every position in the node sequence to attend over all positions in the input sequence. 3 While this design allows for significantly more parallelization during training and computation-saving incrementality during testing, 4 it inherently neglects the edge information. We attempted to alleviate this problem by incorporating the idea of Strubell et al. (2018) that applies auxiliary supervision at attention heads to encourage them to attend to each node's parents in the AMR graph. However, we did not see performance improvement. We attribute the failure to the fact that the neural attention mechanisms on their own are already capable of learning to attend to useful graph elements, and the auxiliary supervision is likely to disturb the ultimate parsing goal.

metadata.title: AMR Parsing via Graph Sequence Iterative Inference *

text: Following the methodology of Cai and Lam (2019), we employ a similar strategy to encode the incrementally expanding graph. Specifically, the graph is represented as a sequence of nodes (concepts) ordered chronologically by their time of insertion. We utilize a multi-layer Transformer architecture equipped with masked self-attention and source-attention; this setup ensures that each position in the node sequence attends only to itself and its predecessors, while allowing every node position to attend to the full input sequence. Although this design enables greater parallelization during training and efficient incrementality during inference, it inherently overlooks edge information. To mitigate this, we experimented with the approach of Strubell et al. (2018) by applying auxiliary supervision to the attention heads, encouraging them to focus on each node’s parents within the AMR graph. However, this did not result in performance gains. We attribute this outcome to the inherent capacity of neural attention mechanisms to learn relevant graph dependencies autonomously, suggesting that auxiliary supervision may instead disrupt the optimization of the primary parsing task.

Mr. Venn’s so tall he banged his head right on the low beam when he took a little skip as he passed underneath it. Mrs. Venn rushed over immediately, all flustered and worried, and she’s pressing her palm to his head now, feeling around to check if a lump has formed. And now everyone’s laughing again like absolutely nothing out of the ordinary just went down.

She looked so charming and fair that his cool mood about Weatherbury people was changed. He had not expected her to exercise this power over him in the twinkling of an eye. Should he go on, and care nothing? He could not bring himself to do that. Beyond a politic wish to remain unknown, there suddenly arose in him now a sense of shame at the possibility that his attractive young wife, who already despised him, should despise him more by discovering him in so mean a condition after so long a time. He actually blushed at the thought, and was vexed beyond measure that his sentiments of dislike towards Weatherbury should have led him to dally about the country in this way.

She seemed so lovely and delicate that his former indifference toward the people of Mellstock gave way at once. He had not imagined that she could so suddenly exert this dominion over him, and in a moment. Was he to go on, and care for nothing? He could not persuade himself to do so. More than any prudent desire to remain unrecognized, there sprang up in him a feeling of shame at the thought that his comely young wife, who already held him in contempt, might despise him yet further on discovering him after so many years in so pitiful a plight. He positively colored at the notion, and was bitterly annoyed that his aversion to Mellstock folk should have led him to loiter about the countryside in this fashion.

It was true that her declaration faltered plainly in the middle, but she smoothed over the break with more tears and kisses and broken murmurs, and Adrian understood it as her plea that he forgive her for the time when she had not believed in him and had pressed him to find work.

Users of the Penman library can programmatically construct graphs and then encode them to PENMAN notation. Penman allows users to directly append to the list of triples and assign epigraphical markers, or to assemble small graphs and use set-union operations to combine them together. Another option is to assemble the tree directly, which may make more sense for some systems. Once the tree is configured or constructed, users can use transformations such as rearrange and reset-variables to make the PENMAN string more canonical in form. Fig. 7 illustrates using the Python API to construct and encode a graph. Another possibility is for graph augmentation, where users rely on Penman to parse a string to a graph which they then modify, e.g., to add surface alignments or wiki links, then serialize to a string again. This allows them to focus on their primary task without worrying about the details of parsing and formatting.

metadata.title: Penman: An Open-Source Library and Tool for AMR Graphs

text: Users of the Penman library can programmatically construct graphs and then encode them in PENMAN notation. Penman allows users to append directly to the list of triples and assign epigraphical markers, or to assemble small graphs and combine them using set-union operations. Another option is to construct the tree directly, which may be more appropriate for some systems. Once the tree has been configured or built, users can apply transformations such as rearrange and reset-variables to make the PENMAN string more canonical in form. Figure 7 illustrates the use of the Python API to construct and encode a graph. Another possibility is graph augmentation, in which users rely on Penman to parse a string into a graph, modify it—for example, by adding surface alignments or wiki links—and then serialize it back to a string. This enables them to focus on their primary task without worrying about the details of parsing and formatting.

Because many of our test suites exploit ambiguities that arise from incremental processing, we restrict evaluation to left-to-right language models; future work could involve evaluation of bidirectional models (Devlin et al., 2018; on an appropriate subset of our test suites, and/or adaptation of our suites for use with bidirectional models (Goldberg, 2019) . Training ran until convergence of perplexity on a held-out validation set. Wherever possible, we trained multiple seeds of each model class and corpus size. We use the model sizes and training hyperparameters reported in the papers introducing each model (Table 2) . 4 The full parameter counts and perplexity scores for each model × corpus combination are given in Tables 3 and 4 , respectively.

metadata.title: A Systematic Assessment of Syntactic Generalization in Neural Language Models

text: To investigate the influence of inductive bias and dataset scale, we trained a collection of models with distinct inductive biases on each corpus. Since many of our test suites target ambiguities arising during incremental interpretation, we limit evaluation to left-to-right language models; future investigations could assess bidirectional models (Devlin et al., 2018) on a suitable subset of our suites, or adapt our suites for bidirectional evaluation (Goldberg, 2019). Training proceeded until perplexity converged on a held-out validation partition. When feasible, we trained several random seeds for every model class and corpus size. We adopt the model sizes and training hyperparameters specified in the original publications introducing each architecture (Table 2). The complete parameter counts and perplexity values for each model–corpus pairing are provided in Tables 3 and 4, respectively.

The above definitions help clarify the way we address polysemy. 2 Ambiguity on the target-side 2 In short, our work evades this problem by dealing with lexical entries exclusively on the type-rather than the senselevel. From a lexicological perspective, this may seem like a strong assumption. From a modeling perspective, however, it appears almost obvious as it aligns well with the major components of our methodology, i.e., lexicons, embeddings, and translation. The lexicons we work with follow the design of behavioral experiments: a stimulus (word type) is given to may result in multiple source entries translating to the same target-side word. 3 This circumstance leads to "partial duplicates" in TargetMT, i.e., groups of entries with the same word type but different emotion values (because they were derived from distinct Source entries). Such overlap could do harm to the integrity of our evaluation since knowledge may "leak" from training to validation phase, i.e., by testing the model on words it has already seen during training, although with distinct emotion labels. The proposed data partitioning eliminates such distortion effects. Since partial duplicates receive the same embedding vector, the prediction model assigns the same emotion value to both, thus merging them in TargetPred.

metadata.title: Learning and Evaluating Emotion Lexicons for 91 Languages
metadata.section: A Novel Approach to Lexicon Creation

text: The preceding definitions clarify our treatment of polysemy. 2 Target-side ambiguity 2 In essence, we sidestep this issue by operating solely on the type level rather than the sense level for lexical entries. From a lexicological standpoint, this might appear to be a restrictive premise. From a modeling standpoint, however, it seems almost natural, as it harmonizes with the core pillars of our approach—namely, lexicons, embeddings, and translation. The lexicons we employ mirror the structure of behavioral experiments: a stimulus (word type) is presented, which may cause multiple source entries to map onto the same target-side word. 3 This situation produces “partial duplicates” in TargetMT, i.e., clusters of entries sharing the same word type but bearing different emotion values (because they originate from distinct Source entries). Such overlap could compromise the soundness of our evaluation, as knowledge might “leak” from the training phase into validation—specifically, by testing the model on words encountered during training, albeit with different emotion labels. Our proposed data partitioning removes these distorting effects. Since partial duplicates are assigned the same embedding vector, the prediction model attributes an identical emotion value to both, thereby consolidating them in TargetPred.

It was the rush and roar of rain that he typified, and it stopped him, for no voice could be heard in it. A memorable storm of thunder and lightning broke with that sweep of water, and there was not a moment’s interval in crash, and fire, and rain, until after the moon rose at midnight.

He became the very image of the downpour’s clamour and surge, and it brought him to a halt, for no sound could rise above it. With that deluge came an unforgettable tempest of thunderbolts and blinding flashes, and there was no pause in the cracking, the blazing, and the torrent until the moon climbed into the sky at midnight.

“Oh, Justine! Forgive me for having for one moment distrusted you. Why did you confess? But do not mourn, dear girl. Do not fear. I will proclaim, I will prove your innocence. I will melt the stony hearts of your enemies by my tears and prayers. You shall not die! You, my playfellow, my companion, my sister, perish on the scaffold! No! No! I never could survive so horrible a misfortune.”

“Oh, Justine! Forgive me for the one instant in which I doubted you. Why did you confess? But do not grieve, dear girl. Do not be afraid. I will declare, I will establish, your innocence. I will soften the hard hearts of your enemies with my tears and my prayers. You shall not die! You, my playmate, my companion, my sister, die on the scaffold? No! No! I could never endure such a dreadful calamity.”

He had often come up here without stating his purpose to his mother; but this was the first time that he had been ostensibly frank as to his purpose while really concealing it. It was a moral situation which, three months earlier, he could hardly have credited of himself. In returning to labour in this sequestered spot he had anticipated an escape from the chafing of social necessities; yet behold they were here also. More than ever he longed to be in some world where personal ambition was not the only recognized form of progress—such, perhaps, as might have been the case at some time or other in the silvery globe then shining upon him. His eye travelled over the length and breadth of that distant country—over the Bay of Rainbows, the sombre Sea of Crises, the Ocean of Storms, the Lake of Dreams, the vast Walled Plains, and the wondrous Ring Mountains—till he almost felt himself to be voyaging bodily through its wild scenes, standing on its hollow hills, traversing its deserts, descending its vales and old sea bottoms, or mounting to the edges of its craters.

He had often before gone up there without telling his mother why; but never yet had he seemed so open about his errand while in truth he was hiding it. Three months earlier, he could scarcely have believed himself capable of such a moral posture. When he came back to work in that remote place, he had hoped to find release from the abrasion of social demands; and yet here, too, they had followed him. More than ever he yearned for some realm in which private ambition was not the sole accepted measure of advancement—perhaps such a world as might once have been reflected in the argent sphere shining down upon him then. His gaze roved across the breadth and length of that far-off country: the Bay of Rainbows, the dark Sea of Crises, the Ocean of Storms, the Lake of Dreams, the immense Walled Plains, and the marvelous Ring Mountains—until he almost seemed to be travelling there in body, standing upon its hollow hills, crossing its deserts, descending its valleys and ancient sea floors, or climbing to the lips of its craters.

Her eyes wandered along the strong throat, dimly seen in the starlight, and over the firm-poised head, and the old desire to lay her hands upon his neck came back to her. The strength she abhorred attracted her. Her feeling of loneliness became more pronounced, and she felt tired. Her position on the heeling boat irked her, and she remembered the headache he had cured and the soothing rest that resided in him. He was sitting beside her, quite beside her, and the boat seemed to tilt her toward him. Then arose in her the impulse to lean against him, to rest herself against his strength—a vague, half-formed impulse, which, even as she considered it, mastered her and made her lean toward him. Or was it the heeling of the boat? She did not know. She never knew. She knew only that she was leaning against him and that the easement and soothing rest were very good. Perhaps it had been the boat’s fault, but she made no effort to retrieve it. She leaned lightly against his shoulder, but she leaned, and she continued to lean when he shifted his position to make it more comfortable for her.

Her gaze drifted over the broad line of his throat, barely visible in the faint starlight, then trailed up to his steady, held-high head, and that familiar old urge to rest her palms against the nape of his neck bubbled up again in her. That raw, unyielding strength she claimed to loathe drew her in anyway. Her loneliness swelled sharper, heavier, and a bone-deep weariness settled over her. Perched on the tilting sailboat felt awkward and uncomfortable, and she thought back to the throbbing headache he’d once eased for her, how just being near him wrapped her in a quiet, calming rest. He was right next to her, so close their arms almost brushed, and every small lurch of the boat seemed to tip her straight into his space. Then a faint, half-formed impulse flickered to life in her chest: to slump against him, to let his steady strength hold her weight. She’d barely even registered the thought before it took over, pulling her slowly in his direction. Or was that just the boat shifting under them? She couldn’t tell. She never could, later, when she thought back to the moment. All she was aware of in that second was the press of her body against his, and how good that soft, uncomplicated relief felt, how the quiet calm she’d remembered wrapped around her at once. Maybe it really had just been the boat’s unsteady tilt, but she didn’t make any move to right herself, to pull back. Her weight rested light on his shoulder, but it was there, solid, and she didn’t shift away even when he adjusted his posture to make the spot she was leaning against softer, more comfortable for her.

He did his honest best, but lord, it was no great things. He looked as humble as the leaning tower at Pisa. It is the most you could say of it. Indeed, it was such a thundering poor success that it raised wondering scowls all along the line, and a gorgeous flunkey at the tail end of it raised his whip; but I jumped in time and was under it when it fell; and under cover of the volley of coarse laughter which followed, I spoke up sharply and warned the king to take no notice. He mastered himself for the moment, but it was a sore tax; he wanted to eat up the procession. I said:

Her grandfather had returned, and was busily engaged in pouring some gallons of newly arrived rum into the square bottles of his square cellaret. Whenever these home supplies were exhausted he would go to the Quiet Woman, and, standing with his back to the fire, grog in hand, tell remarkable stories of how he had lived seven years under the waterline of his ship, and other naval wonders, to the natives, who hoped too earnestly for a treat of ale from the teller to exhibit any doubts of his truth.

Old Master Thorne had come home, and was occupied with decanting several gallons of fresh-delivered spirits into the cubic flasks of his heavy sideboard. When these private stores ran dry, he would betake himself to The Mute Virgin, where, warming his coat-tails at the hearth with a steaming glass in his grip, he would recount extraordinary fables of surviving a full decade beneath the keel of a man-of-war, along with various other maritime miracles, to the local laborers, who were far too eager for a free draught of bitter to voice the slightest skepticism regarding his veracity.

It seemed as though Mr. Direck would be unable to write any letters until his wrist had mended. Teddy tried him with a typewriter, but Mr. Direck was very awkward with his left hand, and then Mr. Britling suddenly remembered a little peculiarity he had which it was possible that Mr. Direck might share unconsciously, and that was his gift of looking-glass writing with his left hand. Mr. Britling had found out quite by chance in his schoolboy days that while his right hand had been laboriously learning to write, his left hand, all unsuspected, had been picking up the same lesson, and that by taking a pencil in his left hand and writing from right to left, without watching what he was writing, and then examining the scrawl in a mirror, he could reproduce his own handwriting in exact reverse. About three people out of five have this often quite unsuspected ability. He demonstrated his gift, and then Miss Cecily Corner, who had dropped in in a casual sort of way to ask about Mr. Direck, tried it, and then Mr. Direck tried it. And they could all do it. And then Teddy brought a sheet of copying carbon, and so Mr. Direck, by using the carbon reversed under his paper, was restored to the world of correspondence again.

It looked as if Mr. Direck wouldn’t be able to write any letters until his wrist had fully recovered. Teddy attempted to get him started on a typewriter, but Mr. Direck’s left hand proved far too clumsy. Suddenly, Mr. Britling recalled a strange quirk he possessed, one that Mr. Direck might also have without realizing it: the knack for mirror writing with his left hand. Back in his school days, Mr. Britling had stumbled upon the fact that while his right hand was painstakingly learning to write, his left hand had been secretly absorbing the same lessons. He found that by moving a pencil from right to left with his left hand, without focusing too hard on the page, he could create a perfect reverse of his usual script. Apparently, about three out of five people possess this hidden ability. After he demonstrated the trick, Miss Cecily Corner—who had stopped by casually to check on Mr. Direck—gave it a try, and then Mr. Direck followed suit. They all succeeded. Teddy then fetched a sheet of carbon paper, and by placing it backwards under the stationery, Mr. Direck was finally able to rejoin the world of correspondence.

Eustacia, who had reddened when she perceived the effect of her exclamation upon the group below, was no longer to be seen at the window, though Yeobright scanned it wistfully. While he stood there the men at the well succeeded in getting up the bucket without a mishap. One of them went to inquire for the captain, to learn what orders he wished to give for mending the well-tackle. The captain proved to be away from home, and Eustacia appeared at the door and came out. She had lapsed into an easy and dignified calm, far removed from the intensity of life in her words of solicitude for Clym’s safety.

Isabella, her cheeks flushing as she noted the stir her outcry had caused among the company below, had retreated from the casement, though Julian continued to search the dark pane with a yearning eye. During his vigil, the laborers at the cistern finally drew up the bucket, the task completed without further accident. One fellow went to seek the Major, intending to ask how the broken windlass should be mended. The old soldier was found to be abroad, and in his stead Isabella appeared at the entrance and stepped forth. She had reassumed a graceful and lofty composure, quite distinct from the spirited desperation that had marked her earlier concern for Julian’s welfare.

Never, since the first meeting, had they seemed so far apart as that day. His confession, told in frankness, with the spirit of revolt behind, had repelled her. But she was more shocked by the repulsion itself than by the cause of it. It pointed out to her how near she had drawn to him, and once accepted, it paved the way for greater intimacy. Pity, too, was aroused, and innocent, idealistic thoughts of reform. She would save this raw young man who had come so far. She would save him from the curse of his early environment, and she would save him from himself in spite of himself. And all this affected her as a very noble state of consciousness; nor did she dream that behind it and underlying it were the jealousy and desire of love.

From the day they first met right up to that moment, the gulf between them had never felt so wide. The unvarnished confession he’d laid bare, sharp with the fire of rebellion running under every word, had made her recoil. Yet what startled her more than the reason for that reaction was the reaction itself. That jolt of distaste drove home just how close she’d grown to him, and once she let herself acknowledge that truth, it cleared a path for them to grow even closer. It also stirred up pity in her, along with soft, idealistic fantasies of helping him turn his life around. She would rescue this rough, unrefined young man who’d already come such a long way on his own. She would pull him free of the heavy curse of his troubled upbringing, and she would save him from his own worst impulses, even if he fought her every step of the way. To her, this entire impulse felt like the height of selfless, noble virtue; it never once crossed her mind that tangled beneath it all were the sharp, unacknowledged pangs of jealousy and the hungry want of romantic love.

The broad sky seemed on fire. Rising into the air with showers of sparks, and rolling one above the other, were sheets of flame, lighting the atmosphere for miles round, and driving clouds of smoke in the direction where he stood. The shouts grew louder as new voices swelled the roar, and he could hear the cry of Fire! mingled with the ringing of an alarm-bell, the fall of heavy bodies, and the crackling of flames as they twined round some new obstacle, and shot aloft as though refreshed by food. The noise increased as he looked. There were people there—men and women—light, bustle. It was like new life to him. He darted onward—straight, headlong—dashing through brier and brake, and leaping gate and fence as madly as his dog, who careered with loud and sounding bark before him.

The wide sky looked as though it were ablaze. Sheets of fire rose into the air, throwing off showers of sparks and tumbling one over another, lighting the air for miles around and driving clouds of smoke toward the place where he stood. The shouts grew louder as more voices joined the uproar, and he could hear the cry of Fire! mixed with the pealing of an alarm bell, the crash of falling heavy objects, and the crackle of flames as they wound around some fresh obstruction and shot upward as if fed and revived. The clamor swelled as he watched. There were people there—men and women—movement, confusion, stir. It was like a new kind of life to him. He sprang forward at once, headlong and straight, crashing through brier and brush, vaulting gate and fence as wildly as his dog, which raced ahead of him with loud, ringing barks.

Mr. Lorry and Monsieur Defarge had made all ready for the journey, and had brought with them, besides travelling cloaks and wrappers, bread and meat, wine, and hot coffee. Monsieur Defarge put this provender, and the lamp he carried, on the shoemaker’s bench (there was nothing else in the garret but a pallet bed), and he and Mr. Lorry roused the captive, and assisted him to his feet.

Mr. Lorry and Monsieur Defarge had finished all preparations for their journey, and brought along more than just traveling cloaks and warm wraps: they had also packed bread, meat, wine, and steaming hot coffee. Monsieur Defarge set this stock of provisions, along with the lamp he’d been carrying, on the shoemaker’s workbench—there was nothing else in the cramped garret save for a thin pallet bed—before he and Mr. Lorry roused the captive and helped him to his feet.

Adverbial clauses (advcl) have relatively low values on main diagonals and a high percentage of single edges corresponding to multi-edge paths. This reflects the wide semantic range of advcl: in addition to modifying the matrix predicate (died by drowning), they can also denote sequential and parallel events (published a paper sparking a debate). The latter two cases naturally give rise to conj and complement clauses (cf. published a paper and sparked a debate / published a paper to spark a debate), the most common other path in En-Ru and En-Fr respectively. As we show in § 5.2, there is also a converse phenomenon: sequences of events represented using coordinated clauses, ccomp, or xcomp in En are translated with advcl in East Asian languages.

metadata.title: Fine-Grained Analysis of Cross-Linguistic Syntactic Divergences

text: Adverbial clauses (advcl) exhibit relatively low values on the main diagonals and a high proportion of single edges corresponding to multi-edge paths. This pattern reflects the broad semantic range of advcl: in addition to modifying the matrix predicate (died by drowning), they can also express sequential and parallel events (published a paper sparking a debate). The latter two uses naturally give rise to conj and complement clauses (cf. published a paper and sparked a debate / published a paper to spark a debate), which constitute the most frequent alternative path in En-Ru and En-Fr, respectively. As we show in § 5.2, there is also a converse pattern: sequences of events represented in English with coordinated clauses, ccomp, or xcomp are translated with advcl in East Asian languages.

Yet he suddenly began to feel himself drifting into the old track of manœuvring on Thomasin’s account.

The dead man disposed of, and the crowd being under the necessity of providing some other entertainment for itself, another brighter genius (or perhaps the same) conceived the humour of impeaching casual passers-by, as Old Bailey spies, and wreaking vengeance on them. Chase was given to some scores of inoffensive persons who had never been near the Old Bailey in their lives, in the realisation of this fancy, and they were roughly hustled and maltreated. The transition to the sport of window-breaking, and thence to the plundering of public-houses, was easy and natural. At last, after several hours, when sundry summer-houses had been pulled down, and some area-railings had been torn up, to arm the more belligerent spirits, a rumour got about that the Guards were coming. Before this rumour, the crowd gradually melted away, and perhaps the Guards came, and perhaps they never came, and this was the usual progress of a mob.

With the dead man disposed of, and the crowd forced to seek some other diversion, another brighter wit—or perhaps the same one—hit upon the idea of accusing random passers-by of being Old Bailey spies, and taking vengeance on them. In pursuit of this fancy, dozens of harmless people, who had never come within sight of the Old Bailey in their lives, were chased, roughly seized, and ill-used. From this it was an easy and natural step to the amusement of smashing windows, and then to the plundering of public-houses. At length, after several hours, when various summer-houses had been pulled down and some area railings torn up to furnish weapons for the more pugnacious, a rumour spread that the Guards were on their way. At that, the mob slowly drifted apart; and perhaps the Guards did come, and perhaps they did not, for this was the ordinary course of a crowd.

From this point onward Mr. Britling's notes became more fragmentary. They had a consecutiveness, but they were discontinuous. His thought had leapt across gaps that his pen had had no time to fill. And he had begun to realise that his letter to the old people in Pomerania was becoming impossible. It had broken away into dissertation.

Beyond this point, Mr. Britling’s jottings became more scattered. They retained a certain sequence, yet they were no longer continuous. His thoughts had bounded across intervals that his hand lacked the time to record. He was starting to see that his letter to the elderly couple in Pomerania was becoming an impossibility. It had veered off into a formal academic discourse.

She stumbled and crumpled to the ground, her vision nearly completely obscured by blood pouring in thick streams from a deep gash across her forehead. Fighting through waves of sharp, disorienting pain, she strained to push herself up onto her knees, fumbling inside her bodice to draw out a white handkerchief—Rose Maylie’s very own. Clasping it tight in her folded hands, she lifted it as high toward the heavens as her waning strength would permit, then breathed out a quiet, shaking prayer begging her Creator for mercy.

This evening Bathsheba was unusually excited, her red cheeks and lips contrasting lustrously with the mazy skeins of her shadowy hair. She seemed to expect assistance, and the seat at the bottom of the table was at her request left vacant until after they had begun the meal. She then asked Gabriel to take the place and the duties appertaining to that end, which he did with great readiness.

That night, Bathsheba was stirred by an uncommon fervor, her flushed cheeks and vivid lips gleaming against the tangled darkness of her hair. She appeared to be awaiting support, and at her insistence, the chair at the table's foot remained empty until the meal was underway. Then she called upon Gabriel to occupy that seat and assume its attendant responsibilities, which he accepted without hesitation.

Mr. Van der Pant did not seem to be angry with the Germans. But he manifestly regarded them as people to be killed. He denounced nothing that they had done; he related. They were just an evil accident that had happened to Belgium and mankind. They had to be destroyed. He gave Mr. Britling an extraordinary persuasion that knives were being sharpened in every cellar in Brussels and Antwerp against the day of inevitable retreat, of a resolution to exterminate the invader that was far too deep to be vindictive.... And the man was most amazingly unconquered. Mr. Britling perceived the label on his habitual dinner wine with a slight embarrassment. "Do you care," he asked, "to drink a German wine? This is Berncasteler from the Moselle." Mr. Van der Pant reflected. "But it is a good wine," he said. "After the peace it will be Belgian.... Yes, if we are to be safe in the future from such a war as this, we must have our boundaries right up to the Rhine."

Mr. Van der Pant showed no fury toward the Germans. Yet he plainly saw them as creatures marked for death. He did not condemn their actions; he simply recounted them. They were a dark misfortune that had befallen Belgium and the world. They must be wiped out. He left Mr. Britling with the vivid sense that in every cellar across Brussels and Antwerp, blades were being honed for the moment of inevitable withdrawal, a determination to erase the occupier so profound it lay beyond revenge… And the man was utterly unbroken. Mr. Britling noticed the label on his usual dinner wine with a touch of unease. "Would you mind," he asked, "drinking a German wine? This is a Berncasteler from the Moselle." Mr. Van der Pant paused. "But it is a fine wine," he said. "After the peace, it will be Belgian… Yes, if we are to be shielded in the future from a war like this, our borders must reach all the way to the Rhine."

metadata.title: Will-They-Won't-They: A Very Large Dataset for Stance Detection on Twitter

text: Table 4 presents the experimental results. Despite its straightforward architecture, SiamNet achieves the highest performance as measured by both averaged and weighted averaged F₁ scores. Consistent with earlier work (Mohammad et al., 2017), the SVM model proves to be a remarkably strong and robust baseline. The relative improvements of CrossNet over BiCE, and of HAN over TAN, consistently mirror the results reported for these models on the SemEval 2016-Task 6 corpus (Xu et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2018).

Position-Aware Graphs. PacSum is a recently proposed graph-based method to select salient sentences from multiple documents (Zheng and Lapata, 2019) . In PacSum, a sentence is more likely to be selected if it has higher average similarity with its succeeding sentences and lower average similarity with its preceding sentences. This strategy allows PacSum to prioritize the selection of earlyposition and "semantically central" sentences. We further extend PacSum by using SBERT to measure sentences similarity (the resulting method is denoted as SPS) and consider both the individualand global-graph versions of SPS.

metadata.title: SUPERT: Towards New Frontiers in Unsupervised Evaluation Metrics for Multi-Document Summarization

text: Position-Aware Graphs. Zheng and Lapata (2019) introduced PacSum, a graph-based approach for identifying salient sentences across multiple documents. Within this framework, a sentence's importance is weighted such that higher selection probability is assigned to nodes demonstrating greater average similarity with subsequent sentences compared to preceding ones. This mechanism allows PacSum to emphasize sentences that are both "semantically central" and located in early document positions. We extend this methodology by employing SBERT for similarity computations—denoted as SPS—and investigate both the individual and global graph variants of this approach.

“Jerry, if you wish to take something to eat, you can. But, keep in the way. You will be sure to hear when the jury come in. Don’t be a moment behind them, for I want you to take the verdict back to the bank. You are the quickest messenger I know, and will get to Temple Bar long before I can.”

“Jerry, if you’d like to grab a bite, go ahead. Just stay close by. You’ll definitely hear when the jury returns. Don’t fall a second behind them—I need you to carry the verdict straight to the bank. You’re the fastest courier I’ve got, and you’ll reach Temple Bar well ahead of me.”

Clarence was with me as concerned the revolution, but in a modified way. His idea was a republic, without privileged orders, but with a hereditary royal family at the head of it instead of an elective chief magistrate. He believed that no nation that had ever known the joy of worshiping a royal family could ever be robbed of it and not fade away and die of melancholy. I urged that kings were dangerous. He said, then have cats. He was sure that a royal family of cats would answer every purpose. They would be as useful as any other royal family, they would know as much, they would have the same virtues and the same treacheries, the same disposition to get up shindies with other royal cats, they would be laughably vain and absurd and never know it, they would be wholly inexpensive; finally, they would have as sound a divine right as any other royal house, and “Tom VII, or Tom XI, or Tom XIV by the grace of God King,” would sound as well as it would when applied to the ordinary royal tomcat with tights on. “And as a rule,” said he, in his neat modern English, “the character of these cats would be considerably above the character of the average king, and this would be an immense moral advantage to the nation, for the reason that a nation always models its morals after its monarch's. The worship of royalty being founded in unreason, these graceful and harmless cats would easily become as sacred as any other royalties, and indeed more so, because it would presently be noticed that they hanged nobody, beheaded nobody, imprisoned nobody, inflicted no cruelties or injustices of any sort, and so must be worthy of a deeper love and reverence than the customary human king, and would certainly get it. The eyes of the whole harried world would soon be fixed upon this humane and gentle system, and royal butchers would presently begin to disappear; their subjects would fill the vacancies with catlings from our own royal house; we should become a factory; we should supply the thrones of the world; within forty years all Europe would be governed by cats, and we should furnish the cats. The reign of universal peace would begin then, to end no more forever.... Me-e-e-yow-ow-ow-ow--fzt!--wow!”

Clarence supported my revolutionary goals, though with a particular caveat. He favored a republic that abolished social castes but retained a hereditary monarchy at its helm instead of an elected leader. He argued that any people who had experienced the delight of idolizing a royal family could never be deprived of it without withering away from profound sadness. When I pointed out that kings were a hazard, he suggested substituting them with cats. He was certain a feline dynasty would serve every necessary function. They would be just as effective as any human royals, possess equal intellect, and display the same blend of virtues and vices, including a tendency to pick fights with other royal cats. They would be hilariously conceited and absurd without ever realizing it, and they would cost absolutely nothing. Furthermore, their claim to divine right would be as legitimate as any other royal line; "Tom VII, or Tom XI, or Tom XIV, King by the Grace of God," would sound just as dignified as the same title applied to a typical human monarch in his finery. 

"Moreover," he noted in his polished, modern tongue, "the temperament of these cats would generally be far superior to that of the average king. This would provide a tremendous moral boost to the public, as a nation always patterns its ethics after its ruler. Since the veneration of royalty is based on emotion rather than logic, these elegant and harmless animals would easily become as hallowed as any other sovereign—perhaps even more so. People would eventually observe that they never hang, behead, or imprison anyone, nor do they perpetrate any cruelties or injustices. Thus, they would be entitled to a deeper level of affection and respect than a standard human king, and they would surely receive it. The gaze of a troubled world would soon settle upon this kind and gentle model, causing royal despots to vanish. Their subjects would fill those empty thrones with kittens from our own royal stock; we would become a sort of factory, exporting monarchs to every nation. Within forty years, all of Europe would be under feline rule, and we would be the ones supplying the cats. An age of eternal global peace would then begin... Me-e-e-yow-ow-ow-ow—fzt!—wow!"

Oak was amused, perhaps a little astonished, and hanging up the hat in his hut, went again among his ewes. An hour passed, the girl returned, properly seated now, with a bag of bran in front of her. On nearing the cattle-shed she was met by a boy bringing a milking-pail, who held the reins of the pony whilst she slid off. The boy led away the horse, leaving the pail with the young woman.

Oak felt a flicker of amusement, tinged perhaps with mild surprise, and after hanging the hat inside his hut, he returned to his flock. An hour slipped by, and the girl came back, now seated properly, a sack of bran balanced before her. As she approached the cattle-shed, a boy carrying a milking-pail met her and steadied the pony’s reins while she dismounted. The boy led the horse away, leaving the pail in the young woman’s care.

Mr. Direck's eye had come to rest upon the barn, and its expression changed slowly from lazy appreciation to a brightening intelligence. Suddenly he resolved to say something. He resolved to say it so firmly that he determined to say it even if Mr. Britling went on talking all the time.

Mr. Direck’s gaze settled upon the barn, his expression shifting gradually from one of idle pleasure to a look of sharpening insight. Suddenly, he felt a firm compulsion to speak. He was so determined to voice his thought that he resolved to do so even if Mr. Britling continued his monologue without a single interruption.

Baldly as he had stated it, in his eyes was a rich vision of that hot, starry night at Salina Cruz, the white strip of beach, the lights of the sugar steamers in the harbor, the voices of the drunken sailors in the distance, the jostling stevedores, the flaming passion in the Mexican’s face, the glint of the beast-eyes in the starlight, the sting of the steel in his neck, and the rush of blood, the crowd and the cries, the two bodies, his and the Mexican’s, locked together, rolling over and over and tearing up the sand, and from away off somewhere the mellow tinkling of a guitar. Such was the picture, and he thrilled to the memory of it, wondering if the man could paint it who had painted the pilot-schooner on the wall. The white beach, the stars, and the lights of the sugar steamers would look great, he thought, and midway on the sand the dark group of figures that surrounded the fighters. The knife occupied a place in the picture, he decided, and would show well, with a sort of gleam, in the light of the stars. But of all this no hint had crept into his speech. “He tried to bite off my nose,” he concluded.

Bare as his words were, his gaze brimmed with the lush scene of that sweltering, star-scattered night at Salina Cruz—the pale ribbon of shore, the glow of sugar freighters anchored in the bay, the far-off shouts of tipsy sailors, the shoving dockhands, the fierce heat in the Mexican’s expression, the animal shine of his eyes under the stars, the cold bite of the blade at his throat, the surge of blood, the press of bodies and noise, the two of them, his own form and the Mexican’s, clenched together, churning over and over and gouging the sand, while somewhere distant a guitar rang out softly. That was the image, and he shivered at the recollection, asking himself whether the man who had rendered the pilot schooner on the wall could capture this too. The pale beach, the stars, and the lights of the sugar boats would be striking, he mused, and right in the middle of the sand the dark knot of onlookers circling the brawlers. The knife deserved a place in the composition, he figured, and would stand out, catching a faint glint in the starshine. Yet not a trace of this had slipped into what he said. “He tried to bite off my nose,” he finished.

text:
The vanishing spectacle recedes,
The core of a maker’s spirit or a sage’s lifelong inquiry,
The grueling labors of the soldier, the martyr, and the brave,
To shape a phantom likeness of the soul.

The dialogue faltered, leaving a hollow space in the air between them. With a flicker of uncertainty, she inquired about the thin, silver line marking his temple. He sensed then her struggle to inhabit his rugged dialect, and he decided instantly to discard his own speech and follow her into the softer terrain of hers.

His complex anger flamed afresh, and Ruth was in terror of him. Never had she seen him so angry, and it was all mystified and unreasonable to her comprehension. And yet, through her very terror ran the fibres of fascination that had drawn and that still drew her to him—that had compelled her to lean towards him, and, in that mad, culminating moment, lay her hands upon his neck. She was hurt and outraged by what had taken place, and yet she lay in his arms and quivered while he went on muttering, “The beasts! The beasts!” And she still lay there when he said: “I’ll not bother your table again, dear. They do not like me, and it is wrong of me to thrust my objectionable presence upon them. Besides, they are just as objectionable to me. Faugh! They are sickening. And to think of it, I dreamed in my innocence that the persons who sat in the high places, who lived in fine houses and had educations and bank accounts, were worth while!”

A new surge of his intricate rage flared up, and Ruth felt a cold terror take hold of her. She had never seen him so consumed by fury, a state that remained entirely baffling and irrational to her understanding. Even so, through her fear ran the same magnetic threads of fascination that had always pulled her toward him—the same impulse that had forced her to lean in and, in a frantic, climactic moment, clasp her hands behind his neck. Though she felt wounded and insulted by what had transpired, she stayed trembling in his arms as he continued to mutter, “The animals! The wretched animals!” She remained there as he added, “I won’t trouble your dinner table again, my dear. They have no liking for me, and it is a mistake for me to impose my unwelcome presence on them. In truth, they are just as loathsome to me. It’s revolting. To think that in my ignorance I actually believed those in high society, with their grand estates, educations, and fortunes, were people of any real value!”

metadata.title: Sta n z a : A Python Natural Language Processing Toolkit for Many Human Languages

text: For every neural processor, Stanza offers command-line interfaces that allow users to train their own customized models. To accomplish this, users must prepare training and development data in the required formats—specifically, CoNLL-U format for the Universal Dependencies pipeline and BIO-format column files for the NER model. The command below trains a neural dependency parser using user-provided training and development data: Table 2 : Performance comparisons of neural pipelines on the Universal Dependencies (v2.5) test treebanks. For our system, we report macro-averaged results across all 100 treebanks. We also compare our system with UDPipe and spaCy on treebanks for five major languages for which the corresponding pretrained models are publicly available. All results are F 1 scores computed by the official evaluation script from the 2018 UD Shared Task.

If any one had told him he was going to be stunned for five or six minutes, cut about the brow and face and have a bone in his wrist put out, and that as a consequence he would find himself pleased and exhilarated, he would have treated the prophecy with ridicule; but here he was lying stiffly on his back with his wrist bandaged to his side and smiling into the darkness even more brightly than he had smiled at the Essex landscape two days before. The fact is pain hurts or irritates, but in itself it does not make a healthily constituted man miserable. The expectation of pain, the certainty of injury may make one hopeless enough, the reality rouses our resistance. Nobody wants a broken bone or a delicate wrist, but very few people are very much depressed by getting one. People can be much more depressed by smoking a hundred cigarettes in three days or losing one per cent. of their capital.

Had someone prophesied that he would shortly be dazed for several minutes, slashed across the forehead and cheeks, suffer a dislocated wrist-bone, and emerge from the ordeal oddly content and buoyant, he would have scoffed at the forecast. Yet there he was, stretched rigidly upon his back, his injured wrist strapped tightly against his ribs, beaming into the blackness with a radiance keener than the smile he had bestowed upon the Essex countryside two mornings earlier. Pain, in truth, stings or vexes, but by itself it does not render a soundly built man wretched. The dread of agony, the certitude of harm may breed despair; the actual event summons our defiance. No one seeks a fractured bone or a fragile joint, yet scarcely anyone sinks into gloom upon acquiring one. A man can be far more dejected by smoking a hundred cigarettes over three days or by forfeiting one per cent of his fortune.

Its condition is recorded therein as that of heathy, furzy, briary wilderness—“Bruaria.” Then follows the length and breadth in leagues; and, though some uncertainty exists as to the exact extent of this ancient lineal measure, it appears from the figures that the area of Egdon down to the present day has but little diminished. “Turbaria Bruaria”—the right of cutting heath-turf—occurs in charters relating to the district. “Overgrown with heth and mosse,” says Leland of the same dark sweep of country.

This forgotten, outworn, bypassed tract of earth is entered in the Great Survey. Its character is preserved there as a scrubby, thorn-choked, desolate wasteland—“Erica.” Then follow its dimensions in archaic leagues; and, though the true scale of those ancient units remains a matter of some debate, the figures indicate that the reach of Blackwood Moor has scarcely contracted since that distant era. “Privilegium Fodiendi”—the entitlement to harvest peat—is cited in old scrolls pertaining to the region. “Clothed in bracken and gray lichen,” noted Camden of this same brooding expanse of land.

Intellectual “work” is misnamed; it is a pleasure, a dissipation, and is its own highest reward. The poorest paid architect, engineer, general, author, sculptor, painter, lecturer, advocate, legislator, actor, preacher, singer is constructively in heaven when he is at work; and as for the musician with the fiddle-bow in his hand who sits in the midst of a great orchestra with the ebbing and flowing tides of divine sound washing over him--why, certainly, he is at work, if you wish to call it that, but lord, it's a sarcasm just the same. The law of work does seem utterly unfair--but there it is, and nothing can change it: the higher the pay in enjoyment the worker gets out of it, the higher shall be his pay in cash, also. And it's also the very law of those transparent swindles, transmissible nobility and kingship.

The label "intellectual labor" is a misnomer; it is a delight, an indulgence, and carries its own supreme compensation. The most underpaid architect, engineer, general, writer, sculptor, painter, speaker, lawyer, lawmaker, performer, minister, vocalist is essentially in paradise while engaged in his craft; and as for the musician, bow in hand, seated within a vast orchestra while surging currents of sublime sound cascade over him—well, yes, he is working, if you insist on calling it that, but heavens, the term is a mockery all the same. The principle of work appears profoundly unjust—yet there it stands, immutable: the greater the pleasure a worker derives from his toil, the greater his monetary reward will be as well. And this is precisely the same rule governing those blatant frauds, hereditary aristocracy and monarchy.

So, being a practical-minded Connecticut fellow, I promptly pushed the entire puzzle out of my head until its designated moment arrived, freeing me to focus completely on the here and now, staying sharp and poised to squeeze every drop of advantage from whatever came my way. My rule is: tackle one thing at a time—and play that hand for all it’s worth, even if you’re holding nothing but a low pair and a jack. I settled on two resolutions: if this was still the nineteenth century and I was trapped among madmen with no escape, I’d soon run that asylum or demand a damn good explanation; and if, instead, it truly was the sixth century, fine—I wouldn’t ask for an easier deal: I’d take command of the entire realm within three months, since I figured I’d have a head start on the kingdom’s most learned man by something like thirteen hundred years or more. I’m not one to dawdle once my mind’s fixed and there’s work to be done, so I turned to the page and spoke.

We train a DDI-detection model to predict whether a given candidate sentence provides evidence of an interaction between two drug entities. Our DDI-detection model uses pre-trained BERT models (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) (Devlin et al., 2018) to encode input sequences. These models have been shown to be effective at domain transfer, and are able to achieve high performance using small amounts of task-specific annotated data. In particular, we use the large version of the pre-trained RoBERTa model, a further-optimized BERT model, that has approximately 340M parameters (Liu et al., 2019) . We fine-tune the pre-trained embeddings of the RoBERTa language model using labeled data for DDI classification, and we call the resulting model RoBERTa-DDI.

metadata.title: SUPP.AI: Finding Evidence for Supplement-Drug Interactions

text: We developed a drug-drug interaction (DDI) detection model designed to classify whether a given candidate input sentence provides evidence of a functional interaction between two identified drug entities. This DDI detection framework leverages pre-trained Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT; Devlin et al., 2018) architectures to encode input text sequences. BERT-derived models are well-documented to exhibit strong cross-domain transfer performance, and can attain high task accuracy even when fine-tuned on limited volumes of task-specific annotated training data. For this implementation, we specifically use the large variant of the pre-trained RoBERTa model, an optimized iteration of the core BERT architecture with approximately 340 million trainable parameters (Liu et al., 2019). We fine-tune the pre-trained contextual embeddings of this base RoBERTa language model using labeled DDI classification training data, and designate the resulting fine-tuned system as RoBERTa-DDI.

I attempted to accompany them and proceeded a short distance from the house, but my head whirled round, my steps were like those of a drunken man, I fell at last in a state of utter exhaustion; a film covered my eyes, and my skin was parched with the heat of fever. In this state I was carried back and placed on a bed, hardly conscious of what had happened; my eyes wandered round the room as if to seek something that I had lost.

I tried to follow after them, moving no more than a few paces beyond the door, but the world began to spin, my legs stumbled as though I were drunk, and I collapsed at length in complete fatigue. A mist dimmed my sight, and my flesh burned dry with fever’s heat. They bore me inside and laid me upon a bed, while I barely grasped what had occurred; my gaze drifted across the chamber, searching for something I had misplaced.

A chance turn of events one day drew her into a strangely intimate conversation with Gabriel about her predicament. It brought her a measure of relief—though of a flat and comfortless sort. They were going over the accounts together, and something in the course of their work prompted Oak to remark, with reference to Boldwood, “He’ll never forget you, ma’am, never.”

Another approach to text editing is to view the problem as sequential tagging instead of encoderdecoder. In this way, the efficiency of learning and prediction can be significantly enhanced. Vu and Haffari (2018) and conduct automatic post-editing and text simplification on the basis of edit operations and employ Neural Programmer-Interpreter (Reed and De Freitas, 2016) to predict the sequence of edits given a sequence of words, where the edits include KEEP, DROP, and ADD. Malmi et al. (2019) propose a sequential tagging model that assigns a tag (KEEP or DELETE) to each word in the input sequence and also decides whether to add a phrase before the word. Our proposed approach is also based on sequential tagging of actions. It is designed for fact-based text editing, not text-to-text generation, however. Table- to-text generation is the task which aims to generate a text from structured data (Reiter and Dale, 2000; Gatt and Krahmer, 2018) , for example, a text from an infobox about a term in biology in wikipedia (Lebret et al., 2016 ) and a description of restaurant from a structured representation (Novikova et al., 2017) . Encoder-decoder models can also be employed in table-to-text generation with structured data as input and generated text as output, for example, as in (Lebret et al., 2016) . Puduppully et al. (2019) and Iso et al. (2019) propose utilizing an entity tracking module for document-level table-to-text generation.

metadata.title: Fact-based Text Editing

text: Another perspective on text editing frames the task as sequential tagging rather than as an encoder–decoder problem, which can markedly improve both learning efficiency and prediction speed. Vu and Haffari (2018) perform automatic post-editing and text simplification by modeling edit operations, using the Neural Programmer-Interpreter (Reed and De Freitas, 2016) to predict a sequence of edits—KEEP, DROP, and ADD—conditioned on a sequence of words. Malmi et al. (2019) introduce a sequential tagging model that assigns each word in the input a tag (KEEP or DELETE) and simultaneously determines whether a phrase should be inserted before that word. Our proposed method likewise relies on the sequential tagging of actions, but it is designed for fact-based text editing rather than for general text-to-text generation. Table-to-text generation is the task of producing natural language from structured data (Reiter and Dale, 2000; Gatt and Krahmer, 2018), such as generating a description from a Wikipedia infobox about a biological term (Lebret et al., 2016) or a restaurant description from a structured representation (Novikova et al., 2017). Encoder–decoder architectures can also be applied to table-to-text generation, taking structured data as input and outputting generated text, as demonstrated by Lebret et al. (2016). Puduppully et al. (2019) and Iso et al. (2019) propose incorporating an entity tracking module for document-level table-to-text generation.

“If we ever get back to our native land,” said Miss Pross, “you may rely upon my telling Mrs. Cruncher as much as I may be able to remember and understand of what you have so impressively said; and at all events you may be sure that I shall bear witness to your being thoroughly in earnest at this dreadful time. Now, pray let us think! My esteemed Mr. Cruncher, let us think!”

“If we ever make it back to our own country,” said Miss Pross, “you can count on me to tell Mrs. Cruncher as much as I can remember and make sense of from what you have said so impressively; and at the very least, you may be sure I shall testify that you are entirely sincere in this dreadful hour. Now, please, let us think! My respected Mr. Cruncher, let us think!”

My cosmopolite was named E. Rushmore Coglan, and he will be heard from next summer at Coney Island. He is to establish a new “attraction” there, he informed me, offering kingly diversion. And then his conversation rang along parallels of latitude and longitude. He took the great, round world in his hand, so to speak, familiarly, contemptuously, and it seemed no larger than the seed of a Maraschino cherry in a _table d’hôte_ grape fruit. He spoke disrespectfully of the equator, he skipped from continent to continent, he derided the zones, he mopped up the high seas with his napkin. With a wave of his hand he would speak of a certain bazaar in Hyderabad. Whiff! He would have you on skis in Lapland. Zip! Now you rode the breakers with the Kanakas at Kealaikahiki. Presto! He dragged you through an Arkansas post-oak swamp, let you dry for a moment on the alkali plains of his Idaho ranch, then whirled you into the society of Viennese archdukes. Anon he would be telling you of a cold he acquired in a Chicago lake breeze and how old Escamila cured it in Buenos Ayres with a hot infusion of the _chuchula_ weed. You would have addressed a letter to “E. Rushmore Coglan, Esq., the Earth, Solar System, the Universe,” and have mailed it, feeling confident that it would be delivered to him.

My self-proclaimed citizen of the world went by K. Thorne Hartwell, and you’d hear his name everywhere come next summer on the Atlantic City boardwalk. He was set to launch a new “spectacle” there, he told me, offering entertainment fit for royalty. From there, his conversation bounded across every parallel and meridian on the globe. He held the whole round world in his palm, so to speak, casual and dismissive, and it seemed no bigger than the pit of a maraschino cherry tucked inside a prix-fixe ruby grapefruit. He scoffed at the equator, hopped from continent to continent like they were cobblestones in a lane, jeered at the temperate and tropical zones, blotted at the high seas with the edge of his linen napkin. With a flick of his wrist he’d mention a hidden spice bazaar in Ahmedabad. Whoosh! Next you were carving cross-country skis through Arctic Norway’s snow drifts. Snap! You were riding frothy breakers with native Hawaiian surfers at Honolua Bay. Quick as a wink, he dragged you through a tupelo swamp in southern Mississippi, let you dry off for a minute on the wind-scoured alkali flats of his Wyoming ranch, then spun you straight into the drawing rooms of Austrian countesses. A moment later he’d be recounting the hacking cold he caught off a Lake Michigan gust in Milwaukee, and how old Doña Marisol had cured it in Lima with a steaming steep of the rare quechua herb. You could have addressed an envelope to “K. Thorne Hartwell, Esq., The Earth, Solar System, The Entire Universe,” dropped it in any post box, and felt fully confident it would be delivered straight to him.

After an intensive post-processing step (cf. Appendix A.2), this process results in a dataset of 13,667 sentences extracted from half the Common-Crawl corpus. 7 To evaluate the quality of the resulting data set, we randomly picked 3 times 100 sentences, and genuine NArabizi sentences were manually identified, which allowed us to assess the accuracy of our corpus as reaching 97%. Table 2 presents the results of the evaluation of the two classification methods performed on both the development and test sets of the original NArabizi corpus. 8 Results show that the fastText classifier and its n-gram features is more precise than its nonneural counterpart and its language-id feature vectors.

metadata.title: Building a User-Generated Content North-African Arabizi Treebank: Tackling Hell

text: Following a rigorous post-processing stage (detailed in Appendix A.2), we produced a dataset containing 13,667 sentences harvested from 50% of the Common-Crawl corpus. To verify the quality of the resulting data, we manually annotated three random samples of 100 sentences each; the identification of authentic NArabizi instances confirmed a corpus accuracy of 97%. The evaluation results for the two classification paradigms, conducted on the development and test partitions of the baseline NArabizi corpus, are detailed in Table 2. Empirical results indicate that the fastText architecture, utilizing n-gram features, achieves greater precision than the non-neural model relying on language-identification feature vectors.

“And yet in reason it should clearly be the other way; it should be five hundred times as easy to foretell the last as the first, for, indeed, it is so close by that one uninspired might almost see it. In truth, the law of prophecy doth contradict the likelihoods, most strangely making the difficult easy, and the easy difficult.”

By every rule of intellect, the opposite should surely hold; it ought to be a thousandfold more effortless to envision the finish than the start, since the former looms so near that a man without vision might almost perceive it. Indeed, the art of augury upends all reason, curiously casting the obvious into shadow while illuminating the impossible.

Open-domain dialog system evaluation is one of the most difficult challenges in the dialog community. Open-domain chatbots have a user-centric goal: to provide human with enjoyable user experience. However, user experience is difficult to quantify due to bias and variance among different users. Previous research has optimized on automatic dialog evaluation metrics such as BLUE (Papineni et al., 2002) , which measures the difference between the generated responses and the reference responses. Due to the contrast between the one-tomany nature of open-domain conversations and the limited number of available references, such met-rics correlate poorly with human judgments (Liu et al., 2016; Lowe et al., 2017; Novikova et al., 2017) . Designing a fully automatic dialog evaluation metric is still an open research problem.

metadata.title: Beyond User Self-Reported Likert Scale Ratings: A Comparison Model for Automatic Dialog Evaluation

text: Evaluating open-domain dialog systems remains one of the most challenging problems in the dialog research community. Open-domain chatbots are designed with a user-centered objective: to provide humans with an enjoyable user experience. However, such experience is inherently difficult to measure because it varies across users and is subject to both bias and variance. Prior work has therefore relied on automatic dialog evaluation metrics such as BLUE (Papineni et al., 2002), which assess the discrepancy between generated responses and reference responses. Yet, because open-domain conversations are one-to-many in nature and only a limited number of references are available, these metrics exhibit weak correlation with human judgments (Liu et al., 2016; Lowe et al., 2017; Novikova et al., 2017). As a result, developing a fully automatic metric for dialog evaluation remains an open research problem.

“Come, dearest Victor; you alone can console Elizabeth. She weeps continually, and accuses herself unjustly as the cause of his death; her words pierce my heart. We are all unhappy; but will not that be an additional motive for you, my son, to return and be our comforter? Your dear mother! Alas, Victor! I now say, Thank God she did not live to witness the cruel, miserable death of her youngest darling!

“Victor, my heart, please come. Only you can soothe Elizabeth now. She cries without end, blaming herself without reason for his passing, and each word she speaks cuts me to the soul. Sorrow grips us all—yet should that not move you, my son, to come back and be the balm for our wounds? Your sweet mother! Oh, Victor, I can only murmur, Thank Heaven she was spared the sight of her youngest beloved’s cruel, wretched end.”

Our model is inspired by the biaffine dependency parser of Dozat and Manning (2016) , but stands in contrast in many aspects. They use a bidirectional LSTM to encode the sequential information of the tokens, and the biaffine attention itself does not model the sequence. Each cell s i,j in their output matrix S is interpreted as the score of a dependency arc (i, j). They use a Maximal Spanning Tree algorithm to obtain a tree that maximizes the total score of the arcs in the tree.

metadata.title: Fast and Accurate Non-Projective Dependency Tree Linearization

text: The design of our model takes inspiration from the biaffine dependency parser introduced by Dozat and Manning (2016), but differs substantially from this existing work across numerous key characteristics. Their implementation leverages a bidirectional LSTM to encode the sequential information associated with input tokens, while the biaffine attention mechanism itself does not model sequential structure. Each cell \(s_{i,j}\) in their model’s output matrix S corresponds to the assigned score for the directed dependency arc (i, j). To extract the final parse structure, they utilize a Maximal Spanning Tree algorithm to generate a tree that maximizes the total score of all arcs included in the resulting tree.

Eustacia started up. This had been a well-known signal in old times when Wildeve had used to come secretly wooing to Mistover. She at once knew that Wildeve was outside, but before she could consider what to do her husband came in from upstairs. Eustacia’s face burnt crimson at the unexpected collision of incidents, and filled it with an animation that it too frequently lacked.

Eustacia jolted upright. That was the exact signal she’d known by heart years earlier, back when Wildeve used to sneak to Mistover to court her in secret. She realized right away that Wildeve was waiting just outside, but before she could even work out what to do next, her husband came down the stairs and into the room. Eustacia’s face burned bright crimson at this unplanned, jarring collision of circumstances, the flush gifting her features a sharp liveliness that far too rarely graced them.

I stand at noon upon the peak of Heaven,
Then with unwilling steps I wander down
Into the clouds of the Atlantic even;
For grief that I depart they weep and frown:
What look is more delightful than the smile
With which I soothe them from the western isle?

metadata.title: The Complete Poetical Works of Percy Bysshe Shelley — Complete

text:
I pause at midday on the crown of the sky,  
Then, step by heavy step, descend into  
The Atlantic’s dusk, a slowly darkening sigh;  
Because I leave, the clouds in somber hue  
Grieve and gather—yet no gaze could be as mild  
As mine that calms them from the farthest western isle.

Bathsheba knew more of him now; he had entirely bared his heart before her, even until he had almost worn in her eyes the sorry look of a grand bird without the feathers that make it grand. She had been awe-struck at her past temerity, and was struggling to make amends without thinking whether the sin quite deserved the penalty she was schooling herself to pay. To have brought all this about her ears was terrible; but after a while the situation was not without a fearful joy. The facility with which even the most timid women sometimes acquire a relish for the dreadful when that is amalgamated with a little triumph, is marvellous.

Bathsheba understood him far better these days; he’d laid his whole heart bare to her, to the point that he struck her as looking almost pitiful, like a majestic bird stripped of the plumage that made it so imposing in the first place. She’d been stunned to realize how recklessly bold she’d been back then, and was fumbling to make things right, even though she hadn’t stopped to consider if her mistake actually warranted the heavy penance she was forcing herself to carry out. Having kicked up this whole mess that now crashed down around her felt awful at first; but as time passed, she couldn’t deny the quiet, giddy thrill tangled up in the terror of it all. It’s astonishing how easily even the most shy, cautious women can learn to savor something awful, just as long as there’s a small spark of victory mixed in with the chaos.

“That won’t work, Father—combining approaches. It wouldn’t be proper professional conduct either. Two in the same line can’t undercut one another. We’d end up slashing prices anyway; that’s where it would finally lead. Merlin holds the contract, and no other magician can lay a hand on it until he lets it go.”

In Bathsheba’s heated fancy the innocent white countenance expressed a dim triumphant consciousness of the pain she was retaliating for her pain with all the merciless rigour of the Mosaic law: “Burning for burning; wound for wound: strife for strife.”

Eliza’s features were encircled by those pale, flaxen tresses, and the provenance of the ringlet Arthur had guarded so closely was now beyond any dispute. To Isabel’s fevered mind, the blameless, ivory countenance appeared to glow with a faint, mocking triumph, as if the girl were paying back her own suffering with the pitiless precision of the ancient scriptures: “Fire for fire; bruise for bruise; stripe for stripe.”

To-day there were more changes on the bill of fare than usual. The soups were lighter; pork was eliminated from the entrées, figuring only with Russian turnips among the roasts. The gracious spirit of spring pervaded the entire menu. Lamb, that lately capered on the greening hillsides, was becoming exploited with the sauce that commemorated its gambols. The song of the oyster, though not silenced, was _dimuendo con amore_. The frying-pan seemed to be held, inactive, behind the beneficent bars of the broiler. The pie list swelled; the richer puddings had vanished; the sausage, with his drapery wrapped about him, barely lingered in a pleasant thanatopsis with the buckwheats and the sweet but doomed maple.

Today the menu had shifted more than usual. The soups were thinner; pork had vanished from the entrées, appearing only beside Russian turnips among the roasts. A gentle breath of spring ran through every dish. Lamb, which not long ago had frolicked on the greening slopes, was now being dressed in a sauce that recalled its playful leaps. The oyster’s voice, though not yet hushed, was fading in a tender decrescendo. The frying-pan seemed to rest idly behind the kindly shelter of the broiler. The list of pies grew longer; the heavier puddings had disappeared; the sausage, wrapped snugly in its own covering, lingered faintly in a quiet meditation on mortality alongside the buckwheat cakes and the sweet, soon-to-be-sacrificed maple.
