Whereupon Mr. Grimsby flushed a vivid scarlet; for he was a man of reputed shrewdness who had lately gone bankrupt—to the profound shock of the neighborhood, as there appeared no logical pretext for his ruin—and had succeeded in furnishing the populace with much laughter and his lenders with precious little comfort, by settling his accounts at a rate of less than two cents to the dollar.

Bathsheba went home, her mind occupied with a new trouble, which being rather harassing than deadly was calculated to do good by diverting her from the chronic gloom of her life. She was set thinking a great deal about Oak and of his wish to shun her; and there occurred to Bathsheba several incidents of her latter intercourse with him, which, trivial when singly viewed, amounted together to a perceptible disinclination for her society. It broke upon her at length as a great pain that her last old disciple was about to forsake her and flee. He who had believed in her and argued on her side when all the rest of the world was against her, had at last like the others become weary and neglectful of the old cause, and was leaving her to fight her battles alone.

Bathsheba headed home, her thoughts caught up in a fresh anxiety. Since this trouble was more an irritation than a tragedy, it proved beneficial by drawing her away from the persistent melancholy of her existence. She pondered Oak’s behavior and his apparent wish to avoid her, recalling various recent interactions. Though these moments seemed minor individually, they collectively suggested a clear reluctance to be near her. Eventually, it hit her with a sharp sense of grief that her final loyal follower was about to desert her. The man who had trusted her and defended her when the rest of the world turned away had finally, like everyone else, grown tired of the old loyalty, leaving her to face her struggles entirely alone.

And so Oliver remained all that day, and for the greater part of many subsequent days, seeing nobody, between early morning and midnight, and left during the long hours to commune with his own thoughts. Which, never failing to revert to his kind friends, and the opinion they must long ago have formed of him, were sad indeed.

And so Oliver remained there all that first day, and for the greater part of many days that came after, seeing not a single soul between the break of dawn and the deep of midnight, left to occupy the long, empty hours in conversation with his own thoughts. Those thoughts never strayed long from his kind old friends, and the poor opinion they must surely have formed of him by now, which made them bitter with sadness indeed.

“And another thing,” he swept on. “You love me. But why do you love me? The thing in me that compels me to write is the very thing that draws your love. You love me because I am somehow different from the men you have known and might have loved. I was not made for the desk and counting-house, for petty business squabbling, and legal jangling. Make me do such things, make me like those other men, doing the work they do, breathing the air they breathe, developing the point of view they have developed, and you have destroyed the difference, destroyed me, destroyed the thing you love. My desire to write is the most vital thing in me. Had I been a mere clod, neither would I have desired to write, nor would you have desired me for a husband.”

“And there is another thing,” he went on. “You love me. But why do you love me? The very thing in me that drives me to write is the same thing that wins your love. You love me because I am in some way unlike the men you have known and might have loved. I was not made for the desk and the counting-house, for small business wrangles and legal bickerings. Force me to do such things, force me to become like those other men, to do the work they do, breathe the air they breathe, and acquire the point of view they have acquired, and you destroy the difference, destroy me, destroy the thing you love. My urge to write is the most living thing in me. Had I been a mere lump of clay, I should never have wanted to write, nor would you have wanted me for a husband.”

He had no other opportunity: for the Jew remained very surly and silent till night: when he prepared to go abroad.

Stopped at last! A clever blow. He is down upon the pavement; and the crowd eagerly gather round him: each new comer, jostling and struggling with the others to catch a glimpse. “Stand aside!” “Give him a little air!” “Nonsense! he don’t deserve it.” “Where’s the gentleman?” “Here he is, coming down the street.” “Make room there for the gentleman!” “Is this the boy, sir!” “Yes.”

Stopped at last! That was a deft, well-timed blow. He falls hard onto the pavement, and the crowd surges eagerly around him, each new arrival jostling and struggling past the others to catch a glimpse. “Stand aside!” “Give him a little air!” “Nonsense! He doesn’t deserve it.” “Where’s the gentleman?” “There he is, coming down the street now.” “Make room there for the gentleman!” “Is this the boy, sir?” “Yes.”

Having at this moment reached the end of a wistful gaze into mid-air, Henry did not refuse. He was a man of more than middle age, with eyebrows high up in his forehead, who laid it down that the law of the world was bad, with a long-suffering look through his listeners at the world alluded to, as it presented itself to his imagination. He always signed his name “Henery”—strenuously insisting upon that spelling, and if any passing schoolmaster ventured to remark that the second “e” was superfluous and old-fashioned, he received the reply that “H-e-n-e-r-y” was the name he was christened and the name he would stick to—in the tone of one to whom orthographical differences were matters which had a great deal to do with personal character.

Right as he pulled himself out of a soft, wistful stare into empty space, Henry didn’t turn the offer down. He was well past middle age, with eyebrows set high on his forehead, and he held firm to the belief that the entire governing order of the world was rotten. When he spoke on the subject, he’d fix a weary, long-suffering gaze right through whoever he was talking to, as if he was staring directly at the flawed world he described, the version of it that played out so clearly in his imagination. He always signed his name “Henery,” and would strenuously defend that spelling whenever it was questioned. If some passing schoolteacher dared to remark that the second “e” was unnecessary and hopelessly old-fashioned, he’d retort that “H-e-n-e-r-y” was the name he’d been given at his christening, and the only one he’d ever answer to. He spoke in the tone of someone who considered differences in spelling to be matters deeply tied to a person’s core character.

“It must be a piercing thing, if it can work its way through your heart,” said Mr. Sikes. “Give him something to drink, Nancy. Burn my body, be quick! It’s enough to make a man sick, seeing his thin old carcass trembling like that, like some ugly ghost just dragged up from the grave.”

He fled to his stateroom, where he lurked until the steamer was clear of the dock. In the dining saloon, at luncheon, he found himself in the place of honor, at the captain’s right; and he was not long in discovering that he was the great man on board. But no more unsatisfactory great man ever sailed on a ship. He spent the afternoon in a deck-chair, with closed eyes, dozing brokenly most of the time, and in the evening went early to bed.

He retreated to his cabin and stayed hidden there until the steamer had pulled away from the pier. When he entered the dining saloon for lunch, he was shown to the seat of honor, directly at the captain’s right hand, and it didn’t take him long to realize he was regarded as the most important person aboard. Yet no more discontented celebrity had ever traveled on that vessel. He passed the afternoon slumped in a deck-chair, eyes shut, slipping in and out of a fitful doze, and retired to his berth early that evening.

Well, I liked the king, and as king I respected him--respected the office; at least respected it as much as I was capable of respecting any unearned supremacy; but as MEN I looked down upon him and his nobles--privately. And he and they liked me, and respected my office; but as an animal, without birth or sham title, they looked down upon me--and were not particularly private about it, either. I didn't charge for my opinion about them, and they didn't charge for their opinion about me: the account was square, the books balanced, everybody was satisfied.

I was fond of the king, and honored him in his royal role—respected the crown, at least as much as I could ever bring myself to respect any unearned authority. But as ordinary men? I thought little of him and his nobles, and kept that judgment strictly to myself. For their part, they liked me well enough, and respected the post I held. But as a man of no noble birth or counterfeit title, they looked down on me openly, with no pretense of secrecy. I never hid what I thought of them, and they never hid what they thought of me: the scales were even, the accounts tallied perfectly, and no one had cause to complain.

“Oh, miss—I’m almost embarrassed to say it—he’s a real playboy! But I know him to be sharp and refined, the sort who could have made a fortune and lived like a gentleman. He is such a clever, stylish young man! He is a doctor’s son by name, which is quite a lot; but in his very nature, he is the son of an earl!”

A miserable journey. A desolate silence everywhere. Even in London itself. Traffic had ceased; men did not talk or laugh, or go in groups, or even in couples; they moved aimlessly about, each man by himself, with his head down, and woe and terror at his heart. The Tower showed recent war-scars. Verily, much had been happening.

A sodden, gut-wrenching trek. A desolate hush hung over every stretch of ground, and it seeped even into the heart of Veyra, the capital that had once thrummed with noise at all hours. All wheeled traffic had ground to a halt; no one laughed or called out greetings, or clustered in groups, or even walked two abreast. They drifted listlessly through the streets, each person entirely alone, gaze fixed on the dirt at their feet, grief and cold dread coiled tight in their chests. The city’s ancient Grey Spire bore fresh shrapnel gouges and smoke streaks across its weathered stone face. Truly, unthinkable upheaval had unfolded in the days past.

Although the current state-of-the-art method for abstractive summarization could only achieve a ROUGE-1 score of less than 40 on the Gigaword dataset, generated headlines actually look very fluent. This is probably because the encoder-decoder model acquired a strong language model from the vast amount of supervision data. However, some studies reported that the generated headlines often deviate from the content of the original document (Cao et al., 2018b; Kryściński et al., 2019) . They addressed the problem where an abstractive model made mistakes in facts (e.g., tuples of subjects, predicates, and objects).

metadata.title: Improving Truthfulness of Headline Generation

text: While current state-of-the-art abstractive summarization models yield ROUGE-1 scores below 40 on the Gigaword benchmark, the resulting headlines exhibit a high degree of linguistic fluency. This quality is likely a consequence of the encoder-decoder architecture internalizing a robust language model from extensive supervised training data. Nevertheless, several studies have observed that these generated headlines frequently diverge from the source document's underlying content (Cao et al., 2018b; Kryściński et al., 2019). These works specifically highlight instances where abstractive models introduce factual inconsistencies, such as errors involving subject-predicate-object tuples.

Nancy was busily engaged in preparing breakfast. It was not yet daylight; for the candle was still burning, and it was quite dark outside. A sharp rain, too, was beating against the window-panes; and the sky looked black and cloudy.

Nancy was busily occupied in getting breakfast ready. It was not yet daylight; for the candle still burned, and all was dark without. A hard rain, too, was striking the window-panes; and the sky looked black and overcast.

“Justine, you may remember, was a great favourite of yours; and I recollect you once remarked that if you were in an ill humour, one glance from Justine could dissipate it, for the same reason that Ariosto gives concerning the beauty of Angelica—she looked so frank-hearted and happy. My aunt conceived a great attachment for her, by which she was induced to give her an education superior to that which she had at first intended. This benefit was fully repaid; Justine was the most grateful little creature in the world: I do not mean that she made any professions I never heard one pass her lips, but you could see by her eyes that she almost adored her protectress. Although her disposition was gay and in many respects inconsiderate, yet she paid the greatest attention to every gesture of my aunt. She thought her the model of all excellence and endeavoured to imitate her phraseology and manners, so that even now she often reminds me of her.

You surely recall Elara, who was always a particular favorite of yours; I remember your saying that if ever you fell into a melancholy, a single glimpse of her would suffice to dispel it. She possessed that quality of candid cheerfulness which the poets often ascribe to their most radiant muses. My mother became so deeply fond of her that she saw fit to provide the girl with an education far more sophisticated than was first planned. This generosity was met with the quietest, most sincere devotion; Elara never resorted to empty protestations of thanks, yet one could read a world of adoration in her eyes. Though her spirit was naturally buoyant and sometimes heedless, she remained keenly observant of my mother’s every nuance. Viewing her as the absolute standard of grace, she strove to adopt her very way of speaking and carrying herself, so that even now, Elara’s movements frequently bring my mother back to my mind.

“Those were his words,” said Nancy, glancing uneasily round, as she had scarcely ceased to do, since she began to speak, for a vision of Sikes haunted her perpetually. “And more. When he spoke of you and the other lady, and said it seemed contrived by Heaven, or the devil, against him, that Oliver should come into your hands, he laughed, and said there was some comfort in that too, for how many thousands and hundreds of thousands of pounds would you not give, if you had them, to know who your two-legged spaniel was.”

“That’s what he said,” Nancy replied, her gaze flicking nervously about the room—a habit she’d barely broken since she started talking, for Sikes’s specter never left her. “And worse. When he mentioned you and the other woman, claiming it felt like Heaven itself, or the devil, had schemed against him by putting Oliver in your path, he let out a laugh and said even that brought a kind of satisfaction, because how many thousands and hundreds of thousands of pounds wouldn’t you hand over, if you could, just to learn who your two-legged lapdog really was.”

I was fast getting adjusted to my situation and circumstances. For a time, I used to wake up, mornings, and smile at my “dream,” and listen for the Colt's factory whistle; but that sort of thing played itself out, gradually, and at last I was fully able to realize that I was actually living in the sixth century, and in Arthur's court, not a lunatic asylum. After that, I was just as much at home in that century as I could have been in any other; and as for preference, I wouldn't have traded it for the twentieth. Look at the opportunities here for a man of knowledge, brains, pluck, and enterprise to sail in and grow up with the country. The grandest field that ever was; and all my own; not a competitor; not a man who wasn't a baby to me in acquirements and capacities; whereas, what would I amount to in the twentieth century? I should be foreman of a factory, that is about all; and could drag a seine down street any day and catch a hundred better men than myself.

I was swiftly growing accustomed to my new state and surroundings. For a while, I’d rise at dawn, grinning at what I took for a “vision,” and strain to hear the factory horn from Colt’s; but that phase faded little by little, until at length I could fully grasp that I dwelt truly in the sixth century, in King Arthur’s realm, and not in some madhouse. Once that settled in, I felt as native to that age as I could to any other; and if it came to choosing, I’d not have swapped it for the twentieth. Consider the prospects here for a fellow of learning, wit, nerve, and ambition to plunge in and rise alongside the land itself. The noblest arena ever conceived; and mine alone; no rival; not a soul who wasn’t an infant next to me in knowledge and gifts; whereas, what would I count for in the twentieth century? A factory foreman, more or less; and I could haul a net down any street and land a hundred men better than me.

We further study the ability of expert human raters to perform the same task. We find that rater accuracy varies wildly, but has a median of 74%, which is less than the accuracy of our bestperforming discriminator. Most interestingly, we find that human raters and discriminators make decisions based on different qualities, with humans more easily noticing semantic errors and discriminators picking up on statistical artifacts. In our experiments, these artifacts are most prominent with top-k sampling. However, any strategy that oversamples high-likelihood words is susceptible. As the p in nucleus sampling is set increasingly lower to achieve more fluent text (some systems are already using p as low as 0.5 (Miculicich et al., 2019) ), the distributional deviations that plague top-k text will surface in nucleus sampling as well. Holtzman et al. (2020) explain how a unique attribute of human language is that it dips in and out of low probability zones. This variance in likelihood is what makes human-written text interesting and exciting to read. Today's generation systems have not yet solved the problem of mimicking the human cadence without introducing poor word choices that are easy for humans to detect. Generation systems often optimize for fooling humans without acknowledging the trade-off that exists between human perception of quality and ease of automatic detection. We therefore suggest three prongs for future research:

metadata.title: Automatic Detection of Generated Text is Easiest when Humans are Fooled

“‘You see, gentlemen,’ said I, with my hands still resting upon her breast, ‘how ill I serve you in the condition to which you have brought me! Had I foreseen what sight awaited me, I might have come properly prepared. As it is, we shall lose precious time. In this desolate place, there are no medicines to be had.’

We concluded to make a tour between the inner fences. We elected to walk upright, for convenience's sake; we argued that if discerned, we should be taken for friends rather than enemies, and in any case we should be out of reach of swords, and these gentry did not seem to have any spears along. Well, it was a curious trip. Everywhere dead men were lying outside the second fence--not plainly visible, but still visible; and we counted fifteen of those pathetic statues--dead knights standing with their hands on the upper wire.

We resolved to venture along the path between the inner barriers. We chose to move erect, for ease of motion; we reasoned that if spotted, we would be mistaken for allies rather than foes, and besides, we would remain beyond the sweep of blades, and these fellows appeared to carry no lances with them. It was, I must say, a strange journey. On every side fallen men lay scattered beyond the second paling—not starkly exposed, yet still discernible; and we tallied fifteen of those sorrowful figures—slain soldiers upright with their fingers clutching the topmost strand.

But come--never mind about that; let's--have you got such a thing as a map of that region about you? Now a good map--”

But come on—let that go; tell me—do you happen to have a map of that area with you? Now a decent map—”

Then there was Ruth. She had liked him for himself, that was indisputable. And yet, much as she had liked him she had liked the bourgeois standard of valuation more. She had opposed his writing, and principally, it seemed to him, because it did not earn money. That had been her criticism of his “Love-cycle.” She, too, had urged him to get a job. It was true, she refined it to “position,” but it meant the same thing, and in his own mind the old nomenclature stuck. He had read her all that he wrote—poems, stories, essays—“Wiki-Wiki,” “The Shame of the Sun,” everything. And she had always and consistently urged him to get a job, to go to work—good God!—as if he hadn’t been working, robbing sleep, exhausting life, in order to be worthy of her.

Then there was Ruth. She had liked him for himself, that was beyond question. And yet, for all her liking of him, she had valued the bourgeois scale of judgment more highly. She had been against his writing, and chiefly, it seemed to him, because it brought in no money. That had been her objection to his “Love-cycle.” She, too, had pressed him to get a job. Until he can with joy rest on our Father’s knee;
Then I will rise and smooth his silver hair,
And be like him, and then he’ll love me.

Mr. Lawrence Carmine, who had dropped in, was suddenly reminded of that lugubrious old ballad, "The Mistletoe Bough," and recited large worn fragments of it impressively. It tells of how a beautiful girl hid away in a chest during a Christmas game of hide-and-seek, and how she was found, a dried vestige, years afterwards. It took a very powerful hold upon Herr Heinrich's imagination. "Let us now," he said, "make an examination of every box and cupboard and drawer. Marking each as we go...."

Mr. Lawrence Carmine, who had stopped by unannounced, found himself abruptly recalling the mournful old song “The Mistletoe Bough,” and began to intone its frayed, lingering verses with somber gravity. The ballad relates how a lovely young woman concealed herself in an oaken chest during a Christmas game of hide-and-seek, only to be discovered years later as a withered relic. The tale seized Herr Heinrich’s fancy with extraordinary force. “Come,” he said, “let us now inspect every trunk, every closet, every drawer. And mark each one as we proceed….”

I am sent by the rich gentleman at the Woman, that we used to call the landlord, to tell ’ee that Mrs. Wildeve is doing well of a girl, which was born punctually at one o’clock at noon, or a few minutes more or less; and ’tis said that expecting of this increase is what have kept ’em there since they came into their money.”

“Aye, the dry spell holds, and a mercy it is. But I’ve come to bring word of a matter quite removed from our recent family trials. I was dispatched by the monied master of the Golden Lion—him we used to call the publican—to inform you that Mrs. Vane is doing bravely with a new daughter, born just as the clock struck one this afternoon, or thereabouts; and the talk is that waiting for this little soul is what has anchored them to the village ever since they came into their great fortune.”

Where had they fled when the next morning I awoke? All of soul-inspiriting fled with sleep, and dark melancholy clouded every thought. The rain was pouring in torrents, and thick mists hid the summits of the mountains, so that I even saw not the faces of those mighty friends. Still I would penetrate their misty veil and seek them in their cloudy retreats. What were rain and storm to me? My mule was brought to the door, and I resolved to ascend to the summit of Montanvert. I remembered the effect that the view of the tremendous and ever-moving glacier had produced upon my mind when I first saw it. It had then filled me with a sublime ecstasy that gave wings to the soul and allowed it to soar from the obscure world to light and joy. The sight of the awful and majestic in nature had indeed always the effect of solemnising my mind and causing me to forget the passing cares of life. I determined to go without a guide, for I was well acquainted with the path, and the presence of another would destroy the solitary grandeur of the scene.

Where had they gone when I woke the next morning? With sleep, everything that had lifted my spirits was gone, and a heavy gloom settled over every thought. The rain was coming down in sheets, and dense mist concealed the mountain peaks, so that I could not even see the faces of those grand companions. Yet I still longed to push through that veil of cloud and find them in their hidden retreats. What were rain and storm to me? My mule was brought to the door, and I decided to ride up to the summit of Montanvert. I recalled the effect the sight of the vast, ever-changing glacier had once had on me when I first beheld it. It had filled me then with a sublime rapture, one that seemed to give the soul wings and lift it out of the dim world into light and joy. The vision of nature’s terrible and magnificent works had always, in fact, solemnized my mind and made me forget life’s fleeting troubles. I resolved to go without a guide, for I knew the path well, and another person’s presence would spoil the lonely grandeur of the scene.

“But that’s not it, exactly. If a man changes his name and so forth, and takes steps to deceive the world and his own wife, he’s a cheat, and that in the eye of the law is ayless a rogue, and that is ayless a lammocken vagabond; and that’s a punishable situation.”

“But it isn’t quite that, exactly. If a man alters his name and the rest of it, and contrives to deceive the world and even his own wife, he’s a fraud, and in the eyes of the law a sheer rogue, and by that reckoning a downright truant vagabond; and that is a matter fit to be punished.”

Darkling I listen; and, for many a time
I have been half in love with easeful Death,
Call'd him soft names in many a mused rhyme,
To take into the air my quiet breath;
Now more than ever seems it rich to die,
To cease upon the midnight with no pain,
While thou art pouring forth thy soul abroad
In such an ecstasy!
Still wouldst thou sing, and I have ears in vain--
To thy high requiem become a sod.

metadata.title: Keats: Poems Published in 1820

text:
In twilight hush I wait; and often before  
I've nearly adored calm, unhurried Death,  
Coaxed him with tender words in verse I bore,  
To draw my silent breathing from its depth;  
Now death feels richer than it ever could,  
To end at midnight free of any ache,  
While you release your spirit to the wood  
In rapturous break!  
You'd still sing on, though I hear not a strain—  
Turned to mere earth beneath your high refrain.

For AT, the pseudo sentence generation step and NMT training step are interleaved. Take the target language view AT shown in Figure 1 (a) for example, we extract anchored source sentences as one batch, and decode them into pseudo target sentences; then we use the same batch to train the NMT model of target-to-anchored source. In the meantime, a batch of target sentences are decoded into pseudo anchored source sentences, and then we use the same batch to train the NMT model of anchored source-to-target. The above process repeats until AT converges.

metadata.title: Bilingual Dictionary Based Neural Machine Translation without Using Parallel Sentences

text: For adversarial training (AT), the pseudo-sentence generation step and neural machine translation (NMT) training step are executed in an interleaved manner. As a concrete illustration, consider the target-language perspective AT pipeline depicted in Figure 1(a): we first sample a batch of anchored source sentences, decode these inputs to generate corresponding pseudo target sentences, and then leverage the identical batch to optimize the target-to-anchored-source NMT model. Concurrently, a separate batch of target sentences is decoded to produce pseudo anchored source sentences, after which we use this same batch to train the anchored-source-to-target NMT model. This paired two-way update process is repeated iteratively until the AT workflow reaches convergence.

The possible reason is that our model utilizing GSL shifts the higher attention from the word "Now" with Temporal information to the word pairs (little, very), (euphoria, bad) and (euphoria, mood) with Comparison relation. metadata.title: TransS-Driven Joint Learning Architecture for Implicit Discourse Relation Recognition

text: Figure 3(b) presents the interaction information for argument representations when the GSL is incorporated. The results indicate that the model exhibits high correlation around the words “little” and “very” with respect to comparative information. A plausible explanation is that our model, through the use of GSL, redirects higher attention from the temporally associated word “Now” to the word pairs (little, very), (euphoria, bad), and (euphoria, mood), which are linked to the Comparison relation. By integrating geometric structure information, our GSL-enhanced model jointly leverages these features alongside semantic information to support discourse relation identification.

Oak sat down meditating for nearly an hour. During this time two black spiders, of the kind common in thatched houses, promenaded the ceiling, ultimately dropping to the floor. This reminded him that if there was one class of manifestation on this matter that he thoroughly understood, it was the instincts of sheep. He left the room, ran across two or three fields towards the flock, got upon a hedge, and looked over among them.

Oak sat meditating for nearly an hour. During this time two black spiders, of the sort commonly found in thatched cottages, made their way along the ceiling and at last dropped to the floor. This reminded him that if there was any class of phenomena in this matter he understood thoroughly, it was the instincts of sheep. He left the room, ran across two or three fields toward the flock, climbed upon a hedge, and looked down among them.

What she uttered was a lengthened sighing, apparently at something in her mind which had led to her presence here. There was a spasmodic abandonment about it as if, in allowing herself to utter the sound the woman’s brain had authorized what it could not regulate. One point was evident in this; that she had been existing in a suppressed state, and not in one of languor, or stagnation.

What escaped her was a long, drawn-out sigh, as though prompted by some thought that had brought her to this place. The sound came with a sudden, unguarded release, as if her mind had permitted what it could no longer control. One thing was clear from this: she had been living in a state of restraint, not of weariness or stillness.

At times, he turned, with desperate determination, resolved to beat this phantom off, though it should look him dead; but the hair rose on his head, and his blood stood still, for it had turned with him and was behind him then. He had kept it before him that morning, but it was behind now—always. He leaned his back against a bank, and felt that it stood above him, visibly out against the cold night-sky. He threw himself upon the road—on his back upon the road. At his head it stood, silent, erect, and still—a living grave-stone, with its epitaph in blood.

Occasionally, he would spin around with frantic resolve, intent on confronting the apparition even if the sight of it killed him. Yet his scalp prickled and his pulse froze, for the thing had pivoted with him, remaining always at his back. During the morning, he had managed to keep it in his line of sight, but now it lingered behind him—constant and inescapable. When he pressed his spine against a dirt embankment, he sensed it looming over him, its silhouette etched clearly against the frigid night sky. He flung himself down onto the road, lying flat on his back. There it remained by his head, mute and motionless—a living headstone, its epitaph written in blood.

He spoke this with a voice so modulated to the different feelings expressed in his speech, with an eye so full of lofty design and heroism, that can you wonder that these men were moved? They looked at one another and were unable to reply. I spoke; I told them to retire and consider of what had been said, that I would not lead them farther north if they strenuously desired the contrary, but that I hoped that, with reflection, their courage would return.

His oratory was delivered with a resonance so perfectly attuned to the shifting passions of his soul, and with a gaze so alight with noble ambition, that it was no marvel the mariners stood transfixed. They traded wordless glances, their tongues bound by the sheer weight of his conviction. I stepped forward then, urging them to withdraw and weigh his exhortations in the quiet of their own minds; I pledged that I would not force our prow deeper into the frozen latitudes should they remain set against it, yet I harbored a silent hope that, with prayerful reflection, their guttering spirits might once more catch fire.

Painters have painted their swarming groups and the centre-figure of all,
From the head of the centre-figure spreading a nimbus of gold-color’d light,
But I paint myriads of heads, but paint no head without its nimbus
of gold-color’d light,
From my hand from the brain of every man and woman it streams,
effulgently flowing forever.

metadata.title: Leaves of Grass

text:
Other painters lay down thronging crowds, anchor one central figure at their heart,
a gilded nimbus fanning outward only from that singular figure’s crown.
I paint unnumbered faces, though, and not a single one goes without its own soft golden halo,
It pours from my brush, from the living spark of every man and woman, glowing bright, unending.

Bathsheba’s was an impulsive nature under a deliberative aspect. An Elizabeth in brain and a Mary Stuart in spirit, she often performed actions of the greatest temerity with a manner of extreme discretion. Many of her thoughts were perfect syllogisms; unluckily they always remained thoughts. Only a few were irrational assumptions; but, unfortunately, they were the ones which most frequently grew into deeds.

Marwenna carried an impulsive, fiery streak beneath a cool, deliberative outward bearing. Endowed with the shrewd, strategic mind of Eleanor of Aquitaine and the reckless, romantic spirit of Isabella of France, she often pulled off acts of staggering boldness with a demeanor of quiet, unflappable discretion. Most of her ruminations were perfectly constructed, logically sound syllogisms; unluckily, they never progressed beyond the realm of idle thought. Only a tiny fraction were ungrounded, impulsive leaps of assumption; yet those, unfortunately, were the notions that most often took root and grew into tangible action.

It amused Silas to his very soul to learn of so peculiar a figure; nevertheless, the news also served to arm him against the coming calamities. In my own experience, when one wanders the roads with a monarch who is feigning a lowlier state—and who manages to recall the deception only half the time—no degree of caution can ever be considered excessive.

“Ah, you cannot know. You have seen farther than I have, and have been into towns and among persons of whom I have only ever heard, and have lived more years than I; yet in this one thing I am older than you. There was another man whom I loved once, and now it is you that I love.”

Not a single bleat. He went to the hedge; a gap had been broken through it, and in the gap were the footprints of the sheep. Rather surprised to find them break fence at this season, yet putting it down instantly to their great fondness for ivy in winter-time, of which a great deal grew in the plantation, he followed through the hedge. They were not in the plantation. He called again: the valleys and farthest hills resounded as when the sailors invoked the lost Hylas on the Mysian shore; but no sheep. He passed through the trees and along the ridge of the hill. On the extreme summit, where the ends of the two converging hedges of which we have spoken were stopped short by meeting the brow of the chalk-pit, he saw the younger dog standing against the sky—dark and motionless as Napoleon at St. Helena.

There was not a single bleat to be heard. He walked over to the hedgerow, where a gap had been torn through the thick, tangled growth, and sheep hoof prints were clearly pressed into the soft earth at the opening. He was rather surprised that the flock would break through fencing at this time of year, but he instantly chalked the behavior up to their well-documented fondness for ivy in the cold winter months, and a great deal of the vine grew thick in the plantation just beyond the hedge. He clambered through the gap. The sheep were nowhere to be seen in the plantation. He called out for them again, his voice bouncing off the valley walls and the farthest hilltops, ringing bright as the cries of sailors invoking the lost Hylas on the Mysian shore; still there was no sign of the flock, no answering bleat. He pushed through the tree line and tramped along the hill’s ridge. At the very summit, where the two converging hedges we have spoken of stop short where they meet the steep brow of the chalk pit, he spotted the younger dog silhouetted against the open sky—dark, utterly motionless, as stoic and still as Napoleon stranded on St. Helena.

He gave her a full account of Clym’s affliction, and of the state in which he was living; then, referring to Thomasin, touched gently upon the apparent sadness of her days. “Now, ma’am, depend upon it,” he said, “you couldn’t do a better thing for either of ’em than to make yourself at home in their houses, even if there should be a little rebuff at first.”

He walked her through every detail of Clym’s debilitating condition and the sparse, isolated rhythm his daily life had fallen into, before shifting the conversation to Thomasin and gently noting the quiet, unmissable sadness that seemed to hang over all her days lately. “Look, ma’am, you can take my word for it,” he said, “there’s no kinder, more useful thing you could do for either of them than to drop by their homes and make yourself comfortable like you belong there, even if you get a small, cold rebuff the first time you show up.”

text: “I know that the sympathy of a stranger can be but of little relief to one borne down as you are by so strange a misfortune. But you will, I hope, soon quit this melancholy abode, for doubtless evidence can easily be brought to free you from the criminal charge.”

“I understand how little solace an unfamiliar voice can offer to someone crushed beneath the weight of such a peculiar calamity. Yet I trust you shall soon depart this sorrowful place, since surely proof can swiftly be gathered to absolve you of this accusation.”

In many cases, capturing the style of a document is equally important as its content: translating a lover's greeting as "I am entirely pleased to see you" might be semantically correct, but seems out of place. Demographic factors (age, gender, etc.) all manifest in language, and therefore influence style: we do not expect a 6-year old to sound like an adult, and would not translate a person to seem differently gendered. However, in this paper, we show such a change is essentially what happens in machine translation: authors sound on average older and more male.

metadata.title: "You Sound Just Like Your Father" Commercial Machine Translation Systems Include Stylistic Biases

text: In many instances, reproducing the style of a document matters as much as conveying its content: rendering a lover’s greeting as “I am entirely pleased to see you” may be semantically accurate, yet it feels incongruous. Demographic variables—such as age and gender—are all expressed in language and consequently shape style: we would not expect a six-year-old to sound like an adult, nor would we translate someone in a way that alters their apparent gender. In this paper, however, we demonstrate that precisely this kind of shift occurs in machine translation: on average, authors end up sounding older and more male.

The _Falaba_ crime, in which the German sailors were reported to have jeered at the drowning victims in the water, was followed by the sinking of the _Lusitania_. At that a wave of real anger swept through the Empire. Hate was begetting hate at last. There were violent riots in Great Britain and in South Africa. Wretched little German hairdressers and bakers and so forth fled for their lives, to pay for the momentary satisfaction of the Kaiser and Herr Ballin. Scores of German homes in England were wrecked and looted; hundreds of Germans maltreated. War is war. Hard upon the _Lusitania_ storm came the publication of the Bryce Report, with its relentless array of witnesses, its particulars of countless acts of cruelty and arrogant unreason and uncleanness in Belgium and the occupied territory of France. Came also the gasping torture of "gas," the use of flame jets, and a new exacerbation of the savagery of the actual fighting. For a time it seemed as though the taking of prisoners along the western front would cease. Tales of torture and mutilation, tales of the kind that arise nowhere and out of nothing, and poison men's minds to the most pitiless retaliations, drifted along the opposing fronts....

The sinking of the _Lusitania_ came fast upon the _Falaba_ atrocity, where German seamen were said to have mocked the drowning as they struggled in the sea. A swell of genuine fury then swept across the Empire. At last, hatred was breeding hatred. Fierce riots erupted in Britain and in South Africa. Pitiful little German barbers and pastry-cooks and the like ran for their lives, paying the price for the Kaiser’s and Herr Ballin’s fleeting gratification. Dozens of German dwellings in England were gutted and plundered; many Germans suffered brutal handling. War is war. Close behind the _Lusitania_ uproar arrived the Bryce Report, with its unsparing procession of witnesses, its details of innumerable deeds of cruelty, arrogant unreason, and foulness in Belgium and occupied France. There came too the choking agony of "gas," the deployment of liquid fire, and a fresh sharpening of the ferocity of the front-line struggle. For a while it seemed as if the taking of captives along the western line might end altogether. Stories of torment and dismemberment, stories of the sort that spring from nowhere and out of nothing, and poison the mind toward the most merciless vengeance, drifted up and down the opposing trenches.

Mr. Carmine thought it would be a long struggle; Mr. Britling thought that the Russians would be in Berlin by the next May. He was afraid they might get there before the end of the year. He thought that the Germans would beat out their strength upon the French and Belgian lines, and never be free to turn upon the Russian at all. He was sure they had underrated the strength and energy of the French and of ourselves. "The Russians meanwhile," he said, "will come on, slowly, steadily, inevitably...."

Mr. Carmine foresaw a protracted, grinding conflict; Mr. Britling, by contrast, imagined Russian troops marching into Berlin by May. He even feared they might arrive before the year was out. In his view, the Germans would exhaust themselves hammering against the French and Belgian fronts, never gaining the freedom to wheel eastward against Russia. He felt certain the strength and resolve of both France and Britain had been sorely underestimated. “The Russians, all the while,” he declared, “will keep advancing—slowly, steadily, inexorably…”

It returns, as the new state, the old state with a "?" on the price.

metadata.title: Zero-Shot Transfer Learning with Synthesized Data for Multi-Domain Dialogue State Tracking

text: As an example, consider the SLOTQUESTION template shown in Fig. 2. In this case, the non-terminals NAME, NP, and ADJ SLOT are expanded into the domain-specific phrases “Curry Garden,” “Indian restaurant in the south of town,” and “expensive,” respectively, and the outputs of their semantic functions, name, np, and adj slot, are (sets of) slot-value pairs: name = “Curry Garden”; {food = “Indian”, area = “south”}; price = “expensive”. The semantic function of SLOTQUESTION verifies that the input state does not already contain a value for the price slot, and that the price has not been mentioned by the agent on this turn. It then returns, as the new state, the previous state with a “?” assigned to the price.

At this moment his heart bounded to hear footsteps running towards him, and two men, roused by Thomasin, appeared at the brink above. They ran to where Venn was, and helped him in lifting out the apparently drowned persons, separating them, and laying them out upon the grass. Venn turned the light upon their faces. The one who had been uppermost was Yeobright; he who had been completely submerged was Wildeve.

At that instant, his pulse leaped at the sound of footsteps racing toward him, and two men, alerted by Thomasin, emerged at the top of the bank. They hurried down to Venn and aided him in hauling out the seemingly drowned figures, parting them from one another and stretching them upon the grass. Venn directed the lantern onto their faces. The one who had lain on top was Yeobright; the one who had been fully underwater was Wildeve.

Monday was a public holiday, the First Monday in August, and the day consecrated by long-established custom to the Matching's Easy Flower Show in Claverings Park. The day was to live in Mr. Britling's memory with a harsh brightness like the brightness of that sunshine one sees at times at the edge of a thunderstorm. There were tents with the exhibits, and a tent for "Popular Refreshments," there was a gorgeous gold and yellow steam roundabout with motor-cars and horses, and another in green and silver with wonderfully undulating ostriches and lions, and each had an organ that went by steam; there were cocoanut shies and many ingenious prize-giving shooting and dart-throwing and ring-throwing stalls, each displaying a marvellous array of crockery, clocks, metal ornaments, and suchlike rewards. There was a race of gas balloons, each with a postcard attached to it begging the finder to say where it descended, and you could get a balloon for a shilling and have a chance of winning various impressive and embarrassing prizes if your balloon went far enough--fish carvers, a silver-handled walking-stick, a bog-oak gramophone-record cabinet, and things like that. And by a special gate one could go for sixpence into the Claverings gardens, and the sixpence would be doubled by Lady Homartyn and devoted next winter to the Matching's Easy coal club. And Mr. Britling went through all the shows with his boys, and finally left them with a shilling each and his blessing and paid his sixpence for the gardens and made his way as he had promised, to have tea with Lady Homartyn.

Monday fell upon the First Monday in August, a bank holiday hallowed by immemorial rite to the Matching's Easy Flower Show within the precincts of Claverings Park. That day would sear itself into Mr. Britling’s recollection with a stark brilliance, akin to the glare that sometimes flares along the hem of a gathering storm. There stood pavilions housing the displays, and a marquee devoted to "Popular Refreshments"; a resplendent gold-and-yellow steam carousel whirled with motorcars and chargers, while another, sheathed in green and silver, bore ostriches and lions that rose and fell in wondrous undulation—each machine possessed a steam-driven organ. Cocoanut shies abounded, together with an array of artful shooting, dart-hurling, and quoit-tossing booths, every one proffering a dazzling hoard of crockery, timepieces, gilt ornaments, and similar trophies. A race of gas-balloons was held, each bearing a postcard that entreated the discoverer to record where it alighted; for a shilling one might purchase a balloon and stand to gain sundry imposing yet awkward prizes should it travel far enough—fish carvers, a silver-mounted walking-stick, a bog-oak cabinet for gramophone records, and objects of that ilk. And through a private gate, for the sum of sixpence, one could enter the gardens of Claverings, that sixpence to be matched by Lady Homartyn and consecrated the following winter to the Matching's Easy coal fund. Mr. Britling traversed the whole fête with his sons, at length bestowing upon each a shilling and his paternal goodwill, then tendered his sixpence at the garden gate and bent his steps, as he had pledged, toward tea with Lady Homartyn.

She talked on in her indignant strain, but he was not following her. He was smiling to himself as he looked up into her virginal face, so innocent, so penetratingly innocent, that its purity seemed always to enter into him, driving out of him all dross and bathing him in some ethereal effulgence that was as cool and soft and velvety as starshine. _We know there are nasty things in the world_! He cuddled to him the notion of her knowing, and chuckled over it as a love joke. The next moment, in a flashing vision of multitudinous detail, he sighted the whole sea of life’s nastiness that he had known and voyaged over and through, and he forgave her for not understanding the story. It was through no fault of hers that she could not understand. He thanked God that she had been born and sheltered to such innocence. But he knew life, its foulness as well as its fairness, its greatness in spite of the slime that infested it, and by God he was going to have his say on it to the world. Saints in heaven—how could they be anything but fair and pure? No praise to them. But saints in slime—ah, that was the everlasting wonder! That was what made life worth while. To see moral grandeur rising out of cesspools of iniquity; to rise himself and first glimpse beauty, faint and far, through mud-dripping eyes; to see out of weakness, and frailty, and viciousness, and all abysmal brutishness, arising strength, and truth, and high spiritual endowment—

Eating his last slices of bread and ham, and drinking from the bottle of cider he had taken the precaution to bring with him, he got into the lonely waggon. Here he spread half of the hay as a bed, and, as well as he could in the darkness, pulled the other half over him by way of bed-clothes, covering himself entirely, and feeling, physically, as comfortable as ever he had been in his life. Inward melancholy it was impossible for a man like Oak, introspective far beyond his neighbours, to banish quite, whilst conning the present untoward page of his history. So, thinking of his misfortunes, amorous and pastoral, he fell asleep, shepherds enjoying, in common with sailors, the privilege of being able to summon the god instead of having to wait for him.

Finishing the last of his bread and ham, and taking a draught from the flask of cider he had wisely brought with him, he climbed into the desolate cart. There he made one half of the hay serve him for a couch, and, as far as the blackness would allow, drew the rest over him in place of coverings, so that he was hidden wholly beneath it, and, in body at least, felt as easy as ever he had done in his days. Yet the inward heaviness it was beyond a man like Dale, who brooded more deeply than those about him, altogether to dismiss while turning over this ill-starred chapter of his life. Thus, meditating on his troubles, both tender and rural, he sank at last to sleep, shepherds possessing, like mariners, the rare advantage of being able to call up the deity rather than wait to be summoned by him.

"Exactly. The village was a little startled. The cottager who had let the rooms came to me privately. Teddy is rather touchy on the point of his personal independence, he considers any demand for explanations as an insult, and probably all he had said to the old lady was, 'This is Letty--come to share my rooms.' I put the matter to him very gently. 'Oh, yes,' he said, rather in the manner of some one who has overlooked a trifle. 'I got married to her in the Christmas holidays. May I bring her along to see Mrs. Britling?' We induced him to go into a little cottage I rent. The wife was the daughter of a Colchester journalist and printer. I don't know if you talked to her."

Precisely. The local community was somewhat taken aback. The woman who had leased him the rooms came to see me in private. Teddy is quite defensive about his personal freedom; he perceives any request for an explanation as a slight. He probably told the elderly woman nothing more than, "This is Letty—she’s staying in my rooms now." I approached the topic with him very delicately. "Oh, that," he remarked, much like someone who had forgotten a minor detail. "We got married during the Christmas break. Would it be alright if I brought her to meet Mrs. Britling?" Eventually, we persuaded him to move into a small cottage I own. His wife is the daughter of a journalist and printer from Colchester. I’m not sure if you’ve had a conversation with her yet.

• MERGE NODES: each node in the tree represents only one word, which may lead to a large and noisy semantic graph especially for long documents. To ensure that the semantic graph only retains important semantic relations, we merge consecutive nodes that form a complete semantic unit. To be specific, we apply a simple yet effective rule: merging a node v with its child c if they form a consecutive modifier, i.e., both the type of v and c are modifier, and v and c is consecutive in the sentence.

metadata.title: Semantic Graphs for Generating Deep Questions

text: MERGE NODES: each node in the tree represents only a single word, which may result in a large and noisy semantic graph, particularly for long documents. To ensure that the semantic graph preserves only salient semantic relations, we merge consecutive nodes that together constitute a complete semantic unit. Specifically, we adopt a simple yet effective rule: a node v is merged with its child c when they form a consecutive modifier, that is, when both v and c are of the modifier type and they appear consecutively in the sentence.

He was still too weak to get up to breakfast; but, when he came down into the housekeeper’s room next day, his first act was to cast an eager glance at the wall, in the hope of again looking on the face of the beautiful lady. His expectations were disappointed, however, for the picture had been removed.

Philip, speedily recovering from the fainting fit into which Mr. Harcourt’s sudden exclamation had thrown him, found the matter of the portrait carefully shunned, both by the old man and by Mrs. Garland, in the talk that followed; which, indeed, had no bearing on Philip’s past or future, but was kept to such subjects as might divert him without stirring his feelings. He was still too feeble to rise for breakfast; but when, on the next day, he came down into the steward’s room, his first impulse was to cast a hungry glance toward the wall, hoping once more to see the fair lady’s face. His hope, however, was vain; for the picture had been taken away.

All the air round was so thick and dark, the people were so passionately revengeful and fitful, the innocent were so constantly put to death on vague suspicion and black malice, it was so impossible to forget that many as blameless as her husband and as dear to others as he was to her, every day shared the fate from which he had been clutched, that her heart could not be as lightened of its load as she felt it ought to be. The shadows of the wintry afternoon were beginning to fall, and even now the dreadful carts were rolling through the streets. Her mind pursued them, looking for him among the Condemned; and then she clung closer to his real presence and trembled more.

The atmosphere was so dense and oppressive, the crowds so savagely vindictive and erratic, the blameless so frequently executed on mere suspicion and dark spite, and it was so impossible to dismiss the thought that many as guiltless as her husband and as cherished by others as he was by her daily met the doom he had narrowly escaped, that her spirit could not shake off its burden as she knew it should. The gloom of the winter dusk was creeping in, and even at that hour the grim tumbrels were rumbling through the thoroughfares. Her thoughts tracked them, seeking his face among the doomed; then she pressed nearer to his living form and shivered more violently.

Some hours passed thus; but by degrees, as the sun declined towards the horizon, the wind died away into a gentle breeze and the sea became free from breakers. But these gave place to a heavy swell; I felt sick and hardly able to hold the rudder, when suddenly I saw a line of high land towards the south.

Several hours elapsed in this fashion; but slowly, as the sun dipped toward the horizon, the wind softened into a light breeze and the breaking waves subsided. These were replaced by a heavy, rolling swell; I felt overcome by nausea and could scarcely maintain my grip on the rudder, when suddenly I glimpsed the silhouette of high land toward the south.

The words of the true poems give you more than poems,
They give you to form for yourself poems, religions, politics, war,
peace, behavior, histories, essays, daily life, and every thing else,
They balance ranks, colors, races, creeds, and the sexes,
They do not seek beauty, they are sought,
Forever touching them or close upon them follows beauty, longing,
fain, love-sick.

text: The wine was red wine, and had stained the ground of the narrow street in the suburb of Saint Antoine, in Paris, where it was spilled. It had stained many hands, too, and many faces, and many naked feet, and many wooden shoes. The hands of the man who sawed the wood, left red marks on the billets; and the forehead of the woman who nursed her baby, was stained with the stain of the old rag she wound about her head again. Those who had been greedy with the staves of the cask, had acquired a tigerish smear about the mouth; and one tall joker so besmirched, his head more out of a long squalid bag of a nightcap than in it, scrawled upon a wall with his finger dipped in muddy wine-lees--BLOOD.

The wine was red wine, and it had stained the ground of the narrow street in the Saint Antoine suburb of Paris where it had been spilled. It had stained many hands as well, and many faces, and many bare feet, and many wooden shoes. The hands of the man sawing the wood left red marks on the billets; and the forehead of the woman nursing her baby was marked by the stain of the old rag she had wound around her head again. Those who had been greedy with the staves of the cask had gained a savage-looking smear around their mouths; and one lank joker, so begrimed that his head seemed more out of a long filthy nightcap-bag than in it, wrote on a wall with his finger dipped in the muddy dregs of the wine—BLOOD.

With boys it was different. Such as have been under our training from seven to ten years have had no acquaintance with the Church's terrors, and it was among these that I found my fifty-two. Oliver had looked from one to the other, while these words were being spoken, as if he were bewildered, and could scarecely understand what passed; but when Bill Sikes concluded, he jumped suddenly to his feet, and tore wildly from the room: uttering shrieks for help, which made the bare old house echo to the roof.

As the conversation played out, Oliver flicked his gaze back and forth between the two speakers, stupefied as though in a trance, barely able to grasp what was unfolding around him. The second Bill Sikes stopped speaking, however, he bolted abruptly to his feet, tore wildly out of the room, and shrieked for help so loud his cries bounced off the walls of the bare, aging house and rang all the way up to its roof.

But Martin did not shout out. The thought gnawed in his brain, an unceasing torment, while he smiled and succeeded in being tolerant. As he grew silent, Bernard Higginbotham got the reins and did the talking. He was a success himself, and proud of it. He was self-made. No one had helped him. He owed no man. He was fulfilling his duty as a citizen and bringing up a large family. And there was Higginbotham’s Cash Store, that monument of his own industry and ability. He loved Higginbotham’s Cash Store as some men loved their wives. He opened up his heart to Martin, showed with what keenness and with what enormous planning he had made the store. And he had plans for it, ambitious plans. The neighborhood was growing up fast. The store was really too small. If he had more room, he would be able to put in a score of labor-saving and money-saving improvements. And he would do it yet. He was straining every effort for the day when he could buy the adjoining lot and put up another two-story frame building. The upstairs he could rent, and the whole ground-floor of both buildings would be Higginbotham’s Cash Store. His eyes glistened when he spoke of the new sign that would stretch clear across both buildings.

But Martin did not cry out. The idea kept gnawing at his mind, a constant torment, while he kept smiling and managed, somehow, to stay polite. When he fell silent, Bernard Higginbotham took over the reins and the conversation. He was a success himself, and he knew it. He had made himself. No one had given him a hand. He owed nothing to anybody. He was doing his duty as a citizen and raising a large family. And then there was Higginbotham’s Cash Store, that proof of his own hard work and ability. He loved Higginbotham’s Cash Store the way some men loved their wives. He unburdened himself to Martin, showing with what care and what vast foresight he had built up the store. And he had plans for it, big plans. The neighborhood was growing quickly. The store was really too cramped. If only he had more space, he could install a dozen labor-saving and money-saving improvements. And he would do it yet. He was putting all his energy into the day when he could buy the lot next door and put up another two-story frame building. He could rent the upper floor, and the entire ground floor of both buildings would become Higginbotham’s Cash Store. His eyes shone as he talked about the new sign that would run all the way across both buildings.

Shepherds who attended with their flocks from long distances started from home two or three days, or even a week, before the fair, driving their charges a few miles each day—not more than ten or twelve—and resting them at night in hired fields by the wayside at previously chosen points, where they fed, having fasted since morning. The shepherd of each flock marched behind, a bundle containing his kit for the week strapped upon his shoulders, and in his hand his crook, which he used as the staff of his pilgrimage. Several of the sheep would get worn and lame, and occasionally a lambing occurred on the road. To meet these contingencies, there was frequently provided, to accompany the flocks from the remoter points, a pony and waggon into which the weakly ones were taken for the remainder of the journey.

Shepherds arriving from distant regions set out two or three days, or sometimes a full week, before the fair. They drove their livestock slowly, covering no more than ten or twelve miles a day, and stopped each night at pre-selected fields rented along the way. There, the animals finally grazed after a day spent on the move without food. The shepherd walked at the back of his flock, a pack containing his supplies for the week cinched to his shoulders, using his crook as a staff for the long journey. Along the route, some sheep would inevitably grow weary or start to limp, and occasionally a lamb was born mid-trek. To manage these occurrences, a pony and cart often accompanied the flocks from the more remote areas, providing a ride for the weak or injured for the rest of the trip.

Little by little, Mrs. Vale collapsed like a starched garment slipping from a hook. Long afterward, folds remained in her mind and in her body. Now and then her inquisitive lodgers would ask what the doctor had said to her.

“It seems so babyish for me to be going to night school. But I wouldn’t mind that if I thought it would pay. But I don’t think it will pay. I can do the work quicker than they can teach me. It would be a loss of time—” he thought of her and his desire to have her—“and I can’t afford the time. I haven’t the time to spare, in fact.”

“It feels so childish, attending evening classes at my age. Still, I could bear that if I believed it would be worth the effort. But I doubt it will be. I can pick things up faster than any instructor could impart them. It would only squander my hours—” his mind turned to her and the ache to possess her—“and time is the one luxury I cannot afford. Truthfully, I have none to lose.”

RNNG Recurrent neural network grammars (RNNG; Dyer et al., 2016) model the joint probability of a sequence of words and its syntactic structure. RNNG requires labeled trees that contain complete constituency parses, which we produce for BLLIP sentences with an off-the-shelf constituency parser (Kitaev and Klein, 2018) . 5 To compute surprisals from RNNG, we use wordsynchronous beam search (Stern et al., 2017) to approximate the conditional probability of the current word given the context. Transformer Transformer models (Vaswani et al., 2017) have recently gained popularity in language processing tasks. We use GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019) as a representative Transformer model and train it from scratch on our BLLIP corpora. 6

text: Next, we take a random subset of 100,000 segment pairs from the corpus for training a logistic regression classifier. To extract features for the logistic regression to be trained on, we use another set of filters from the OpusFilter toolbox: Figure 1a shows histograms of the scores over the 100,000 training segments pairs in the data, produced by the opusfilter-scores tool. The distribution of the cross-entropy values is quite unimodal, indicating that such a score alone does not make a clear division of the segment pairs as clean or noisy. Language identification scores are mostly close to one, but zero for a small fraction of the segments, indicating that they contain incorrect languages. Also, non-zero numerals and terminal punctuation scores show that a small number of samples look problematic. Word alignment scores have an interesting close-to-bimodal distribution. Smaller values indicate better alignment, so the lower peak is for more problematic segment pairs.

metadata.title: OpusFilter: A Configurable Parallel Corpus Filtering Toolbox

text: We then sample a random subset of 100,000 segment pairs from the corpus to train a logistic regression classifier. To derive features for this classifier, we employ an additional collection of filters from the OpusFilter toolbox. Figure 1a presents score histograms for the 100,000 training segment pairs, generated with the opusfilter-scores tool. The cross-entropy score exhibits a largely unimodal distribution, suggesting that this metric alone cannot cleanly separate clean and noisy segment pairs. Language identification scores concentrate near one, with a small fraction at zero, signaling that those segments contain incorrect languages. Similarly, non-zero scores for numerals and terminal punctuation indicate that a limited number of samples appear problematic. The word alignment score displays a notably near-bimodal distribution; since lower values reflect better alignment, the lower peak corresponds to more problematic segment pairs.

“I knew,” said Defarge, looking down at his wife, who stood at the bottom of the steps on which he was raised, looking steadily up at him; “I knew that this prisoner, of whom I speak, had been confined in a cell known as One Hundred and Five, North Tower. I knew it from himself. He knew himself by no other name than One Hundred and Five, North Tower, when he made shoes under my care. As I serve my gun that day, I resolve, when the place shall fall, to examine that cell. It falls. I mount to the cell, with a fellow-citizen who is one of the Jury, directed by a gaoler. I examine it, very closely. In a hole in the chimney, where a stone has been worked out and replaced, I find a written paper. This is that written paper. I have made it my business to examine some specimens of the writing of Doctor Manette. This is the writing of Doctor Manette. I confide this paper, in the writing of Doctor Manette, to the hands of the President.”

“I was certain,” Villon declared, casting his gaze down toward Elise, who remained at the foot of the dais, her eyes fixed unwaveringly upon his own. “I was aware that the captive in question had been interred within a chamber designated as Cell Sixty-Four of the West Wing. This fact I learned from his own lips. While he labored at the cobbler’s bench under my supervision, he recognized no identity save for that of Sixty-Four, West Wing. On the day I manned the cannon, I vowed that should the fortress be taken, I would search that very room. The stronghold yielded. Accompanied by a turnkey and a compatriot from the Council, I ascended to the quarters. I scrutinized the space with the utmost care. Within a cavity in the hearth, where a block had been loosened and then reset, I discovered a manuscript. This is the document I recovered. I have taken pains to study various examples of Professor Valerius’s script. This script belongs to Professor Valerius. I now entrust this record, penned by the hand of Professor Valerius, to the custody of the Magistrate.”

Thus encouraged, Oliver tapped at the study door. On Mr. Brownlow calling to him to come in, he found himself in a little back room, quite full of books, with a window, looking into some pleasant little gardens. There was a table drawn up before the window, at which Mr. Brownlow was seated reading. When he saw Oliver, he pushed the book away from him, and told him to come near the table, and sit down. Oliver complied; marvelling where the people could be found to read such a great number of books as seemed to be written to make the world wiser. Which is still a marvel to more experienced people than Oliver Twist, every day of their lives.

Urged forward by this kindness, Edwin gave a soft knock upon the library door. At Mr. Ashworth’s summons to enter, he stepped into a modest rear chamber, densely lined with volumes, its casement overlooking a cluster of tranquil garden plots. Drawn close to the window stood a writing desk, where Mr. Ashworth sat absorbed in a book. Upon noticing Edwin, he slid the volume aside and bade him approach the desk and take a seat. Edwin obeyed, wondering all the while where enough readers could be discovered to peruse so vast a collection of books—books that seemed designed to render the world more knowing. A wonder, indeed, that still perplexes souls far older than Edwin Ashworth, each and every day of their existence.

“That story was perfectly grand,” she announced, straightening up from the wash-tub with a tired sigh and wiping the sweat from her forehead with a red, steamy hand; “but it makes me sad. I want to cry. There is too many sad things in the world anyway. It makes me happy to think about happy things. Now if he’d married her, and—You don’t mind, Mart?” she queried apprehensively. “I just happen to feel that way, because I’m tired, I guess. But the story was grand just the same, perfectly grand. Where are you goin’ to sell it?”

“That story was totally fantastic,” she said, pushing herself upright from the laundry tub with a weary sigh, swiping beaded sweat off her forehead with one red, steamy hand still damp from the hot soapy suds; “but it makes me feel so down. I could burst into tears right now. There’s already way too much sad stuff in the world as it is. I’m so much happier when I’m thinking about nice, upbeat things. If he’d just ended up marrying her, y’know, and—you don’t mind me saying that, right, Mart?” she asked, a little anxious she’d rambled out of turn. “I’m probably just reacting like this ‘cause I’m dead tired today, is all. But the story really was amazing, don’t get me wrong, totally great. Where are you planning to sell it, anyway?”

For a long time I was their only care. My mother had much desired to have a daughter, but I continued their single offspring. When I was about five years old, while making an excursion beyond the frontiers of Italy, they passed a week on the shores of the Lake of Como. Their benevolent disposition often made them enter the cottages of the poor. This, to my mother, was more than a duty; it was a necessity, a passion—remembering what she had suffered, and how she had been relieved—for her to act in her turn the guardian angel to the afflicted. During one of their walks a poor cot in the foldings of a vale attracted their notice as being singularly disconsolate, while the number of half-clothed children gathered about it spoke of penury in its worst shape. One day, when my father had gone by himself to Milan, my mother, accompanied by me, visited this abode. She found a peasant and his wife, hard working, bent down by care and labour, distributing a scanty meal to five hungry babes. Among these there was one which attracted my mother far above all the rest. She appeared of a different stock. The four others were dark-eyed, hardy little vagrants; this child was thin and very fair. Her hair was the brightest living gold, and despite the poverty of her clothing, seemed to set a crown of distinction on her head. Her brow was clear and ample, her blue eyes cloudless, and her lips and the moulding of her face so expressive of sensibility and sweetness that none could behold her without looking on her as of a distinct species, a being heaven-sent, and bearing a celestial stamp in all her features.

For years I stood as the sole focus of my parents’ devotion. My mother had long ached for a little girl to raise alongside me, but I remained their only child for nearly a decade. When I was about five years old, while we were traveling through the borderlands of southern France, my parents spent a week camped along the shores of Lac d’Annecy. Their generous natures often drew them to stop at the humble cottages of local peasant families. For my mother, these visits were far more than an obligation; they were a compulsion, a deep calling, for she remembered her own childhood of hardship, and the strangers who had lifted her out of it, and she longed to pay that kindness forward as a guardian to those suffering misfortune. On one of their afternoon rambles, a ragged little cottage tucked into the crook of a valley caught their eye, looking unusually forlorn, the cluster of shivering, half-dressed children loitering on its threshold making plain the family was crippled by dire poverty. One day, when my father had traveled alone to Grenoble on business, my mother brought me with her to call on the household. Inside, she found a farmer and his wife, both hardworking but bowed low by constant worry and backbreaking toil, doling out a meager crust of bread and thin broth to their five ravenous small children. Of all the children, one caught my mother’s gaze instantly, holding it far longer than the rest. She seemed to come from an entirely different line. The four other children had dark, flashing eyes and the rough, sturdy bearing of little urchins used to running wild in the hills; this girl was slight, and pale as a lily blossom. Her hair was the color of spun sunlight, and even tangled and unkempt beneath her tattered shawl, it looked like a gilded crown marking her apart. Her forehead was smooth and broad, her blue eyes as clear as a summer sky, and the curve of her lips and the soft shape of her face held such gentle warmth and quiet sensitivity that no one could look at her without thinking she was something other than ordinary, a creature sent down from the heavens, every line of her face bearing the mark of something divine.

The sun went down in an ochreous mist; but they sat, and talked on, and grew as merry as the gods in Homer’s heaven. Bathsheba still remained enthroned inside the window, and occupied herself in knitting, from which she sometimes looked up to view the fading scene outside. The slow twilight expanded and enveloped them completely before the signs of moving were shown.

The sun sank into a thick, amber mist; yet they remained, talking on until they were as joyous as the gods of Homeric myth. Bathsheba kept her seat by the window, busy with her knitting, though she would look up every so often to observe the darkening world outside. The slow-creeping twilight deepened and eventually swallowed them whole before any move was made to depart.
