The fountain stood slightly apart, where the street widened into a small square, perhaps ten or twelve yards across. When the tall man abruptly rose from the earth and rushed toward the carriage, Monsieur the Marquis swiftly laid his hand upon the hilt of his sword for a brief moment.

Slavery was dead and gone; all men were equal before the law; taxation had been equalized. The telegraph, the telephone, the phonograph, the typewriter, the sewing-machine, and all the thousand willing and handy servants of steam and electricity were working their way into favor. We had a steamboat or two on the Thames, we had steam warships, and the beginnings of a steam commercial marine; I was getting ready to send out an expedition to discover America.

Chattel slavery had been fully abolished; every person stood equal under the nation’s legal code; tax burdens had been standardized fairly across all ranks of society. Wire telegraphs, domestic telephones, wax cylinder phonographs, office typewriters, motorized sewing machines, and thousands of other nimble, obedient innovations powered by steam and electricity were steadily growing in popularity across households and workplaces. We already operated a pair of steam ferries up and down the Severn, boasted a small fleet of steam-powered naval warships, and were laying the first foundations of a transoceanic steam merchant fleet; I was just putting the final touches on plans to launch an exploratory expedition to discover the Americas.

“Our poor Parson Thirdly can’t get no money to buy such rings,” said Matthew Moon, thoughtfully. “And as good a man as ever walked. I don’t believe poor Thirdly have a single one, even of humblest tin or copper. Such a great ornament as they’d be to him on a dull afternoon, when he’s up in the pulpit lighted by the wax candles! But ’tis impossible, poor man. Ah, to think how unequal things be.”

Our poor Reverend Thirdly hasn't a penny to purchase rings like those," said Matthew Moon, musing. "And he's as fine a soul as ever lived. I doubt poor Thirdly owns even one, not the simplest tin or copper band. What a splendid adornment they'd make for him on a dreary afternoon, standing in the pulpit under the glow of the wax candles! But it can't be done, the poor fellow. Ah, just think how uneven life is.

If you do not know Bogle’s Chop House and Family Restaurant it is your loss. For if you are one of the fortunate ones who dine expensively you should be interested to know how the other half consumes provisions. And if you belong to the half to whom waiters’ checks are things of moment, you should know Bogle’s, for there you get your money’s worth—in quantity, at least.

If you’ve never stepped foot in Mabel’s Hearth Grill and Family Diner, you’re missing out. Because if you count yourself among the fortunate few who regularly splurge on high-end meals, you ought to be curious about how the other half eats their fill. And if you belong to the group for whom every line on a server’s check feels like a pressing concern, you need to seek out Mabel’s: there, you get every bit of what you pay for, in sheer volume at the very least.

"That's just how I feel about this war business. There's no real death over here. It's laid out and boxed up. And accidents are all padded about. If one got a toss from a horse here, you'd be in bed and comfortable in no time.... And there; it's like another planet. It's outside.... I'm going outside.... Instead of there being no death anywhere, it is death everywhere, outside there. We shall be using our utmost wits to kill each other. A kind of reverse to this world."

“That’s exactly how I feel about this whole war business. There isn’t any real death over here. Everything is arranged and packed away neatly. Even accidents are cushioned on all sides. If a man were thrown from a horse here, he’d be lying in bed, comfortable, before long.... And there—it’s like some other planet. It’s outside.... I’m going outside.... Instead of there being no death anywhere, death is everywhere out there, outside. We’ll have to use all our ingenuity to kill one another. It’s the opposite of this world.”

From this day, Oliver was seldom left alone; but was placed in almost constant communication with the two boys, who played the old game with the Jew every day: whether for their own improvement or Oliver’s, Mr. Fagin best knew. At other times the old man would tell them stories of robberies he had committed in his younger days: mixed up with so much that was droll and curious, that Oliver could not help laughing heartily, and showing that he was amused in spite of all his better feelings.

Henceforth, Julian was rarely granted a moment’s privacy, being kept in the perpetual company of the two urchins who daily reenacted their habitual charade with the master. Whether these exercises were meant to sharpen their own dexterity or to entice Julian’s, the old man kept to himself. In the quiet hours, the elder would regale them with chronicles of the thefts he had committed in his prime, weaving in such whimsical and eccentric details that Julian found himself laughing aloud, his mirth surfacing in direct defiance of his more virtuous instincts.

The structure of the paper is as follows. In Section 2 we describe the recent related work. In Section 3 we present, compare and analyze the corpora used to train our contextualized embeddings, and the treebanks used to train our POS tagging and parsing models. In Section 4 we examine and describe in detail the model used for our contextualized word representations, as well as the parser and the tagger we chose to evaluate the impact of corpora in the embeddings' performance in downstream tasks. Finally we provide an analysis of our results in Section 5 and in Section 6 we present our conclusions.

metadata.title: A Monolingual Approach to Contextualized Word Embeddings for Mid-Resource Languages

text: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 reviews recent related work. In Section 3, we present, compare, and analyze the corpora used to train our contextualized embeddings, as well as the treebanks employed for our POS tagging and parsing models. Section 4 provides a detailed description of the model used for our contextualized word representations, alongside the parser and tagger selected to evaluate the impact of training corpora on embedding performance in downstream tasks. Finally, Section 5 offers an analysis of our results, and Section 6 presents our conclusions.

In this paper, we conducted a thorough evaluation of the TACRED RE task. We validated the 5k most challenging examples in development and test set and showed that labeling is a major error source, accounting for 8% absolute F1 error on the test set. This clearly highlights the need for careful evaluation of development and test splits when creating datasets via crowdsourcing. To improve the evaluation accuracy and reliability of future RE methods, we provide a revised, extensively relabeled TACRED. In addition, we categorized model misclassifications into 9 common RE error categories and observed that models are often unable to predict a relation, even if it is expressed explicitly. Models also frequently do not recognize argument roles correctly, or ignore the sentential context. In an automated evaluation we verified our error hypotheses on the whole test split and showed that two groups of ambiguous relations are responsible for most of the remaining errors. We also showed that models adopt heuristics when en-tities are unmasked and proposed that evaluation metrics should consider an instance's difficulty.

metadata.title: TACRED Revisited: A Thorough Evaluation of the TACRED Relation Extraction Task

text: In this paper, we carried out a comprehensive evaluation of the TACRED RE task. We manually validated the 5k most difficult examples in the development and test sets and demonstrated that labeling is a major source of error, accounting for an absolute 8% F1 loss on the test set. This clearly underscores the importance of carefully evaluating development and test splits when constructing datasets through crowdsourcing. To improve the accuracy and reliability of future RE evaluations, we release a revised and extensively relabeled version of TACRED. Moreover, we classified model mispredictions into 9 common RE error categories and found that models are often unable to predict a relation even when it is explicitly expressed. Models also frequently fail to identify argument roles correctly, or they disregard the sentential context. In an automated analysis, we verified our error hypotheses on the full test split and showed that two groups of ambiguous relations account for most of the remaining errors. We also found that models rely on heuristics when entities are unmasked and proposed that evaluation metrics should take an instance’s difficulty into account.
